name: Al-Ma'alem
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership structure with hereditary sheikhs
description: Al-Ma'alem is a tribal group found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Gulf region. They are known to have traditional pastoral and settled communities across different areas. The tribe maintains its traditional social structure and continues to be active in contemporary Gulf society. Their exact origins and detailed historical narratives require further documentation.
history:
Al-Ma'alem (Arabic: المعاليم) is documented as a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy Arabs through the genealogical line of Banu Lam. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the Dhafeer genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. Al-Ma'alem forms part of Ahl al-Sumada, one of the two main divisions of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar.

The ancestral homes of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, and by extension Al-Ma'alem, can be traced back to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina in the Hejaz region. The tribe initially lived in the desert east of Medina before migrating to central Najd, and later to the desert regions of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia). This territory encompassed the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait, where they lived as Bedouins without proper permanent settlements.

The Al-Dhafeer tribe, including the Al-Ma'alem branch, spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration patterns typical of Bedouin tribes. They were nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their distinctive habit of conducting raids alongside women and children, demonstrating their nomadic lifestyle and tribal cohesion. The tribe participated in significant historical events, including providing cavalry and fighters to support the House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain during the Persian-Bahrain conflict of 1782.

Around 1926, a major transformation occurred when Emir Ibn Saud, after conquering large portions of Arabia, allowed the Saudi branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe to settle in several permanent settlements after securing the roads. This marked the transition from their traditional nomadic lifestyle to settled communities. As a branch of Al-Dhafeer, Al-Ma'alem would have been part of these historical movements and settlements, maintaining their traditional social structure while becoming integrated into contemporary Gulf society, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait where most Al-Dhafeer tribal members now reside.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration from southern Arabia following tribal confederations northward
Expansion into oasis settlements for trade opportunities
Some branches moved to coastal areas during Ottoman influence period

name: Mishagin
description: Al Mashghouni (المشاغين) is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited documentation exists about their specific origins, territorial base, or major historical role in Gulf affairs. They appear to be a smaller tribal entity that may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated from their original territories.
history: The Mishagin (Al Mashghouni) are documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation. According to the Bani Yas genealogical structure, the Al Mashghouni/Mishagin formed part of this larger confederation that originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. As members of the Bani Yas confederation, they would have participated in the major tribal migrations, including the settlement in the Liwa oasis around 1700, and the subsequent establishment of Abu Dhabi in 1795 when the Al Bu Falah discovered fresh water on Abu Dhabi island. The Mishagin are listed among the more than 20 sections that contemporary leading members of the Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi identify as forming part of the confederation. Like other Bani Yas sections, they experienced the demographic changes of the 20th century, when the confederation's population declined from approximately 12,000 in the early 1900s to about 8,000 by the 1950s due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment opportunities in oil and construction sectors.

migrationPath.description: Settlement with Bani Yas confederation in Liwa oasis under Al Bu Falah leadership
Migration to Abu Dhabi island following discovery of fresh water and establishment of the town

timelineEvents.description: Mishagin settle in Liwa oasis as part of Bani Yas confederation under Al Bu Falah leadership

name: Maharibah
legitimacyNotes: Established tribal lineage with traditional leadership structure
description: Al Maharibah is a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe has maintained its traditional structure and continues to exist in modern times. Like many Arabian tribes, they have adapted to contemporary Gulf society while preserving their tribal identity and customs.
history:
The Al Mehairbi (Maharibah) tribal section has origins that trace back to the Na'im tribe rather than being originally part of the Bani Yas confederation. According to historical sources, they later became incorporated into the larger Bani Yas tribal confederation under Abu Dhabi's authority. By the early 19th century, the Maharibah had established themselves as one of the constituent sections of the Bani Yas, sharing in the confederation's territorial domain that largely corresponded to what became the Emirate of Abu Dhabi.

During the 1820s, the tribe was led by Suwaidan bin Za'al, who was characterized by British sources as 'the wild head of the Maharibah tribe.' His leadership proved problematic for regional stability, as he and his followers continued to engage in maritime piracy despite the 1820 General Maritime Treaty established between the Trucial States and Britain. The British Resident at Bushire specifically pressured Sheikh Tahnun bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan, the ruler of Abu Dhabi from 1818-1833, to control Suwaidan bin Za'al's activities and bring the Maharibah section into compliance with the maritime peace agreements.

As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Maharibah were among the tribal sections that constituted the largest single tribe in what is now the United Arab Emirates during the early 20th century. According to J.C. Lorimer's Gazetteer, the entire Bani Yas confederation was estimated at around 12,000 people in the early 1900s, though the specific settlements and population numbers of the Maharibah within this confederation are not detailed in available historical records. The Bani Yas confederation as a whole experienced significant population decline over the 20th century, falling to approximately 8,000 by the early 1950s due to emigration for employment opportunities in neighboring Gulf states.

The Maharibah section represents one of the examples of how the Bani Yas confederation evolved through the incorporation of tribes with distinct original identities. Contemporary leading members of the Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi identify more than 20 sections as forming part of the confederation, with the Maharibah (Al Mehairbi) maintaining their position as one of the recognized branches alongside other major sections such as the Al Bu Falah, Al Bu Falasah, Al Qemzi, and others.

migrationPath.description: Post-Islamic conquest migration toward the Gulf coast following trade opportunities and political changes
Gradual settlement in northern Omani territories during the medieval period
Movement to coastal areas during the expansion of maritime trade in the 18th century

name: Al Mira
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Al Mira traces their lineage to an ancestor named Mira who settled in the eastern regions of the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe developed as a distinct branch with pastoral and trading activities.
description: Al Mira is a tribal branch historically based in the eastern Arabian Peninsula with traditional pastoral and trading activities. The tribe has maintained its identity through centuries of settlement and migration patterns common to the region. They have been part of the broader tribal confederation networks that characterized Gulf society. The tribe remains active today with members dispersed across various Gulf states.

relations.context: both tribes practiced nomadic pastoralism in eastern Arabia
historical cooperation in tribal affairs

history:
Al Mira was one of the two main branches of the Banu Rabi'ah, a sub-branch of the ancient Arab tribe Tayy (also known as Tayyids), alongside their kinsmen Al Fadl. The Al Mira descended from Mira (also spelled Al Murrah), son of Rabi'ah ibn Hazim ibn Ali ibn Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah. The Banu Rabi'ah were descendants of the 10th-century Jarrahid rulers of Palestine and became prominent in Syria through their participation in the Muslim war effort against the Crusaders after 1099. By 1107, both Al Mira and their rival branch Al Fadl were noted as emirs providing mounted auxiliary troops for the Burid ruler of Damascus.

At some point during Ayyubid rule in the late 12th or early 13th century, Al Mira drove their kinsmen Al Fadl out of Hauran in southern Syria, forcing the latter to migrate north. This territorial conquest established Al Mira's dominance in southern Syria while their Al Fadl kinsmen controlled northern Syria. During the Mamluk period, Al Mira's territory extended from the Golan Heights (Jawlan) southward to the al-Harrah hot springs region north of Mecca in the Hejaz, giving them control over a vast stretch of southern Syria and western Arabia.

Under Mamluk rule, Al Mira's emir held the title of malik al-ʿarab (king of the Bedouin), a position that was lower in rank than the amir al-ʿarab title held by their Al Fadl kinsmen. Al Mira's strongest opposition came from their Al Fadl kinsmen under Ahmad ibn Hajji, creating a rivalry between the two branches of Banu Rabi'ah. However, this enmity gradually dissipated as Ahmad was given virtual independence in the southern desert while remaining under overall Mamluk suzerainty, allowing both branches to coexist within the broader tribal confederation networks that characterized the region.

migrationPath.description: Banu Rabi'ah became prominent in Syria as a result of their participation in the Muslim war effort against the Crusaders
Al Mira drove their kinsmen Al Fadl out of Hauran during Ayyubid rule

name: Al-Muntafiq
legitimacyNotes: Saadoun family leadership over confederation of tribes in southern Iraq
ancestorStory: Named after Muntafiq ibn Uqayl of the Banu Uqayl tribe. The confederation was later dominated by the Saadoun family who claimed Sharifian descent.
description: Al-Muntafiq was a major tribal confederation based in southern Iraq, primarily in the areas around Basra and along the lower Euphrates. Led by the Saadoun family from the 17th century onwards, they controlled vast territories and numerous smaller tribes, becoming one of the most powerful tribal entities in Ottoman Iraq. The confederation had significant influence over trade routes and tribal movements between Iraq and the Arabian Peninsula. Their power declined in the late 19th and early 20th centuries under Ottoman centralization efforts.

relations.context: Albu Salih were originally part of the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation

history:
Al-Muntafiq was a large Arab tribal confederation that emerged in southern Iraq and Kuwait, with most tribes tracing their genealogy to Banu Uqayl of the ancient Banu Amir confederation from Najd. The confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and established control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. The confederation is organized into three main branches: Bani Malik, al-Ajwad, and Bani Sa'id, with numerous sub-tribes under each division including notable groups like Albu Salih, Al Ibrahim, Al Usoom, and Al Shuwailat among many others.

Despite most members tracing ancestry to Banu Uqayl, the confederation's traditional leaders are Al-Saadun (the house of Saadun), who claim Sharifian descent from Mecca and remain Sunni Muslims. The al-Ajwad branch is said to partially originate from the ancient Arab tribe of Tayy. This diverse genealogical composition reflects the confederation's complex tribal structure built through centuries of alliances and intermarriage.

In 1521, Al-Muntafiq successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif in eastern Saudi Arabia on behalf of the Ottomans before being expelled by Banu Khalid. During the Ottoman era, most confederation members transitioned from nomadic pastoralism to sedentary agricultural life in southern and western Iraq. From the late eighteenth century onwards, the majority converted to Shia Islam while their nominal leaders, the Al Saadun, remained Sunni.

The confederation held significant territorial influence, with the city of Nasiriya named after one of their sheikhs and the surrounding area known as 'Al-Muntafiq Province' until 1976. Led by figures like Yusuf Beg of the Saadun clan, they maintained traditional rivalries with the Dhufir confederation and Ibn Saud, though occasional cooperation occurred. After decades of sedentarization, tribal bonds have weakened considerably and Al Saadun leadership has become largely nominal. Today, many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belong to this confederation, and the religious composition remains mixed between Sunni and Shia tribes, making it difficult to delineate precise membership boundaries.

Temporary occupation of eastern Saudi territories on Ottoman behalf before expulsion by Banu Khalid

name: Al Murrah
description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

relations.context: Both tribes historically inhabited areas in Qatar alongside other Bedouin tribes
Al Murrah are descended from the Banu Yam tribe
Joined together in 1885 to renounce allegiance to Qatar and later fought alongside each other

Cousin tribe sharing same Arabic dialect group

history:
The Al Murrah (Arabic: آل مرة) are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe, which belongs to the Qahtanite branch of Arabian tribes, specifically the group known as Banu Hamdan, making them native to southwestern Arabia. The Banu Yam was the progenitor tribe of both the Al Murrah and the 'Ujman of eastern Saudi Arabia and the Arabian Gulf coast. Unlike their parent tribe Yam, whose majority follows the Sulaymani Isma'ili branch of Shi'ite Islam, the Al Murrah branches are Sunni Muslims.

Historically, the Al Murrah were camel-herding nomads who controlled and travelled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula, including the formidable Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali). They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals, with seven clans making up the Saudi branch of the tribe. Their nomadic lifestyle was characterized by extensive migrations, traveling as much as 3,000 kilometres annually. The tribe maintains a presence across multiple Gulf states including Qatar, Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, and Bahrain.

Historically, the Al Murrah constituted a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population and played significant roles in the country's political affairs. In 1885, internal tribal divisions led to a military confrontation when some Al Murrah tribespeople, along with members from the Bani Hajer tribe, renounced their allegiance to Qatar and joined with the hostile Ajman tribe. A regiment of 1,200 partisans composed of loyal Al Murrah and Bani Hajer branches engaged the rebel coalition of 2,000 men, inflicting heavy losses despite being outnumbered.

The tribe's modern political involvement in Qatar proved tumultuous during the late 20th and early 21st centuries. They were strong supporters of Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani, the former Emir who was deposed in 1995 by his son. Eight months after this coup, 119 Al Murrah members attempted to overthrow the new Emir Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996 but failed. In February 2000, 19 alleged perpetrators were sentenced to death and 33 to life imprisonment, though none of the death sentences were executed. Political tensions continued into the 21st century: in 2017, Qatar revoked citizenship of the sheikh and 54 tribespeople, and Al Murrah members were barred from the 2021 Qatari general election, leading to protests. The Al Murrah maintain friendly relations with UAE and Saudi Arabia while opposing the current Iranian regime.

name: Al Musallam
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on descent and consensus among sub-clans
ancestorStory: Descended from Musallam, a tribal progenitor who established the lineage in the eastern Arabian Peninsula. The tribe traces its origins to the broader tribal confederations of the region.
description: Al Musallam is a tribal branch with historical presence in the eastern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in areas that now constitute parts of the UAE and eastern Saudi Arabia. They have maintained traditional pastoral and trading activities across the Gulf region. The tribe has had historical tensions with the Bani Utbah confederation, reflecting broader tribal competition for resources and territory. Today they remain active as part of the broader tribal structure in the GCC states.

relations.context: Fought military skirmishes in Qatar, Al Musallam had Ottoman support

history:
Al Musallam was a powerful tribal branch that emerged as a dominant force in the eastern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the coastal regions that now constitute parts of Qatar and the UAE. The tribe held significant territorial control in northwestern Qatar during the mid-18th century, ruling over settlements including Al Huwaila and exercising authority as local representatives of the broader Bani Khalid confederation under nominal Ottoman support. Historical records document that by the 1760s, Al Musallam controlled key coastal areas and maintained practical autonomy over their domain, demanding tribute from new arrivals in their territory.

The tribe's power peaked during the 1760s when they controlled strategic coastal positions and acted as intermediaries for the Bani Khalid's territorial administration. Al Musallam's authority was challenged by the arrival of the Bani Utbah confederation, particularly the Al Khalifa branch, who established themselves in the nearby settlement of Zubarah. When the Al Khalifa refused to pay the taxes and tribute demanded by Al Musallam, escalating tensions led to a series of military confrontations between the two groups.

Despite their established position and Ottoman backing, Al Musallam ultimately lost their territorial control following successful Al Khalifa military assaults on their stronghold of Al Huwaila. This defeat forced the Al Musallam leadership to flee to Najd, marking the end of their dominance in the northwestern Qatar coast. The expulsion of Al Musallam represented a significant shift in the regional balance of power, as the Bani Utbah consolidated control over the area and established Zubarah as a major commercial center.

The defeat of Al Musallam contributed to the broader decline of Bani Khalid influence in the Gulf region during the late 18th century. Their loss of coastal territories to emerging tribal confederations like the Bani Utbah would shape the future political landscape of what would become the modern Gulf states. The tribe's historical role as representatives of the Bani Khalid confederation and their territorial disputes with the Al Khalifa became part of the complex tribal dynamics that characterized the pre-modern Arabian Peninsula.

name: al-Nammir ibn Qasit
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afsa ibn Du'mi ibn Jadilah
ancestorStory: Al-Nammir was the son of Qasit from the ancient Adnanite lineage. The tribe traces its origins to the pre-Islamic Arabian Peninsula as part of the larger Rabi'ah confederation.
description: Al-Nammir ibn Qasit was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Adnanite branch through Rabi'ah. They were primarily nomadic pastoralists who roamed the northern Arabian Peninsula and had connections to the broader Qasit tribal confederation. The tribe is mentioned in classical Arab genealogical sources as part of the ancient tribal structure of Arabia. Like many pre-Islamic tribes, their influence waned with the rise of Islam and the subsequent tribal reorganizations, though their genealogical legacy remains documented in Arab tribal histories.

relations.context: Both tribes trace descent through the Rabi'ah lineage

history:
Al-Nammir ibn Qasit was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Rabi'ah branch of the Adnanite tribal confederation. According to the research, they are explicitly listed as one of the sub-tribes under Bakr ibn Wa'il in eastern Najd, indicating their position within the broader Rabi'ah genealogical structure. The tribe's placement under the Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation suggests they shared the general territorial and cultural patterns of this major tribal group.

Geographically, al-Nammir was located in eastern Najd, as confirmed by their listing among the eastern Najd tribes alongside Taghlib ibn Wa'il and Anz ibn Wa'il. This positioning placed them within the sphere of influence of the larger Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation, which according to the research had its origins in Najd before many of its sections migrated northward to Upper Mesopotamia after their victory at the Battle of Dhi Qar against the Sasanian Empire.

As part of the Bakr ibn Wa'il tribal structure, al-Nammir would have been connected to a confederation known for its military prowess and involvement in major pre-Islamic conflicts. The Bakr ibn Wa'il tribes were noted for their role in significant battles, their control of trade routes between Hejaz and Mesopotamia, and their eventual conversion to Islam during the early Islamic period. However, the specific historical activities and fate of al-Nammir ibn Qasit as a distinct sub-tribal unit are not detailed in the available research.

The tribal genealogy places al-Nammir within the Adnanite lineage through the Rabi'ah branch, specifically under Bakr ibn Wa'il, making them part of one of the most ancient and prominent Arab tribal confederations. Their inclusion in the eastern Najd tribal listings suggests they maintained their identity as a recognized sub-tribal unit within this larger confederation structure during the pre-Islamic period.

migrationPath.description: Migration northwards and eastwards from original Rabi'ah homelands in Tihamah as part of broader tribal movements

name: Al Nasrallah
legitimacyNotes: Religious appellative name suggesting divine favor and blessing
description: Al Nasrallah is a tribal name found across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf states. The name, meaning 'Victory of Allah,' appears among different tribal groupings and may represent multiple unrelated clans sharing the same appellative name. Without specific geographical or genealogical context, it is difficult to determine their exact origins, historical significance, or current tribal affiliations in the GCC region.
history: The Al Nasrallah name, meaning 'Victory of Allah,' appears across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf states as both a given name and surname. According to Wikipedia sources, the surname often indicates that the family adopted the name Nasrallah from one of its patrilineal ancestors. In Kuwait specifically, the most notable family carrying this name is known as Al-Nasrallah, who trace back to the Anazzah tribe and migrated from Sudair region. They reside mainly in Jiblah and Jahrah areas of Kuwait. Research reveals that Al Nasrallah families exist as part of larger tribal confederations - notably appearing in the Al-Musawi family genealogical records as a related family branch, and significantly within the Al Faiz family of Karbala. The Al Faiz family, descendants of Ibrahim al-Mujab (9th century grandson of the seventh Shi'ite Imam Musa al-Kadhim), includes Al Nasrallah as one of their six main family branches that emerged from their eponymous ancestor Muhammad Abu al-Faiz. The Karbala-based Al Nasrallah branch owns vast lands across Ayn al-Tamur and Karbala, with their grandfather Yunis al-Faizi having purchased the muqata'a Maal Yunis extending from Arba' Nahran to al-Jayya. The family has held custodianship of both Husayn and Abbas shrines and is noted as the only Alid family with the right to serve in both shrines, possessing three designated crypts in the Husayn shrine. The name appears among different tribal groupings across the region, and without specific geographical context, represents multiple potentially unrelated clans sharing the same appellative name.

name: Al Nasur/Nassour
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage with historical presence in Gulf region
description: Al Nasur (Al Nassour) is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. The name suggests connections to the concept of 'victory' or 'assistance' in Arabic nomenclature. Limited historical documentation makes specific details about their origins and traditional territories unclear. They appear to maintain tribal identity in the contemporary Gulf states.
history:
Al Nasur (Al Nassour) is documented as one of the authentic Arab Huwala tribes, representing original Arabs who migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Iranian coast of the Persian Gulf between the 13th and 19th centuries. The tribe was part of a larger tribal confederation known as the Huwala, which included other authentic Arab tribes such as the Qawasem, Hammadi, Obaidli, and Bani Tamim. These tribes were collectively referred to as 'Arab El-Sahel' (Arabs of the Eastern Coast) and maintained their Sunni Islamic faith, distinguishing them from the majority Twelver Shia Persian population in Iran.

According to contemporary historians including Niebuhr, Lorimer, and David Seton, the true Huwala Arabs, including Al Nasur, inhabited a specific geographical region from Bandar Kanj in the south to Bandar Kangan in the north, extending from the Arabian Gulf coast to the Shibkoh mountains in the east. This region was primarily in what is now Iran's Hormozgan and Fars provinces, particularly around areas near Bandar Lengeh, Qishm, and Bandar Abbas. The Al Nasur, like other Huwala tribes, were involved in various maritime activities including sailing, trading, fishing, pearl diving, and cultivation along the Iranian coast.

Saudi historian Jalal Al-Haroon classifies Al Nasur as part of the first type of Huwalas - the original Arabs who migrated to Southern Iran from Coastal Eastern Arabia during the 16th and 17th centuries. The tribe experienced significant return migration to the Arabian Peninsula between the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the final major wave occurring in the 1930s due to restrictive economic policies imposed by Reza Shah Pahlavi. This return migration was part of a larger pattern affecting most Huwala tribes, who faced economic restrictions and cultural pressures under Iranian centralization policies.

In the contemporary period, Al Nasur maintains tribal identity in the Gulf states, having successfully reintegrated into Arabian Peninsula society following their return from Iran. The tribe's historical experience reflects the broader pattern of Arabian tribal mobility across the Persian Gulf, demonstrating the fluid nature of tribal territories and the enduring connections between Arabian tribes and their ancestral homelands despite centuries of residence on the Iranian coast.

name: Al Omar
legitimacyNotes: Lineage through descent from Albu Salih tribal confederation
ancestorStory: Descended from Omar, a prominent member of the Albu Salih confederation. The family established themselves as a recognized tribal branch through traditional genealogical ties.
description: Al Omar is a tribal branch within the larger Albu Salih confederation, historically active in the Arabian Peninsula. They maintained traditional pastoral economies and tribal governance structures typical of Qahtani lineages. The tribe has preserved its identity through genealogical records and traditional practices. Today they remain an active tribal group with members across various Gulf states.

history: The research reveals no direct connection between the Al Omar tribal branch and any of the historical Omar/Umar figures mentioned in the sources. Omar al-Mukhtar (1858-1931) was a Libyan resistance leader from the Arab Mnifa tribe in Cyrenaica, who led native resistance against Italian colonization and belonged to the Senussi movement. Umar ibn al-Khattab (c. 584-644) was the second Rashidun caliph from the Banu Adi clan of Quraysh in Mecca, who played a crucial role in early Islamic expansion but has no documented genealogical connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal branches named Al Omar. The Bani Omar of Oman represents a separate tribal entity - they are descended from Maʿamar ibn Zubaid of the Zubaid tribe within the Madhhaj confederation, classified as Qahtanite lineage, and have traditionally inhabited Northern Oman in the wilayats of Liwa, Shinas, Saham, and Ibri. The existing data describing Al Omar as a Qahtani tribal branch within the Albu Salih confederation lacks verification in the provided research materials, which focus on unrelated historical figures and the distinct Omani Bani Omar tribe.

name: Al-Omaro Bani Uqba
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage from Bani Uqba
ancestorStory: Descended from Uqba, a tribal leader whose descendants formed the Al-Omaro branch. The tribe traces its origins to ancient Arabian tribal confederations.
description: Al-Omaro Bani Uqba is a tribal branch descended from the ancient Bani Uqba confederation. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who moved across the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained alliance relationships with other tribal groups including the Mahamid. Their current status and geographic concentration in the modern Gulf states remains unclear from historical records.

relations.context: Allied with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba and other Karak clans when they established their emirate
Both trace descent from Qahtani tribal roots

history: The Al-Omaro Bani Uqba are documented as key allies of the Mahamid tribe in the Levant, specifically in the Karak region of present-day Jordan. According to historical records, the Mahamid tribe, which originated from the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation and initially resided in Yemen and the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, underwent significant migrations northward. When the Mahamid expanded beyond their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, they first moved to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia before continuing their migration toward the Levant. Upon reaching the Karak region, the Mahamid found themselves in such large numbers that they outnumbered the existing major tribes in the area. It was in this context that they formed a strategic alliance with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, among other local Karak clans. This alliance proved significant as the Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid in the region, with the majority rule held by the Al-Amru clan. The emirate maintained control for an extended period, though historical sources suggest their rule became increasingly harsh, eventually prompting various clans to unite against them. The specific role of Al-Omaro Bani Uqba within this political structure and their subsequent fate following the emirate's challenges remains unclear from available records.

name: Al-Oraif
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on lineage and local consensus
description: Al-Oraif is a smaller tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Like many Arabian clans, they maintain their tribal identity and genealogical connections. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins or major historical roles in Gulf politics. They continue to exist as part of the broader tribal structure in the region.
history: Al-Oraif (Arabic: العريف) is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, one of the major Bedouin Arab tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Al-Dhafeer tribe descends from Tayy and traces its genealogical origins to Banu Lam from the Arabs of the Hejaz. According to multiple historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes were near Medina, specifically from a place called al-Dhaghn. Al-Oraif belongs to the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe historically lived in the desert of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), spanning the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe was known for its mobile lifestyle, spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and migration rather than maintaining fixed settlements. They were nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children. In 1782, Al-Dhafeer warriors participated in the Persian-Bahrain conflict, joining forces sent by the Emir of Kuwait to help Sheikh Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa confront Persian rule in Bahrain, culminating in the victory at the Battle of Zubarah. The tribe also fought in the Battle of Qaysar in 1924, where they successfully defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe in the southern desert of Iraq. Around 1926, when Emir Ibn Saud consolidated control over much of Arabia, the tribe's branches in Saudi Arabia were allowed to settle in various settlements after the securing of regional roads.

Expansion to northern regions including areas of modern Kuwait and eastern Saudi Arabia

name: Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah
legitimacyNotes: Ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah emirate, hereditary sheikhdom established through maritime dominance
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Qasim ibn Razzaq, who established the family's prominence in the coastal regions. The dynasty rose to power through control of maritime trade routes and pearling operations in the 18th century.
description: The Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah are the ruling family of the emirate and one of the most prominent maritime dynasties of the Arabian Gulf. Historically based in Ras Al Khaimah, they established a powerful confederation controlling much of the lower Gulf's sea lanes from the 18th century. They faced conflicts with British forces in the early 19th century over maritime activities, leading to various treaties. Today, they continue as the ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah, one of the seven emirates of the UAE.

Both branches of the Al Qasimi dynasty ruling separate emirates
Part of the broader Qawasim tribal confederation

history: The Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah trace their origins to the Qawasim tribal confederation, descended from Huwala tribes who migrated between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia. They claim Hashemite ancestry through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki, descending from Husayn ibn Ali. During the 18th century, they established themselves as rulers of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, embracing the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement and becoming allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. The first recorded ruler was Sheikh Rahma Al Qasimi (1708-1731), described by the Dutch as the 'Emir of Julfar' and one of the richest Arab merchants. By the early 19th century, the Qawasim had emerged as a powerful maritime force controlling territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf, including Ras Al Khaimah, Sharjah, Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh on the Persian coast. Their strategic position near the Strait of Hormuz brought them into conflict with the Omani Empire and eventually with Britain. The British branded them as 'pirates' due to their allegiance to the Wahhabi movement and attacks on British-allied shipping. This led to two major British military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819, culminating in their defeat and the signing of the General Maritime Treaty of 1820. Following the 1819 campaign, Sheikh Sultan Bin Saqr Al Qasimi was reinstated by the British and ruled until 1866. The family continued to rule through various successions, with periods where Ras Al Khaimah was incorporated into Sharjah (1900-1921). Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi ruled for 62 years (1948-2010), initially opposing UAE formation due to disputes over the Tunb islands, but eventually joining the federation on February 10, 1972. His son Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi has ruled since 2010, with his son Mohammed bin Saud designated as heir presumptive.

name: Al Qasimi of Sharjah

ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Qasim ibn Rahmah, who established their power in the lower Gulf in the 17th century. They became prominent maritime rulers controlling key trade routes between the Arabian coast and Persia.
description: The Al Qasimi of Sharjah are the ruling family of the Emirate of Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates, descended from the broader Qawasim confederation. Historically based along the Trucial Coast, they were major maritime powers controlling trade routes in the lower Gulf from the 17th-19th centuries. They faced conflicts with British forces in the early 19th century but later signed treaties establishing the Trucial States system. Today they remain the ruling family of Sharjah under Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.

history:
The Al Qasimi of Sharjah are the ruling dynasty of the Emirate of Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates, tracing their lineage through the Adnanite Arab genealogical tradition. According to Islamic and Arab genealogical sources, the Adnanites descend from Adnan, who is traditionally regarded as a descendant of Ishmael (Ismāʿīl), son of Abraham (Ibrahim). This places the Al Qasimi within the broader framework of Northern Arabian tribes, distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of Southern Arabia who trace their lineage to Qahtan.

The Adnanite lineage holds particular significance in Islamic tradition, as the Prophet Muhammad also descended from this line through the Quraysh tribe. The genealogical chain from Adnan to Muhammad passes through Ma'add, Nizar, Mudar, and other ancestral figures who were considered chiefs ruling parts of the Arabian Peninsula. According to tradition, Adnan married Mahdad bint Laham and had two sons: Ma'add ibn Adnan, who became the ancestor of many Northern Arabian tribes, and Akk ibn Adnan, who migrated to Yemen and integrated with the Asharites.

The name Adnan appears in pre-Islamic Arabic poetry and North Arabian inscriptions, including Thamudic and Nabataean texts, where it held significance as an honorable ancestral figure. Pre-Islamic Arab poets such as Lubayb Ibn Rabi'a and Abbas Ibn Mirdas mentioned Adnan in their verses, and tribal groups used Adnanite ancestry as a source of pride and honor when competing with Qahtanite tribes. Some Nabataeans even named their children Abd Adnon (servant of Adnan), indicating the veneration accorded to this ancestral figure.

Historically, the distinction between Adnanites and Qahtanites formed a fundamental division in Arabian tribal identity, with the Adnanites representing the Northern Arabs of the Hejaz and other regions, while the Qahtanites were considered the original Arabs of Southern Arabia. However, some modern historians question whether this traditional distinction reflects historical reality or developed later during factional conflicts in the Umayyad period. The Al Qasimi's claim to Adnanite ancestry connects them to this broader network of Northern Arabian tribes that played crucial roles in pre-Islamic and Islamic Arabian history.

name: Al Qawasim
legitimacyNotes: Maritime power and control of strategic waterways, recognized rulers of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah
ancestorStory: The confederation traces its origins to Qasim ibn Rahmah, who established their presence in the northern Emirates region. They became a dominant maritime force controlling trade routes in the Arabian Gulf.
description: The Al Qawasim are a major tribal confederation that became the ruling families of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah in the UAE. Historically based in the northern Emirates and southern Iran, they were a dominant maritime power controlling pearling and trade routes in the Arabian Gulf during the 18th and 19th centuries. They engaged in conflicts with British forces during the so-called 'Piracy Wars' before signing treaties in the early 19th century. Today, branches of Al Qawasim continue to rule as the Al Qasimi families in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, playing important roles in UAE federal politics and development.

history:
The Al Qawasim (القواسم) are a tribal confederation of Hashemite origin, tracing their lineage through Husayn ibn Ali to their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasim are classified as Huwala, maintaining ancestral connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining their rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until its annexation by Iran in 1887. The confederation's specific historical origins trace to figures like Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi (1722-1747) and subsequent rulers who established their dominance in the northern Emirates region.

During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, becoming close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they had emerged as a formidable maritime power controlling both southern shores at Ras Al Khaimah and northern Persian Gulf territories including Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh. Their confederation was composed of Sunni tribes in the southeastern Gulf region surrounding the cities of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, and they faced strong rivalry with the Omani empire for naval domination along the Persian Gulf.

The Qawasim's control of trade routes in the Persian Gulf led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire over border disputes, religious differences, and naval supremacy. Beginning in 1804, increased Qawasim naval attacks on trading vessels led the British to label them as 'pirates,' resulting in two major military campaigns - the 1809 Persian Gulf campaign and the more successful 1819 expedition under William Keir Grant. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty forced the Qawasim to cease their maritime activities and carry a distinctive peace flag with a red rectangle contained within a white border.

Following the treaties, the Al Qawasim evolved into the ruling dynasties of Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah, becoming founding members of the United Arab Emirates in 1971. The confederation also maintained extensive commercial and military activities beyond the Persian Gulf, with ties extending to the Gulf of Aden and as far west as Mocha on the Red Sea. They had numerous commercial ties with Somalis and regularly attended trade fairs in Berbera and Zeila. In the 1830s, the Isaaq Sultan Farah Guled and Haji Ali requested military assistance from Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi of Ras Al Khaimah for joint religious war against the British. Today, branches of Al Qawasim continue to rule as the Al Qasimi families in Ras Al Khaimah under Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi and in Sharjah under Sheikh Sultan bin Muhammad Al Qasimi.

name: Al Qemzi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional pearling and maritime trading authority in coastal UAE waters
ancestorStory: The Al Qemzi trace their lineage to Qamzi, who established the family as skilled pearl divers and seafarers along the UAE coast. They became prominent in the pearling trade and developed strong maritime traditions.
description: The Al Qemzi are a prominent UAE maritime tribe historically based along the coastal areas of the Emirates, particularly associated with Dubai and the northern emirates. They gained prominence as skilled pearl divers, boat builders, and maritime traders during the height of the Gulf pearling industry. The tribe maintained strong relationships with other coastal trading families and played an important role in the pre-oil economy of the UAE. Today, they remain an active and respected family within UAE society, with members involved in business and public service.

Maritime cooperation along northern UAE coast

history: The Al Qemzi (Arabic: القمزي/القمزان) are one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which traces its origins to the Najd region of Central Arabia. According to established genealogical traditions, the Bani Yas confederation, including the Al Qemzi, descended from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, sharing this Adnanite lineage with other prominent branches such as the Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah) and Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah) ruling families. The confederation represents one of the major Adnanite tribal groups that historically inhabited the Arabian Peninsula, distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of Southern Arabia. Under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah house, the Al Qemzi and other Bani Yas clans settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700, establishing their presence in what is now Abu Dhabi emirate. The confederation later expanded their territorial control when they acquired property in the Buraimi Oasis and established settlements in Al Ain, demonstrating their strategic expansion across key oasis regions of southeastern Arabia. Following the discovery of fresh water on Abu Dhabi island in 1793, many Bani Yas families, likely including the Al Qemzi, participated in the significant migration to coastal areas. The Al Bu Falah formally established Abu Dhabi town in 1795, which became the seat of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi with the construction of Qasr al-Hosn. As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Al Qemzi maintained close relations with the Dhawahir tribe while traditionally opposing the Na'im and Bani Ka'ab tribes in the Buraimi region. Historical population estimates by J.C. Lorimer in the early 1900s placed the total Bani Yas confederation at around 12,000 people, including approximately 2,000 in Dubai and 2,800 in Abu Dhabi town, though specific figures for the Al Qemzi branch were not recorded. The confederation experienced population decline by the 1950s, falling to approximately 8,000 members, largely due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment opportunities, before experiencing return migration following Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's accession and subsequent development of Abu Dhabi.

name: Al-Rabbab
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with established genealogical records
ancestorStory: Al-Rabbab traces its lineage to Rabab ibn Malik, an ancient Arabian ancestor. The tribe has maintained its genealogical traditions through oral and written records.
description: Al-Rabbab is an ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic origins, traditionally associated with the northern Arabian Peninsula. The tribe has historical presence in areas that overlap with modern Gulf states, particularly in Saudi Arabia. They maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle centered on camel herding and trade routes. Today, members of Al-Rabbab are integrated into modern Gulf societies while maintaining their tribal identity.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history: Al-Rabbab is confirmed through the research as one of the four main branches of the powerful Banu Tamim tribe, alongside Amr, Banu Hanzala, and Banu Sa'd. The Banu Tamim trace their lineage through an established genealogical chain: Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan, connecting them directly to Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham). As an Adnanite tribe descended from Adnan, Al-Rabbab shares this ancient northern Arabian heritage. The Banu Tamim were historically concentrated in the central and northern parts of Najd before the spread of Islam in the 6th century, playing an important role at the beginning of Islam when they came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah. Following the Islamic conquests, the tribe spread across the Arabian Peninsula and then migrated to areas ruled by subsequent caliphates, extending their presence from Morocco in the west to Khuzestan in the east. The research shows that Banu Tamim held significant power for centuries in various regions through dynasties like the Aghlabids, and continue to maintain a strong presence today in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, Kuwait, Iraq, Oman, Jordan, Lebanon, and North African countries including Algeria, Morocco, Palestine, Tunisia, and Libya.

migrationPath.description: Pre-Islamic expansion from traditional Najd territories to northern regions as part of Banu Tamim tribal movements
Post-Islamic conquest migration following expansion of Islamic caliphates
Westward migration as part of Islamic expansion, establishing presence from Morocco to Tunisia

name: Al Rumaiyadh
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and recognized lineage
ancestorStory: Al Rumaiyadh trace their lineage to their eponymous ancestor Rumaidh. The tribe established itself as part of the broader tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Rumaiyadh is a tribal branch with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They are part of the Qahtani tribal lineage system and have maintained their tribal identity through traditional genealogical connections. The tribe has historically been involved in the pastoral economy typical of Arabian tribes. Their current status and specific regional influence within the GCC states requires further documentation.
history: The Al Rumaiyadh are a clan within the Bani Malik section of the broader Albu Salih tribal confederation, with their lineage tracing to their eponymous ancestor Rumaidh. During the 18th century, they became part of a significant transformation when the Albu Salih evolved into a powerful tribal confederation opposing Ottoman influence in the region. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh (1845-1942), the Al Rumaiyadh became the dominant force within the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate, with Shaikh Badr rising to become the paramount Shaikh of the entire Bani Malik region. The tribe's territory was centered in what is now southern Iraq, particularly around the great marshes in the Al Islah district of Dhi Qar province. The Al Rumaiyadh practiced both settled agriculture and semi-nomadic pastoralism, with their nomadic branches migrating seasonally south through the Wadi Batin valley into northeastern Arabia, including northern and western Kuwaiti territories, before returning north for summer. Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh became renowned for his fierce resistance against British colonial forces during World War I and the subsequent British Mandate of Mesopotamia. He fought alongside Ottoman forces at the Battle of Shaiba in 1915, and after losing two sons in battle, vowed never to surrender to British occupation. His resistance included successfully reclaiming tribal lands from British forces and renaming their fort from 'Qalat Al-Fatihi' to 'Qalat Al-Nasri.' He remained a wanted outlaw for years and played a major role in the 1920 Iraqi Revolution. Today, members of the Al Rumaiyadh are found across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, with the current leader being Shaikh Sabah bin Fares bin Mohsen bin Bader Al Rumaiyadh.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal autumn migration of nomadic branches through Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and northeastern Arabian territories for winter grazing

name: Al-Sa'id
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Sa'id, though specific genealogical details vary by regional branch.
description: Al-Sa'id is a tribal name found across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with different branches having distinct histories and locations. Some branches are found in the Gulf region, particularly in parts of Saudi Arabia and other GCC states. The name is relatively common among Arab tribes, with various unrelated groups sharing this designation. Current branches remain active in their respective regions, maintaining traditional tribal structures alongside modern state integration.
history:
The research reveals three distinct historical figures and dynasties associated with the name Sa'id, but no evidence of a unified 'Al-Sa'id' tribal entity. The most prominent is the Al Bu Said dynasty of Oman, founded by Ahmad bin Said Al Busaidi in 1744. This dynasty traces its lineage through the Azd tribe, specifically to al-'Atik b. al-Asad b. Imran who settled in Dibba. The Azd originally migrated from Yemen following the destruction of the Marib Dam and established themselves as a leading force in early Islamic conquests under generals like Al Muhallab ibn Abi Suffrah (Abu Said), who became the progenitor of the Busaid tribe. Ahmad bin Said rose to power after successfully defending Sohar against Persian forces for nine months, leading to his election as Imam on November 20, 1744, marking the beginning of one of Arabia's oldest surviving royal dynasties.

Under Said bin Sultan (1804-1856), known as 'Said the Great,' the Al Bu Said dynasty reached its zenith as a commercial and political maritime power. The empire extended from Oman to Zanzibar, parts of the East African coast including Mombasa and Pemba Island, southern Iran including Bandar-Abbas, and Gwadar in Balochistan. Said established Zanzibar as his second capital in 1832 and initiated large-scale clove cultivation. The empire was divided in 1856 after his death, creating separate sultanates in Oman and Zanzibar. The Zanzibar branch ended with the 1964 revolution, while the Omani branch continues under Sultan Haitham bin Tariq who succeeded Sultan Qaboos in 2020.

Separately, the research documents Sa'id ibn Zayd (c. 593-671), a companion of Prophet Muhammad from the Adi clan of Quraysh. He was instrumental in the conversion of Umar ibn al-Khattab to Islam and served as Muhammad's secretary, recording Quranic verses. Later he served as Governor of Kufa under Caliph Muawiya. Another historical figure was Abu Sa'id al-Jannabi (845/855-913/914), a Persian founder of the Qarmatian state in Bahrayn who established a communal society based on egalitarian principles. These represent distinct lineages and are not connected to any overarching 'Al-Sa'id' tribal confederation.

name: House of Saud
legitimacyNotes: Religious legitimacy through Wahhabi alliance, conquest and unification of Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The Al Saud trace their origins to Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin who settled in Diriyah in the 15th century. The family rose to prominence through alliance with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in the 18th century.
description: The House of Saud is the ruling royal family of Saudi Arabia, originally from Najd region. They established three successive Saudi states, with the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia founded in 1932 by Abdulaziz ibn Saud. Through alliance with the Wahhabi religious movement and military conquest, they unified most of the Arabian Peninsula. They remain the absolute monarchs of Saudi Arabia and custodians of Islam's holiest sites.

relations.context: Members of the tribe played key roles in supporting the early Saudi state militarily and politically. These connections were solidified through intermarriage, most notably between King AbdulAziz Al Saud and Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi
Staunch enemies who fought multiple battles over territorial control and ideological differences regarding Wahhabism

history:
The House of Saud (Al Saud) traces its origins to Mani' ibn Rabiah Al-Muraydi, who settled in Diriyah in 1446-1447 with his clan, the Mrudah. The Mrudah are believed to be descended from the Banu Hanifa branch of the larger Rabi'ah tribal confederation, which forms part of the Adnani lineage. Mani' was invited to Diriyah by a relative named Ibn Dir, who handed him two estates called al-Mulaybeed and Ghusayba. The region was renamed Al Diriyah after their benefactor, and the Mrudah became rulers of this settlement along the banks of Wadi Hanifa. As the clan grew larger, power struggles ensued, with branches splitting off - one leaving for nearby Dhruma and another branch (the 'Al Watban') departing for az-Zubayr in southern Iraq. The Al Muqrin became the ruling family among the Mrudah in Diriyah, and the dynasty's name derives from Sheikh Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725.

The First Saudi State was founded in 1727 by Muhammad bin Saud through a crucial alliance with the religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, establishing a strict Islamic polity that expanded across most of the Arabian Peninsula. This state reached its greatest extent under Saud ibn Abdulaziz, with raids extending into Yemen, Oman, Syria, and Iraq. The Saudis referred to themselves as the Muwahhidun or Ahl al-Tawhid ('the monotheists'), later called Wahhabis after their religious founder. The first state ended dramatically in 1818 when Ottoman-Egyptian forces destroyed Diriyah, executed the Saudi leadership, and sent family members into exile. The Second Saudi State emerged in 1824 under Turki ibn Abdallah with its capital in Riyadh, but was marked by severe internal conflicts and less territorial expansion, eventually falling to the Rashidis at the Battle of Mulayda in 1891.

The modern Saudi state began in 1902 when Abdulaziz ibn Saud (Ibn Saud) recaptured Riyadh's Masmak fort with 40 followers, beginning three decades of conquest that would unify most of the Arabian Peninsula. Through strategic alliances with the British, creation of the Ikhwan military-religious brotherhood, and systematic conquest, he defeated his main rivals: the Al Rashid clan (1921), the Ottomans in al-Hasa (1913), and the Sharifs of Mecca in the Hijaz (1925). Ibn Saud proclaimed the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932, and the discovery of oil in 1938 transformed the kingdom into a global power. The royal family comprises an estimated 15,000 members, with the ruling faction primarily descended from Ibn Saud, whose sons have ruled in succession: Saud (1953-1964), Faisal (1964-1975), Khalid (1975-1982), Fahd (1982-2005), Abdullah (2005-2015), and currently Salman (2015-present).

The House of Saud forms a subtribe of the prominent ancient Banu Hanifa tribe, from which the 7th century Arabian theologian Maslama ibn Ḥabīb originated. The family structure includes cadet branches descended from Muhammad bin Saud's brothers: the Al Jiluwi, Al Thunayan, Al Mishari, and Al Farhan branches, who hold high government positions but are not in the line of succession. Members often intermarry within the Al Saud to maintain lineage and influence. The succession system was traditionally based on agnatic seniority among Ibn Saud's sons, but was modified in 2006 to include election by a committee of princes. King Salman broke with tradition in 2017 by appointing his son Mohammed bin Salman as crown prince, marking the first father-to-son succession since Ibn Saud's death in 1953.

nameEtymology: The name 'Al Saud' is formed by adding the word 'Al' (meaning 'family of' or 'House of') to the personal name of the ancestor Saud ibn Muhammad ibn Muqrin, who died in 1725 and was the father of the dynasty's 18th century founder Muhammad bin Saud.

name: Al-Shaleikh
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority and historical settlement in Gulf region
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Shaleikh. They established themselves as part of the broader tribal confederation system in the Arabian Gulf region.
description: Al-Shaleikh is a tribal group found in the Arabian Gulf region, particularly associated with the broader tribal systems of the GCC states. They maintain traditional tribal structures and have been part of the historical settlement patterns in the Gulf. The tribe continues to exist as an active social unit within the modern Gulf states, preserving their tribal identity and customs. Their specific historical role and current distribution across Gulf states requires further documentation.
history: Al-Shaleikh is documented as a branch that descended from the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد, al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The parent Mahamid tribe originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the Mahamid tribe grew large and their original areas became crowded in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz, tribal leaders gathered to decide their course of action, ultimately choosing migration over conquest of neighboring Harb sub-clans. The Mahamid undertook extensive migrations, moving north to Dumat Al-Jandal, then toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid and became one of the largest tribes in the area. Al-Shaleikh emerged as one of the documented branches of the Mahamid specifically in Jordan, alongside other branches including Al Abdul Dayem, Al-Bahri, Ayal Abdo, and the Abu Karaki family. The research indicates that various Mahamid branches formed alliances with local tribal groups including Al-Huwaitat, Bani Abbad, Bani Hamida, and Zoubi, suggesting Al-Shaleikh likely participated in the broader tribal confederation systems that characterized the region's political landscape.

name: Al Subaih
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical settlement patterns
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Subayh. They are considered part of the broader tribal confederation structure of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Subaih is a tribal branch found primarily in the Arabian Peninsula region. They are traditionally associated with nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal identity through genealogical connections. The tribe has historical presence in areas that are now part of modern GCC states. They continue to exist as an active tribal group with traditional social structures intact.

relations.context: Historical migration patterns in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history: Al Subaih is identified in the historical sources as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation. The research documents Al Subaih among the core branches of Bani Khalid, specifically listed alongside Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, and Mahashir & Nahood. As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, Al Subaih participated in the broader tribal political dynamics of Eastern Arabia from the 15th through 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid dominated the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif during this period, establishing the Khalidi Emirate under Barrak ibn Ghurayr in 1670 after expelling Ottoman forces. The confederation faced significant challenges from the expanding Saudi-Wahhabi state, with the Khalidi chief ordering the emir of Uyayna to cease support for Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and expel him, leading to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's move to Diriyyah where he allied with the Al Saud. The Bani Khalid, including Al Subaih, remained staunch enemies of the Saudis and attempted to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to stop Saudi expansion. After Saudi conquest of Nejd, the confederation's domain in Al-Hasa was invaded in 1789, marking the decline of Khalidi power. Final defeats came through battles with tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and with the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, ending Bani Khalid rule. Members of the confederation, including Al Subaih, subsequently dispersed across the region, with many settling in Iraq, Kuwait, Palestine, Lebanon, and other areas of the Arabian Peninsula.

Settlement along the Trucial Coast for pearling and fishing opportunities

name: Sabais
description: Al Sabais (السبايس) is a tribal group with presence in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They appear to maintain tribal identity in the modern era, though their historical role and influence in Gulf affairs requires further research.
history: The Sabais, also known as Al Subousi (السبايس/السبوسي), are documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. According to the UK Memorial on the Buraimi dispute, the Bedouin group referred to as the Jabais is generally identified with the Sabais, indicating their nomadic heritage within the broader Bani Yas structure. As part of the Bani Yas confederation, the Sabais shared in the tribal movement from the Najd region of Central Arabia, where the Bani Yas originated, descending along with the Awamir from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. Historical records from J.C. Lorimer's Gazetteer (1908-1915) estimated the total Bani Yas population at around 12,000, including approximately 2,000 nomadic members who generally remained within Abu Dhabi territory throughout the year. The Sabais would have been part of this nomadic element of the confederation. By the early 1950s, the overall Bani Yas population had declined to approximately 8,000, including about 1,700 Bedouin, due to emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment in oil and construction sectors. As members of the Bani Yas confederation, the Sabais fell under the authority of the Al Bu Falah (Al Nahyan) leadership and were part of the political structure that established the Emirate of Abu Dhabi.

name: Al Suhoob
description: Al Suhoob is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula, though specific historical documentation about their origins and activities is limited in available sources. Like many smaller tribal branches, they maintain their genealogical identity while being integrated into modern Gulf society. Their current status and geographic distribution within the GCC region requires further research for accurate characterization.
history:
Al Suhoob is one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid confederation includes eleven main branches: Al Asfour, Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood, with chieftainship traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. The genealogical origins of Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob, remain disputed among Arab genealogists, with claims ranging from descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid of Banu Makhzum (Quraysh), to origins in Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, or from Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe.

As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, Al Suhoob participated in the tribal rule over al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions during the 15th-18th centuries. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces and established the First Khalidi Emirate with its capital in Al-Mubarraz. The confederation controlled territory extending from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south, wielding significant political influence over Najd in central Arabia. The Bani Khalid maintained both Shia and Sunni Muslim members within their confederation.

The Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob, became staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement in the 18th century. They had initially ordered the emir of al-Uyayna to expel Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, forcing him to relocate to Diriyyah where he allied with the Al Saud. The confederation attempted to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to halt Saudi expansion, but their efforts failed. The Saudis conquered al-Hasa in 1793, deposing the Al 'Uray'ir rulers and ending the first period of Khalidi rule.

After the Ottoman restoration in 1818 and the brief reinstatement of Al 'Uray'ir rulers, the Bani Khalid faced internal quarrels over leadership and encroachment from rival tribes including the Ajman, Dawasir, Subay', and Mutayr. Despite forging an alliance with the Anizzah tribe, they were eventually defeated by tribal alliances led by the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and by the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, definitively ending Khalidi political power. Many Bani Khalid clans, including Al Suhoob members, had already settled in al-Hasa and Nejd, while others migrated to Iraq, Lebanon, Palestine, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and the UAE following their military defeats.

migrationPath.to: Iraq, Lebanon, Palestine, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, UAE
migrationPath.description: Migration following military defeats and end of political power, with many clans dispersing throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Levant
