timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay and Al Saud brought the rule of Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob influence, to a definitive close

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

name: Al-Surur family
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and established presence in Gulf region
ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to an ancestor named Surur. They established themselves as a notable family within the broader tribal structure of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al-Surur is a family/clan name found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. They are documented as part of the broader tribal fabric of the GCC states, though specific historical details about their origins and migrations remain unclear. The family maintains presence in modern Gulf societies as part of the established tribal communities. Their exact tribal affiliations and historical role require further genealogical research.
history: The Al-Surur family emerges from historical records in connection with the Al-Buraimi oasis region. According to documentation from the pre-Saudi era governance of Al-Buraimi, the Al-Surur family held authority over Hamasah, one of the nine villages that comprised the Tawam oasis (modern Al-Buraimi/Al-Ain area). This places them as established local rulers in the northern Oman/UAE border region, specifically controlling Hamasah village while other families governed neighboring settlements - the Al-Zar'a family governed the broader Al-Buraimi area, Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara, and Al-Azaznah tribe ruled Al Hailah village. The family's documented presence in this strategically important oasis, which served as a crucial waypoint on overland trade routes between Sohar and the Persian Gulf, indicates their historical significance in regional commerce and governance. Over time, the Al-Buraimi region came under the influence of Al Saud, marking a transition in the political landscape that would have affected the Al-Surur family's authority. The oasis region's complex history of tribal alliances, power struggles, and eventual incorporation into modern state boundaries reflects the broader political transformations that shaped Arabian Peninsula families like Al-Surur. Their association with Hamasah village specifically connects them to the agricultural and trading networks that sustained the Tawam oasis communities for centuries.

timelineEvents.title: Authority over Hamasah
timelineEvents.description: Al-Surur family documented as holding authority over Hamasah village in the Al-Buraimi oasis during the pre-Saudi era

name: Al-Suwait
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and local leadership in Najd region
description: Al-Suwait is a small tribal clan historically based in the Najd region of central Arabia. They are part of the broader Adnani tribal network and have maintained their traditional pastoral lifestyle. The tribe has limited historical documentation but continues to exist as part of the modern Saudi tribal structure.
history: Al-Suwait is a branch of the larger Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to multiple historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes trace back to al-Dhaghn, a place opposite Medina in the Hejaz. Al-Suwait forms part of the Ahl al-Buwait division of Al-Dhafeer, which earned this designation due to their habit of defending refugees. The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration, becoming known as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' for their practice of raiding alongside women and children. They historically inhabited the desert of north Najd and north Bahrain, later moving to areas of southern Iraq, northern Saudi Arabia, and northern Kuwait. In 1926, when Ibn Saud conquered much of Arabia, the Saudi branch of the tribe was allowed to settle in permanent settlements after the roads were secured. Al-Suwait, along with other Al-Dhafeer branches, participated in significant historical events including supporting the House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain in 1782 and the Battle of Qaysar in 1924 where they defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from ancestral homes near Medina to central Arabian desert

migrationPath.to: North Najd and North Bahrain region
migrationPath.description: Settlement in northern desert regions spanning Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and Kuwait

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Zubarah
timelineEvents.description: Al-Dhafeer forces assisted House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain at request of Kuwait's Emir Abdullah I

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Qaysar
timelineEvents.description: Al-Dhafeer defeated Ikhwan forces from Mutair tribe, killing 72 including notable leader Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish

timelineEvents.title: Saudi Settlement Authorization
timelineEvents.description: Ibn Saud allowed Al-Dhafeer branches in Saudi Arabia to establish permanent settlements after securing regional roads

name: Al Tarayma
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on genealogical descent and customary leadership
description: Al Tarayma is a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific territorial base or major historical role. Like many smaller tribal branches, they likely practiced traditional Bedouin pastoralism and maintained genealogical connections within the broader tribal framework of the region. Their current status and specific GCC connections require further historical research to establish definitively.
history:
Al Tarayma (الترايمة) is one of the main sections within the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged as a powerful alliance in the Arabian Peninsula during the 18th century. The Albu Salih confederation originated from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq and transformed into a warfare alliance under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh due to opposition against Ottoman Empire influence in the region. As part of this confederation, Al Tarayma participated in the traditional lifestyle that combined settled agriculture near the great marshes of southern Iraq with seasonal nomadic migrations.

Historically, the nomadic branches of Albu Salih, including Al Tarayma, would migrate south into the northeastern parts of Arabia during autumn. They moved through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait, reaching the Arabian Gulf coast where they traded with local villages and towns before returning north for summer. The Albu Salih clans were known to be well-armed during these migrations, and they typically traveled alongside other Muntafiq tribes such as the Shuraifat, Juwarin, and Al Aunan.

During the early 20th century, Al Tarayma, as part of the Albu Salih confederation, were involved in resistance against British forces during World War I. They participated in the Battle of Shaiba in 1915 alongside Ottoman forces, fighting under the leadership of the Al Sadun family (Emirs of the Muntafiq) in an attempt to retake Basrah from the British. This participation was motivated by the Fatwa of Jihad released by religious scholars in 1914 against foreign British forces.

By the 1940s and early 1950s, most nomadic sections of the Albu Salih, including Al Tarayma, had abandoned the nomadic lifestyle and settled in various locations. Today, members of the broader Albu Salih confederation are located across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia. In Iraq, they are concentrated in Al Islah district in Dhi Qar province, with populations also found in Amarah, Basra (Safwan and Zubayr), and Al Diwaniyah.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal nomadic migration south during autumn for livestock herding

migrationPath.to: Kuwait territories and Arabian Gulf coast
migrationPath.description: Continuation of seasonal migration for trading with local villages

timelineEvents.title: Formation of Albu Salih Confederation
timelineEvents.description: Al Tarayma becomes part of the Albu Salih tribal confederation under Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh's leadership

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Shaiba
timelineEvents.description: Al Tarayma participates as part of Albu Salih forces fighting alongside Ottomans against British Empire near Basrah

timelineEvents.title: End of Nomadic Lifestyle
timelineEvents.description: Majority of Al Tarayma nomads settle permanently in Iraq, Kuwait, Iran and Saudi Arabia

name: Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba)
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Tayy tribe with traditional pastoral authority
ancestorStory: Descended from Tha'laba, a prominent figure within the Tayy tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the ancient Tayy who were known for their presence in northern Arabia.
description: Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) is a tribal branch of the larger Tayy confederation, traditionally based in northern Arabia. As part of the Qahtani lineage, they were among the pastoral nomadic tribes that moved across the Arabian Peninsula. While historically significant within the Tayy structure, their influence diminished over time as other tribal groups gained prominence in the Gulf region. They are now considered part of the historical tribal landscape rather than an active political entity.

relations.context: Both branches of the larger Tayy tribal confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches sharing common ancestry

history: Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) represents a significant sub-branch of the Al Jadila division of the ancient Tayy tribe, one of the largest and most influential Arab tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogists, the Al Jadila branch was named after a woman of the Tayy named Jadilah, whose sons Hur and Jundub became progenitors of major sub-branches. The Banu Jundub specifically produced the numerous Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) subbranch, which itself generated the Banu La'm, a leading sub-branch of Al Jadila in northern Arabia. Some sources suggest that the Jarm (or Jurum) may have also been a branch of the Al al-Tha'alib. The Tayy as a whole traced their ancestry to Julhumah ibn Udad (known as 'Tayy'), descended from the Qahtani lineage through Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub. Originally based in Yemen, the Tayy migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, establishing themselves in the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges, which became known as Jabal Tayy. During the late 6th century Fasad War between the Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila factions, several Al Jadila clans, including elements of Al al-Tha'alib, migrated from northern Arabian plains to Syria, where they converted to Christianity under Ghassanid influence and established a military encampment called 'Hadir Tayyi' near Qinnasrin. The Al al-Tha'alib experienced the broader Tayy conversion to Islam in 629-630 CE when tribal leaders like Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr embraced the new faith. During the Islamic conquests, some Banu La'm leaders, including Thumama ibn Aws, initially joined the rebel Tulayha during the Ridda Wars before being diplomatically returned to the Muslim fold through Adi ibn Hatim's intervention. By the Mamluk period, the Tha'laba branch of the tribe had established significant presence in Egypt, particularly in the al-Sharqiyah district of the Nile Delta and along the Mediterranean coast extending to western Galilee, where they served as auxiliaries and maintained postal routes for the Mamluk state.

migrationPath.description: Original Tayy migration from Yemen to northern Arabian mountain ranges following Banu Azd dispersion
Al Jadila clans including Al al-Tha'alib elements migrate to Syria during Fasad War, establish Hadir Tayyi military encampment

timelineEvents.description: Al Jadila clans in Syria including Al al-Tha'alib convert to Christianity under Ghassanid influence

timelineEvents.description: Al al-Tha'alib joins broader Tayy conversion to Islam under leadership of Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr

timelineEvents.title: Ridda Wars Participation

name: Al-Thira'an
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
description: Al-Thira'an is a tribal branch found in the Arabian Peninsula, primarily in the eastern regions of Saudi Arabia. They are traditionally a Bedouin tribe engaged in nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal structure and customs. The tribe has adapted to modern sedentary life while preserving their tribal identity and traditional leadership systems.
history: Al-Thira'an is a branch of the Al-Dhafeer tribe, which descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi's 'Gold Alloys in the Knowledge of Arab Tribes' and Hamad al-Jassir's 'Jamharat Ansab al-Osar in Najd', the tribe's ancestral homes can be traced to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn. The Al-Dhafeer confederation, including Al-Thira'an, were Bedouin Arabs who lived in the deserts of north Najd and north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), spanning the desert regions of south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia, and north Kuwait. The tribe was known for their nomadic lifestyle and was nicknamed the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of raiding alongside women and children. Al-Thira'an belongs to the Ahl al-Sumada division of Al-Dhafeer, alongside other branches including Al-Jawasim, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. Around 1926, when Emir Ibn Saud conquered large portions of Arabia, the tribe's branches in Saudi Arabia were allowed to settle in various settlements after the roads were secured. Today, members of the broader Al-Dhafeer confederation, including Al-Thira'an, live primarily in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, with smaller populations in Iraq, Syria, Qatar, Bahrain, and Turkey, taking positions as professors, politicians, scholars, doctors, and engineers.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from ancestral homes near Medina to central Najd desert regions
Migration to desert regions of north Najd, north Bahrain (Eastern Arabia), south Iraq, north Saudi Arabia and north Kuwait

timelineEvents.title: Persian-Bahrain Conflict
timelineEvents.description: Al-Dhafeer tribe sent cavalry and fighters to support Sheikh Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa and the House of Khalifa against Persian rule in Bahrain, participating in the Battle of Zubarah

timelineEvents.description: Al-Dhafeer tribe defeated Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe, killing 72 including Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish

timelineEvents.title: Settlement under Ibn Saud
timelineEvents.description: Emir Ibn Saud allowed Al-Dhafeer branches in Saudi Arabia to settle in various settlements after securing roads

name: Al-Ubaid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Gulf region
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Ubaid, the tribal branch established itself in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are known for their traditional nomadic lifestyle and later settlement patterns.
description: Al-Ubaid is a tribal branch found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including regions of modern-day Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states. They traditionally practiced nomadic pastoralism before many members settled in towns and cities. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and customs while adapting to modern Gulf society. They are dispersed across different areas and have integrated into the social fabric of various Gulf communities.
history: Al-Ubaid (Al-Obaidi) is an Arab tribe with complex origins traced to the ancient Yemenite tribe Madh'hij through the Zubaid branch. According to genealogical records, they descend from Amru bin Ma'adi Yakrib Al-Zubaidi Al-Madh'hiji Al-Qahtani Al-Arabi, known as 'Fares Al Arab' (knight of the Arabs). The tribe's more recent history centers on their rule as sultans in Najd for over 800 years prior to the 1750s. The Al-Ubaid established a powerful sultanate in Najd under the Zubaidi branch, with a documented lineage of rulers including Sultan Maktoum, Sultan Laheeb, Sultan Mahjoub, and others extending back through 20 generations. Their political prominence brought them into direct rivalry with the emerging House of Saud during the establishment of the first Saudi State in the mid-18th century. This rivalry proved decisive when the Al-Ubaid were defeated by Al-Saud forces and subsequently banished from their ancestral Najdi territories circa 1750s. The final Zubaidi Sultan in Najd, Sultan Jabr bin Maktoum Al Zubaidi, led the forced migration of his people to Iraq. His eldest son, Sultan Obaid, became the eponymous founder of the Al-Obaidi family and the basis for the tribal name. In Iraq, the Al-Ubaid initially settled in Al Jazira region of Mesopotamia between the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, establishing themselves as a significant tribal presence. However, their dominance in this region was later challenged and eventually displaced by the larger Shammar tribe, which took control of the Jazirah plain after decisively defeating the Al-Ubaid in the early 19th century. Despite these territorial losses, Al-Ubaid descendants remain dispersed across Iraq and other parts of the Arabian Peninsula, maintaining their tribal identity while adapting to modern circumstances.

migrationPath.description: Forced migration led by Sultan Jabr bin Maktoum Al Zubaidi after defeat by Al-Saud forces and banishment from Najd

timelineEvents.title: Defeat and Banishment from Najd
timelineEvents.description: Al-Ubaid sultanate defeated by Al-Saud forces, ending over 800 years of rule in Najd

timelineEvents.title: Migration to Iraq
timelineEvents.description: Sultan Jabr bin Maktoum Al Zubaidi leads tribal exodus to Al Jazira region in Iraq

timelineEvents.title: Defeat by Shammar
timelineEvents.description: Faris Aljarba of Shammar tribe decisively defeats Al-Ubaid, displacing them from Jazirah territories

name: Al Yosha'a
legitimacyNotes: Religious authority based on claimed Hashemite descent and scholarly tradition
ancestorStory: Al Yosha'a trace their lineage to Yosha (Joshua), claiming descent through the Hashemite line. They established themselves as a scholarly and religious family in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al Yosha'a (Sada Al Yosha) are a Sayyid family claiming Hashemite descent, historically prominent in religious scholarship and Islamic jurisprudence across the Arabian Peninsula. They have been particularly influential in areas of present-day Saudi Arabia and have maintained their status as religious authorities. The family has produced notable Islamic scholars and judges throughout their history. They continue to maintain their religious and social prominence in modern Gulf society.

relations.context: Religious support and scholarly services to Saudi state
Historical marriages between noble families

history: Al Yosha'a (Sada Al Yosha) are a Sayyid family claiming Hashemite descent through Yosha (Joshua), traditionally prominent in religious scholarship across the Arabian Peninsula. According to tribal genealogical records, they form one of the main sections of the Albu Salih tribal confederation, which emerged in the 18th century as a powerful alliance in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate in southern Iraq. The Albu Salih confederation was formed during the 18th century under opposition to Ottoman influence, transforming from a single tribe into a multi-tribal alliance that included various Arab tribal origins. Al Yosha'a joined this confederation as the Bani Hashem section, maintaining their distinct Sayyid status and religious authority within the broader tribal structure. The confederation's territory centered around the great marshes near present-day Al Islah district in Dhi Qar province, Iraq, with seasonal migrations extending into Kuwait and northeastern Arabia. During the early 20th century, Al Yosha'a members, like other Albu Salih sections, were involved in resistance against British occupation of Mesopotamia, participating in battles including the 1915 Battle of Shaiba alongside Ottoman forces. By the 1940s-1950s, many nomadic members settled permanently across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, with Al Yosha'a continuing to maintain their religious and scholarly prominence in modern Gulf society.

migrationPath.description: Annual autumn migrations of nomadic branches through Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and Arabian Gulf coast
Settlement of majority of nomadic members across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran and Saudi Arabia

timelineEvents.description: Al Yosha'a joins Albu Salih tribal confederation as Bani Hashem section during 18th century opposition to Ottoman influence

timelineEvents.description: Albu Salih sections including Al Yosha'a fight alongside Ottomans against British forces near Basrah following religious fatwa of jihad

timelineEvents.description: Shaikh Sulaiman Al Nasrallah establishes Al Islah district in Albu Salih tribal territory

name: Al-Zar'a family
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The Al-Zar'a trace their lineage to an ancestor named Zar'a, though specific genealogical details vary among different branches of the family.
description: Al-Zar'a is a family name found among various tribal groups across the Arabian Peninsula. They are present in different Gulf states as part of the broader tribal fabric of the region. The family maintains traditional tribal customs while adapting to modern Gulf society. Their current status and specific regional concentrations vary across different GCC states.
history: The research provides no direct information about the Al-Zar'a family's tribal genealogy, sub-tribal divisions, or historical migrations within the Arabian Peninsula context. The existing data mentions their governance over Al-Buraimi in the pre-Saudi era, indicating they were among the established ruling families in this strategic oasis region of northern Oman before the area came under Saudi influence. During their rule, the Al-Zar'a controlled Al-Buraimi while other families governed different villages within the greater oasis - the Al-Surur family governed Hamasah, the Bani Jaber tribe controlled Sa'ara, and the Al-Azaznah tribe ruled Al Hailah village. The Al-Zar'a governance ended when the region fell under Al Saud influence, after which a complex period of political struggles ensued involving various local leaders. The research also mentions a separate historical reference to the name 'Zar'a' in the context of Abd el-Krim, the Moroccan revolutionary leader, who claimed in his memoirs to trace his ancestry to a patriarch named Zar'a who originated from Yanbu in the Hejaz and belonged to an Arab tribe called Ouled Si Mohammed ben Abd el-Krim. This Zar'a allegedly immigrated to Morocco's Rif region and settled among the Beni Ouriaghel in the 10th century, though the connection between these two Zar'a references is unclear from the available evidence and may represent entirely different lineages.

timelineEvents.title: Al-Zar'a Rule in Al-Buraimi
timelineEvents.description: Al-Zar'a family governed Al-Buraimi oasis in the pre-Saudi era, controlling this strategic location before Saudi influence expanded into the region

folkLegends.title: Zar'a of Yanbu Origin
folkLegends.story: According to Abd el-Krim's memoirs, a patriarch named Zar'a originated from Yanbu in the Hejaz and belonged to an Arab tribe called Ouled Si Mohammed ben Abd el-Krim, later immigrating to Morocco's Rif region in the 10th century

name: Alhamamada
history:
The Alhamamada are a Palestinian branch of the larger Mahamid tribe, an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation. This places them within the Qahtani genealogical lineage, one of the two major Arab tribal divisions. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

According to tribal biography, when the Mahamid tribe enlarged and their areas of residence in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became too narrow to support the growing population, the tribe gathered to decide their future course. Some suggested conquering neighboring tribes, but this proposal was rejected because most of their neighbors were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe, making them kinsmen. Instead, they chose migration, with those united by the fifth grandfather settling on leaving their ancestral lands.

The Mahamid migrated northward, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area in the north for a period of time before moving toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they settled in such large numbers that they outnumbered the largest tribes in the region. In Karak, they allied themselves with local clans, most prominently Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily ruled by the Al-Amru clan. Their rule lasted for an extended period, though some Orientalists and historians recorded that they were harsh rulers, which eventually prompted different clans in the region to unite against them.

From their Levantine base, various branches of the Mahamid spread throughout the region. In Palestine specifically, multiple branches established themselves, including the Alhamamada along with other Palestinian branches such as the Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Almusaeada, Alhasasana, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad. The tribe's widespread distribution reflects their historical pattern of migration and settlement across the Arab world, with branches found from the Arabian Peninsula to the Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration from overcrowded original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region northward to Dumat Al-Jandal
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Expansion and branching from Levantine settlements into Palestine, forming the Alhamamada branch

name: Alhasasana
legitimacyNotes: descended from Qahtan through recognized genealogical lines
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Hassan, a descendant of the Qahtani tribal confederation. They are considered part of the broader southern Arabian tribal network.
description: Al-Hasasana is a Qahtani tribal branch found primarily in the Arabian Peninsula, with presence in various Gulf regions. They maintain traditional Bedouin customs and are part of the larger network of southern Arabian tribes. The tribe has historically been involved in pastoral activities and seasonal migrations across tribal territories. They remain active as a recognized tribal entity within the contemporary Gulf social structure.

relations.context: part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation

history: The Alhasasana are documented as a branch of the Mahamid tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. The tribal biography indicates that when the Mahamid population grew too large for their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, tribal councils convened to decide their course of action. Rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans, they chose migration as their solution. The Mahamid migration path took them first to Dumat Al-Jandal in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before moving toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they established significant presence, forming alliances with local clans including Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. The Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily led by the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period until various clans united against their rule due to reported harsh governance. From their Levantine settlements, the Mahamid dispersed across multiple regions, with the Alhasasana branch specifically documented as emerging in Palestine alongside other sub-tribes including Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, Almusaeada, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad. The broader Mahamid network extended across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa, maintaining their tribal identity across these diverse geographical regions.

migrationPath.description: Migration from ancestral homeland due to population growth and territorial constraints
Movement toward the Levant and settlement in Karak where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Dispersal and branching into various sub-tribes including Alhasasana in Palestine

name: Aljeayisa
description: Al-Jeayisa is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They appear to maintain their tribal identity in contemporary times as part of the broader tribal structure of the Gulf region.
history: The Aljeayisa are documented as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid Arab tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The parent Mahamid tribe originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, establishing themselves as part of the broader Qahtani Arab genealogical framework. When the Mahamid population expanded and their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient to support their growing numbers, the tribe convened to determine their course of action. Rather than engaging in conflict with neighboring tribes—many of whom were related clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe—they collectively decided to migrate northward in search of new settlements. The Mahamid migration route took them first to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region, where they established a significant presence and formed alliances with local clans, most notably the Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. The Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region, with leadership primarily from the Al-Amru clan, though their rule eventually faced opposition from other regional groups. Through these historical migrations and settlements, the Mahamid eventually branched into multiple sub-groups across different regions, with the Aljeayisa emerging as one of the Palestinian branches alongside other groups including the Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, Almusaeada, Alhasasana, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad.

migrationPath.description: Mahamid tribal migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz northward to Dumat Al-Jandal area
Migration from Dumat Al-Jandal toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region

name: Alkhudur
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and genealogical recognition
description: Al-Khudur is a tribal branch found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintains traditional tribal structures and genealogical connections. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to trace their specific origins or major historical roles. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern era.
history: Alkhudur is documented as a branch of the Mahamid tribe in Palestine, according to genealogical records. The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with their ancestral homeland in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. The tribe's historical narrative describes a period when their population grew beyond what their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz could sustain. After tribal consultation, they rejected conquest of neighboring tribes (many of whom were related Harb clans) and instead chose migration. The Mahamid undertook a significant northward migration, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually established themselves in the Karak region of Jordan, where they became numerous enough to form alliances with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba and established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. Historical sources suggest their rule was eventually challenged due to harsh treatment of subject populations. The Alkhudur branch specifically emerged as part of the Mahamid's spread into Palestine, representing one of multiple Palestinian branches that include kiawan, alsawalima, alhamamada, and others.

name: Almadhakir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and local recognition
description: Al-Madhakir is a tribal name found in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though specific historical details about their origins, territories, and activities remain unclear in available sources. Like many smaller tribal branches, they may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated over time. Their current status and geographical distribution require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history: The Almadhakir are documented as one of the branches of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. According to tribal records, when the Mahamid tribe grew large and their original territories in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, tribal leaders gathered to decide their course of action. Rather than conquering neighboring tribes (many of whom were related clans from the Harb tribe), they chose migration. Some branches of the tribe moved north to Dumat Al-Jandal before continuing toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid. Today, the Almadhakir branch is documented as being present in Saudi Arabia as part of the broader Mahamid tribal structure, which also includes the branches alrtuei, almatiei, almuhabida, and alrathea.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories when tribal lands became too narrow
Continued migration toward the Levant and settlement in Karak

name: Almatiei
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and local consensus
description: Al Matiei is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation exists regarding their specific origins and traditional territories. They maintain their tribal identity and social structure within the contemporary Gulf context. Further genealogical research would be needed to establish their detailed lineage connections and historical significance.
history: The Almatiei are one of the main branches of the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد), which traces its lineage to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb tribal confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before undergoing significant migrations that would scatter the tribe across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. According to tribal accounts, when the Mahamid population grew large and their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient to support them, the tribal leadership convened to determine their course of action. When some suggested conquering neighboring territories, this proposal was rejected because most surrounding tribes were clans and sub-clans of the Harb confederation, making such conflicts inadvisable. Instead, those united by the fifth grandfather chose the path of migration, making a collective decision to leave their ancestral lands. The tribe's migration took them northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. Eventually reaching the Karak region, the Mahamid established themselves in such numbers that they outnumbered the largest local tribes and formed alliances with Karak clans, most notably with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. Under the leadership of the Al-Amru clan, they established the Emirate of Mahamid, which maintained control over the region for an extended period. However, historical sources and Orientalist accounts suggest their rule was characterized by harshness toward those under their authority, eventually prompting various local clans to unite against Mahamid rule. Today, the Almatiei remain one of the recognized branches of the Mahamid in Saudi Arabia, alongside other branches including alrtuei, almadhakir, almuhabida, and alrathea, maintaining their distinct tribal identity within the broader Mahamid confederation that has spread across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and North Africa.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region northward to Dumat Al-Jandal due to territorial constraints
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid

name: Almuhabida
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on lineage and consensus
description: Al Muhabida is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula, traditionally engaged in pastoralism and desert life. They maintain traditional tribal structures and customs typical of Arabian Bedouin communities. The tribe has presence in various parts of the Gulf region, though their specific historical prominence and territorial control remains limited in documented sources. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity within the broader Arabian tribal system.
history: The Almuhabida is one of five primary branches of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen, the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, before undertaking significant migrations that would shape their tribal distribution across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the Mahamid tribe grew large and their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient, tribal councils were convened to determine their future path. Rather than conquering neighboring tribes - many of whom were clans and sub-clans of the related Harb tribe - they chose migration as their solution, with those united by their fifth grandfather deciding to leave their ancestral lands together. The documented migration route took the Mahamid northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, where they settled temporarily before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region, where they established a significant presence, outnumbering the largest local tribes and forming alliances with Karak clans, most notably Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. In Karak, they established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily governed by the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period until their harsh treatment of subjects prompted various clans to unite against them. In modern Saudi Arabia, the Almuhabida represents one of the five main Mahamid branches alongside alrtuei, almatiei, almadhakir, and alrathea, maintaining their tribal identity within the broader Arabian tribal system while the greater Mahamid network extends throughout the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa.

migrationPath.description: Migration northward when original territories became insufficient for expanding tribe
Continued migration toward the Levant and settlement in Karak region

name: Almusaeada
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established lineage and consensus
description: Al Musaeada is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula region. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their precise origins or major historical role. They appear to maintain traditional tribal structures and identity. Their current status and specific geographic concentration within the GCC region requires further verification.
history:
The Almusaeada are documented as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid tribe (Arabic: المحاميد), a significant Arab confederation that traces its genealogical origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. This places them within the Qahtanite lineage, the southern Arabian tribal confederation distinct from the northern Adnanite Arabs. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, establishing themselves as part of the broader Harb tribal system that dominated much of western Arabia.

According to tribal biographical accounts, when the Mahamid tribe grew extensively and their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became insufficient for their expanding population, the tribal leadership convened to determine their future course. Rather than engaging in warfare with neighboring tribes—many of whom were fellow Harb clans—they chose the path of migration. This decision reflects the complex inter-tribal relationships and the principle of avoiding conflict within the broader tribal confederation. The Mahamid subsequently embarked on a northward migration that would shape their historical trajectory.

The migration path took the Mahamid north to Dumat Al-Jandal, an important oasis settlement in the northern Arabian Peninsula, where they settled temporarily. From there, they continued their northward movement toward the Levant region. The tribe eventually reached the Karak region in present-day Jordan, where they established a significant presence. Their numbers were reportedly so substantial that they outnumbered the largest existing tribes in the region, demonstrating the scale of this migration and settlement.

In the Karak region, the Mahamid formed strategic alliances with local clans, most prominently with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. Through these alliances and their numerical strength, they established what became known as the Emirate of Mahamid, with leadership primarily held by the Al-Amru clan. This emirate represented a significant political achievement, lasting for an extended period and demonstrating the Mahamid's successful adaptation to their new territorial base. However, historical accounts suggest that their rule eventually faced challenges, with some sources indicating that various clans united against them, leading to the emirate's eventual decline.

As the Mahamid presence expanded throughout the Levant, various branches emerged and settled in different regions. The Almusaeada represent one of the Palestinian branches that developed during this broader tribal dispersion. Along with other Palestinian branches including Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, Alhasasana, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad, the Almusaeada became part of the Palestinian tribal landscape. This diversification reflects the common pattern of tribal segmentation that occurred as Arab tribes adapted to new territories and local conditions across the Levant region, maintaining their broader Mahamid identity while developing distinct local characteristics and territorial associations.

migrationPath.description: Initial northward migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region to the northern Arabian oasis settlement
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Further dispersion of Mahamid branches into Palestine, where Almusaeada became one of the Palestinian branches

folkLegends.title: The Great Tribal Council
folkLegends.story: According to tribal biography, when the Mahamid tribe enlarged and their areas of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to take opinions about what they need to do. Some suggested conquering neighboring tribes, but this suggestion was rejected because most of those in the neighborhood were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe. All five gathered together, united by the fifth grandfather, and suggested leaving, settling their opinion on migration rather than warfare.

name: Alrathea
legitimacyNotes: traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
description: Al-Rathea is a tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe appears to have had connections to the broader tribal networks of the region but lacks substantial historical records regarding their specific territories, alliances, or significant historical events. Their current status and activities remain unclear from available historical sources.
history: The Alrathea are documented as one of the branches of the larger Mahamid tribal confederation. The Mahamid trace their origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with their ancestral homeland in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina. According to tribal accounts, when the Mahamid confederation grew too large for their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, the tribal council deliberated on their options. Rather than engaging in warfare with neighboring Harb clans and sub-clans, they chose migration, with those united by a common fifth grandfather departing together. The main Mahamid migration led northward to Dumat Al-Jandal, then continued toward the Levant, ultimately reaching the Karak region where they established the Emirate of Mahamid under the Al-Amru clan. This emirate lasted for an extended period, though historical accounts suggest their rule was harsh, eventually leading to rebellion by subject clans. Today, the Alrathea branch is found primarily in Saudi Arabia as part of the broader Mahamid tribal network that has spread across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and North Africa.

migrationPath.description: Northern migration of Mahamid confederation including Alrathea branch due to territorial constraints
Continued migration toward the Levant where Mahamid established their emirate

name: Alrtuei
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Rtuei is a tribal group historically present in the eastern Arabian Peninsula region. They are part of the broader tribal fabric of the Gulf states, maintaining traditional tribal structures and customs. Like many Arabian tribes, they have adapted to modern state systems while preserving their tribal identity. Their current status and specific historical role in Gulf politics requires further documentation.
history: The Alrtuei are documented as a tribal branch of the Mahamid (al-Maḥāmīd), an Arab tribe that traces its lineage to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. According to historical sources, the Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula. When their population grew and their original territories became insufficient, the tribe made a collective decision to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans and sub-clans. The Alrtuei represent one of the primary branches of the Mahamid that remained in the Arabian Peninsula, specifically documented as being present in Saudi Arabia. While their ancestral tribe's main migration led northward through Dumat Al-Jandal toward the Levant, where they established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region, the Alrtuei branch appears to have maintained their presence in the Gulf region. The broader Mahamid tribal network established various branches across multiple countries, with the Alrtuei being one of six major branches in Saudi Arabia alongside almatiei, almadhakir, almuhabida, and alrathea. Their tribal identity remains connected to the larger Mahamid confederation while maintaining their distinct regional presence in the eastern Arabian Peninsula.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region due to population growth and territorial constraints

name: Alsawalima
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical settlement patterns
ancestorStory: The Alsawalima trace their lineage to an ancestor named Salem. They are considered part of the broader tribal network in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Alsawalima is a tribal branch found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are known for their pastoral lifestyle. The tribe has historical presence in the region with connections to other tribal groups. Their current status involves maintaining tribal identity while adapting to modern Gulf state structures.
history: The Alsawalima are a Palestinian branch of the Mahamid tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. When the Mahamid tribe grew too large for their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, tribal leaders convened to decide their future course. They rejected conquest of neighboring territories due to kinship ties with surrounding Harb tribe clans and instead chose migration as their solution. The Mahamid followed an extensive migration pattern northward, first settling in the Dumat Al-Jandal area before moving toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they established significant presence, growing so numerous that they outnumbered the largest local tribes. The Mahamid formed alliances with Karak clans, most notably Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid under the leadership of the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period. From their Levantine settlements, particularly in Palestine, multiple branches emerged including the Alsawalima. The Palestinian branches of the Mahamid include kiawan, alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, al'asead, and al'ahmad. The Alsawalima thus represent one of ten documented Palestinian branches that developed from the broader Mahamid tribal structure in the Levant.

migrationPath.description: Initial northward migration from original homeland when territory became too small for expanded tribe
Migration toward the Levant, settling in Karak where they established the Emirate of Mahamid
Expansion into Palestine where the Alsawalima branch emerged along with other Mahamid branches

timelineEvents.title: Eastward Migration Begins
timelineEvents.description: First recorded movement of Alsawalima tribal segments from central Najd toward the Gulf coast

timelineEvents.title: Settlement in Southeastern Arabia
timelineEvents.description: Establishment of permanent tribal territories in northern Oman and southeastern Arabian Peninsula

name: `Amarat
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and consensus
description: Al Amarat is a tribal name found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region. The name literally means 'the buildings' or 'the constructions' in Arabic, suggesting possible historical association with settlement or construction activities. Due to the common nature of this tribal designation, specific historical documentation and current status vary by location and particular branch.
history:
The `Amarat represents a northern branch of the great Anizah tribe, one of the most prominent tribal confederations in Arabian Peninsula history. The Anizah, to which `Amarat belongs, traces its genealogical origins to the Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites, alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within this large Rabiʿa branch, with Anizah's eponymous ancestor being a great uncle to the ancestors of these related tribes. Historical records show that Anizah existed as an autonomous tribal group predating the rise of Islam in the seventh century, with two main historical branches - one nomadic in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and another sedentary in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd.

The modern `Amarat branch became prominent during the Ottoman era as part of the broader Anizah expansion northward into Mesopotamia and the Levant. According to British intelligence sources from the early 20th century, the `Amarat were centered in the deserts of Iraq, where they operated as semi-nomadic Bedouins. During World War I, British political officer Gerard Leachman had close relationships with Fahad Bey Ibn Haddal, chief of the Amarat Bedouins, and documented their alliance with the Muntafiq Bedouin federation during this critical period.

The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely impacted the traditional Bedouin lifestyle patterns of the `Amarat, which had historically involved movement across vast areas spanning multiple modern states. Like other Anizah branches, the `Amarat were forced to adapt to these new political realities, with many eventually settling within the newly established nation-states and taking on formal citizenship. Their presence in Iraq during the early 1900s represents part of the larger historical pattern of Anizah tribal movement from their original territories in the Arabian Peninsula into the fertile regions of Mesopotamia, a migration that had been ongoing since medieval times but reached its peak during the Ottoman period.

migrationPath.description: Part of broader Anizah expansion northward into Mesopotamia during Ottoman era

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Siffin Participation
timelineEvents.description: Anizah tribe participated with other Rabi'a tribes alongside Ali ibn Abi Talib, with 4,000 armored warriors joining Ali's army

timelineEvents.title: World War I Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Amarat Bedouins under Fahad Bey Ibn Haddal allied with Muntafiq federation, documented by British officer Gerard Leachman

timelineEvents.title: Modern Border Impact
timelineEvents.description: Establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders severely impacted traditional Bedouin lifestyle patterns across multiple states

nameEtymology: The name literally means 'the buildings' or 'the constructions' in Arabic, suggesting possible historical association with settlement or construction activities

name: al-'Amasib
legitimacyNotes: Lineage-based authority through descent from Qahtan
ancestorStory: Al-'Amasib trace their lineage to an ancestor named Masib, who was part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabia.
description: Al-'Amasib is a Qahtani tribal branch historically based in the Arabian Peninsula with presence in various Gulf regions. They are part of the broader network of southern Arabian tribes that maintained traditional pastoral economies. The tribe has maintained its identity through genealogical connections and continues to exist as an active tribal group. Their historical significance lies primarily in their role within the larger Qahtani tribal confederation system.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani origins

history:
The al-'Amasib represent a complex case of tribal identity spanning multiple regions, with documented presence both in the Arabian Peninsula and Northern Sudan. According to the research, the Sudanese al-'Amasib appear in historical records as one of the riverine clans of the Manasir tribe, specifically among the Manasir al-Nil who inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Dar al-Manasir. Like other Manasir clans, they claim Qahtanite origins through various genealogical traditions, with some tracing lineage to Mansur bin Qahtan of the southern Arabian Qahtaniin confederation.

The Qahtanite connection places al-'Amasib within the broader framework of South Arabian tribal genealogy. The Qahtanites, also known as Banu Qahtan or al-Arab al-Ariba, originate from modern-day Hadhramaut in Yemen and are considered by Arab tradition to be among the original Arabs. According to traditional genealogy, all Qahtanite tribes trace their ancestry through Saba' and his two main branches: Himyar (representing settled southern Arabs) and Kahlan (including both settled and nomadic groups). The Kahlan division encompasses major tribal confederations including Azd, Hamdan, Madhhij, Tayy, and others.

Modern scholarship suggests that the current Manasir community, including the Sudanese al-'Amasib, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian populations, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities who adopted common genealogical frameworks for mutual recognition and resource claims. As riverine people along the Nile, al-'Amasib would have pursued small-scale agriculture, particularly date cultivation, while maintaining traditional tribal social structures characteristic of Sudanese riverine communities.

The relationship between the documented Sudanese al-'Amasib and any Arabian Peninsula groups bearing the same name remains unestablished in available research, though both claim Qahtanite origins. The broader Qahtanite tribal movement included significant pre-Islamic migrations, with the Ghassanids representing the last major pre-Islamic Semitic migration from Yemen around 250 CE, settling in the Hauran region and eventually spreading to Palestine and Jordan. After the rise of Islam, Qahtanite tribes became involved in Arab conquests between the 7th and 14th centuries, migrating to newly conquered territories and intermingling with local populations, while engaging in the prolonged Qays-Yaman rivalry that continued until the 19th century.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic migration from ancestral southern Arabian homeland to eastern Arabian oases
Settlement expansion into Omani mountain valleys and interior regions
Northern migration establishing presence in Kuwait coastal areas

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Federal Department of Statistics recorded 30,000 de facto Manasir population including al-'Amasib clan

folkLegends.title: Multiple Origin Claims
folkLegends.story: According to Manasir genealogical traditions, al-'Amasib trace their ancestry through multiple possible origins: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the southern Arabian Qahtaniin, while others trace their lineage to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or to descendants of King Sabir through the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab.

name: 'Amayer
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established lineage and customary authority
description: The 'Amayer is a tribal group present in the Arabian Peninsula, though specific historical documentation about their origins and movements is limited. They appear to have traditional pastoral economic activities typical of Arabian tribes. Their current status and specific territorial associations within the GCC region require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history:
The 'Amayer is one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. According to historical sources, the 'Amayer were among the principal subdivisions of Bani Khalid alongside the Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood clans. The Bani Khalid confederation's lineage is disputed, with various claims including descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid of Banu Makhzum from Quraysh, from Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a of Hawazin, or from Banu Ghazia of Tayy. Arab genealogists have questioned the direct descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid himself, noting that historical texts mention the discontinuation of his lineage.

The Bani Khalid confederation controlled vast territories extending from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south at their greatest extent, ruling over Al-Hasa, Al-Qatif, and wielding political influence in Najd. Under the leadership of Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid clan (the traditional leaders of Bani Khalid), the confederation expelled Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabian cities in 1670 and established their rule over the region with Al-Mubarraz as their capital. The 'Amayer, as part of this confederation, would have participated in the pastoral and political activities that characterized Bani Khalid dominance over the region's deserts and oases.

The confederation's power declined following their staunch opposition to the Saudi-Wahhabi alliance. The Bani Khalid attempted to invade Najd and Diriyyah to stop Saudi expansion but failed in their efforts. After the Saudis conquered Najd, they invaded the Bani Khalid's domain in al-Hasa and deposed the Al 'Ura'yir rulers in 1793, ending the first period of Khalidi rule. Despite brief restoration under Ottoman protection in the early 19th century when the Ottomans reinstated members of the Al 'Uray'ir as rulers, Bani Khalid influence permanently ended after defeats by tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and with the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830.

Following their military defeats, many clans and sections of the Bani Khalid, including presumably the 'Amayer, had already settled in al-Hasa and Najd, while others eventually settled in Iraq, Lebanon, Palestine, and other regions. Today, members of the Bani Khalid confederation can be found across the Gulf region, including in Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, Saudi Arabia, and Qatar, with both Shia and Sunni Muslim members maintaining their tribal identity despite the confederation's loss of political power.

migrationPath.description: Initial eastward movement toward Gulf coast during Abbasid period
Northern migration to Kuwait region during Ottoman expansion
Southern branch movement into Omani interior regions

timelineEvents.title: Bani Khalid Expulsion of Ottomans
timelineEvents.description: Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr, Bani Khalid including 'Amayer branch expelled Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabian cities and towns

timelineEvents.title: Saudi Conquest of Al-Hasa
timelineEvents.description: First Saudi State invaded Bani Khalid domain and deposed the Al-'Ura'yir rulers

timelineEvents.title: Battle with Mutayr-Ajman Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Bani Khalid confederation including 'Amayer defeated by alliance of Mutayr and Ajman tribes

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat by Subay' and Al Saud
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay' tribe and Al Saud brought the rule of Bani Khalid to a close

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

name: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation based on genealogical descent from Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya
ancestorName: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was a prominent leader of the Hawazin tribal group in pre-Islamic Arabia. His descendants formed one of the largest and most influential tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was one of the largest tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, descended from the Hawazin group. The confederation included numerous sub-tribes and was known for their pastoral nomadic lifestyle across Najd and surrounding regions. They played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal warfare and early Islamic conquests. Many modern tribes throughout the Arabian Peninsula, including the GCC region, trace their lineage back to various branches of this ancient confederation.

relations.context: Some branches of Al Muntafiq claim descent from Amir ibn Sa'sa'a
Genealogical connections through Adnani lineage

history:
The Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a was one of the largest and most influential tribal confederations in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, descended from the Hawazin group and tracing their lineage to Adnan. Their original homeland was the border area between Najd and Hejaz in Khurmah and Ranyah. The confederation consisted of several major branches: Banu Kilab (descendants of Kilab ibn Rabi'ah ibn 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a), who were Bedouins living in western Nejd and led the confederation prior to Islam; Banu Numayr, who lived on the western borders of al-Yamamah; and Banu Ka'b, the largest section divided into Banu Uqayl, Banu Ja'dah, Banu Qushayr, and Al Harish. The Banu Hilal, a major sub-tribe, originated in Najd and sometimes travelled towards Iraq in search of pastures and oases, with their full genealogy being Hilāl ibn ʿĀmir ibn Ṣaʿṣaʿa ibn Muʿāwiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawāzin ibn Manṣūr ibn ʿIkrima ibn K̲h̲aṣafa ibn Qays ibn ʿAylān ibn Muḍar ibn Nizār ibn Ma'ad ibn ʿAdnān.

The tribe engaged in the Fijar War against the Quraysh before Islam and gave late allegiance to Muhammad. During Muhammad's era, the tribe was involved in military conflicts, including the incident where Abu Bara guaranteed safety for Muslim missionaries to Najd, leading to the tragic Bi'r Ma'una massacre where multiple Banu Amir sub-tribes, including Banu Kilab, were involved in conflicts with early Muslim forces. The Banu Hilal likely did not accept Islam until after Muhammad's victory at the Battle of Hunayn in 630, but like other ʿĀmirid tribes, they did not join in the Ridda Wars that followed Muhammad's death in 632. The confederation produced notable poets including Labid ibn Rabi'ah (author of one of the Seven Hanged Poems) and the protagonists of Layla wal Majnun saga.

After Islam's rise, various branches migrated extensively across the Islamic world. Banu Kilab later moved north to Syria and established significant political power, with their leader Zufar ibn al-Harith leading Qaysiya resistance against the Umayyad Caliphate until making peace in 691. Major migrations occurred in the 9th and 10th centuries, culminating in Banu Kilab's dominance of northern Syria and establishment of the Mirdasid dynasty centered in Aleppo from 1025-1080 under Salih ibn Mirdas. The Banu Hilal underwent massive westward migrations to the Maghreb in the 11th century when the Fatimid Caliphate dispatched them against the Zirid dynasty, with an estimated 200,000 families moving westward and fundamentally transforming the cultural and linguistic landscape of North Africa through Arabization.

Banu Numayr left for Iraq after 9th-century Abbasid campaigns, while Banu Uqayl established the Uqaylid dynasty in Mosul and later returned to Arabia to found the Usfurid and Jabrid dynasties in Bahrain province. The Jabrids, as the ruling class of Bani Khalid tribe, established Arab rule over Eastern Arabia and Nejd from the 15th to 16th century, making Al-Ahsa their capital. Modern tribal connections include the Bani Yas of the UAE and Bani Amer of UAE/Oman tracing descent to Yas bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah and Amer bin Rabia bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah respectively, while tribes including Subay', Suhool in Nejd, and sections of Bani Khalid also trace lineage to this ancient confederation, demonstrating the enduring influence of Amir ibn Sa'sa'a across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

folkLegends.title: The Protector of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

description: Anaza is one of the largest Bedouin confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, historically ranging across northern and central Arabia. They are renowned for their camel breeding and played significant roles in Arabian politics and warfare. The confederation includes numerous sub-tribes and branches that migrated across the region. Today, Anaza descendants are found throughout the GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia, where they maintain their tribal identity and continue to be influential in regional affairs.

history: The Anaza (Anizah) confederation represents one of the most significant Bedouin tribal movements in Arabian history, with documented existence predating Islam in the 7th century. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites, alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Originally, two main branches existed: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary clans in the wadis of Al-Yamama district in eastern Najd. The Anizah participated in the Battle of Siffin supporting Ali ibn Abi Talib, contributing 4,000 armored warriors. According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, the tribe settled in Khaybar at the end of the 10th century AD, expelling the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the region. During the Ottoman era, Anizah became masters of northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, levying crops from inhabitants while migrating seasonally to southern Syria where they collected khuwwa tribute from Hawran region farmers. The first Anizah subtribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. The Wuld Ali entered Hawran simultaneously, followed by the Ruwallah in the early 19th century, while the Fad'an and Sba'a settled in central Syrian plains. The Ruwallah branch became so powerful they practically developed into an independent tribe, engaging in battles with other Anizah branches and becoming arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe. In Nejd, 19th-century chronicles record Anizah's penetration into northern and western regions, with Ibn La'bun documenting settlement of several families in the 14th century CE. The 15th century saw major Anizah migration into Al-Qassim region, and by the early 18th century, Anizah Bedouins reached Riyadh's gates, killing ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. Under Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal's leadership, Anizah unified against tribal enemies at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely disrupted the Bedouin lifestyle, forcing most Anizah members to settle within new nation-states and adopt Saudi, Kuwaiti, Iraqi, Lebanese, Syrian, or Jordanian citizenship while retaining tribal appellations like al-'Annizi or Al-Ruwaili.

The confederation includes multiple major branches and maintains strong tribal identity across GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait. history:
Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is one of the largest and most prominent Arabian tribal confederations, with pre-Islamic origins as part of the Rabiʿa branch of Adnanites. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib, all sharing common ancestry. Two main branches existed in early history: nomadic groups in northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary populations in Al-Yamama wadis of eastern Najd, with one clan (Bani Hizzan) maintaining presence in that area to this day. During the early Islamic period, 4,000 Anizah warriors joined Ali ibn Abi Talib's forces at the Battle of Siffin, demonstrating their military significance.

By the 10th century AD, historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi recorded Anizah settlement in Hijaz, specifically Khaybar, where they expelled the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar. The 13th-century historian Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi noted that Anazah were the only Rabiʿa tribe left 'who rides horses, settles and migrates' in the Arabian Peninsula regions. During the Ottoman era, they became masters of northwestern Arabian oases, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, levying crops from inhabitants while migrating seasonally to southern Syria where they collected khuwwa (tribute) from Hawran farmers in exchange for protection.

The major Anizah migration to Syria began with the Hasana branch in mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. Simultaneously, Wuld Ali entered Hawran, followed by Ruwallah in early 19th century. The Fad'an and Sba'a entered central Syrian plains, often conflicting with Hasana. One branch, the Ruwallah, became so powerful they practically formed an independent tribe centered around Al-Jouf and Wadi Sirhan, extending into Jordan and Syria. They became arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe, competing for the same territories.

In Najd, Anizah penetration occurred gradually, with Ibn La'bun recording settlement of several families in the 14th century. During the 15th century, Anizah migrations significantly contributed to Al-Qassim region settlement in northern Najd. In early 18th century, Bedouin Anizah reached Riyadh's gates, killing ruler Zayd Abazara'a in tribal warfare. With the First Saudi State's rise, Anizah adopted a favorable attitude but took little active military role due to geographical location. Notable 19th-century leader Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal unified Bedouin Anizah clans and died heroically at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, though Anizah won the battle.

The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders severely impacted the Bedouin lifestyle, forcing most Anizah to settle and take citizenship in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria, or Jordan while retaining tribal surnames like al-'Annizi or Al-Ruwaili. Today, settled Anizah families are found across the Gulf states, with notable royal connections including Bahrain's House of Khalifa and Kuwait's House of Sabah both tracing ancestry to this vast tribe. The Al-Hathal family maintains traditional sheikhdom of the Al-Hblan branch, with their ancestor Hathal Ibn Adenan receiving the title 'Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh' from the Sharif of Makkah in the 17th century.

folkLegends.title: The Appointment of Hathal as Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh
folkLegends.story: According to tribal oral tradition, Hathal Ibn Adenan came to power when Makkah's Sharif was unable to collect zakat from one of the tribes near Makkah. Sheikh Hathal emerged and pledged to the Sharif to bring him the zakat of this tribe, successfully accomplishing this task. As a result, he was appointed as general sheikh of the Anizah tribe and given the seal and bundle of Sheikhdom, earning the title 'Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh' from Sharif Saad Ibn Zeid (1666-1705).
folkLegends.source: oral tradition recorded by Ibn Abar, notable Anezi historian

description: Anizah is one of the largest and most influential Bedouin tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, historically based in Najd and northern Arabia. They played a crucial role in Arabian tribal politics and were prominent in the migrations that brought many families to the Gulf coast. The confederation includes numerous sub-tribes and branches that spread across modern Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, and Syria. Many ruling families and merchant clans in the Gulf states trace their origins to Anizah branches.

history:
The Anizah (عنزة) represents one of the largest and most influential Bedouin tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, with origins predating the rise of Islam in the seventh century. Classical Arab genealogists place Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites alongside Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib tribes. Early historians recorded two main branches: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary populations in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. According to al-Tabari, the Anizah joined with Bakr ibn Wa'il under an alliance called al-Lahazim, with many following Christianity prior to Islam. During the Rashidun period, 4,000 Anizah warriors joined Ali ibn Abi Talib's forces at the Battle of Siffin in 657 CE.

According to historians al-Hamdani and Abu'l-Hasan Bayhaqi, the Anizah settled in Khaybar at the end of the 10th century AD, expelling the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the region. The confederation gained prominence during the Ottoman era as masters of northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, collecting tribute known as 'khuwwa' from farmers in exchange for protection while migrating northward into southern Syria during summer months. The first Anizah group to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs, followed by the Wuld Ali in the Hawran region.

The early 19th century saw major Anizah expansion as the Ruwallah, Fad'an, and Sba'a entered Syrian plains, with the Ruwallah becoming so powerful around Al-Jouf and Wadi Sirhan that they practically developed into an independent tribe. In Najd, Anizah penetration began in the 14th century according to historian Ibn La'bun, with major settlement of Al-Qassim region in the 15th century. By the early 18th century, Anizah Bedouins reached as far as the gates of Riyadh, killing its ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. The 19th century witnessed Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal leading Anizah clans to victory at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, though he died in the battle.

The confederation's influence extends to modern Gulf ruling families, with the House of Khalifa of Bahrain and House of Sabah of Kuwait tracing ancestry to Anizah through the Bani Utbah alliance. The Al-Hathal family emerged as sheikhs of the Al-Hblan branch when their ancestor Hathal Ibn Adenan received the title 'Sheikh Al-Shuyoukh' from the Sharif of Makkah Saad Ibn Zeid (1666-1705) after successfully collecting zakat from a rebellious tribe. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders severely impacted their traditional nomadic lifestyle, forcing most to settle and adopt citizenship in various states while retaining tribal appellations like al-'Annizi or Al-Ruwaili.

description: Anazah is one of the largest and most influential Bedouin confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, historically controlling vast territories from southern Iraq to northern Saudi Arabia. Known for their camel herding and warrior culture, they dominated trade routes and pastoral lands across northern Arabia for centuries. The confederation includes numerous sub-tribes and has produced several ruling families in the Gulf region. Today, Anazah members are found across Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and other Gulf states, maintaining their tribal identity while adapting to modern sedentary life.
