history:
The Anizah (Arabic: عنزة, Għanża) is one of the largest and most influential Bedouin confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, tracing their lineage to the Adnani branch of Arab tribes through Wael ibn Qasit. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites alongside Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates the rise of Islam in the seventh century, with early historians recording two main branches: nomadic groups in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and sedentary clans in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. The Anizah participated in the Battle of Siffin (657 CE) supporting Ali ibn Abi Talib with 4,000 armored warriors.

During the 10th century, the Anizah settled in the Hijaz region, specifically Khaybar, where they fought and expelled the descendants of Ja'far al-Tayyar from the area. Under Ottoman rule, they became prominent as masters of northwestern Arabian oases, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, collecting tribute known as 'khuwwa' from inhabitants while migrating seasonally between winter settlements and summer pastures in southern Syria. The first Anizah tribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, followed by the Wuld Ali, Ruwallah, Fad'an, and Sba'a. The Ruwallah branch became so powerful they developed into a practically independent tribe, engaging in battles with other Anizah branches and becoming arch-enemies of the Shammar tribe.

In Nejd, sparse chronicles relate a process of penetration into northern and western Nejd beginning in the 14th century, where they claimed winter pastures. In the 15th century, the region of Al-Qassim in northern Nejd was rapidly settled through migration, with the majority of this activity by members of Anizah. In the early 18th century, Bedouins of Anizah reached as far as the gates of Riyadh, killing its ruler Zayd Abazara'a in battle. With the rise of the First Saudi State in the late 18th century, Anizah adopted a favorable attitude towards this new power but took little active military part due to their geographical location.

In the 19th century, Sheikh Mashaan Ibn Hathal gained fame leading Anizah clans and unifying them against enemies at the Battle of Al Shimasiyah on June 18, 1825, where he died but the tribe achieved victory. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely impacted their nomadic lifestyle, forcing most to settle within new nation-states and adopt Saudi, Kuwaiti, Iraqi, Lebanese, Syrian, or Jordanian citizenship while retaining tribal appellations. Notable ruling families descended from Anizah include the Al-Sabah of Kuwait and Al-Khalifa of Bahrain, both through the Utub Alliance. The relationship of Saudi Arabia's Al-Saud family to Anizah is disputed, with claims of maternal connection while they are officially recognized as part of Banu Hanifa.

name: Anz ibn Wa'il
legitimacyNotes: Lineage authority through descent from Wail ibn Qasit of Rabi'a confederation
ancestorStory: Anz was son of Wail ibn Qasit, part of the great Rabi'a tribal confederation. The tribe descended from this eponymous ancestor in pre-Islamic times.
description: Anz ibn Wa'il was a tribal branch of the larger Rabi'a confederation, descended from Wail ibn Qasit. They were part of the northern Arabian tribal network that dominated much of the Arabian Peninsula before Islam. The tribe was primarily nomadic, moving through the northern and central Arabian territories. Like many pre-Islamic tribal branches, they eventually merged or were absorbed into larger confederations during the Islamic expansion period.

relations.context: Both descended from Rabi'a confederation through different branches

history: Anz ibn Wa'il was a tribal branch of the great Rabi'a confederation, descended from Wail ibn Qasit through the eponymous ancestor Anz. According to classical Arab genealogists, they were part of the northern Arabian tribal network alongside their cousins Banu Bakr, Taghlib, Anizah, and other Rabi'a branches. The tribe inhabited southern Arabia and were mentioned by 13th-century historian Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi, who noted that 'the Banu Anz are in the region of Tabalah' when asked about remaining Rabi'a tribes in the Arabian Peninsula. Like other Rabi'a tribes, they were said to have worshiped an idol named al-Sa'eer in pre-Islamic times. The tribe suffered significant losses during the plague epidemic of the 13th century, which reportedly decimated their numbers. Despite this catastrophe, descendants claiming connection to Anz ibn Wa'il are said to survive, with a modern tribe named 'Rabī'a' in the 'Asir region being identified as their potential remnant. The tribe's historical presence in southern Arabia, particularly around Tabalah, places them in the southwestern highlands of the Arabian Peninsula, distinct from their northern Rabi'a cousins who migrated toward Mesopotamia and Syria.

timelineEvents.title: Plague Decimation

name: Asad
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented genealogical ties to Adnanite lineage
ancestorStory: Descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah, a prominent figure in pre-Islamic Arabian genealogy. The tribe traces its lineage through the Adnanite branch to Ishmael.
description: Banu Asad was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Adnanite lineage, historically based in the Najd region of central Arabia. They were known for their poetry, horsemanship, and played significant roles in pre-Islamic and early Islamic history. The tribe produced notable figures including poets and early Islamic converts, and participated in the Ridda Wars following Prophet Muhammad's death. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula tribal politics, their direct relevance to modern GCC state formation is limited as they were largely absorbed into other tribal confederations over time.

relations.context: The two tribes became allies and intermarried after Tayy migration to Jabal Tayy, though Asad lost some territory initially
Tayy branch invaded mountains of Ajaa and Salma from Banu Assad

history:
Banu Asad (Arabic: بَنُو أَسَدْ) is an Adnanite Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, tracing their lineage through Ishmael to Abraham. The tribe shares common ancestry with Prophet Muhammad through their ancestor Khuzaimah ibn Mudrikah, making them genealogical cousins of the Prophet. In the 6th century CE, Banu Asad rose to significant prominence when they revolted against the Kingdom of Kindah, killing King Hujr (father of the last Kindah king Imru' al-Qais) and ultimately causing the end of the Kingdom of Kindah, forcing Imru al-Qais to flee the Najd region.

The tribe produced notable literary figures including the illustrious Arabian mu'allaqat poet 'Abid bin al-Abras. In the 7th century, Banu Asad migrated from their original Najd territories to Iraq, settling primarily in Kufa near the banks of the Euphrates river, with additional communities established in Basra and Ahvaz alongside Banu Tamim. During the early Islamic civil wars, the tribe aligned with Ali ibn Abi Talib, participating in the Battle of the Camel and playing a crucial role in the Battle of Karbala (680 CE).

At Karbala, many prominent Banu Asad members died alongside Husayn ibn Ali, including Habib ibn Muzahir (commander of the left flank) and Muslim ibn Awsaja al-Asadi. Following the massacre, members of Banu Asad had the honor of burying the bodies of Husayn, his family, and companions three days later, earning them lasting respect among Shia Muslims. In 998 CE, Ali ben Mazyad of Banu Asad established the virtually independent Mazyadid emirate in the Kufa area, founding the city of Hillah in 1101 which became their capital. The Mazyadids wielded considerable influence for a century and a half through their powerful tribal army.

Today, Banu Asad members are distributed across multiple regions: Iraqi cities including Basra, Najaf, Kufa, Karbala, Nasiriyah, Amarah, Kut, Hillah, Diyala and Baghdad; Northern Sudan (Banu Kahil branch); Iran's Khuzestan province; Kuwait (Al Mazeedi family); Yemen (Hadhramaut); Oman; and India (Karnataka). The tribe maintains particular reverence among Shia Muslims for their role in burying Husayn's body and their martyrdom at Karbala.

name: Athbaj
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Hilal confederation with traditional pastoral authority
ancestorStory: Descended from Athbaj, a son of Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. The lineage traces back to the great Hilalian migrations from Najd.
description: The Athbaj are a tribal branch of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from the Adnanite lineage through Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. Historically based in the Arabian Peninsula's interior regions, they were part of the great Hilalian tribal movements that characterized early Islamic expansion. As pastoralists, they maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle centered on camel and sheep herding. The tribe's influence waned over time as sedentarization and modern state formation transformed traditional tribal structures in the region.

relations.context: Both tribes participated in historical migrations across the Arabian Peninsula

history: The Athbaj (Arabic: الاثبج, al-Athbaj) is a major sub-tribe of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from the Adnanite lineage through Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. Originally based in the Najd region of central Arabian Peninsula, the Athbaj were one of the three main divisions of Banu Hilal alongside the Riyah and Zughba. In the early 8th century, some Athbaj moved to Egypt along with other Hilalian tribes. In the 970s, they joined the Qarmatians in attacks against the Fatimid Caliphate, resulting in their forced relocation to Upper Egypt by Fatimid caliph al-Aziz in 978. The major transformation came in the 11th century during the great Hilalian migration to the Maghreb, when the Fatimids dispatched the Banu Hilal westward to punish the rebellious Zirids of Ifriqiya. According to Ibn Khaldun, the Athbaj was composed of two main branches: the Garfa and the Drid, and was considered one of the most important Hilalian tribes during this invasion period. In the 12th century, the Athbaj inhabited areas south and east of the Zughba territory, which stretched from Tlemcen to Algiers. Leo Africanus noted their presence in Doukkala and the plains of Tadla in Morocco, where they served as al-Mansur's main recruits, numbering 100,000 fighting men with more than half mounted. Following the Almohad conquest of the Banu Hilal, the Athbaj were resettled throughout the Maghreb al-Aqsa under Abd al-Mu'min and continued under Yaqub al-Mansur, with some clans settled in the plains of Tamasna according to Ibn Khaldun's records.

name: Awamir
legitimacyNotes: A large tribe that originated in the steppes to the north of the Hadhramut and settled in the north through a migration process over 500 years. Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage rather than a political confederation.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: The Awamir is a Bedouin Arab tribe in the United Arab Emirates and Oman, closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. Warlike and fiercely independent, they were traditionally camel breeders, raiders and occasionally date farmers before settling in the 1960s. They roamed across the whole Oman peninsula, from Muscat and Nizwa to Abu Dhabi and Liwa, and were characterized as reputed brave and warlike but also crafty, treacherous and predatory.

relations.context: Desert nomads allied with Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi
By the 1920s, the Awamir fought alongside the Manasir, Duru and Bani Qitab in conflicts around Buraimi and Abu Dhabi town.
The Awamir had long been in conflict with the Duru, and in the 1940s this ongoing conflict led the headman of the Bedouin Awamir to lead his people to Al Hasa in 1943.

history:
The Awamir is a large Bedouin Arab tribe that originated in the steppes north of the Hadhramut and migrated northward over a 500-year period to settle across the Arabian Peninsula from Muscat and Nizwa to Abu Dhabi and Liwa. By the early 20th century, they numbered approximately 10,000, with 3,500 being nomadic Bedouin and 4,000 settled outside of Oman proper. The Awamir were closely affiliated with the Bani Yas confederation of Abu Dhabi, frequently supporting them in conflicts while maintaining their reputation as warlike, fiercely independent camel breeders and raiders. A subsection called 'Afar was linked to Dhafrah. The tribe was characterized as reputed brave and warlike but also crafty, treacherous and predatory, roaming across the whole Oman peninsula.

In 1848, the Awamir played a crucial role in helping Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan expel Wahhabi forces from Buraimi Oasis, forming part of his tribal confederacy that achieved decisive strategic victory by 1850. They continued supporting Abu Dhabi's rulers through the 1880s wars with Qatar under Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. During the 1930s economic depression following the decline of pearling, increased Awamir raiding activities led to conflicts including the 1946 war between Dubai and Abu Dhabi, forcing British intervention.

Internal tribal conflicts with the Duru in the 1940s caused a schism when headman Salim bin Hamad bin Rakkad led part of the tribe to Al Hasa in 1943, with this group eventually becoming Saudi citizens while the remainder stayed at Buraimi under Abu Dhabi protection. By the 1950s, about 50 Awamir families had acquired date plantations in Buraimi, and by 1968, a census identified 1,721 members, many having transitioned from nomadic life to employment with oil companies, marking the end of their traditional lifestyle. The Manasir were noted as fighting against the Duru, Bani Qitab and Awamir in Buraimi in 1923, indicating inter-tribal conflicts in the region.

migrationPath.description: 500-year migration process from the steppes north of Hadhramut to northern territories

name: Awanat
description: The Awanat appear to be a historical tribal group with documented alliance connections to the Naqbiyin. Limited historical records survive regarding their specific territorial base or major role in Gulf politics. Their current status and lineage details remain unclear from available sources.

relations.context: Both tribes settled together in Khatt village

history:
The Awanat are documented as a historical tribal group that settled in Khatt, a mountainous village located southeast of Ras Al Khaimah in the United Arab Emirates. They formed one of three main tribes inhabiting Khatt alongside the Naqbiyin and Sharqiyin under Al Qasimi rule. The village of Khatt represents a site of remarkable historical continuity, with archaeological evidence spanning over 5,000 years of human occupation from the Stone Age through various periods including Ubaid, Hafit, Umm Al-Nar, Wadi Suq, and Sasanian periods.

Under Al Qasimi governance, Khatt achieved significant political importance. The village's Sheikh, Hassan bin Rahman, served as a signatory to the General Maritime Treaty of 1820 with the British, being formally recognized as 'Sheikh of Hatt and Falna.' This indicates that the Awanat and their tribal neighbors had established a substantial settlement with recognized political authority. The community engaged in extensive agricultural activities, particularly date palm cultivation, which formed the economic foundation of their settlement.

The Awanat faced periodic challenges from interior Bedouin groups who conducted raids on the settled communities. In 1888, conflicts erupted between Ras Al Khaimah townspeople and the mountain-dwelling Shihuh tribe, resulting in the destruction of over 200 date palms in Khatt. By 1908, British administrative records documented Khatt as a prosperous settlement containing 100 houses and 20,000 palm trees, demonstrating the agricultural success and demographic stability of the Awanat and their fellow inhabitants.

name: Aws and Khazraj
legitimacyNotes: Historical legitimacy as major pre-Islamic Arabian tribes and Ansar (supporters) of Prophet Muhammad in Medina
ancestorName: Aws ibn Haritha and Khazraj ibn Haritha
ancestorStory: Both tribes descended from Haritha ibn Tha'laba of the Azd confederation, migrating from Yemen to settle in Yathrib (later Medina). They became the dominant Arab tribes of the oasis before Islam.
description: The Aws and Khazraj were two major Arab tribes that dominated Medina (Yathrib) before and during the early Islamic period. Originally from Yemen as part of the Azd confederation, they migrated north and established themselves as the primary inhabitants of the Medina oasis. They are historically significant as the Ansar (supporters) who welcomed Prophet Muhammad and the early Muslim community to Medina in 622 CE. While not directly part of the modern GCC region, their Yemeni Azd origins connect them to tribal lineages that later spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula, including those in current Gulf states.
history:
The Aws and Khazraj were two major Arab tribes of Qahtanite origin who migrated from Yemen and settled in Yathrib (later Medina) in the pre-Islamic period. According to traditional genealogical sources, both tribes descended from the larger Azd confederation that had migrated northward from Yemen, likely following disruptions to the agricultural and political systems there. The tribes established themselves as the dominant Arab population in the Medina oasis, where they coexisted alongside three major Jewish tribes: Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qurayza, as well as smaller Jewish clans.

For nearly a century before the arrival of Islam, the Aws and Khazraj were locked in bitter rivalry and periodic warfare, with each tribe seeking to gain dominance over the oasis and its resources. This conflict was complicated by their relationships with the Jewish tribes, who were often drawn into the disputes as allies of one side or the other. The rivalry reached its climax in the Battle of Bu'ath around 617 CE, where the two tribes fought a decisive engagement that left both sides exhausted and their leadership depleted.

The arrival of Islam fundamentally transformed the relationship between the two tribes. In 620 CE, six members of the Khazraj encountered Muhammad during a pilgrimage to Mecca and converted to Islam. This initial conversion spread throughout both tribes, facilitated by the preaching of Mus'ab ibn Umayr, who had been sent to Medina by Muhammad. By 622 CE, a substantial number from both tribes had embraced Islam and pledged their support to Muhammad in what became known as the pledges of Aqaba. Notable early converts included Sa'd ibn Mu'adh from the Aws, whose conversion reportedly led to the immediate conversion of his entire Abdul-Ashhal clan.

When Muhammad and his followers migrated to Medina in 622 CE during the Hijra, the Aws and Khazraj collectively became known as the Ansar (the Helpers), welcoming and supporting the Meccan Muslims (Muhajirun). They were formally incorporated into the Constitution of Medina as 'one nation with the Believers,' ending their centuries-old rivalry in favor of Islamic unity. Both tribes played crucial roles in the early military campaigns of Islam, including the battles of Badr, Uhud, and the Trench. Sa'd ibn Mu'adh of the Aws became particularly prominent, serving as the judge who determined the fate of the Jewish Banu Qurayza tribe after their siege, before dying from wounds sustained in the Battle of the Trench. The descendants of both tribes maintained their significance in Islamic history, with some branches establishing lasting legacies including custodial roles in Jerusalem and claims to later dynastic foundations in Al-Andalus.

name: Ayal Abdo
legitimacyNotes: Tribal lineage and traditional leadership structure
ancestorStory: The clan traces its lineage to an ancestor named Abdo, though specific historical details of the progenitor are not well documented.
description: Ayal Abdo is a small tribal clan found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Like many smaller tribal groups, they maintain their genealogical identity while being integrated into larger tribal confederations or modern state structures. Their historical significance and territorial base are not well documented in major historical sources. They continue to exist as an active tribal identity within contemporary Gulf society.
history: Based on the research provided, Ayal Abdo is identified as a branch of the Mahamid tribe (al-Maḥāmīd), which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen, the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. According to tribal biography, when the tribe grew large and their original territories became insufficient, they made a collective decision to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans. The tribe moved northward to the Dumat Al-Jandal area, then continued toward the Levant, eventually reaching the Karak region where they established a significant presence. In Jordan, the Mahamid became so numerous that they outnumbered the largest local tribes and formed alliances with established Karak clans, particularly Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. They established the 'Emirate of Mahamid' with the Al-Amru clan providing leadership for an extended period. However, historical sources suggest their rule was marked by harsh treatment of subjects, which eventually led to unified resistance from other clans. Today, Ayal Abdo exists as one of the recognized branches of the Mahamid in Jordan, alongside other branches including Al Abdul Dayem, Al-Shaleikh, and Al-Bahri. The tribe maintains alliances with several other tribal groups including Al-Huwaitat, Bani Abbad, Bani Hamida, and Zoubi, reflecting the complex tribal confederation system of the region.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz region to northern Arabian settlement
Continued migration toward the Levant, settling in Karak region where they established significant presence

name: Azd
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented pre-Islamic inscriptions from 210-230 CE confirming their territorial presence. Genetic evidence suggests J-BY74 haplogroup associated with Azdite tribes originated in Northern Arabia or the Levant.
ancestorStory: In the 3rd century CE, the Azd branched into four sub-branches, each led by one of the sons of Muzayqiya
traditionalEconomy: trade and conquest - chief merchant group of Oman and Al-Ubulla, organized trading diaspora with settlements on coasts of Kirman and Makran
description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Zahran
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This appears to be a genuine blood lineage tracing back to the ancient Azd confederation, with well-documented genealogical records and historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula.
subTribes.ancestorName: Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd
subTribes.ancestorStory: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'. The full lineage traces back to Al-Azd through Zahran ibn Ka'ab.
subTribes.description: Zahran (Arabic: زهران), also known as Banū ʿZahrān ibn Kaʿab, is one of the oldest Arabian tribes in the Arabian Peninsula and is regarded as one of the largest tribes in Al Bahah Province. Al Baha is the homeland of Zahran and Ghamid. The tribe is well-known from pre-Islamic times and many joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The name Zahran means 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic.

subTribes.name: Ghamid
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Documented blood lineage tracing back through Azd Shanu'ah to Qahtan, with clear genealogical records and tribal presence maintained from pre-Islamic times to present
subTribes.ancestorStory: The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage back to their forefather Ghamid, who is Amr ibn Ka'b — and some say Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Ka'b — ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr, who is Shanu'ah of the Azd. There are different etymological explanations for the name, with Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i stating it derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning abundant water.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Agriculture and trade (benefited from Arabia Felix region with more rainfall and productive fields)
subTribes.description: The Ghamd (also transliterated as Ghamid) is an Azd Arab tribe of the Hejaz Region, predominantly Sunni, and considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Ghamid people are thought to be closely related to the neighboring tribe of Zahran. They have maintained their presence in their historical homeland from pre-Islamic times to the present day, and were significantly involved in early Islamic expansion and various historical conflicts.

relations.context: The Azd bordered Sabaeans in modern-day Yemen
Zahran is an offshoot of the Azd tribe confederation

history:
The Azd is an ancient Arabian tribe with Qahtani origins, tracing its lineage through Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd. According to pre-Islamic Sabaic inscriptions from Sha'r Awtar's reign (210-230 CE), the Azd occupied lands west of Bisha, extending through the southwestern heights between al-Baha and Asir provinces in modern Saudi Arabia. Medieval Yemeni folklore claims they originally lived in Yemen until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE forced their migration across the Arabian Peninsula, though genetic analysis of the Azdite haplogroup J-BY74 suggests Northern Arabian or Levantine origins rather than purely Yemeni ancestry.

In the 3rd century CE, the Azd underwent a major tribal division under the four sons of Muzayqiya. Imran bin Amr led the bulk of the tribe to Oman, establishing the Azd presence in eastern Arabia and later expanding to southern Persia, becoming known as 'Azd Uman' after Islam's emergence. Jafna bin Amr migrated to Syria where he founded the Ghassanid kingdom, which would later produce Roman Emperor Philip the Arab (244-249 CE) and Byzantine Emperor Leo III the Isaurian (717-741 CE). Thalabah bin Amr settled in the Hijaz near Yathrib, becoming ancestor to the Aws and Khazraj tribes who later became the Muslim Ansar. Haritha bin Amr remained in the Tihamah region, fathering the Bariq, Khuza'a, and Aslam tribes.

The Azd 'Uman became the dominant Arab force in the eastern caliphate from 637 CE, driving conquests in Fars, Makran, and Sindh. They established major commercial networks from their bases in Bahrain and Oman, with their influence peaking under Abu Said Al-Muhallab ibn Abi Sufra who extended Muslim conquests eastward until his death in 702 CE. The Azd maintained strong trading relationships with India and established settlements of Persianized Arabians on the coasts of Kirman and Makran. Their political fortunes fluctuated with Umayyad governance changes, suffering under Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf but recovering under subsequent caliphs like Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik.

The Zahran branch of the Azd played particularly significant roles in Arabian history. The Al-Jadara family from Zahran settled in Mecca and were known as wall-masons, with their progenitor Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama reportedly being the first to reconstruct the Kaaba's walls after Abraham and Ishmael. They formed alliances with the Quraysh through marriage, notably when Kilab ibn Murrah married Fatimah bint Sa'd Al-Jadari, producing Qusay ibn Kilab, an ancestor of Prophet Muhammad. Malik ibn Fehm of Zahran became one of the first Arabians to settle in Oman and founded the Tanukh confederation centered in Al-Hira, with his son Jadhima becoming king of Tanukh in the second half of the 3rd century CE. Modern Azdite descendants include the Al Said dynasty of Oman, the Ghassanids, the Nasrid dynasty of Al-Andalus, and various Gulf ruling families including Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and Al Maktoum of Dubai.

folkLegends.title: The Great Migration from Yemen
folkLegends.story: According to traditional medieval Yemeni folklore, the Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula due to unfavorable living conditions.

folkLegends.title: The Four Sons Division
folkLegends.story: In the 3rd century CE, the Azd branched into four sub-branches under the sons of Muzayqiya, with each son leading his people to different regions: Imran to Oman, Jafna to Syria, Thalabah to Hijaz, and Haritha remaining in Tihamah.

folkLegends.title: Amer the Wall-Mason
folkLegends.story: Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama of the Zahran branch is said to be the first to reconstruct the walls of the Kaaba after Abraham and Ishmael, earning him the title 'Amer the Builder' and his family the name 'Al-Jadara' (the Wall-Masons).

ancestorStory: Descended from Azd ibn al-Ghawth, a major Qahtani tribal ancestor. The Azd Uman branch migrated to Oman in pre-Islamic times and became the dominant tribal confederation in the region.
peakPowerEra: early Islamic period to Umayyad era
description: Azd Uman represents the major Qahtani tribal confederation that dominated Oman from pre-Islamic times. They are the ancestral confederation for many of Oman's major Hinawi tribes and established the foundation of Omani tribal society. The confederation played a crucial role in early Islamic expansion and maintained significant political influence throughout Omani history. Today, many prominent Omani families trace their lineage back to this confederation.

relations.context: Al Bu Said ruling family claims Azd ancestry
Al Nuaimi trace ancestry to Azd confederation

history: The Azd Uman represents one of the most significant tribal confederations in Arabian Peninsula history, originating from the great Azd tribal migration of the 3rd century CE. According to both historical sources and traditional folklore, the original Azd tribe inhabited the Sarawat Mountains region of what is now Saudi Arabia, particularly the areas between Bisha and Al-Bahah provinces, bordering the ancient Sabaean territories. The confederation's defining moment came around 200 CE following the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in Yemen, which triggered a massive tribal exodus known as the Azd migration. Under the leadership of Imran bin Amr, son of Muzayqiya, the bulk of the Azd tribe migrated eastward to Oman, where they established what became known as the 'Azd Uman' - distinguishing them from other Azd branches that settled in Syria (Ghassanids), the Hijaz (Aws and Khazraj), and Tihamah. The Azd settlers in Oman were specifically descendants of Nasr bin Azd and became known as 'Al-Azd of Oman.' Seventy years after this initial settlement, another significant Azd migration occurred under Malik bin Fahm, who is recorded as leading over 6,000 armed men to Oman, first settling in Qalhat before engaging in the legendary Battle of Salut against Persian forces. During the early Islamic period, the Azd Uman emerged as the dominant Arab confederation in the eastern realms of the expanding Caliphate. They became the driving force behind the Muslim conquests of Fars, Makran, and Sindh from 637 CE onwards. Their commercial prowess was equally impressive - they controlled the chief merchant networks of Oman and Al-Ubulla, organizing a vast trading diaspora with settlements extending from the coasts of Kirman and Makran into Sindh. Between 665-683 CE, the Azd Uman reached the peak of their political influence under Abu Said Al-Muhallab ibn Abi Sufra's governorship, which extended Muslim rule deep into Central Asia. However, their fortunes declined after 702 CE with the rise of Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf. The conversion of Omanis to Islam occurred during the 7th century through the mission of Amr ibn al-As, sent by Prophet Muhammad to the Julanda rulers of Oman. The Azd Uman's adoption of Ibadism, founded by Abd Allah ibn Ibad in Basra, became a defining characteristic. When conflicts with the Umayyad governor al-Hajjaj forced many Ibadi scholars back to Oman, including the influential Jaber bin Zaid, it greatly strengthened the Ibadi movement that would become Oman's predominant faith. The first elective Imamate of Oman was established around 750-755 CE, with the Azd Uman forming the backbone of this unique political-religious system that would characterize Omani governance for centuries. Today, the Azd Uman confederation remains the ancestral foundation for many of Oman's major Hinawi tribal groupings, and numerous prominent Omani families, including the ruling Al Bu Said dynasty, trace their lineage back to this ancient confederation.

name: Bakil
legitimacyNotes: Major tribal confederation with traditional authority in northern Yemen highlands
ancestorStory: Descended from Bakil ibn Jusham ibn Khashabah, one of the main branches of Hamdan. Along with Hashid, forms one of the two major Yemeni tribal confederations.
description: Bakil is one of Yemen's two largest tribal confederations, historically based in the northern highlands around Sa'ada and extending into areas near the Saudi border. The confederation has played a significant role in Yemeni politics and has had interactions with Saudi Arabia due to their border proximity. While primarily Yemeni-based, some Bakil elements have historical presence in southern Saudi regions. The confederation remains politically active in contemporary Yemen.

relations.context: Border conflicts and political tensions in northern Yemen-Saudi border regions

history:
Bakil (بكيل) is the largest tribal confederation in Yemen, comprising over 10 million people across four main sections: Arhab, Marhaba, Nihm, and Shaker. As a subtribe of the ancient Sabaean Banu Hamdan, Bakil shares origins with their sister confederation Hashid, both tracing descent from Hamdan bin Malik bin Zaid. The confederation's earliest historical mention dates to the 6th century BCE in Sabaean inscriptions, where they were referenced as part of the Kingdom of Sheba's federal structure. Ancient texts describe Hamdan's territory as 'Ardam Hamdan' (the land of Hamdan), referring to the barren highlands they inhabited around present-day Sa'ada and Amran.

During the Middle Sabaean period (1st-4th centuries CE), Bakil consisted of three main divisions: Raydah, Amran, and Shibam. In the 3rd century CE, most Hamdan tribes migrated to Syria, but Hashid and Bakil remained in Yemen, switching their allegiance from Sheba to the rising Himyarite Kingdom. The confederation's political significance grew after most other ancient tribal unions disappeared, leaving only Hashid and Bakil as the surviving major confederations by the 10th century.

Bakil's conversion to Islam occurred in 622 CE when Prophet Muhammad sent Khalid ibn Al-Walid to Yemen, though initial responses were lukewarm among highland Hamdani tribes. Ali ibn Abi Talib proved more successful in converting the Hamdan confederation. After Muhammad's death, Bakil remained steadfast to Islam and did not join the ridda (apostasy) movements. The confederation aligned with Ali ibn Abi Talib during the early Islamic civil wars, with many Bakil fighters participating in the armies of Ali at the battles of Siffin and the Camel.

In 893-897 CE, Bakil tribes invited Imam al-Hadi Yahya bin al-Hussain to the northern highlands at Sa'ada, establishing the foundation of Zaidi Imamate rule. This relationship earned Hashid and Bakil the designation as 'the two wings of the Zaidi imamate,' though not all tribal members accepted the imams' temporal authority. Throughout medieval and early modern periods, Bakil successfully resisted various foreign occupations, including the Ottomans, whom they helped expel in 1644 alongside other northern confederations.

Modern Bakil leadership is held by the Alshaif family under Naji bin Abdulaziz Alshaif. Unlike Hashid, Bakil lacks centralized authority due to multiple competing sheikhdoms within the confederation, maintaining their independence through decentralized leadership structures. The confederation remains politically active in contemporary Yemen, with their northern highland territories extending to areas near the Saudi border, giving them strategic importance in regional politics.

name: Bakr Bin Wail
legitimacyNotes: ancient tribal confederation with established lineage tracing to pre-Islamic Arabia
ancestorStory: Bakr bin Wail descended from the Rabi'a tribal branch, establishing one of the most prominent pre-Islamic tribal confederations in northeastern Arabia. The tribe's name comes from their progenitor Bakr, son of Wail bin Qasit.
description: Bakr bin Wail was a major pre-Islamic tribal confederation of the Rabi'a branch, historically based in northeastern Arabia including parts of present-day eastern Saudi Arabia and southern Iraq. They were known for their military prowess and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal wars, including conflicts with their Rabi'a cousins Banu Taghlib. During the early Islamic period, they participated in the Ridda wars and subsequent Arab conquests. Many of their descendants merged into other tribal formations or migrated, though their genealogical influence remains traced in various Gulf tribal lineages.

relations.context: both descended from Rabi'a tribal branch in pre-Islamic Arabia

history: Bakr bin Wail was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribal confederation of the Rabi'a branch, descended from the Adnanite lineage. Their progenitor Bakr was the eldest son of Wail bin Qasit, who established leadership over the clan due to his exceptional qualities as a horseman and warrior. Originally based in Najd in central Arabia, the tribe controlled strategic waterways and grazing lands, engaging in dairy production, wool export, and caravan protection services between Hejaz and Mesopotamia, with a permanent camp near Hafar al-Batin. The Banu Bakr were renowned warriors who participated in major pre-Islamic conflicts including the Basus War and the First Battle of Kulab, achieving their greatest military victory at the Battle of Dhi Qar against the Sasanian Empire before Islam. Following this victory, most bedouin sections migrated northward to Upper Mesopotamia along the Euphrates, eventually conquering territory along the Tigris and reaching as far as Anatolia and the Caucasus - the region of Diyar Bakr in southern Turkey derives its name from them. During Muhammad's era, the tribe was involved in various military conflicts and retaliatory wars, with Muhammad criticizing their actions as ungodly. After accepting Islam, they joined the early Islamic conquests under Caliph Umar, serving as cavalry guides in the conquest of the Levant and later as officers in the Ottoman Empire until World War I. The 1257 Mongol siege of Baghdad forced their retreat to inner Arabia, with most eventually resettling in the Hauran region where descendants remain today.

name: Bani Abbad
legitimacyNotes: descended from Abbad ibn Qays, traditional tribal lineage authority
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Abbad ibn Qays, they trace their lineage through the ancient Arab tribal system. The tribe established itself in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula through traditional migration patterns.
description: Bani Abbad is a tribal branch that traces its lineage to pre-Islamic Arabian roots through Abbad ibn Qays. Historically they were pastoral nomads who moved across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained traditional alliances with other Arab tribes including the Mahamid confederation. Today they remain an active tribal entity with members across the GCC region, maintaining their cultural identity while adapting to modern Gulf society.

relations.context: The Mahameed alliance with Bani Abbad

history: The research reveals significant confusion regarding Bani Abbad, as multiple distinct groups bear this name across different historical periods and regions. The most documented is the Abbadid dynasty of al-Andalus (1023-1091), descended from the Banu Lakhm tribe of al-Hirah, who ruled the Taifa of Seville following the fall of the Caliphate of Cordoba. This dynasty, founded by Abu al-Qasim Muhammad ibn Abbad in 1023, became the most powerful Taifa state, absorbing neighboring territories through military conquest and political maneuvering. The Abbadids were known for their patronage of arts, sciences, and literature, with rulers like Abbad II al-Mu'tadid (1042-1069) and Muhammad al-Mu'tamid (1069-1095) being accomplished poets themselves. However, their reign ended in 1091 when they were overthrown by the conservative Almoravids under Yusuf ibn Tashfin. The research also mentions a contemporary Arabian Peninsula tribe called Bani Abbad that formed alliances with other Bedouin groups like the Mahamid confederation and participated in raids alongside Ta'amireh and other tribes, notably in a 1605 raid on Beit Sahour. Additionally, there is reference to a companion of Prophet Muhammad named Abbad ibn Bishr (c.597-632) from the Banu Abd al-Ash'al clan. The relationship between these various Abbad groups remains unclear from the available research, and the existing data's claim of descent from 'Abbad ibn Qays' cannot be verified through the provided sources.

name: Bani Ghafir
legitimacyNotes: No information about Bani Ghafir found in the provided text

description: The provided text does not contain information about the Arabian tribal entity 'Bani Ghafir'. The text discusses two completely different entities: Bani Hafiz (a village in Pakistani Kashmir with a family lineage from Hafiz Jamal ud din) and Reziq Bani Hani (a Jordanian footballer). Neither of these relates to the Arabian Gulf tribal system or the specific tribe 'Bani Ghafir' that was requested.
history: Based on the research provided, Bani Ghafir emerges as a significant tribal entity within the broader Ghafiri confederation, one of the two major tribal groupings of Oman and the Trucial States (today the UAE). The Ghafiri confederation, characterized as Nizari (Nejdi) from central and northern Arabia with Sunni affiliations, arrived in Oman approximately 2000 years ago and predominantly settled in the northwestern part of Oman. The Bani Ghafir tribe played a crucial leadership role within this confederation, with Mohammed bin Nasir Alghafiri of the Beni Ghafir tribe emerging as the paramount leader who united the Ghafiri tribes during the 18th century conflicts with their rivals, the Hinawi confederation. These serious conflicts between the two factions only ended when the sub-tribes were united under strong leaders from both sides - the Ghafiris gathering under Mohammed bin Nasir Alghafiri while the Hinawis united under Khalaf bin Mubarak Alhinai from the Bani Hina tribe. The almost equal strength of the two alliances led to the end of the major feuds. The Ghafiri-Hinawi rivalry played a decisive role in shaping Oman's political history, with all Omani tribes historically affiliating themselves with either alliance. A contemporary reference exists to a village called Sanaa Bani Ghafir in Oman's Al Khaburah wilayat in North Al Batinah Governorate, indicating the continued presence and territorial holdings of this tribal group in modern times.

description: The Bani Hadiyah is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, originating in Ras Al Khaimah and also in areas of Northern Oman. They are one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, along with the Bani Shatair. The tribe is divided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. Traditionally, they practiced seasonal migration, working in Ras Al Khaimah during the pearling season and farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter.

subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine blood lineage tribe with territorial base and distinct cultural identity, speaking Shehhi dialect with ancient Himyarite origins

subTribes.description: The Habus is an Arab tribe primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. They are an agrarian mountain people who cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The tribe is known for speaking the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic with Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. Fiercely independent, they successfully resisted taxation attempts and maintained semi-autonomous status well into the 1960s. Their name is derived from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain.

history:
The Bani Hadiyah (Arabic: بني هدية) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates and northern Oman, constituting one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, alongside the Bani Shatair. The tribe is further subdivided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. The Bani Hadiyah inhabit the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains, with their territory spanning the border between the UAE and northern Oman.

Traditionally, the Bani Hadiyah practiced a seasonal migration pattern that defined their economic and social life. During the pearling season, they would relocate to work in Ras Al Khaimah, particularly in the coastal villages of Sha'am and Rams. In winter, they would return to their mountain strongholds in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains, where they engaged in farming activities. This dual economic system allowed them to participate in both the lucrative pearl trade and mountain agriculture.

The relationship between the Shihuh tribes, including the Bani Hadiyah, and the rulers of Ras Al Khaimah was historically fractious and marked by political tensions. Both Rams and Sha'am, villages where the Bani Hadiyah worked during pearling season, experienced multiple secessions from Ras Al Khaimah rule. These political disputes reflected the independent nature of the Shihuh tribes and their resistance to centralized authority.

The political status of Bani Hadiyah territories was finally resolved in 1921, when the villages of Sha'am and Rams definitively became part of the recognized Trucial State of Ras Al Khaimah. This incorporation marked the end of the recurring cycles of secession and represented the formal integration of these traditionally Shihuh-inhabited areas into the emerging political structure of what would eventually become the United Arab Emirates.

description: Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, are an influential Qahtanite tribe in the Arabian Peninsula. Originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub), they are known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia. They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

history:
Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, are a Qahtanite Arab tribe originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub) in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula. In the early 13th century, they migrated northward to Lower Najd under the leadership of Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an. When leadership passed to Shafi bin Safar bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, the tribe migrated eastward to the village of Al Ahsa'a in the Eastern Province. The tribe became known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia, establishing themselves as influential merchants in the region.

During the Battle of Kinzan in Al Ahsa'a (1333 AH/1914-1915 AD), Bani Hajer played a crucial role in King Abdulaziz Al Saud's victory against the Al Ajman tribe. Led by Shafi bin Salim Al Sahfi Al Hajri, they joined the battle when the King's forces were in dire need, helping turn the tide when Al Ajman had ambushed and nearly killed King Abdulaziz (killing his brother Sa'ad Bin Abdulrahman). For their bravery, King Abdulaziz granted them the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) and awarded them the land from Ain Dar to the Qatar border (known as 'Jouf Bani Hajer'), plus immunity from capital punishment.

Today, Bani Hajer are spread across Saudi Arabia's southern and eastern regions, from Mount Ladam in the north to al Saaa'dani in the west. The tribe has significant presence in Qatar, where an entire district in Al Rayyan municipality is named after them, reflecting their historical settlement in the area alongside other Bedouin tribes such as the Al Murrah, Dawasir and Al Qahtani. Though traditionally nomadic Bedouins, many have settled in cities like Ain Dar, Salasil, Yakrub, and others, with members holding government positions. The tribe also has presence in other Gulf states (UAE, Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman) and Egypt, where they use the family surname Al-Hajri. Their current sheikh is Nasser Bin Hamoud Bin Sahfi Bin Salim Bin Shafi Bin Safar Bin Mohammed Bin Shaba'an.

Historically, Bani Hajer have maintained complex relationships with other major tribes in the region. In 1885, a number of Bani Hajer tribespeople, along with several members from the Al Murrah tribe, renounced their allegiance to Qatar and departed from the country, joining with the Ajman tribe who harbored hostilities toward Qatar. While encamped between Qatar and Oman, a regiment of 1,200 partisans composed of branches of Al Murrah and Bani Hajer loyal to the sheikh of Qatar engaged the group. Although the three opposing tribes boasted a combined force of 2,000 men, they were inflicted with heavy losses, demonstrating the internal divisions that sometimes occurred within these tribal confederations.

name: Bani Ham Mazyud
history: The Bani Ham Mazyud is a subsection of the Shihuh tribe, specifically part of the larger Bani Hadiyah section. The Shihuh, whose singular name is Al Shehhi, are divided into two main sections: the Bani Hadiyah and Bani Shatair. The Bani Hadiyah further splits into the Bani Muhammad, Bani Ali, Bani Ham Mazyud, and Khanazirah. At the turn of the 20th century, the Shihuh numbered approximately 21,500 people settled around the Ruus Al Jibal mountains. The Bani Hadiyah, which includes the Bani Ham Mazyud, were mostly found on the western coast of the UAE, with Khasab, Oman serving as their principal village. As part of the Shihuh tribe, the Bani Ham Mazyud would have participated in the seasonal migration pattern between mountain agriculture during winter months and coastal work during warmer periods, including date harvesting and pearl fishing. The tribe's origins incorporate both Arab elements, thought to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves including Malik bin Fahm immigrants in the second century, as well as Iranian elements. The Bani Ham Mazyud, as part of the broader Shihuh confederation, maintained their independence and identity while being frequently involved in regional conflicts with the Al Qasimi rulers and various coastal settlements.

name: Bani Hamida
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and pastoral leadership
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestral progenitor Hamida, from whom the tribal name derives. The tribe traces its lineage through established Arabian genealogical traditions.
description: Bani Hamida is a traditional Arabian tribe with Qahtani lineage, historically engaged in nomadic pastoralism across the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin social structures and economic practices, with seasonal migrations for grazing. They formed alliances with other tribal groups including the Mahamid confederation. Today they maintain their tribal identity while adapting to modern settlement patterns in the Gulf region.

relations.context: The Mahameed alliance with Bani Hamida
Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

history: The Bani Hamida are a traditional nomadic Bedouin tribe with documented presence in Jordan since the pre-Islamic period. The tribe gained historical notoriety in 1869 when they destroyed the Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele) at Dhiban, Jordan. This significant archaeological artifact, dating to around 840 BCE, was smashed by tribal members as an act of defiance against Ottoman authorities who had demanded its surrender to Germany. The destruction occurred after they lit a fire under the stone and poured cold water over it, shattering it into fragments. Several Bani Hamida members were killed in the aftermath of this incident. During the 19th century, explorer Charles M. Doughty noted in 1876 that the Bani Hamida had a reputation for breeding 'the best blood horses in Moab.' The tribe experienced various conflicts with neighboring groups, including fighting with the Beni Sakher tribe in 1891. By the late 19th century, European travelers reported mixed interactions with the tribe - some describing harassment in the 1890s, while others noted civil treatment when traveling with Ottoman permissions. The tribe's territory was centered around the ancient Moabite region east of the Dead Sea, including areas near Madaba. In 1985, twelve women from the tribe launched a weaving project in the village of Mukawir, producing traditional textiles using ground looms assembled with stones and sticks. This project has continued to the present day, with products sold throughout Jordan and internationally, representing the tribe's adaptation to modern economic opportunities while maintaining traditional crafts.

name: Bani Hammad
legitimacyNotes: Lineage-based authority through descent from Hammad bin Sabiq
ancestorStory: Descended from Hammad bin Sabiq, a prominent tribal leader who established the lineage in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe traces its origins to the Qahtani Arab genealogical tradition.
description: Bani Hammad is a tribal branch with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in areas of modern-day Saudi Arabia and the Gulf region. They are traditionally pastoral nomads who have maintained their tribal identity through genealogical connections to their ancestor Hammad bin Sabiq. The tribe has various sub-branches scattered across the GCC region, with some members settling in urban areas while others maintain traditional lifestyles. They remain active participants in contemporary Gulf tribal networks.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani genealogical tradition

history: The Bani Hammad emerged as a significant tribal branch during the 11th-century period of Arab tribal migrations to North Africa, though their direct relationship to the more famous Hammadid dynasty requires careful consideration. The research indicates extensive Arab tribal movements during this era, particularly involving Qahtani lineages, which aligns with the existing genealogical data placing Bani Hammad within the Qahtani tradition through their ancestor Hammad bin Sabiq. The 11th century witnessed massive Arab migrations from the Arabian Peninsula to the Maghreb, with historians estimating 500,000 to 1 million Arab nomads participating in these movements. While the Banu Hilal and Banu Sulaym were the most prominent tribes in these migrations, numerous other Arab tribal groups also participated in this westward expansion. The Hammadid dynasty, established by Hammad ibn Buluggin in 1007-1008 CE in present-day Algeria, created a powerful emirate that controlled much of the central Maghreb and briefly extended influence from Tlemcen to Tunis. Though the tribal Bani Hammad's specific role in these events is not detailed in the available sources, their Qahtani lineage and pre-Islamic origins suggest they were part of the broader Arab tribal networks that shaped the medieval Islamic world. The tribe's traditional pastoral nomadic lifestyle and presence across the Gulf region indicates they maintained their Arabian Peninsula connections while some branches may have participated in the broader Arab diaspora. Contemporary Bani Hammad members remain active in Gulf tribal networks, with some settling in urban areas while others maintain traditional lifestyles, reflecting the broader pattern of Bedouin adaptation to modern circumstances described in the research.

name: Bani Hizzan
history: Bani Hizzan is a clan of the Anizah tribe, one of the major Arabian tribes belonging to the Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites. According to early historians, the Anizah tribe had two main branches in pre-Islamic times: one nomadic in the northern Arabian steppes, and another sedentary living in the wadis of Al-Yamama district in eastern Najd. Bani Hizzan was part of this sedentary branch, and families tracing their origin to Anizah through Hizzan still exist in that area today, located just south of their cousins the Bani Hanifa who inhabited the modern-day Riyadh region. The Anizah tribe's genealogical position places them within the same family as other prominent Rabi'a tribes including Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib, with their eponymous ancestor being a great uncle to all these tribal groups. As part of the broader Anizah confederation, Bani Hizzan shares in the tribe's ancient heritage that predates Islam and their historical alliance with Bakr ibn Wa'il known as al-Lahazim.

In the GCC context, some branches are present in Saudi Arabia where they maintain their tribal identity and customs.

Without direct research evidence about the Arabian Peninsula Bani Hussein's specific history, migrations, or political activities beyond their claimed Hashemite descent from Hussein ibn Ali, a detailed historical narrative cannot be constructed from the provided sources.

name: Bani Kaab
traditionalEconomy: Mixed pastoral nomadic and settled agriculture
description: The Bani Kaab is an Arab tribe in Oman and the United Arab Emirates, also evident in other Gulf countries. The tribe is associated with the area around and to the north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and to areas of the Emirates to the East of Buraimi, including the Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, most sections had settled with a population of some 7,250 of whom 1,150 were Bedouin.

relations.context: Despite their alliance under the Bani Yas, the Bani Kaab were rivals to the Na'im and had pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain. In the 1940s they came together when oil concessions loomed.
The Bani Kaab were rivals to the Bani Qitab during the 19th century

history:
The Bani Kaab (Arabic: بني كعب, singular Al Kaabi الكعبي) is an Arab tribe with origins tracing to the Najd region, specifically descended from the ancient Banu Amir confederation through the lineage of Kaab bin Rabia bin Aamer bin Sa'sa'ah. The tribe comprises numerous subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat. By the turn of the 20th century, most sections had settled with a total population of approximately 7,250, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin. Only the Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections maintained nomadic lifestyles while other sections had adopted settled life in areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and territories in the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor.

Historically, the Bani Kaab engaged in the complex tribal politics of southeastern Arabia, forming shifting alliances based on political and economic interests. By 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation aimed at expelling Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. Despite this alliance, they maintained rivalry with the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes, successfully pushing sections of the Na'im northward to the Jiri plain. A century later, demonstrating the fluid nature of tribal allegiances, the Bani Kaab had aligned with Saudi Arabia by the 1940s, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.'

Interestingly, traditional rivals Na'im and Bani Kaab temporarily united in the 1940s when oil concessions became a possibility, opposing oil companies, the Trucial Sheikhs, the Sultan of Muscat, and British authorities alike. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion led by Shaikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and actively fought against British forces, specifically the Trucial Oman Levies. In the final military action at Buraimi in October 1955, a Bedouin force of some 200 men from the Al Bu Shamis and Bani Kaab under Sheikh Obaid bin Juma put up spirited resistance to the British-backed Trucial Oman Levies, demonstrating their continued martial capabilities and loyalty to their Saudi allies.

The broader Banu Ka'b confederation, of which the Gulf Bani Kaab represents one branch, had migrated from the Najd region in multiple waves since the 6th century AH due to Islamic conquests, drought, and tribal conflicts. Different branches settled across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond - some went to Iraq (eventually converting to Shia Islam), others to northern Morocco, and still others to the mountains and coastal areas of Oman and the UAE where they maintained Sunni Islamic practices. The tribe has produced notable modern figures including Noura bint Mohammed Al Kaabi (UAE Minister of State), Saad Sherida al-Kaabi (Qatar's Minister of Energy), and various professional athletes and officials across the Gulf states.

name: Bani Malik
legitimacyNotes: Lineage-based legitimacy through established genealogical connections
ancestorStory: Descended from Malik, with various branches tracing lineage through different genealogical paths. Multiple distinct Bani Malik groups exist across the Arabian Peninsula with separate origins.
description: Bani Malik refers to multiple tribal groups across the Arabian Peninsula sharing the name but often having distinct origins and territories. Various Bani Malik branches are found in different regions including parts of Saudi Arabia and other Gulf areas. The name is common in Arabian tribal genealogy, with different groups tracing descent through various lineages. These groups remain active in their respective regions with traditional pastoral and settled lifestyles.
history:
Bani Malik represents multiple tribal confederations across the Arabian Peninsula, most prominently tracing descent from Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakh'ei, a renowned military commander who fought alongside Ali ibn Abi Talib during the early Islamic period. The historical Malik al-Ashtar belonged to the Nakh'a tribe, itself an offshoot of the larger Madhhij confederation from South Arabia. According to genealogical sources, these groups claim Qahtani origins through a complex lineage connecting them to the ancient Azd tribal confederation, specifically through the Khuza'a branch that settled in Makkah and fought alongside the Prophet Muhammad against the Banu Bakr.

The Al-Ali branch emerged as one of the most significant offshoots of Bani Malik, establishing themselves across Iraq, the Arabian Gulf, and beyond by the 16th century. In Iraq, their territories extended from al-Diwaniyah in the north to al-Rumaitha in the south, with settlements concentrated along the Euphrates River system including the al-Rafi, al-Yusufyia, and al-Lawah rivers. When these waterways dried up around 1900, many Al-Ali families relocated to areas around the al-Hindyiah branch of the Euphrates River. The tribe was organized into multiple sub-branches including Al-Faraj (the largest), Al-Ismail, Al-Awabid, Al-Humaidat, Al-Ibrahim, and Bani Rzaij.

The tribe's political influence reached its zenith through figures like Muhammad Hasan Abi al-Mahasin, a prominent poet and politician who led revolutionary activities against British occupation during and after World War I. He served as president of the Revolutionary Council in 1920 and later as Minister of Education under King Faisal I in 1923. His grandson, Nouri al-Maliki, would later serve as Iraq's Prime Minister from 2006-2014. The Kashif Al-Ghita'a clerical dynasty also emerged from Al-Ali lineages, with Shaikh Ja'afar earning the title 'Kashif Al-Ghita'a' meaning 'revealer of religion and knowledge.'

Historically, Bani Malik lived in various regions including Ahwaz, Qatif, Bahrain, and Iraq, with different branches establishing themselves in Basrah, Nasiriyah, and along the eastern bank of the Euphrates River. By the late 16th century, most Al-Ali tribe members had migrated from what is now central Saudi Arabia to neighboring countries including UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Iraq, and Jordan. Contemporary Bani Malik groups remain active across their traditional territories in Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Bahrain, Qatar, Kuwait, and the UAE, maintaining both pastoral and settled lifestyles while preserving their genealogical connections to the original Malik al-Ashtar lineage.

description: The Bani Qitab is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates and Oman, consisting of a settled southern section and a nomadic northern section. At the turn of the 19th century, the tribe comprised some 2,100 nomadic Bedouin (600 fighting men) and 2,700 settled people. They were highly influential in the political affairs of the interior Trucial States, with their territory stretching from south of Buraimi oasis to the eastern foothills of Hajar Mountains. The Northern branch settled in Dhaid and Al Falayah, while the Southern Bani Qitab (500 households) settled around Aflaj Bani Qitab in the Dhahirah area.

history: The Bani Qitab is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates and Oman, historically organized into a settled southern section and a nomadic northern section. At the turn of the 19th century, the tribe comprised approximately 2,100 nomadic Bedouin (including 600 fighting men) and 2,700 settled people. Their territory stretched from south of the Buraimi oasis to the eastern foothills of the Hajar Mountains, extending to the Jiri plain north of Sharjah and the fertile area around Sharjah's inland oasis town of Dhaid. The Northern branch primarily settled in the inland towns of Dhaid and Al Falayah, while the Southern Bani Qitab (consisting of 500 households) established themselves around Aflaj Bani Qitab in the Dhahirah area. Over time, these southern settlements became separated from the northern section, and in the 20th century, Dhahirah was recognized as a governorate of Oman. The Bani Qitab wielded significant political influence in the interior Trucial States during the early 20th century. In 1905, they constructed a fort at Wadi Hatta and engaged in harassing caravans passing through to the Omani Batina coast, creating conflict with the neighboring Na'im tribe. They maintained strong loyalty to Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla of Umm Al Qawain. In 1922, under the leadership of Sheikh Muhammed bin Ali Al Huwaidan, 100 Bani Qitab men played a crucial role in securing the succession of Sheikh Abdulla bin Rashid Al Mualla II following his father's sudden death. The 1920s witnessed extensive tribal warfare, with the Bani Qitab actively participating in conflicts around Buraimi and conducting raids as far as Al Falayah. They fought alongside various allied tribes including the Awamir, Duru, Manasir, and Bani Yas. The tribe supported Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi when he was deposed as Ruler of Sharjah in 1924 and later played a key role in his accession as Ruler of Kalba in 1937. Even into the 1940s, the Bani Qitab maintained a formidable military presence with 200-250 armed, mounted Bedouin warriors and were courted by various rulers over oil company access rights, demonstrating their continued political and strategic importance in the region's affairs.

description: Bani Rasheed is a prominent Arabian tribe with branches across the northern Arabian Peninsula, including presence in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait. Historically they were known as skilled pastoralists and warriors, controlling important grazing territories and trade routes. The tribe has maintained its traditional structure while adapting to modern Gulf state citizenship. They continue to be an active tribal community with strong genealogical traditions and social cohesion.

history:
Bani Rasheed (Rashaida) are descendants of Rasheed Al-Zaul Al-Absi, a renowned 7th-century warrior from the ancient Banu Abs tribe. The Banu Abs were a powerful Bedouin confederation that formed a branch of the Ghatafan tribal alliance, inhabiting the outskirts of Medina in an area now called Hurrat Bani Rasheed. Their genealogical line traces through Abs ibn Baghid ibn Rayth ibn Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them Northern Adnanite Arabs descended from Ishmael through Adnan.

Historically, the Banu Abs were known for their independence and bravery, being called one of the Jamarat (most powerful tribes) of the Arabs who took allegiance to no one but themselves. The tribe was famous for the 40-year war of al-Dahhas wal Ghabra against their brother tribe Banu Dhubyan, and their traditions recall 3,000 of them repelling a Sasanian attack of 20,000. During the early Islamic period, some Absis like Antarah ibn Shaddad became legendary warrior-poets, while others served as companions to Prophet Muhammad and later held important positions in the Umayyad and Abbasid administrations.

The Banu Abs gained significant influence during the Umayyad period through the marriage of Caliph Abd al-Malik to the Absi noblewoman Wallada, who mothered future caliphs al-Walid I and Sulayman. This connection brought the tribe substantial estates in Syria, particularly around Manbij and Damascus, where they established the town of Hiyar Bani Qa'qa. The tribe remained politically active through the Abbasid period, with members serving as governors and military commanders. As late as the 10th and 11th centuries, the Banu Abs continued to occupy their Syrian territories while maintaining tribal structure and customs despite becoming largely sedentarized.

In 1846, as the First Saudi State consolidated power in Arabia, many Rashaida fled across the Red Sea to settle in the coastal plains from Port Sudan to Massawa, Eritrea. They maintained their traditional Bedouin culture, dress, customs, and Sunni Islamic practices while becoming primarily nomadic pastoralists specializing in camel breeding. The Rashaida populations eventually spread to Kuwait, Sudan, Eritrea, Jordan, Yemen, UAE, Oman, and other regions. A 2022 genetic study confirmed their Arabian origins, showing Sudanese Rashaida Arabs have 95.1% Middle Eastern ancestry, higher than populations from Yemen or Lebanon, confirming their lack of genetic mixing with neighboring African groups. Today, significant populations can be found across the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in Saudi Arabia where they maintain strong genealogical traditions and social cohesion, as well as in their diaspora communities that preserve clan-based traditions including tribal leadership structures and family honor codes.

name: Bani Saad

ancestorStory: Descended from Saad ibn Bakr of the Hawazin confederation, traditionally associated with the Najd region. The tribe claims ancient Arabian lineage through the Hawazin tribal group.
description: Bani Saad is a tribal confederation with roots in the Najd region of central Arabia, traditionally part of the larger Hawazin tribal group. They were historically nomadic pastoralists who migrated across the Arabian Peninsula following seasonal grazing patterns. During the 18th and 19th centuries, various branches of Bani Saad established settlements in different parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including areas that are now part of modern GCC states. Today, members of the tribe can be found across the Gulf region, with some branches having settled and integrated into the modern state systems while maintaining their tribal identity.

history: Based on the research provided, there is limited specific information about the Bani Saad tribal confederation mentioned in the existing data. The research confirms the existence of Khan Bani Saad, a majority Shia Arab city in Diyala Governorate, Iraq, which suggests some connection to a Bani Saad tribal presence in that region. However, the research does not provide detailed historical information about the specific Bani Saad confederation referenced in the existing data. The research does mention the broader context of Arabian tribal genealogy, noting the division between Qahtani and Adnani lineages, with most genealogists classifying Arab peoples into these two main categories based on their origins in Yemen (Qahtani) or Hijaz (Adnani). Within the Harb tribe structure documented in the research, there is a mention of 'Alsawaid (Al-Saadi)' among the Masrooh branch, which may represent a different Saad-related group but cannot be definitively connected to the Bani Saad confederation without additional evidence. The research indicates that tribal affiliations and movements were complex throughout Arabian history, with various groups sharing similar names but having distinct origins and territories. Without more specific documentation about the particular Bani Saad confederation in question, the historical narrative must remain limited to what can be directly supported by the available research materials.

name: Bani Sakher
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on lineage and martial prowess

description: Bani Sakher is one of the largest and most influential Bedouin tribal confederations in Jordan and northern Saudi Arabia. Historically based in the area between Damascus and the Hijaz, they controlled important trade routes and were known as skilled warriors and camel herders. The confederation consists of multiple sub-tribes and has played a significant role in Jordanian politics and society. Today, many members maintain traditional pastoral practices while others have settled in urban areas across Jordan and the northern regions of Saudi Arabia.

Competition for influence in central Jordan

history: The Bani Sakher confederation traces its origins to Sakher bin Tha'labah bin Amro bin Alghouth bin Tayy, who likely lived in the 3rd century CE, making the tribe approximately 18 centuries old. Descended from the ancient Qahtanite Tayy tribe, they originally inhabited the Ha'il Region where they lived on the 'Mountain of Lions.' The confederation consists of three main groups: Al-Twaqa (headed by the Al-Fayez family), Al-Ka'abneh (headed by the Khreisha family), and Al-Khdeir. Beginning in the 16th century, the Bani Sakher migrated from the Hejaz to the Levant, gradually expanding their influence until they became the most powerful tribe in northwestern Arabia by the mid-18th century. During their northward expansion, they conquered territory from Tayma to Al-'Ula, coming into contact with the Bani Harb (resulting in tribal mixing), and engaged in numerous conflicts with the Anazzah and Al-Dhafeer tribes. They defeated Al-Dhafeer twice, including a decisive battle at Wadi Azraq where they killed Sheikh Sultan Ibn Suwit. The tribe initially moved to Al-Balqa but faced resistance from the Al-Adwan, prompting a temporary relocation to Palestine before returning and forming their first alliance with Al-Adwan against the Sardiyya tribe. The most infamous event in Bani Sakher history occurred in 1757 when Sheikh Qa'dan Al-Fayez led the devastating Hajj caravan raid that killed an estimated 20,000 pilgrims, including the Sultan's sister, after Ottoman authorities ceased payments for caravan protection. Under Fendi Al-Fayez in the 19th century, they controlled large parts of Jordan and Palestine, with Kitchener reporting in 1877 that Sheikh Fendi could muster 4,500 fighting men and held a monopoly in the camel trade to pilgrims. Their power was eventually broken by Ottoman forces in 1867, ending their practice of collecting protection money from settlements. The tribe played a crucial role in Jordan's founding when Mithqal Al-Fayez welcomed Emir Abdullah in 1921 and helped establish Amman as the capital. During the 1923 Adwan rebellion, the Bani Sakher supported the Hashemite monarchy, cementing their position as stalwart allies of the ruling family and maintaining significant political influence in modern Jordan.

subTribes.description: The Habus is an Arab tribe primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. They are an agrarian mountain people who cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The tribe is known for speaking the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic with Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. Fiercely independent, they successfully resisted taxation attempts and maintained semi-autonomous status well into the 1960s. Their name is derived from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain.

history:
The Bani Shatair (Arabic: بني شطير) are one of two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, alongside the Bani Hadiyah, inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountain range in the UAE and northern Oman. As a tribal name, they originate in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE and areas of Northern Oman, forming part of the broader Shihuh tribal confederation that inhabits the Musandam Peninsula region.

The Bani Shatair are internally divided into subsections including the Kumzarah and Habus, reflecting the complex sub-tribal structure characteristic of Arabian Peninsula tribes. They practiced a traditional seasonal migration pattern, moving between different economic activities based on the agricultural and maritime calendar. During the pearling season, they would take work in Ras Al Khaimah and Rams along the coast, while during winter months they engaged in farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains.

The village of Sha'am, traditionally a dependency of Ras Al Khaimah, was originally settled entirely by the Bani Shatair, serving as one of their primary settlements. However, their relationship with the Rulers of Ras Al Khaimah was frequently contentious and fractious. This tension led to Sha'am seceding from Ras Al Khaimah several times throughout its history, reflecting the independent character of the Bani Shatair and their resistance to external control.

Historically, the Bani Shatair also inhabited the As Salamah Archipelago (known by the colonial name Quoin Islands) in the Persian Gulf, north of the Musandam Peninsula. These three uninhabited islands - Mumar Island (Great Quoin), Fanaku Island (Gap Island), and Didamar Island (Little Quoin) - were named using the Shihuh dialect. The Shihuh tribe, including the Bani Shatair, named the outer islands Mumar and Didamar, meaning 'mother' and 'daughter' respectively, and called the entire archipelago 'As Salamah wa Banatiha' meaning 'Salamah and her daughters.' The political resolution came in 1921 when Sha'am finally became part of the recognized Trucial State of Ras Al Khaimah, ending the cycle of secessions.
