legitimacyNotes: This is a genuine tribal confederation that formed through political alliance and shared settlement patterns, with the Al Bu Falah clan holding the most power. description: The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin in the United Arab Emirates, consisting of around 20 tribes from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. As the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, they formed a powerful confederation called the Bani Yas Coalition. The Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government.

subTribes.name: Mazrui
subTribes.description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

The Al Qubaisi family shares close relations with the leading royal family of the United Arab Emirates through Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi, mother of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founder of the UAE. As a subset of the Bani Yas tribe, the Al Qubaisi family has been influential in various aspects of Emirati society, including politics, business, and community affairs. They have played roles in the development and progress of the UAE, contributing to various sectors and institutions within the country.

subTribes.description: The Albu Salih is an Arab tribe from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation. During the 18th century, they transformed from a traditional tribe into a powerful tribal confederation (warfare alliance) in opposition to Ottoman Empire influence. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh, they became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The tribe historically consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes and semi-nomadic Bedouins who migrated seasonally between their territory in southern Iraq and the Arabian Gulf coast. They were known to be well-armed and engaged in trade during their migrations. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently.

history:
The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin that descended, along with the Awamir, from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, establishing their lineage within the Banu Amir confederation of the Adnani branch of Arab tribes. Around 1700, the House of Al Bu Falah, which held the most power in the confederation, settled with the Bani Yas clans in the Liwa oasis in present-day Abu Dhabi. The tribe later expanded to Al Ain and Buraimi when the Al Bu Falah clan acquired property and land in the Buraimi Oasis. In 1793, the Al Bu Falah migrated to the island of Abu Dhabi due to the discovery of fresh water, and by 1795 they had established the town of Abu Dhabi, which became the seat of the emirate with the construction of Qasr al-Hosn.

According to J.C. Lorimer's Gazetteer (1908-1915), the Bani Yas constituted the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, with an estimated population of around 12,000 in the early 20th century, including approximately 2,000 nomadic members. Of the roughly 10,000 settled Bani Yas, about half resided in the Liwa Oasis, 2,800 lived in Abu Dhabi town, around 2,000 in Dubai, and the remainder were distributed among the Buraimi Oasis, offshore islands, and smaller coastal and inland settlements. The confederation consisted of more than 20 sections, with contemporary sources identifying groups such as Al Falasi/Al Bu Falasah (from which the Al Maktoum ruling family descends), Al Falahi/Al Bu Falah (from which the Al Nahyan ruling family descends), Al Qemzi, Al Hemairi, Al Mehairbi, Al Remeithi, Al Mashghouni, Al Hameli, Al Mehairi, Al Suwaidi, Al Qubaisi, and Al Mazrouei.

In 1822, the House of Al Maktoum took over Dubai and established the Emirate of Dubai. The tribe maintained close relations with the Dhawahir tribe but was traditionally at odds with the Na'im and Bani Ka'ab in the Buraimi Oasis. In 1835, members of the Bani Yas settled Khor Al Adaid in southeastern Qatar, expanding their territorial influence beyond the UAE. The confederation's political relationships shifted over time, as associated tribes sometimes sought full integration into the Bani Yas under the authority of the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, while changing regional power dynamics on other occasions encouraged assertions of autonomy.

By the early 1950s, the Bani Yas population had declined to approximately 8,000 due to emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment in oil and construction sectors. The 1968 Abu Dhabi census recorded 5,884 members, with significant return migration occurring after Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's accession and the emirate's oil-driven development. Today, the Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government, with the ruler of Abu Dhabi customarily elected as president and the ruler of Dubai as vice president and prime minister.

history: The Banu Abdul Qays (Abd al-Qays) is an ancient Arabian tribe belonging to the Rabi'ah branch of the North Arabian Adnanite tribes, descended from Abd al-Qays ibn Afsa ibn Dubayah. Originally resident in the al-Arid region of South Arabia, they migrated northwestward to areas extending from Sudayr to al-Kharj. The tribe was predominantly Christian before the advent of Islam, reflecting the broader Christianization of eastern Arabia under Sasanian influence. By the 5th century, the Abd al-Qays had shifted to nomadism, dwelling outside the Tuwaiq escarpment in southern Najd. In the 6th century, they undertook a major migration northeastward to the oases of al-Ahsa, Tarout Island, and Qatif in eastern Arabia, establishing themselves as one of the principal tribes of the region including the islands of Bahrain. Their frequent raids into Iran during pre-Islamic times prompted Sasanian king Shapur II (309-379 CE) to lead a punitive expedition against Arabian tribes, during which he massacred most of the Abd al-Qays and relocated survivors to the Iranian province of Kirman. During the Islamic conquests, considerable numbers of Abd al-Qays tribesmen participated in the conquest of Fars, settling near Tavvaz in the Iranian coastal mountains and Basra in lower Mesopotamia. In the early 8th century, 4,000 Abd al-Qays warriors formed part of Qutayba ibn Muslim's army in his Khorasan campaign, demonstrating their continued military significance. The tribe played a crucial role in the political history of eastern Arabia, with two Shi'a members of the Abd al-Qays tribe, Abul-Bahlul al-'Awwam and Abu'l-Walid Muslim, leading a revolt around 1058 that precipitated the collapse of Qarmatian power. This led to the ascendancy of the Uyunids (1077-1253), an Arab dynasty also belonging to the Abdul Qays tribe, who ruled eastern Arabia for 163 years with military assistance from the Great Seljuq Empire. The Jarwanid dynasty of the 14th century is disputed as either belonging to the Banu Uqayl or the Banu Abdul Qays lineage. The majority of the Baharna people of modern Bahrain are descendants of the Banu Abd al-Qays, with minorities from related tribes of Bakr ibn Wa'il and Taghlib. Today, the majority of the tribe resides in the Gulf Cooperation Council countries including Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, Bahrain, Qatar, Oman, and the UAE.

nameEtymology: The name means 'servant of the [god] Qays', indicating their pre-Islamic origins

name: Banu Adi
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic origins and Qurayshi lineage
ancestorStory: Descended from Adi ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy, a branch of the Quraysh tribe. The tribe produced notable early Islamic figures including the second Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab.
description: Banu Adi was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabian tribe, part of the Quraysh confederation based in Mecca. Most famously, they produced Umar ibn al-Khattab, the second Rashidun Caliph, giving them significant prestige in early Islamic history. The tribe was involved in Meccan trade networks and later played important roles in Islamic expansion. While historically significant in the Arabian Peninsula's tribal structure, they are not among the major tribal confederations currently active in the modern GCC states.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Adnan through different branches

history: Banu Adi was a prominent clan of the Quraysh tribe, descended from Adi ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghaleb ibn Fihr. Based in pre-Islamic Mecca, they were part of the al-Ahlaf ('the Confederates') faction alongside the clans of Abd al-Dar, Banu Sahm, Banu Makhzum and Banu Jumah, in rivalry to the al-Mutayyabun faction that included Banu Hashim. The tribe's most significant historical contribution was producing Umar ibn al-Khattab, the second Rashidun Caliph (634-644 CE), whose leadership transformed the early Islamic state. Other notable members included Zayd ibn al-Khattab (Umar's brother who died in 632), Sa'id bin Zayd (a companion of Muhammad who died in 671), and the pre-Islamic monotheist Zayd ibn Amr (died 605). The clan also produced Hafsa bint Umar, who became a wife of Prophet Muhammad, and Al-Shifa' bint Abdullah, noted as the only literate female sahaba. During the early Islamic conflicts, Banu Adi was with the Meccan escort that preceded the Battle of Badr but notably did not join the main Quraysh force in the battle itself. As part of the broader Quraysh confederation, they eventually converted to Islam when Muhammad conquered Mecca in 630 CE and became integrated into the early Islamic elite. The tribe's prominence was primarily tied to their Meccan origins and their role in early Islamic history rather than later tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula.

nameEtymology: Named after their ancestor Adi ibn Ka'b

name: Banu Al Du'al
legitimacyNotes: genealogical descent from Zahrani lineage
ancestorStory: Du'al was a descendant within the Zahrani tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the broader Zahrani tribal structure in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Al Du'al is a tribal branch that emerged from the larger Zahrani confederation in southwestern Arabia. They were historically part of the complex tribal network that dominated the mountainous regions between Hijaz and Asir. The tribe maintained traditional pastoral practices and participated in the broader Zahrani political structure. Their current status appears to be largely historical, with members likely integrated into modern Zahrani communities.

relations.context: Al-Jadara formed direct alliance with Banu Al Du'al in Mecca

history:
Banu Al Du'al represents a complex tribal entity with dual genealogical affiliations in Arabian Peninsula history. According to the research, they are identified as one of the four clans of Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat (alongside Banu Damrah, Banu Laith, and Banu Mudlij), which places them within the Adnanite lineage through Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat ibn Kinana ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. However, they are also described as a tribal branch within the Zahrani confederation, which traces to the Qahtani lineage through Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd. This dual affiliation reflects the complex nature of Arabian tribal genealogies and suggests either multiple tribal branches bearing the same name or intricate historical alliances and integrations.

Historically, Banu Al Du'al established a significant presence in Mecca where they formed crucial strategic alliances. Their most notable partnership was with Al-Jadara, a Zahranid family known as 'the Wall-Masons' whose progenitor, Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama, was reportedly the first to reconstruct the walls of the Kaaba after Abraham and Ishmael. This alliance extended to include the Quraysh tribe, creating a powerful coalition in pre-Islamic Mecca. The pinnacle of these inter-tribal relationships was achieved through a pivotal marital alliance when Kilab ibn Murrah of Quraysh married Fatimah bint Sa'd Al-Jadari, producing two sons: Zuhrah and Qusay ibn Kilab.

The significance of this marital alliance cannot be overstated in Arabian history. Qusay ibn Kilab became a direct ancestor of Prophet Muhammad and established lineages that would later produce the third and fourth Rashidun caliphs (Uthman and Ali), as well as the founding dynasties of the Umayyad, Abbasid, and Fatimid caliphates. This connection places Banu Al Du'al, through their alliance network, at the heart of Islamic political and religious history. As part of the Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat confederation, they were maternal cousins to the Quraysh, though this relationship included periods of both cooperation and rivalry, including conflicts such as the battle of Dhat Naqif.

Religiously, Banu Al Du'al originally practiced South Arabian polytheism like other Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat clans. They converted to Islam following the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE, when the majority of the Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat tribal confederation embraced the new faith. Their current status appears to be largely historical, with descendants likely integrated into modern Zahrani communities and other related tribal groups throughout the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Hijaz region, though some descendants may also be found in Jordan and Egypt as indicated for other Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat clans.

timelineEvents.title: Conquest of Mecca and Conversion to Islam
timelineEvents.description: Following Muhammad's conquest of Mecca, the majority of Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat clans, including Banu Al Du'al, converted to Islam

name: Banu Al-Harith
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani lineage tracing to Harith ibn Ka'b
ancestorStory: Named after Al-Harith ibn Ka'b, a prominent figure in pre-Islamic Arabian tribal genealogy. The tribe claims descent from the ancient Qahtani lineage of southern Arabia.
description: Banu Al-Harith is a large tribal confederation with Qahtani origins, historically spread across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula including areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia and Yemen. The tribe played significant roles during the pre-Islamic period and early Islamic conquests. Various branches of Banu Al-Harith have settled in different regions of the Gulf, with some maintaining their traditional pastoral lifestyle while others have integrated into modern Gulf societies. The confederation remains active today with descendants found throughout the GCC states.

relations.context: Battle in Bisha in 17th century, later reconciled

history: Banu Al-Harith traces its origins to Al-Harith ibn Ka'b, establishing the tribe within the Qahtani lineage of southern Arabia. The tribe is historically significant as one of the Arabian confederations that controlled multiple cities including Najran, Taif, and regions of what is now southern Saudi Arabia, with their capital at Al-Khubah near Najran. During pre-Islamic times, a significant portion of Banu Al-Harith converted to Judaism, working primarily in goldsmithing and arms repair, and wearing the traditional jambiya. They formed strategic alliances with Banu Madh'hij to successfully conquer Najran, establishing themselves as the dominant power ruling the city for centuries alongside Banu Hamdan. This period of dominance ended during the Christian invasion of Najran. In 523 CE, the Himyarite king Dhu Nuwas, who had converted to Judaism, massacred Christians in Najran, an event that involved the Jewish Banu Al-Harith community. Following these upheavals, sub-clans of the tribe dispersed - one group emigrated to the Dhank region of Oman, while another moved south and founded the district of Bani Al Harith in Sanaa. After the rise of Islam, Banu Al-Harith was included in Point 31 of the Constitution of Medina, being honored as allies to the Muslims while retaining their Jewish religion and receiving the same rights as Banu Awf. The small remnants of the Jewish Banu Al-Harith continued living semi-autonomously in Najran until the 1930s when the Saudi-Yemeni War resulted in Saudi conquest of the city in 1934. Under persecution from governor Amir Turki bin Mahdi, who gave them one day to convert to Islam or evacuate, the remaining Jewish Banu Al-Harith fled south to Sanaa and Aden. Their descendants became part of the small Yemenite Jewish population, most of whom eventually migrated to Israel. The research also reveals connections to other Banu Al-Harith branches through marriage alliances, such as Maymuna bint al-Harith's marriage to Prophet Muhammad in 627, and the tribe's inclusion in various Meccan political factions during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods.

name: Banu Amir
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah, claiming noble Hawazin lineage
ancestorStory: Descended from Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah of the Hawazin confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the noble Mudarite branches of northern Arabia.
description: Banu Amir was a major tribal confederation of the Hawazin group in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, primarily based in Najd and the central Arabian Peninsula. They were known for their fierce independence and military prowess, participating in various conflicts including resistance against early Islamic expansion. The confederation included numerous sub-tribes and clans that later dispersed throughout the Arabian Peninsula. While historically significant in Arabian tribal genealogy, their direct political influence in the modern GCC states is limited, though various branches may have integrated into other tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace Adnani origins through central Arabian tribal networks

history:
The Banu Amir was a major Arab tribal confederation that emerged in the historical record around 544-547 CE, originating from western Arabia between Najd and Hejaz in the Khurmah and Ranyah regions. As a powerful branch of the Hawazin confederation, they were descended from Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah and traced their lineage through the Adnani (northern Arabian) genealogical line. The confederation encompassed several major branches including Banu Kilab (the leading branch in pre-Islamic times), Banu Numayr, Banu Ka'b (which included the powerful Banu Uqayl), and notably the Banu Hilal who later became famous for their 11th-century migration to North Africa. The tribe controlled the horse-breeding pastures of Dariyya in western Najd and engaged in significant conflicts including the Fijar War against the Quraysh of Mecca.

During the Islamic period, the Banu Amir experienced complex relations with the Prophet Muhammad, including the tragic Bir Ma'una incident in 625 CE where Muslim missionaries were killed despite tribal protection guarantees. After initially resisting, the tribe eventually converted to Islam under leaders like Alqama ibn Ulatha around 630 CE. The confederation subsequently dispersed across the Islamic world - the Banu Kilab established the Mirdasid dynasty in Aleppo (1025-1080), the Banu Uqayl founded the Uqaylid dynasty in Mosul and later the Usfurid and Jabrid dynasties in eastern Arabia, while the Banu Numayr migrated to Iraq. In the Gulf region, two important branches emerged: the Bani Yas confederation in the UAE (descended from Yas bin Amer bin Sa'sa'ah) which includes the ruling Al Nahyan and Al Maktoum families, and the Bani Amer Bedouin tribe spanning the UAE and Oman. Modern tribal groups including Subay', Suhool in Najd, and sections of Bani Khalid also trace their lineage to the original Banu Amir confederation, though their direct political influence in contemporary GCC states varies significantly.

name: Banu Assad
legitimacyNotes: ancient Arabian tribal lineage with pre-Islamic nobility
ancestorStory: Descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrika, one of the major tribal ancestors of central Arabia. The tribe was known for their nobility and warrior traditions in pre-Islamic times.
description: Banu Assad was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah, part of the Adnani lineage. They were traditionally based in central Arabia, particularly in the Najd region, and were known for their warrior culture and tribal nobility. The tribe played a notable role in early Islamic history and produced several prominent figures including poets and warriors. Their descendants scattered across various regions of Arabia, with some branches integrating into other tribal confederations over time.

relations.context: both trace Adnani origins through central Arabian ancestry
both had historical presence in central Arabian territories

history:
Banu Assad (Arabic: بَنُو أَسَدْ) is an Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzayma ibn Mudrika ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them Adnanite Arabs and cousins of the Prophet Muhammad through their common ancestor Khuzayma ibn Mudrikah. Originally based in central Arabia's Najd region, particularly around the Aja and Salma mountains (later known as Jabal Tayy), the Banu Assad were displaced in the 2nd century CE when the Tayy tribe migrated from Yemen and took over these territories. Despite losing some territory, the two tribes ultimately became allies and intermarried.

In the 6th century, Banu Assad revolted against the Kingdom of Kindah, killing King Hujr (father of the famous poet Imru' al-Qais), which led to a prolonged war that ultimately resulted in the end of the Kindah kingdom. The tribe also formed a confederation with the Tayy and later the Ghaṭafān, though this alliance collapsed when Asad and the Ghaṭafān assaulted both branches of the Tayy and drove them from their territories in Jabal Tayy. However, one Asad leader, Dhu al-Khimarayn Awf al-Jadhami, defected and reestablished the alliance with the Tayy, together restoring their territories.

The tribe produced several prominent figures in early Islamic history, including the famous female warrior Khawla bint al-Azwar, daughter of Azwar al Asadi, one of the chiefs of Banu Assad. She fought alongside her brother Dhiraar in the Muslim conquest of the Levant, participating in decisive battles including Yarmouk in 636 against the Byzantine Empire. The tribe migrated to Iraq in the 7th century, settling near Kufa and along the Euphrates river, where they played a crucial role in early Islamic conflicts.

Banu Assad sided with Ali ibn Abi Talib in the Battle of the Camel and later supported Husayn ibn Ali at the Battle of Karbala in 680 CE, where many tribal members died as martyrs including commanders Habib ibn Muzahir and Muslim ibn Awsaja al-Asadi. After the massacre, Banu Assad members had the honor of burying Husayn and his companions with the help of Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin. In 998, Ali ben Mazyad established the virtually independent Mazyadid emirate in the Kufa area, which lasted for a century and a half and founded the city of Hillah in 1101. The tribe is highly respected by Shia Muslims for their role at Karbala, and today members live primarily in Iraqi cities including Basra, Najaf, Kufa, Karbala, and Baghdad, with branches also found in Sudan (Banu Kahil), Iran's Khuzestan province, and historically in various other regions.

migrationPath.description: Displacement from Jabal Aja and Salma mountains by migrating Tayy tribe, followed by gradual migration to Iraq

name: Banu Aws
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani tribal lineage with historical significance in Medina during early Islamic period
ancestorStory: Aws ibn Haritha was descended from the Azd tribe and migrated to Yathrib (Medina). The tribe became one of the two main Arab tribes of Medina alongside Banu Khazraj.
description: Banu Aws was one of the two major Arab tribes of Medina (ancient Yathrib) during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. They were involved in prolonged conflicts with their neighbors Banu Khazraj before both tribes converted to Islam and became the Ansar (helpers) of Prophet Muhammad. The tribe played a crucial role in early Islamic history, providing support to the Muslim community in Medina. While historically significant in the Hijaz region rather than the Gulf proper, some branches may have migrated to various parts of the Arabian Peninsula over time.

relations.context: Muhammad negotiated with them and Banu Khazraj to mediate conflict during Hijrah
Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage through Azd tribal confederation

history:
The Banu Aws was one of the two major Arab tribes of Medina (ancient Yathrib) during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, alongside their rivals the Banu Khazraj. Both tribes descended from Thaʻlaba bin ʻAmr, son of King Muzayqiya who ruled parts of Yemen in the 2nd century and led the Azd tribe. The Banu Aws trace their lineage to the Qahtani (South Arabian) tribal confederation, having migrated from Yemen as part of the pre-Islamic exodus following damage to the Great Marib Dam around the 2nd century CE according to Abu Muhammad Al-Hasan Ibn Ahmad Al-Hamdani.

Around 300 CE, Thaʻlaba bin ʻAmr separated from his tribe and settled in Yathrib, which was then controlled by three major Jewish tribes: Banu Nadir, Banu Qurayza, and Banu Qaynuqa. Initially, both the Banu Aws and Banu Khazraj (collectively known as Banu Qayla) were subordinate to these Jewish clans. However, around the 5th century, they gained independence when Mālik bin Ajlān of Khazraj asserted control, allowing both Arab tribes to obtain shares of palm groves and strongholds in the oasis.

The Banu Aws engaged in prolonged conflicts with their cousin tribe Banu Khazraj for over a century, with tensions escalating into open warfare. During these conflicts, the Jewish tribes of Medina aligned with different sides - the Banu Aws allied with the Jewish tribes Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza, while Banu Khazraj allied with Banu Qaynuqa. The final and most decisive battle was the Battle of Bu'ath around 617 CE, where the Banu Aws achieved victory over the Banu Khazraj despite initial setbacks.

After Muhammad's arrival in Medina in 622 CE following the Hijrah, both the Banu Aws and Banu Khazraj converted to Islam and became known as the Ansar (helpers). They were included in the Constitution of Medina as allies to the Muslims, being described as 'one nation/community with the Believers.' The tribe played crucial roles in early Islamic history, including the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf (carried out by Banu Aws members) and participation in various military expeditions under Muhammad's leadership. Notable Aws leaders included Sa'ad ibn Mua'dh, who later served as arbitrator to decide the fate of Banu Qurayza after their siege in 627 CE, and Bashir ibn Sa'ad, both of whom held positions of significant influence in the early Muslim community.

migrationPath.description: Thaʻlaba bin ʻAmr, grandfather of al-Aws, separated from the Azd tribe and settled in Yathrib under Jewish control

folkLegends.story: According to the chronicler Al-Hamdani, the Banu Aws and Banu Khazraj were among the tribes pressured out of South Arabia due to the destruction of the Great Marib Dam, leading to their migration northward to settle in Yathrib around the 2nd century CE.

nameEtymology: The name derives from their ancestor Aws ibn Haritha, who was descended from the Azd tribe of Yemen

name: Banu Bakr
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Adnani lineage with established genealogical legitimacy
ancestorStory: Descended from Bakr ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afsa ibn Da'mi ibn Jadila ibn Asad ibn Rabi'a ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. The tribe became one of the major Rabi'a confederations in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Banu Bakr was a major Adnani tribal confederation descended from Bakr ibn Wa'il, prominent in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. They were primarily based in eastern Arabia and Mesopotamia, with various sub-tribes spreading across the region. The confederation played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal warfare, including conflicts with Banu Taghlib and involvement in the famous Basus War. Most of their branches have been absorbed into other tribal formations or evolved into distinct tribal identities over the centuries.

relations.context: Banu Bakr opposed Tayy rule in al-Hira under Iyas ibn Qabisah and began raiding Sasanian territory
Counted by classical Arab genealogists as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr
Joined with Bakr ibn Wa'il under an alliance called al-Lahazim in pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods

history:
Banu Bakr ibn Wa'il was a major Adnani tribal confederation descended from Bakr ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit through the lineage of Adnan. Originally based in Najd and eastern Arabia, particularly around Hafar al-Batin which served as their permanent campground, they controlled vital trade routes between Hejaz and Mesopotamia. The confederation encompassed numerous powerful sub-tribes including Banu Hanifa (sedentary farmers in al-Yamama who founded Hadjr, predecessor to Riyadh), Banu Shayban (nomadic warriors who defeated the Sassanid Empire at the Battle of Dhi Qar before Islam), Banu Qays ibn Tha'labah, Banu Yashkur, and Banu 'Ijl, each maintaining distinct characteristics and territories. The tribe was instrumental in the lengthy Basus War against their Rabi'a cousins Banu Taghlib, demonstrating their military prowess in pre-Islamic Arabian warfare.

During Muhammad's era, various branches had complex relationships with the emerging Islamic state. Most notably, Banu Hanifa initially converted to Islam but then apostatized under the leadership of their self-proclaimed prophet Musaylima, leading to their defeat in the Ridda Wars around 632 CE at the battle of Aqraba. After Islamic conquest, many Bakr sections migrated northward to Upper Mesopotamia, giving their name to the Diyar Bakr region and later Diyarbakır in Turkey. They participated extensively in Muslim conquests of Iraq and Persia, with some reaching as far as the Caucasus and Anatolian Valley.

The 1257 Mongol destruction of Baghdad marked a turning point, scattering many branches southward. Most retreated to the Hauran region in southern Syria where they established new settlements and remained until modern times. Over the centuries, the confederation gradually fragmented - Banu Hanifa was eventually absorbed into the larger Anizah confederation according to Saudi royal genealogists, while other branches evolved into distinct tribal identities or merged with local populations across the Middle East. Despite this fragmentation, many families across Arabia, Iraq, Syria, and Turkey continue to trace their lineage back to the various branches of Banu Bakr ibn Wa'il.

migrationPath.description: Pre-Islamic migration of Bakr sections northward after victory at Battle of Dhi Qar against Sassanids
Participation in Islamic conquests under Caliph Umar, establishing settlements in garrison towns like Kufa
Retreat southward during Mongol invasion of Baghdad, resettling in Hauran where descendants remain

folkLegends.title: Bakr the Patriarch
folkLegends.story: According to tribal tradition, Bakr ibn Wa'il was the oldest son of Wa'il from his Bakry wife. They descended from an Arab clan that named their first-born sons Bakr in reference to their ancestor Bakr the Patriarch. From a young age, Wa'il and his brothers trained their sons to be desert warriors. Due to his fine characteristics and mastery, Wa'il put his son Bakr in charge of the clan. Bakr was described as a skilled horseman strong enough to travel long distances. As he matured, he formed a fighting force with family members and other Arabs into a nomadic federation, starting a war clan alongside Anizah and Banu Hanifa tribes.

nameEtymology: The name derives from Bakr ibn Wa'il, the eponymous ancestor. According to tribal tradition, 'Bakr' was a hereditary name given to first-born sons in reference to an earlier patriarch called 'Bakr the Patriarch'

name: Banu Fahm
legitimacyNotes: ancient Arab tribal lineage with documented pre-Islamic ancestry
ancestorStory: Fahm ibn Ghanm was an ancient Arab tribal ancestor from whom the Banu Fahm trace their lineage. The tribe has roots in the Arabian Peninsula dating to pre-Islamic times.
description: Banu Fahm is an ancient Arab tribe with pre-Islamic origins in the Arabian Peninsula, tracing their lineage to Fahm ibn Ghanm. They were primarily nomadic pastoralists who moved throughout various regions of Arabia in early Islamic history. The tribe is mentioned in classical Arabic genealogical sources but has limited direct connection to modern GCC tribal structures. Their historical presence was more significant in the early Islamic conquests and tribal movements rather than in the formation of modern Gulf states.
history: The research reveals significant confusion regarding Banu Fahm's identity and connections. While the existing data describes Banu Fahm as descendants of Fahm ibn Ghanm with Qahtani (southern Arabian) lineage, the Wikipedia sources present a different picture entirely. According to the Zahran tribe article, Banu Fahm appears as one of three divisions of Banu Daws, which is itself a branch of the Zahran tribe. The Zahran are described as an Azdite offshoot tracing their lineage to 'Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd.' This would place Banu Fahm within the Azd confederation, contradicting the Qahtani classification in the existing data. The Qays article further complicates matters by listing 'Banu Fahm' among the sub-tribes of the Amr branch of Qays Aylan, stating 'The two main sub-tribes of the Amr branch were the Banu Adwan and Banu Fahm, both founded by sons of Amr.' This would make them Adnanite (northern Arabian) rather than Qahtani, and specifically part of the Mudar confederation. Finally, the Amr ibn al-As article mentions that Alexandria was garrisoned by 'soldiers from the Azd and Banu Fahm tribes' during the early Islamic conquests, suggesting Banu Fahm had a military presence in Egypt by the 640s CE. The research indicates there may be multiple tribal groups using the name 'Banu Fahm,' or significant confusion in the genealogical traditions. Without additional sources, it is impossible to determine which genealogical line the 'Banu Fahm' in the existing data actually represents.

timelineEvents.title: Military Service in Egypt
timelineEvents.description: Banu Fahm soldiers served in the Arab garrison of Alexandria during the Islamic conquest of Egypt under Amr ibn al-As

name: Banu Ghassan
legitimacyNotes: Descendants of Azd, established powerful Lakhmid client kingdom under Byzantine Empire
ancestorStory: Ghassan ibn Tha'laba led his people from Yemen northward after the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam. They established themselves in the Hauran region of southern Syria and became vassals of the Byzantine Empire.
description: Banu Ghassan was a powerful Arab Christian confederation that established the Ghassanid Kingdom in southern Syria and Jordan from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. Descended from the Azd tribes of Yemen, they migrated north following the Ma'rib Dam collapse and became Byzantine foederati, serving as a buffer against Persian-allied Arab tribes. They played a crucial role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and were rivals of the Lakhmid kingdom. The tribe was eventually absorbed into the broader Islamic conquests and lost political autonomy after the 7th century.

relations.context: Mudar rose to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century, after supplanting its predecessor, the Banu Ghassan
Both descend from Qahtanite lineage through Azd

history:
Banu Ghassan was a powerful Arab Christian confederation that emerged as one of the most significant pre-Islamic Arabian political entities from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. According to Arab genealogical tradition developed during the early Islamic period, they descended from the Azd tribe of South Arabia through their ancestor Jafna, son of Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir ibn Haritha ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn Azd, though historian Brian Ulrich notes these genealogical links are historically tenuous as these groups appear separately in non-genealogical sources. The tribe's migration northward from South Arabia followed the traditional 'Scattering of Azd' story, with Christian J. Robin's epigraphic studies suggesting they were the main tribal confederation in the Hejaz with a possible base at Medina between the 3rd-4th centuries CE before migrating to Syria.

The Ghassanids established themselves as foederati (allied troops) of the Byzantine Empire in the Levant, serving as a crucial buffer against Persian-allied Arab tribes and Bedouin raids. Their capital was located at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, and they controlled much of the eastern Levant through tribal alliances extending south to Yathrib (Medina). Under rulers like al-Harith ibn Jabalah (529-569 CE), who received the highest imperial title ever bestowed on a foreign ruler by Emperor Justinian I, the Ghassanids reached their zenith of power, exercising collective authority over all Arab allies of Byzantium. They were staunch supporters of Miaphysite Christianity, which eventually led to conflicts with Orthodox Byzantium and their temporary dissolution by Emperor Maurice (582-585 CE).

The confederation was restored when the Byzantines realized they needed Ghassanid military support against the Persians, and they participated in the final Byzantine-Sasanian War of 602-628. Their political independence ended with the Muslim conquests when the last phylarch, Jabala ibn al-Ayham, led his forces alongside the Byzantine army at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636 CE, where they were defeated by the Rashidun forces. After initially embracing Islam, Jabala ultimately withdrew with his followers to Byzantine Anatolia in 639 CE. Significant remnants remained in Syria, particularly around Damascus and the Ghouta countryside, where many gradually converted to Islam under Umayyad rule and became influential in Islamic administration as governors, military commanders, scholars, and court officials.

During the Umayyad period, the Ghassan played a crucial role in Syrian politics, supporting continued Umayyad rule to secure their interests under the dynasty. They participated in nominating Marwan I as caliph during the tribal summit at their old capital of Jabiyah, and Yazid ibn Abi al-Nims led a revolt in Damascus securing the city for Marwan during the Battle of Marj Rahit. The Ghassan thereafter formed part of the Yaman faction opposing the Qays tribes, with their leader Shabib ibn Abi Malik conspiring to assassinate the pro-Qaysi Caliph al-Walid II. Two prominent Damascene Ghassanid scholarly families achieved particular renown: that of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani (d. 750s), known as the sayyid ahl Dimashq (leader of the people of Damascus), and Abu Mushir al-Ghassani (d. 833), a prominent hadith scholar. By the 9th century, the geographer al-Ya'qubi recorded the tribe living settled lives in the Ghouta gardens region of Damascus and in Gharandal in Transjordan, marking their transition from a powerful confederation to integrated components of the broader Islamic world.

migrationPath.description: Migration from South Arabia following Ma'rib Dam collapse, establishing presence in Hejaz
Northern migration to establish kingdom in southern Syria and Jordan
Final withdrawal of remaining Ghassanid forces under Jabala ibn al-Ayham to Byzantine territory

folkLegends.title: The Scattering of Azd
folkLegends.story: According to the traditional 'Scattering of Azd' story, the Azd tribe migrated northward from South Arabia and different groups split off in different directions, with the Ghassan being one such group that eventually settled within the Roman limes. This migration was traditionally linked to the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in Yemen.

folkLegends.title: Ghassan's Genealogical Origins
folkLegends.story: Arab genealogical tradition traces the Ghassanids as descendants of Jafna, son of Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir ibn Haritha ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn Azd, linking them through Jafna's brother Tha'laba to the Ansar tribes (Aws and Khazraj) of Medina.

nameEtymology: The name appears in ancient sources as variations including Kassanitai (Ptolemy), Casani (Pliny the Elder), Gasandoi (Diodorus Siculus), and Kasandreis (Photios I), suggesting the tribal name predates their northern migration and was recognized in classical geographical sources.

name: Banu Hani
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Tayy tribal lineage through Hani ibn Masoud
ancestorStory: Hani ibn Masoud was a descendant of the great Tayy tribal confederation. The Banu Hani emerged as one of the established branches of Tayy in northern Arabia.
description: Banu Hani was a branch of the larger Tayy tribal confederation, tracing their lineage to Hani ibn Masoud. They were historically based in northern Arabia and participated in the complex tribal politics of the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. As part of the Tayy confederation, they were known for their mobility and pastoral lifestyle across the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's influence waned over time as other tribal groups consolidated power in various regions of the Gulf and Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Both branches of the greater Tayy tribal confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches with common ancestry
Related through Tayy confederation lineage

history: Based on the provided research, there is a critical distinction between Banu Hani (descendants of Hani ibn Masoud of the Tayy confederation) and the unrelated Banu Taym (a Qurayshi clan from Mecca). The research reveals that Banu Hani emerged as a branch of the Al al-Ghawth division of Tayy, descended from Hani, son of 'Amr ibn al-Ghawth. According to Arab genealogists, the Banu Hani became a major sub-branch that established themselves in southern Mesopotamia, distinct from their kinsmen who remained in northern Arabia. The Tayy confederation, including Banu Hani, originally resided in Yemen but migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE following the dispersion from Yemen. They settled primarily in the mountain ranges of Aja and Salma (later known as Jabal Tayy, then Jabal Shammar) with Khaybar as their most important oasis. The tribe maintained traditional pastoral lifestyles and participated in the complex tribal politics of pre-Islamic Arabia. During the Islamic period, the Tayy confederation, including its branches like Banu Hani, converted to Islam around 629-630 CE when chieftains Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr pledged allegiance to Muhammad. They subsequently participated in the Ridda Wars, supporting the Muslim side, and played roles in various Islamic conquests. However, the specific activities and later history of the Banu Hani branch in southern Mesopotamia are not detailed in the available research, leaving their post-Islamic political influence and territorial control unclear.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Yemen to northern Arabian mountain ranges following the dispersion of Banu Azd
Banu Hani branch established themselves as major sub-branch in southern Mesopotamia

timelineEvents.title: Tayy Migration to Northern Arabia
timelineEvents.description: Banu Tayy confederation migrates from Yemen to Jabal Aja and Jabal Salma

timelineEvents.title: Conversion to Islam
timelineEvents.description: Tayy chieftains Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr convert to Islam and pledge allegiance to Muhammad

name: Banu Hanifa
legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

subTribes.name: Anizzah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Well-documented blood lineage within the established Rabiʿa-Adnanite genealogical framework, with historical continuity from pre-Islamic times through modern era.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Anizah's eponymous ancestor is described as a great uncle of all these related tribes, indicating they come from the same family and most likely one patriarch.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed economy of pastoral nomadism and tribute collection. Nomadic branches practiced seasonal migration between northern Arabian steppes and Syria, while levying crops from oasis inhabitants and collecting khuwwa (brotherhood tribute) from Syrian farmers in exchange for protection.
subTribes.description: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe with presence across the Arabian Peninsula, Upper Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, and they are classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

relations.context: Counted by classical Arab genealogists as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr
Part of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes
According to King Salman Al Saud, Banu Hanifa assimilated into Anizah through alliances and intermarriage
Described as purported cousins within the Rabiʿa branch. Al Saud family traces ancestry to Bani Hanifa and intermarried with Anizah.

history: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe belonging to the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, with lineage tracing to Adnani origins. The tribe inhabited al-Yamama in central Arabia, primarily living as sedentary farmers in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd, particularly the valley of Al-'Irdh (later known as Wadi Hanifa). Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, though they led an independent existence prior to Islam. According to legend, they moved to al-Yamama from the Hejaz after the original inhabitants, the extinct peoples of Tasm and Jadis, were decimated by war. Sources credit them with founding the towns of Hadjr (predecessor of modern Riyadh) and Manfuha, and managing the granaries of Al-Kharj. During Muhammad's era in 627 AD, the tribe came into conflict with early Muslims when Muhammad ibn Maslamah captured their chief Thumamah bin Uthal Al-Hanafi, who later converted to Islam. Around 632 AD, Banu Hanifa sent a delegation to Muhammad pledging allegiance, but after his death they apostatized under their self-proclaimed prophet Musaylima, triggering the Ridda Wars. The Muslims under Caliph Abu Bakr defeated them only on the third attempt at the battle of 'Aqraba, killing Musaylima and forcing the tribe to rejoin the Islamic state. During the Umayyad and Abbasid eras, members were initially banned from Muslim conquests by Abu Bakr but later joined forces in Iraq under Umar, with some settling in garrison towns like al-Kufa. The tribe also supplied rebellious movements like the Kharijites, with Najdah ibn 'Amir founding a short-lived Kharijite state. In the mid-9th century, harsh rule by the Alid Banul Ukhaidhir dynasty led many to migrate to Basra and Upper Egypt's Wadi al-Allaqi near Aswan, where they worked in gold mining and their leader became 'sultan of al-Allaqi.' Some formed the Banu Kanz dynasty (867-15th century) through intermarriage with Beja princesses, ruling the Egyptian-Nubian frontier. By the 13th-14th centuries, they still held presence in al-Yamama's capital Hadjr, with Ibn Battuta noting most inhabitants belonged to Banu Hanifa. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe through alliances and intermarriage.

migrationPath.description: Legendary migration from Hejaz to al-Yamama after Tasm and Jadis were decimated by war
Migration to Basra due to harsh Ukhaidhirite rule in mid-9th century
Migration with families to Upper Egypt's Wadi al-Allaqi near Aswan for gold mining

name: Banu Hanzala
legitimacyNotes: Lineage from Hanzala ibn Malik, part of Tamim confederation
ancestorName: Hanzala ibn Malik ibn Zayd Manat ibn Tamim
ancestorStory: Descended from Hanzala, son of Malik, within the greater Tamim tribal confederation. Known for their presence in Najd and eastern Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Hanzala was a significant branch of the Tamim tribal confederation, historically based in Najd and parts of eastern Arabia. They were known as one of the prominent sub-tribes of Tamim during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, participating in the tribal politics and raids of central Arabia. The tribe played a role in early Islamic conquests and tribal alliances in the Arabian Peninsula. Their influence diminished over time as larger tribal confederations and later state structures emerged in the region.

relations.context: Both tribes with historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history:
Banu Hanzala was a major branch of the Tamim tribal confederation, descended from Hanzala ibn Malik ibn Zayd Manat ibn Tamim ibn Murr ibn Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. As an Adnanite tribe, they traced their ultimate ancestry through Adnan to Ishmael, son of Abraham, placing them among the northern Arabian tribal groups distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of southern Arabia. According to genealogical tradition, Hanzala had eight sons: Malik, Yarbu', Rabi'a, 'Amr, Murra, Ghalib, Kalfa and Qays, from whom descended the important sub-clans of Banu Darim, Banu Yarbu', Banu Tahiuh, and Barajim.

Historically, Banu Hanzala inhabited al-Yamama in central Arabia and maintained a strong presence throughout Najd and the eastern Arabian Peninsula. During the pre-Islamic period, they actively participated in the Ayyam al-Arab (Days of the Arabs) tribal conflicts, most notably achieving a significant victory at the battle of Yawm Dhi Najab against Banu Amir and the Kindite king Akil al-Murar, who was killed in the engagement while many Banu Amir leaders were captured. As part of the greater Banu Tamim confederation, they came into contact with the Prophet Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam, reflecting the cautious approach many tribal groups took toward the new religious movement.

Following the Islamic conquests, Banu Hanzala maintained their position within the Tamim tribal structure as the broader confederation spread across the Arabian Peninsula and into newly conquered territories. Historical records indicate that during the Sasanian period, several clans of Hanzala were transplanted by Shapur II to Tavvaz, near present-day Dālakī in Fars province of Iran, demonstrating their early involvement in trans-regional migrations. The tribe's influence was subsequently embedded within the larger Tamim political and tribal networks rather than operating as an independent political entity, with their descendants spreading throughout the Islamic world as part of the Tamim diaspora.

Notable descendants of Banu Hanzala include prominent religious scholars and ruling families that emerged from the broader Tamim confederation. These include the House of Al Thani, the ruling family of Qatar since 1847, the Al ash-Sheikh family who have served as Grand Muftis of Saudi Arabia, and influential religious figures such as Ishaq ibn Rahwayh and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the founder of the Nejdi Salafi movement. The tribe's genealogical significance is also reflected in the presence of Tamim tribal elements, including Hanzala branches, among Iranian Arabs in Khuzestan and Fars provinces, where they settled alongside other Arab tribes such as Banu Ka'b and Banu Hammed, maintaining their Arab identity and tribal affiliations over centuries.

migrationPath.description: Transplantation of Hanzala clans by Sasanian ruler Shapur II to Tavvaz near present-day Dālakī in Fars province
Spread across Arabian Peninsula and Islamic conquests as part of Tamim confederation
Part of great influx of Arab-speaking immigrants into Khuzestan from 16th to 19th century

name: Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Fihr ibn Malik, part of the Quraysh tribal structure
ancestorStory: Al-Harith was the son of Fihr ibn Malik, making this branch part of the broader Quraysh confederation. They were among the early Arab tribes established in the Hijaz region.
description: Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr was a tribal branch within the Quraysh confederation, tracing their lineage to al-Harith, son of Fihr ibn Malik. They were historically based in the Hijaz region and played a role in pre-Islamic Arabian tribal politics as part of the broader Quraysh structure. Like other Quraysh branches, they were involved in trade and the management of Meccan affairs. Their historical significance is primarily tied to the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, with their identity later absorbed into broader tribal and regional formations.

relations.context: Both trace Adnani lineage through ancient Arabian tribal structures

history: Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr was a tribal branch within the Quraysh confederation, tracing their lineage to al-Harith, son of Fihr ibn Malik (fl. c. 230-240 CE). According to the Islamic genealogical tradition documented in the research, Fihr ibn Malik is positioned eleven generations before Prophet Muhammad in the Adnani lineage, making this branch part of the broader Quraysh tribal structure that descended from Kinana. The tribe was based in the Hijaz region around Mecca and participated in the complex tribal politics of pre-Islamic Arabia. The genealogical records show that Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr maintained significant political and social integration within the broader Quraysh confederation through strategic marriage alliances. A notable example is the marriage of Al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib, one of Muhammad's uncles, to Ghaziyya bint Qays from Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr, which produced five children including Nawfal, Rabi'ah, Abu Sufyan (Mughirah), Abd Allah, and Arwa. The detailed lineage shows that Ghaziyya was the daughter of Qays ibn Tareef ibn Abd al-Uzza ibn Amir ibn Umayra ibn Wadi'a bin al-Harith ibn Fihr, establishing a clear genealogical connection back to the tribal eponym. Like other Quraysh branches, they were involved in trade and the management of Meccan affairs during the pre-Islamic period. Their historical significance is primarily tied to their role as marriage partners and political allies within the Quraysh confederation, with their tribal identity eventually absorbed into broader formations during the early Islamic period as the traditional tribal structure gave way to the expanding Islamic state.

timelineEvents.title: Fihr ibn Malik's Era
timelineEvents.description: Fihr ibn Malik, ancestor of Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr, active in Mecca, defended the city against Himyarites who intended to relocate the Kaaba to Yemen

timelineEvents.title: Marriage Alliance with Banu Hashim
timelineEvents.description: Al-Harith ibn Abd al-Muttalib married Ghaziyya bint Qays from Banu al-Harith ibn Fihr, strengthening ties between the tribal branches

name: Banu Hashim
legitimacyNotes: Religious authority as descendants of Prophet Muhammad through his great-grandfather Hashim
ancestorStory: Hashim ibn Abd Manaf was a prominent merchant and leader of Quraysh who established trade relationships and fed pilgrims to Mecca. The clan gained ultimate prominence as the lineage of Prophet Muhammad through Hashim's great-grandson.
description: Banu Hashim is the noble clan of Quraysh tribe from which Prophet Muhammad descended, tracing lineage to Hashim ibn Abd Manaf. Historically based in Mecca, they held religious authority over the Kaaba and established the foundation of Islamic political legitimacy. Various Hashemite branches ruled Islamic territories including the Abbasid Caliphate, and modern Hashemite dynasties continue in Jordan and formerly in Iraq and Hejaz. In the Gulf region, their religious prestige makes them respected figures, though they are not a major tribal force compared to Bedouin confederations.

relations.context: Historical rivalry over control of Hejaz and Islamic holy sites

history:
Banu Hashim is the noble clan of the Quraysh tribe from which the Islamic prophet Muhammad descended, tracing their lineage through the Adnani branch to Ishmael. The clan derives its name from Hashim ibn Abd Manaf (c. 464-497 CE), originally named Amr, who was the great-grandfather of Muhammad and a prominent merchant leader of Mecca. Hashim earned his name, meaning 'pulverizer' in Arabic, according to Islamic hagiographical accounts either from providing crumbled bread in broth for pilgrims to the Kaaba during times of famine, or from his role in 'saving the starving' during seasonal famines in Mecca. He revolutionized Arabian trade by establishing the two great caravan journeys of the Quraysh: the Winter Caravan to Yemen and the Summer Caravan to northwest Arabia, Palestine, and Syria, securing trade privileges from the Ghassanid king of Syria and even from the Byzantine Emperor.

The clan's rise to ultimate prominence came through Hashim's great-grandson Muhammad, born around 570 CE, whose prophetic mission established Islam and created the foundation of Islamic political legitimacy. During the early Islamic period, Banu Hashim played a complex role in Meccan politics, initially opposing other Qurayshi clans but later becoming central to Islamic leadership. The clan was divided in the succession disputes following Muhammad's death in 632 CE, with different branches supporting various claimants to the caliphate. The most significant split occurred between supporters of Ali ibn Abi Talib (Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law) and other Qurayshi factions, leading to the First Fitna (civil war) from 656-661 CE.

From the 8th century onward, Hashemite descent became the basis for dynastic legitimization across the Islamic world, with a widely accepted hadith stating that the caliph must be from the Quraysh tribe. Major Hashemite dynasties emerged from different branches of the family: the Abbasids (750-1258 CE in Baghdad, 1261-1517 CE in Cairo) descended from Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib, Muhammad's uncle; the Fatimids (909-1171 CE) who claimed descent through Ali and Fatima; the Idrisids who established the first Islamic state in Morocco (788-990 CE) through Idris ibn Abdallah, a descendant of Ali; and the Alaouite dynasty that has ruled Morocco since 1631. In the Arabian Peninsula, various Hashemite branches maintained religious authority over Mecca and Medina as Sharifs, with their descendants carrying the traditional titles of Sharif and Sayyid.

The modern Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan, established in 1921, continues this legacy of political leadership based on prophetic lineage. Throughout Islamic history, the clan has comprised multiple branches including descendants of Abbas (the Abbasids), Ali through his sons Hassan and Hussein (Alawites and Ashraf), and other lines from Abd al-Muttalib's sons. Today, Hashemites are distributed globally with particular concentrations in Jordan, Morocco, Iraq, and the Hejaz, maintaining their religious prestige as descendants of the Prophet's family, though in the Gulf region they are respected more for their religious authority than as a major tribal confederation compared to the dominant Bedouin tribes.

folkLegends.title: The Conjoined Twins Birth Legend
folkLegends.story: Islamic hagiographers narrate that Hashim and his twin brother Abd Shams were born as conjoined twins, with Hashim's leg attached to his brother's head. Legend says they struggled in their mother's womb to be firstborn, and Hashim was born with one toe pressed into his younger brother's forehead. Their father Abd Manaf ibn Qusai separated the conjoined sons with a sword, and priests believed that the blood flowing between them signified future wars between their progeny - a prophecy fulfilled when the Banu Abbas (branch of Banu Hashim) confronted the Banu Umayya (branch of Banu Abd Shams) in 750 CE. Arabian astrologers predicted that Abd Manaf had committed a grave error in separating them by sword.

folkLegends.title: The Alliance of the Scented Ones
folkLegends.story: When Hashim contested inheritance rights with his brother Abd ad-Dar's clan, he and his brothers gathered at the Kaaba with their allies, dipped their hands in a bowl of rich perfume with nutmeg powder, and swore they would never abandon one another, rubbing their scented hands over the stone of the Kaaba to confirm their pact. Hashim and his allies became known as 'Hilf al-Mutayyabun' (Alliance of the Scented Ones), while their rivals formed the 'Hilf al-Ahlaf' (Alliance of the Confederates). A compromise was reached whereby the Scented Ones retained control of charity tax and food/drink for pilgrims, while the Confederates kept the keys to the Kaaba and running of the House of Assembly.

nameEtymology: Named after Hashim ibn Abd Manaf, originally called Amr, who chose the name Hashim (meaning 'pulverizer' in Arabic) either because he provided crumbled bread in broth for Kaaba pilgrims or because he 'saved the starving' during seasonal famines in Mecca

name: Banu Hina
legitimacyNotes: Well-documented tribal lineage with extensive genealogical records maintained by Arab genealogists. Part of the established Mudar confederation with clear descent lines.
ancestorStory: According to Arab genealogists, their progenitor was Hilal, and their full genealogy traces back through the Mudar tribal confederation: Hilāl ibn ʿĀmir ibn Ṣaʿṣaʿa ibn Muʿāwiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawāzin ibn Manṣūr ibn ʿIkrima ibn K̲h̲aṣafa ibn Qays ibn ʿAylān ibn Muḍar ibn Nizār ibn Ma'ad ibn ʿAdnān.
traditionalEconomy: nomadic pastoralism, cattle and sheep rearing, later adapted to agriculture in some regions
description: The Banu Hilal is a major Arab tribe from Najd in central Arabian Peninsula that migrated to the Maghreb in the 11th century, playing a crucial role in the Arabization of North Africa. They originated as nomadic Bedouins who ruled parts of Najd and campaigned in Iraq-Syria borderlands. The Fatimid Caliphate used them to punish the rebellious Zirids by dispatching them westward around 1050. Their migration, numbering between 150,000-300,000 people, fundamentally transformed the linguistic and cultural landscape of the Maghreb, spreading Bedouin Arabic and nomadic lifestyle across previously agricultural regions. They were organized into six main sub-tribes and became one of the most influential Arab tribal confederations in North African history.

description: Banu Hur was a tribal branch of the greater Tayy confederation, traditionally based in the northern Arabian Peninsula. As part of the Qahtani lineage, they maintained nomadic pastoral traditions and participated in the tribal dynamics of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. Like other Tayy branches, they were known for their mobility between the Najd and northern regions. Their historical significance is primarily documented in early Islamic genealogical sources, with limited direct impact on modern GCC tribal structures.

history: Banu Hur, as documented in genealogical sources, were a distinct tribal branch within the greater Tayy confederation that emerged during the pre-Islamic period in northern Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogists cited in historical sources, Banu Hur descended from Hur, who was the son of Jadilah - a woman of the Tayy tribe whose name became the eponym for the Al Jadilah branch of Tayy. This genealogical connection places Banu Hur within the Al Jadilah division, one of the two main branches of the Tayy alongside Al al-Ghawth. The Tayy tribe itself traced its lineage to Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy), whose ancestry connected to Kahlan ibn Saba, linking them to the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabian origin. During the late 6th century, the extended Fasad War between the Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadilah branches significantly impacted the tribal structure, with several Jadilah clans, potentially including elements of Banu Hur, migrating from northern Arabian plains to Syria where they established military encampments and converted to Christianity under Ghassanid influence. Like other Jadilah sub-branches, Banu Hur would have participated in the complex political dynamics of the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, though specific details of their role in the Muslim conquests or later political developments remain undocumented in the available sources. Their historical significance appears to have been primarily genealogical rather than political, serving as one of the many tribal subdivisions that comprised the vast Tayy confederation.

name: Banu Judham

description: Banu Judham was a major Qahtani tribal confederation that originated in ancient Arabia and played significant roles during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. They were part of the broader Kahlan tribal alliance and participated in the early Islamic conquests, with branches spreading across various regions of the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation was known for their pastoral nomadic lifestyle and tribal warfare prowess. While historically significant, they are primarily remembered as a historical entity rather than maintaining active tribal identity in modern GCC states.

relations.context: Qahtani Yemeni tribe in alliance with Kahlan tribes, settled together in Syria and Northern Egypt

history:
Banu Judham was a major Qahtani tribal confederation that claimed descent from Judham ibn Adi, tracing their lineage through Kahlan ibn Saba to the broader Qahtanite genealogy of southern Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogy, they belonged to the 'Amila-Judham group of Palestine within the Kahlan division of Qahtan. However, historian Werner Caskel suggested their genealogical relationships with claimed brother tribes Lakhm and Amila may have been politically constructed alliances rather than genuine blood ties. By the 5th-8th centuries CE, Judham had established themselves as a dominant confederation controlling strategic territories across the southern Levant and northwestern Arabia, from Tabuk northward through the Wadi Araba valley and Dead Sea regions, including the Balqa area around modern Amman, as well as Madyan, Ma'an, Adhruh, and extending south to Wadi al-Qura.

During the Byzantine period, Banu Judham served as foederati (tribal confederate troops) and nominally converted to Christianity, though the depth of this conversion remains disputed. Some sections maintained inclinations toward Judaism, and notably, the Jewish tribe of Banu Nadir in Medina traced their origins to Judham stock. The tribe initially resisted the rise of Islam, fighting alongside Byzantine forces at the Battle of Mu'ta in 629 and being specifically targeted during Muhammad's expedition to Tabuk in 630. Following their defeat at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636, Judham converted to Islam and became the largest tribal faction within Jund Filastin (the Palestine administrative district).

During the First Muslim Civil War, internal divisions emerged as the confederation split between two rival leaders: Natil ibn Qays, who supported Ali and insisted on maintaining their Qahtani genealogical origins, and Rawh ibn Zinba, who backed Mu'awiya and sought to claim Ma'add (Adnanite) descent to align with the Quda'a confederation. This rivalry reflected broader political and genealogical disputes of the period. Following Marwan I's victory at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684, Judham maintained close alliance with the Umayyad dynasty until its fall in 750.

In later periods, various branches of the confederation dispersed across different regions. By the 10th century, the Banu Bayadh branch had established themselves in northern Sinai, while other sections fused with the Amila tribe in Galilee and southern Lebanon by the 11th century. Mamluk-era sources connect the Banu Sakhr tribe of al-Karak to Judham lineage. The confederation's influence also extended into Egypt through clans like the al-Ayed, whose leadership intermarried with the Circassian Abaza family during the Mamluk period, demonstrating the continued political significance of Judham-descended groups well into the medieval period.

description: Banu Jumah was one of the clans of the Quraysh tribe based in pre-Islamic Mecca. They were known as skilled merchants and played a significant role in Meccan trade networks. The clan produced notable figures including Safwan ibn Umayya, a prominent merchant who initially opposed Islam but later converted. As part of the broader Quraysh confederation, they maintained influence during the early Islamic period but their distinct tribal identity became less prominent as Islamic society developed.

relations.context: Part of Adnani genealogical lineage through Quraysh

history:
Banu Jumah was one of the prominent clans of the Quraysh tribe in pre-Islamic Mecca, descended from Jumah ibn Amr ibn Husays ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghalib ibn Fihr ibn Malik. They were closely related to the Banu Sahm clan, as both descended from Husays ibn Ka'b, forming the larger Banu Husays lineage within Quraysh. The clan was known for their wealth and commercial activities, establishing themselves as skilled merchants who played a significant role in Meccan trade networks connecting the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean.

During the succession dispute following Qusayy ibn Kilab's death in the late 5th/early 6th century CE, Banu Jumah joined the al-Ahlaf ('the Confederates') faction alongside the Banu Sahm, Banu Adi, Banu Makhzum, and Banu Abd al-Dar clans, supporting Abd al-Dar against the claims of Abd Manaf. This factional division would influence Qurayshi politics for generations. The clan held significant religious responsibilities in pre-Islamic Mecca, including the Iysaar - the management of divination arrows used at the sacred idols.

Banu Jumah initially opposed Muhammad and Islam, with prominent clan leader Umayya ibn Khalaf serving as one of Islam's staunchest opponents. Umayya owned and tortured the early Muslim convert Bilal ibn Rabah, an Abyssinian slave who would later become Islam's first muezzin. Umayya was killed at the Battle of Badr in 624 CE, along with other notable clan members. His son Safwan ibn Umayya initially continued the family's opposition to Islam but eventually converted following the Muslim conquest of Mecca in 630 CE.

The clan produced several important early Muslim figures, including the Companion Uthman ibn Mad'hun, who was among the early converts to Islam and participated in the migration to Abyssinia. Other notable members included Umayr ibn Wahb, who initially plotted against Muhammad but later converted, and Said ibn Amir al-Jumahi, who commanded Muslim forces in Syria. According to Shia sources, a group from Banu Jumah participated in the Battle of the Camel in 656 CE alongside A'ishah against Ali ibn Abi Talib. Like other Qurayshi clans, their distinct tribal identity became less prominent as Islamic society developed, though they maintained their genealogical connections within the broader Qurayshi confederation.

description: Banu Jundub was a branch of the larger Tayy tribal confederation, one of the prominent Qahtani tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They were primarily nomadic pastoralists who ranged across various parts of Arabia during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. As part of the Tayy confederation, they participated in the broader tribal movements and political dynamics of ancient Arabia. The clan's historical significance is mainly through their connection to the influential Tayy tribe rather than as an independent political entity.

history: Banu Jundub represents one of the prominent branches of the Al Jadila division within the ancient Tayy tribal confederation. According to traditional Arab genealogists, the clan traces its lineage to Jundub, son of Jadilah, a woman of the Tayy whose offspring formed the Al Jadila branch alongside the Al al-Ghawth. The Tayy tribe as a whole descended from Julhumah ibn Udad (known as 'Tayy'), tracing back through Kahlan ibn Saba to Qahtan, the legendary ancestor of southern Arabian tribes. Originally based in Yemen, the Tayy confederation migrated northward in the late 2nd century CE following the dispersion of other Yemeni tribes, establishing themselves in the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges of northern Arabia, which became known collectively as Jabal Tayy. The Banu Jundub, as part of the Al Jadila branch, were among those who inhabited the plains ('as-Sahiliyyun') rather than the mountains. During the late 6th century Fasad War between the Tayy's main divisions, the Al Jadila including Banu Jundub migrated to Syria, converting to Christianity and establishing the military camp 'Hadir Tayyi' near Qinnasrin under Ghassanid protection. The clan maintained its Syrian presence through the early Islamic period, with Christian members initially fighting alongside Byzantine forces during the Muslim conquest of Syria in 638 CE. Following the Muslim victories, many converted to Islam while others remained Christian and paid the jizya tax. The Banu Jundub produced the prominent Al al-Tha'alib subbranch, which itself generated the influential Banu La'm that became a leading division of Al Jadila in northern Arabia. Through their Tayy heritage, descendants of Banu Jundub participated in the broader tribal political dynamics that shaped medieval Syria and northern Arabia, though their specific historical role diminished as the larger Tayy confederation evolved into the modern tribes of Shammar and others.

name: Banu Kanz
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Arab tribal lineages with historical presence in Upper Egypt and Nubian regions
ancestorStory: Named after Kanz al-Dawla, a prominent tribal leader who established influence in the region between Egypt and Nubia. The tribe emerged as a significant power controlling trade routes along the Nile.
description: Banu Kanz was a prominent Arab tribal group that established control over territories between Upper Egypt and Nubia during the medieval period. They were primarily based along the Nile Valley and controlled important trade routes connecting Egypt with Nubian gold mines and African commerce. The tribe played a significant role in the political dynamics of medieval Upper Egypt and maintained semi-autonomous rule for several centuries. Their influence declined with the rise of the Mamluk Sultanate and later Ottoman control.
history: Banu Kanz was a semi-nomadic Muslim dynasty that controlled the border region between Upper Egypt and Nubia from the 10th to 15th centuries. The tribe originated from the Banu Hanifa, a branch of the Adnanite Rabi'a ibn Nizar tribal confederation, who migrated from al-Yamama in Arabia to Egypt during the Abbasid era between 847-861 CE. In Egypt, these Rabi'a tribesmen intermarried with the indigenous Beja Hadariba tribe, creating a mixed Rabi'ah-Hadariba confederation that controlled the gold mines of Wadi Allaqi and trade routes between Egypt and Nubia. The tribe's name derives from their chief Abu al-Makarim Hibatallah, who was granted the title 'Kanz al-Dawla' (Treasure of the State) by Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim in 1006 CE for capturing the rebel Abu Rakwa. The Banu Kanz controlled crucial territories including Aswan, Wadi Allaqi's gold mines, and the Red Sea port of Aydhab, deriving substantial wealth from mining operations and trade regulation. In 1174, they rebelled against the Ayyubids under Ibn al-Mutawwaj to restore Fatimid rule but were defeated and forced to migrate southward into northern Nubia. There they accelerated the spread of Islam in the predominantly Christian region and eventually assumed control of the Nubian Kingdom of Makuria in 1317, with the Kanz al-Dawla overthrowing the Mamluk-installed ruler Abdallah Barshanbu. However, by the early 15th century, the Mamluks dispatched the Berber Hawwara confederation to combat the Banu Kanz, who gradually supplanted them as the dominant force in Upper Egypt and the frontier regions. The modern descendants of the Banu Kanz are known as the 'Kunuz' and primarily inhabit northern Sudan.

history: The research provided contains no information about the Banu Kathir tribe. The Wikipedia articles focus entirely on Jewish Arabian tribes of early Islamic Arabia - specifically the Banu Qurayza, Banu Nadir, and Banu Qaynuqa who lived in Medina during Muhammad's time (7th century CE). These Jewish tribes were involved in conflicts with the early Muslim community and were ultimately expelled or eliminated from Medina between 624-627 CE. One Arabic Wikipedia entry discusses the Kathiri Sultanate, which was established in 1379 in the Hadhramaut region and ruled until 1967, but this appears to be a different political entity rather than the tribal origins of Banu Kathir. The research also includes general information about Kahlan tribal confederations and mentions that 'Banu Kathir moved to Hadramut in the East of Yemen where they established their own sultanate' as part of Hamdan branches, but provides no specific details about their genealogy, migrations, or tribal structure. Without research material directly addressing the Banu Kathir tribe's origins, genealogical connections, or historical development, no verified information can be provided about their tribal history.

name: Banu Khalid
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with established chieftainship structure through the Al Humaid clan

description: The Bani Khalid were a powerful Arabian tribe that dominated eastern Arabia from the 17th to 19th centuries. They established the Bani Khalid Emirate in 1669 under Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr, controlling the Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions after expelling Ottoman forces. The tribe's chieftainship was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. Known for their military prowess, they were staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. Their power declined after repeated defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, with their rule ending definitively in 1830, though they briefly regained some authority under Ottoman patronage in 1874.
