history: Based on the research provided, there appears to be significant confusion in the existing data between two distinct entities: the historical Banu Makhzum clan of Quraysh from Mecca, and the Arabian Peninsula tribe known as Banu Khalid that established an emirate in eastern Arabia. The research focuses entirely on Banu Makhzum and Khalid ibn al-Walid, not the Banu Khalid tribe mentioned in the existing data. Banu Makhzum was one of the three most powerful clans in pre-Islamic Mecca alongside Banu Hashim and Banu Umayya, dominated by the al-Mughira family line and known for their wealth from trade routes to Yemen and Ethiopia. They initially opposed Islam under Abu Jahl's leadership but eventually converted, with Khalid ibn al-Walid becoming a prominent Muslim military commander around 627-629 CE. The Banu Makhzum's male line reportedly ended around 750 CE during the Umayyad collapse when Khalid's descendants died in a plague in Syria. The research mentions that the Arabian Peninsula's Banu Khalid tribe established a 17th-19th century emirate, but provides no verified information about their origins, genealogy, or historical development beyond what was already in the existing data. This represents a clear disconnect between the research provided (focusing on the Meccan Qurayshi clan) and the subject tribe (the eastern Arabian Banu Khalid).

description: Banu Khazraj was one of the two major Arab tribes of pre-Islamic Medina, alongside their rivals the Aws. They played a crucial role in early Islamic history as key supporters and allies of Prophet Muhammad after his migration to Medina. The tribe provided many prominent companions and leaders during the early Islamic expansion. While historically based in the Hejaz, their influence diminished after the early Islamic period as tribal identities gave way to Islamic governance structures.

relations.context: Muhammad negotiated with them and Banu Aws to mediate conflict during Hijrah

history:
The Banu Khazraj were a major South Arabian Qahtanite tribe that migrated north from Yemen to Medina (then called Yathrib) around the 2nd century CE, alongside their cousin tribe the Banu Aws. According to Abu Muhammad Al-Hasan Ibn Ahmad Al-Hamdani, this migration was part of the Pre-Islamic Exodus of Yemen caused by damage to the Great Marib Dam. The tribe settled in Medina and became one of the two dominant Arab tribes there, but developed a hostile relationship with the Aws that culminated in the Battle of Bu'ath (Battle of Yawm al-Bu'ath) shortly before Muhammad's arrival. During this conflict, the Banu Khazraj allied with the Jewish tribe Banu Qaynuqa, while their rivals the Aws fought alongside the Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza. The Khazraj were defeated in this battle.

When Muhammad migrated to Medina in 622 CE, most of the Banu Khazraj, led by chiefs like Sa'd ibn Ubadah, welcomed the Prophet and became known as the Ansar (helpers), though some leaders like Abd-Allah ibn Ubaiy opposed him and were later labeled as hypocrites (munafiqun). The tribe included several important sub-clans, notably the Banu Najjar, who were the maternal relatives of Muhammad's grandfather Abdul-Muttalib and in whose territory Muhammad initially settled and built his mosque. The Banu Sa'ida clan hosted the famous Saqifah gathering after Muhammad's death where Abu Bakr was chosen as the first caliph.

During the early Islamic period, the Banu Khazraj provided many prominent companions including Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, Jabir ibn Abd Allah, Hassan ibn Thabit, Ubayy ibn Kab, Abd Allah ibn Rawahah, Anas ibn Malik, Muadh ibn Jabal, and others. They participated in major military campaigns and played crucial roles in Islamic expansion. The tribe's influence extended beyond Arabia - the Nusaybah family of Jerusalem, Custodians of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, are descendants of Banu Khazraj who arrived in Jerusalem with the 7th-century Islamic conquest.

Centuries later, the Nasrid dynasty of Granada (1238-1492) claimed descent from the Ansar tribes of Medina, including the Khazraj. In 1228, Ibn al-Ahmar gathered the remains of the Muslim population in Granada and established al-Mamlika al-Nasria, deriving legitimacy from their claimed Ansari lineage. The Nasrids ruled as the last Muslim kingdom in Spain until their surrender to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492, when Muhammad XII (Boabdil) surrendered Granada to the Catholic Monarchs.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Yemen due to Marib Dam damage, part of Pre-Islamic Exodus alongside the Aws tribe

name: Banu Khuza'ah
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic prominence and guardianship of Mecca before Quraysh
ancestorStory: Descended from Khuzā'ah who broke away from the Azd migration from Yemen. They settled in the Hejaz and became guardians of the Kaaba before being displaced by Quraysh.
description: Banu Khuza'ah was a powerful pre-Islamic Arabian tribal confederation descended from Yemeni Qahtani stock. They controlled Mecca and served as guardians of the Kaaba for several centuries before being displaced by the Quraysh tribe. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, with some branches converting to Islam and supporting the Prophet Muhammad. While historically based in the Hejaz, their influence waned after the rise of Quraysh and the Islamic conquests, with descendants scattered throughout the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Allies of Muhammad, conflict with Quraysh client clans broke Treaty of al-Hudaybiya
Some Saudi tribal branches claim descent from Khuza'ah

history: The Banu Khuza'ah stands as one of the most historically significant tribal confederations in Arabian Peninsula history, tracing their origins to either Qahtanite or Adnanite lineages, with scholarly debate continuing between these two genealogical traditions. The majority opinion places them as descendants of Amr ibn Rabiah (known as Luhay), who belonged to the Azd tribal confederation from Yemen. According to traditional accounts, they broke away from their Azd kinsmen during the great migration following the breach of the Marib dam, settling in the Hejaz around the 5th century CE. For approximately 500 years, they ruled Mecca as custodians of the Kaaba, having expelled the previous custodians, the Jurhum tribe, who had become corrupt and committed sacrilege within the sacred precincts. Amr ibn Luhay, their most famous leader, was credited with introducing idol worship to Arabia, traveling to Syria-Palestine and returning with the idol Hubal, fundamentally altering Arabian religious practices from the monotheistic traditions of Ibrahim and Ismail to polytheism. The Khuza'ah's control over Mecca ended when Qusayy ibn Kilab of the Quraysh, through marriage to Hubay bint Halil al-Khuza'i and subsequent political maneuvering, seized control of the Sacred House in the 5th century CE after the decisive 'Day of Abtah' battle. Despite losing political control, they remained influential allies of the Quraysh until the rise of Islam. The tribe played a crucial role in early Islamic history, forming an alliance with Prophet Muhammad that proved pivotal in the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. When the Banu Bakr, allied with Quraysh, attacked the Khuza'ah at al-Watir, it violated the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, providing Muhammad with the justification to march on Mecca. Their leader Amr ibn Salim al-Khuza'i personally appealed to Muhammad for assistance, leading to the peaceful conquest of Mecca. Beyond their Arabian homeland, the Khuza'ah established the powerful Kingdom of Khaza'il in Iraq from 1534 to 1921, ruling the Middle and Lower Euphrates region and playing substantial roles in the establishment of the Abbasid Caliphate. Today, their descendants are scattered across Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Jordan, Palestine, Qatar, Bahrain, and other Middle Eastern countries, maintaining their tribal identity and genealogical connections to this ancient and influential confederation.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Yemen following the breach of Marib dam, breaking away from the Azd confederation
Establishment of the Kingdom of Khaza'il in Iraq, ruling until 1921

folkLegends.story: The Banu Khuza'ah, in collaboration with Banu Bakr ibn Abd Manat, invaded Mecca and expelled the Jurhum tribe after years of anger at the Jurhum's mistreatment of pilgrims and excessive taxation imposed on those visiting the Kaaba.

folkLegends.title: Sale of the Kaaba Keys
folkLegends.story: Qusayy ibn Kilab of the Quraysh succeeded Abu Ghabshan of the Khuza'ah in custodianship of the Kaaba after Abu Ghabshan sold him the keys in exchange for wine.

name: Banu Lahb
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage authority within Ghamid confederation structure
ancestorStory: Lahb is considered a progenitor within the broader Ghamid tribal genealogy. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient Azdi tribal migrations from Yemen.
description: Banu Lahb is a tribal branch within the larger Ghamid confederation, historically based in the southwestern regions of the Arabian Peninsula. They are part of the Qahtani lineage tracing back to the ancient Azdi migrations from Yemen. The tribe maintains traditional social structures and continues to exist as part of the modern Ghamid tribal system. Their historical territory spans areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia's southwestern provinces.

relations.context: Closest tribes in lineage, part of Azd Shanu'ah
Both trace ancestry to Qahtani lineage through Azdi branch

history:
Banu Lahb is a tribal branch within the larger Ghamid confederation, part of the ancient Azd tribal system tracing their Qahtani lineage back to pre-Islamic times. According to genealogical sources, they are among the closest tribes in lineage to Ghamid, alongside Zahran and Thamalah, all forming part of Azd Shanu'ah - the descendants of Malik ibn Nasr ibn Azd ibn Al-Ghawth ibn Nabt ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba' ibn Yashjub ibn Ya'rub ibn Qahtan. The Azd Shanu'ah tribes, including Banu Lahb's confederation, were historically noted for their distinctive physical characteristics, described as having light, muscular builds and well-proportioned frames, with Prophet Muhammad reportedly comparing Moses to the men of Shanu'ah in terms of their notable height and strength.

As part of the Ghamid tribal system, Banu Lahb's historical territory spans the southwestern regions of the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Al-Bahah region of the Hejaz, an area that was part of Arabia Felix which enjoyed more rainfall and productive fields than other parts of Arabia. The broader Ghamid confederation, of which Banu Lahb forms a part, is considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula and has maintained continuous presence in their ancestral homeland since pre-Islamic times. While members of the confederation have spread to various countries including Iraq, Jordan, Oman, Sudan, Egypt, UAE, and Yemen, the core tribal areas remain in their southwestern Arabian homeland.

The Ghamid confederation, including Banu Lahb, participated actively in early Islamic history, with two recorded delegations to Prophet Muhammad - one in Mecca and another in Madinah in the 10th year after Hijrah. Throughout the centuries, the confederation played significant roles in regional conflicts, including confronting Abraha the Abyssinian's army when he marched toward Mecca, and later participating in various battles alongside the Sharifs of Mecca against Ottoman forces in the 18th and 19th centuries. The tribe maintains traditional social structures within the modern Saudi state, continuing to exist as part of the established Ghamid confederation that has preserved its identity and territorial presence for over a millennium.

migrationPath.description: Part of the broader Azdi migration from Yemen to the southwestern Arabian highlands

folkLegends.title: The Naming of Ghamid
folkLegends.story: According to Ibn al-Kalbi, the ancestor Ghamid (Amr ibn Ka'b) was named Ghamid because he covered up a matter that occurred between him and his clan, so the tribal leader of Hadur called him Ghamid. However, Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i rejected this view, stating that the derivation comes from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' (I covered the well), meaning the water in it became abundant.

nameEtymology: The name derives from their inclusion within the Ghamid confederation. The name Ghamid itself has disputed etymology - either from covering up a tribal matter or from the phrase 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning to make a well abundant with water by covering it.

name: Banu Lahyan
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with historical kingdoms in Hijaz
ancestorStory: Banu Lahyan traces its lineage to Lahyan, traditionally considered part of the ancient Arabian tribal groups. The tribe established one of the notable pre-Islamic kingdoms in the Hijaz region.
description: Banu Lahyan was an ancient Arabian tribe that established a significant kingdom in the Hijaz region, particularly around Dedan (modern Al-Ula in Saudi Arabia). They controlled important trade routes between Yemen and the Levant during the pre-Islamic period, reaching their peak between the 5th-6th centuries CE. The tribe is known for their Lihyanite inscriptions found in northwest Arabian Peninsula. Their historical significance is primarily in early Arabian history rather than modern GCC tribal politics.
history:
Banu Lahyan was an ancient Arabian tribe that established a significant kingdom in the Hijaz region, particularly around Dedan (modern Al-Ula in Saudi Arabia). They controlled important trade routes between Yemen and the Levant during the pre-Islamic period, reaching their peak between the 5th-6th centuries CE. The tribe is known for their Lihyanite inscriptions found in northwest Arabian Peninsula, representing one of the notable pre-Islamic kingdoms in the region.

During the early Islamic period, the tribe came into direct conflict with the Muslim community in Medina. In 625 CE, during the Expedition of Abdullah Ibn Unais, Prophet Muhammad sent Abdullah ibn Unais to assassinate Khaled bin Sufyan Al-Hathali, the chief of Banu Lahyan, alleging that he was planning to attack Medina and incite the people of Nakhla and Uranah against the Muslims. The tensions escalated following the Expedition of Al Raji, where 10 Muslims were killed, an incident for which Muhammad held the Banu Lahyan responsible.

In September 627 CE (Rabi' al-awwal or Jumada Al-Awwal, 6 AH), Muhammad launched a direct military campaign against the tribe, leading 200 Muslim fighters in what became known as the Invasion of Banu Lahyan. Muhammad wanted to get justice for the killing of 10 Muslims in the Expedition of Al Raji. The Banu Lahyan were situated deep in the heart of Hijaz on the borders of Mecca, and due to deep-seated blood-revenge between the Muslims and the Arabian tribes, Muhammad seized the opportunity when the power of the allied Confederates collapsed.

The tribe, having received advance warning of the approaching Muslim force, fled to the mountain tops and remained out of Muhammad's reach. Muhammad set out with 200 Muslim fighters, making a feint of heading for Syria, then changing route towards Batn Gharran, the scene where 10 Muslims were killed in the Expedition of Al Raji. The tribe's strategic retreat demonstrated their knowledge of the local terrain and their determination to avoid subjugation to the expanding Islamic state. The campaign lasted 14 days before Muhammad's forces returned to Medina, with the tribe successfully evading the Muslim army through their tactical withdrawal to higher ground.

timelineEvents.title: Assassination of Tribal Chief
timelineEvents.description: Muhammad sends Abdullah ibn Unais to kill Khaled bin Sufyan Al-Hathali, chief of Banu Lahyan, during the Expedition of Abdullah Ibn Unais

timelineEvents.title: Invasion of Banu Lahyan
timelineEvents.description: Muhammad leads 200 fighters against Banu Lahyan to avenge the killing of 10 Muslims in the Expedition of Al Raji. The tribe flees to mountain tops and avoids confrontation

name: Banu La'm
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Tayy confederation with historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Lam ibn Tayy was a descendant of the great Tayy tribal confederation. The Banu La'm emerged as one of the notable branches of Tayy in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Banu La'm was a tribal branch of the larger Tayy confederation in pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who inhabited areas of the northern Arabian Peninsula. As part of the Tayy tribal system, they participated in the complex tribal politics and alliances of the region. Their historical significance is primarily documented in early Islamic historical sources, though they do not appear to have maintained distinct tribal identity in the modern Gulf states.

relations.context: Both branches of the larger Tayy confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches
Common Tayy ancestry and tribal affiliation

history: The Banu La'm emerged as a significant sub-branch of the Banu Tha'laba within the larger Al Jadila division of the ancient Tayy tribal confederation. According to traditional Arab genealogists, the Banu La'm were descendants of the Al al-Tha'alib (Tha'laba) subbranch, which itself descended from Jundub ibn Jadilah, making them part of the Qahtanite lineage tracing back to Kahlan ibn Saba. The tribe derived its name from Lam bin Hareta, identified as a Qahtan chief from the Hejaz region. As part of the greater Tayy confederation, the Banu La'm originally inhabited areas of northern Arabia, particularly in the regions extending from Jabal Tayy (the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges) toward central and western Najd. During the late 6th century Fasad War that split the Tayy confederation, the Al Jadila branch, including the Banu La'm's ancestors, migrated northward to Syria where they established the military encampment of Hadir Tayyi near Qinnasrin and converted to Christianity under Ghassanid influence. However, some clans remained in their traditional territories. During the Ridda Wars following Prophet Muhammad's death in 632, the Banu La'm were among the Tayy clans that initially joined the rebellion under Tulayha of Banu Asad, with their leader Thumama ibn Aws taking the tribe to Buzakha in northern Najd. Through the diplomatic efforts of Adi ibn Hatim, the prominent Tayy leader, the Banu La'm were eventually persuaded to return to Islam without engaging in direct combat against the Muslim forces. The tribe later spread across central Arabia and southern Mesopotamia, with significant settlements in western Nejd between Medina and al-Yamama. By the later Islamic period, the Banu Lam had become one of the major bedouin tribes controlling territories alongside the Shammar in Jabal Aja and Jabal Salma. The tribe eventually split into three main nomadic divisions: the Fudhool, Al Kathir, and Al Mughira, while many clans settled as townspeople in Nejd. During the Ottoman period, members of Bani Lam frequently engaged in conflicts with both Safavid and Ottoman authorities, and during World War I, they fought alongside the Ottomans under Sheikh Gazban's leadership.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Al Jadila clans including Banu La'm ancestors to Syria during the Fasad War, establishing Hadir Tayyi military encampment
Banu La'm under Thumama ibn Aws joined Tulayha's rebellion during the Ridda Wars
Return to traditional territories after reconciliation with Muslim forces through Adi ibn Hatim's diplomacy

name: Banu Makhzum
legitimacyNotes: Prestigious Quraysh clan with strong pre-Islamic merchant tradition and early Islamic military leadership
ancestorStory: Makhzum ibn Yaqaza was a descendant of Fihr ibn Malik of Quraysh. The clan became known for their wealth, military prowess, and opposition to early Islam before eventually converting.
description: Banu Makhzum was one of the most prominent clans of the Quraysh tribe in pre-Islamic Mecca, known for their wealth from trade and their military leadership. They initially opposed the Prophet Muhammad and early Islam, with notable figures like Abu Jahl, but later converted and produced distinguished military commanders like Khalid ibn al-Walid. As a Meccan Quraysh clan, they were part of the broader tribal structure that later influenced Islamic expansion. Their historical significance is primarily tied to early Islamic history rather than modern GCC tribal politics.
history:
The Banu Makhzum was one of the most prominent and powerful clans of the Quraysh tribe in pre-Islamic Mecca, descended from Makhzum ibn Yaqaza. They formed part of the broader Adnani lineage through their Qurayshi ancestry, with the line of al-Mughira ibn Abd Allah ibn Umar ibn Makhzum emerging as the principal family within the clan. During the 6th century CE, they were aligned with the Ahlaf ('allies') faction alongside the clans of Abd al-Dar, Banu Sahm, Banu Jumah and Banu Adi, in rivalry to the Banu Hashim and Abd Shams factions within Quraysh tribal politics.

By the late 6th century, the Banu Makhzum had achieved extraordinary prominence in Meccan society, particularly under Hisham ibn al-Mughira, whose influence was so great that the Quraysh established a dating system beginning with his death. His family dominated the lucrative trade routes with Yemen and Ethiopia, making them one of the wealthiest clans in Mecca. This economic power translated into significant political influence within the Quraysh confederation.

The clan became the foremost opponents of Muhammad and early Islam in the early 7th century, with their chieftain Abu Jahl (Amr ibn Hisham) leading the opposition and organizing a devastating economic boycott of the Banu Hashim around 616-618 CE. Their hostility to Islam was so intense that they tortured early Muslim converts, including Ammar ibn Yasir and his family, who were under Makhzumite control. Abu Jahl personally murdered Sumayya, Ammar's mother, making her the first Muslim martyr.

The Battle of Badr in 624 CE marked a catastrophic turning point for the Banu Makhzum, where seven or eight nobles from the al-Mughira line were killed, severely weakening their power structure and causing them to be surpassed in prominence by the Banu Abd Shams. However, some members like Khalid ibn al-Walid eventually converted and joined Muhammad's forces during the conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death in 632 CE, former Makhzumite opponents became crucial military leaders in early Islamic expansion, with Khalid ibn al-Walid and Ikrima (son of Abu Jahl) playing pivotal roles in the Ridda wars and conquests in Syria and Iraq, though the forty male descendants of Khalid died in a plague during the late Umayyad period.

timelineEvents.description: Abu Jahl of Banu Makhzum organized a boycott of Muhammad's clan, the Banu Hashim, lasting until approximately 618 CE.

timelineEvents.description: Banu Makhzum suffered heavy losses at Badr with seven or eight nobles from al-Mughira line killed, significantly weakening their position in Mecca.

timelineEvents.description: After Muhammad's death, Khalid and Ikrima played active roles in suppressing rebel tribes during the Ridda wars.

timelineEvents.description: Khalid ibn al-Walid scored key victories against Byzantines during the Muslim conquest of Syria, earning legendary status.

name: Banu Manhib
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and genealogical recognition
ancestorStory: Manhib is considered the eponymous ancestor of the tribe. The lineage traces back through established Arabian genealogical traditions.
description: Banu Manhib was a historical Arabian tribe with Qahtani origins mentioned in classical Arab genealogical sources. They were part of the broader tribal structure of the Arabian Peninsula during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. Like many smaller Arabian tribes, their specific territorial boundaries and political activities are not extensively documented in major historical chronicles. Their current status and presence in the modern Gulf states is unclear from available historical records.
history: According to the research provided, Banu Manhib emerges as one of three major divisions within Banu Daws, itself a branch of the ancient Zahran tribe. Zahran (Arabic: زهران), also known as Banū Zahrān ibn Ka'ab, represents one of the oldest Arabian tribes in the Arabian Peninsula with clear Azdite origins, tracing their lineage to Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd. The tribe's homeland was centered in what is now Al Bahah Province, particularly around the valley of Khalasah in Daws territory. Banu Manhib, as part of this broader Zahrani confederation, would have shared in the tribe's pre-Islamic prominence and early Islamic period significance. The Zahran tribe played notable roles in early Arabian history, including migrations to Oman under Malik ibn Fehm's leadership in the 3rd century CE, and had members who joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina. However, the specific historical activities, territorial boundaries, and distinct political role of Banu Manhib within the larger Zahrani tribal structure remain undocumented in the available research, reflecting the common historical pattern where smaller tribal divisions within major confederations had their individual identities subsumed into the broader tribal narrative.

timelineEvents.title: Zahrani Migration to Oman
timelineEvents.description: Many tribes descending from Zahran and Azd settled in Oman under leadership of Malik bin Fehm

timelineEvents.title: Zahrani Conversion to Islam
timelineEvents.description: Many Zahrani tribe members left their homes and joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina

timelineEvents.title: Destruction of Dhu l-Khalasah
timelineEvents.description: King Abdulaziz ordered the destruction of Dhu l-Khalasah idol in Zahrani homeland, carried out by Abdulaziz Al Ibrahim

name: Banu Al-Mashruki
legitimacyNotes: traditional tribal lineage and customary leadership
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Al-Mashrouq, who was known for his eastern migrations. The name relates to 'mashriq' meaning east or sunrise.
description: Banu Al-Mashruki were a tribal branch historically associated with the eastern Arabian Peninsula. They were part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation and engaged primarily in nomadic pastoralism and trade routes. The tribe's name suggests connections to eastern migrations or territories. They are primarily of historical significance with limited contemporary documentation.

relations.context: part of broader Qahtani confederation

history: Banu Al-Mashruki were a branch of the Hamdan tribal confederation, which descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, placing them firmly within the Qahtani lineage. As part of the broader Kahlan confederation in ancient Saba' (Yemen), they were affected by the gradual decline of Saba' from the 2nd century BC and the subsequent rise of Himyar. The Kahlan tribes, including the ancestors of Banu Al-Mashruki, were eventually overpowered by Himyar and forced out of the Yemeni highlands. Most Kahlan tribes remained in the Yemeni desert region around Marib until the destruction of the great dam in the 3rd century AD, which triggered major migrations northward through Arabia. Unlike other Hamdan branches such as Hashid and Bakil who remained in northern Yemen between Marib and Hajja'a, or Banu Yam who settled in Najran, Banu Al-Mashruki undertook a westward migration that eventually brought them to the Levant. They settled in Lebanon, where they established themselves as influential families within the Maronite Christian community. The tribe produced several prominent Maronite families including the Awwad, Massa'ad, Al-Sema'ani, and Hasroun lineages, demonstrating their successful integration and rise to prominence in Lebanese society. Their migration represents one of the notable movements of Yemeni tribes during the post-dam period, illustrating the broader diaspora of Qahtani tribes throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

migrationPath.description: Forced migration northward following destruction of Marib Dam and Himyarite pressure
Settlement in Lebanon, establishing Maronite families

timelineEvents.title: Himyarite Expansion
timelineEvents.description: Saba' begins declining, Himyar settles allied tribes and strengthens in lowlands

timelineEvents.title: Kahlan Displacement
timelineEvents.description: Kahlan tribes overpowered by Himyar and forced from Yemeni highlands

timelineEvents.title: Marib Dam Destruction
timelineEvents.description: Destruction of Marib Dam forces mass migration of Kahlani tribes northward

timelineEvents.title: Lebanese Settlement
timelineEvents.description: Banu Al-Mashruki settle in Lebanon, producing influential Maronite families

name: Banu Muayiya
legitimacyNotes: Descent from Umayyad lineage and traditional tribal authority
ancestorStory: Named after Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan, the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate. The tribal branch claims descent from this Umayyad lineage.
description: Banu Muayiya is a tribal branch that claims descent from the Umayyad caliphal lineage, specifically tracing their name to Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan. They were part of the broader Adnani tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's prominence was largely tied to the early Islamic period when Umayyad authority was at its peak. Their historical presence in the Gulf region was limited, with most branches settling in the Levant and other parts of the former Umayyad territories.

relations.context: Both claim Adnani descent and early Islamic connections

history:
Banu Muayiya was a branch of the Kindah tribe, which formed part of the larger Kahlan tribal confederation descended from Qahtani origins in ancient Yemen. The Kindah originally dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, from where a group moved to Najd and established a powerful vassal kingdom under Himyar. As one of the main Kindite branches, Banu Muayiya ruled much of northern Arabia and Bahrain, maintaining their political power through direct affiliation with the Himyarite kingdom.

The tribe's prominence was intrinsically tied to their Himyarite patrons, and they experienced significant decline after Himyar's fall in 525 AD. Following the collapse of Himyarite authority, Kindite towns controlled by Banu Muayiya and other Kindite branches fell under constant Bedouin raids from Najd. These persistent attacks eventually destroyed the Kindite presence in their territories, with survivors being absorbed into the Najdi tribal federations.

Banu Muayiya's territorial influence extended across northern Arabia and into Bahrain, where they maintained control as part of the broader Kindite political structure. Their decline represents part of the larger pattern of Kindite dissolution that occurred throughout the 6th century AD, as the various Kindite branches lost their political cohesion and territorial control following the end of Himyarite support. This places Banu Muayiya firmly within the Qahtani-Kindite genealogical framework rather than any Adnani lineage.

migrationPath.description: Kindah tribes, including ancestors of Banu Muayiya, were expelled from Bahrain to East Yemen
Kindite group moved to Najd where they established a vassal kingdom under Himyar

timelineEvents.description: Kindah tribes, including Banu Muayiya ancestors, were expelled from Bahrain to East Yemen

timelineEvents.description: Banu Muayiya declined after the fall of their Himyarite patrons

timelineEvents.description: Constant Bedouin raids from Najd destroyed Kindite towns; survivors absorbed into Najdi tribal federations

name: Banu Mustaliq
legitimacyNotes: Pre-Islamic Arabian tribe with documented presence during Prophet Muhammad's time
ancestorStory: Descended from Khuza'a, an ancient Arabian tribal confederation. The tribe was established as a distinct branch of the larger Khuza'i confederation in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Banu Mustaliq was a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Khuza'a confederation, primarily located in the Hijaz region. They are historically significant for their encounter with the Muslim community during the expedition of al-Muraysi in 627 CE, which resulted in their integration into the early Islamic state. The tribe was known for their pastoral lifestyle and control over water sources in their territory. Their historical presence is well-documented in early Islamic sources, though they do not appear to have maintained distinct tribal identity in the modern Gulf states.
history:
The Banu Mustaliq were a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the larger Banu Khuza'a confederation, with their lineage tracing back to the Azdi Qahtanite tribal structure. According to genealogical sources, they were descended from Khuza'a through Amr ibn Luhay, though there is scholarly debate about whether Khuza'a itself originated from Qahtanite or Adnanite lineage. The tribe occupied the strategic territory of Qadid (also called Qudayd) on the Red Sea shore between Jeddah and Rabigh in the Hijaz region, where they controlled vital water sources and maintained a pastoral lifestyle with substantial herds of camels, sheep, and goats.

As a branch of the Banu Khuza'a confederation, the Banu Mustaliq initially maintained alliances with the Quraysh of Mecca before Islam. Their historical significance centers on their encounter with the early Muslim community in December 627 CE (8 AH) during the expedition of al-Muraysi. Following reports that the Banu Mustaliq were preparing attacks against Muhammad, a Muslim force met them in battle at a watering place called al-Muraysi. The battle resulted in a decisive Muslim victory, with the capture of the tribal chief al-Harith and 200 families, along with substantial booty including 200 camels and 5,000 sheep and goats. The Mustaliq lost ten men while only one Muslim was killed.

Among the captives was al-Harith's daughter Juwayriya bint al-Harith, whose husband Mustafa bin Safwan had been killed in the battle. She initially fell to Muhammad's companion Thabit ibn Qays ibn Al-Shammas but was later freed when Muhammad proposed marriage to her in 627-628 CE. This strategic marriage led to the release of nearly 100 Mustaliq families, as the Muslims considered it inappropriate to hold as slaves the kinsmen of the Prophet's wife. The tribal integration into the early Islamic state marked the end of their independent political existence, though they maintained their identity as a branch of the Banu Khuza'a confederation.

The defeat of the Banu Mustaliq represented part of Muhammad's broader military strategy during the Muslim-Quraysh War, where he shifted focus from raiding Quraysh caravans to subjugating northern Arab tribes. The tribe's incorporation into the Islamic state through both military conquest and marriage alliance exemplified the dual approach of force and diplomacy used in early Islamic expansion. While the Banu Mustaliq ceased to exist as an independent political entity after 627 CE, their integration contributed to the broader absorption of Arabian tribal confederations into the emerging Islamic polity.

timelineEvents.title: Battle of al-Muraysi
timelineEvents.description: Decisive defeat of Banu Mustaliq by Muslim forces, resulting in capture of 200 families and tribal chief al-Harith

timelineEvents.title: Marriage of Juwayriya to Muhammad
timelineEvents.description: Marriage between Muhammad and Juwayriya bint al-Harith led to freedom of nearly 100 Mustaliq families and tribal integration into Islamic state

name: Banu Nabhan (Aswadan)
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Tayy confederation, derived from pre-Islamic Arabian tribal structure
ancestorStory: Nabhan was a descendant of the Tayy tribe, with Aswadan being a specific lineage branch. The tribe traces its origins to the ancient Tayy confederation of northern Arabia.
description: Banu Nabhan (Aswadan) was a tribal branch of the ancient Tayy confederation, one of the prominent Qahtani Arab tribes of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who migrated across the northern Arabian Peninsula. As part of the larger Tayy tribal system, they participated in the complex tribal politics of early Arabia and some branches may have migrated to various parts of the Gulf region over time. The tribe is now considered historical, with descendants likely absorbed into other tribal groupings.

relations.context: Both branches of the larger Tayy confederation
Fellow Tayy tribal branches
Common Tayy tribal ancestry

history:
The Banu Nabhan (Aswadan) were a prominent sub-branch of the ancient Tayy confederation, one of the most influential Qahtanite Arab tribes of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. According to traditional Arab genealogists, the tribe traced its ancestry through the Qahtanite lineage to Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy), who was descended from Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan. Specifically, Nabhan was descended from Aswadan, who was a son of Amr ibn al-Ghawth ibn Julhumah. The Banu Nabhan emerged as one of the leading sub-branches of the Al al-Ghawth division of Tayy in northern Arabia, alongside their tribal cousins the Banu Thu'al. The tribe originally migrated from Yemen to the Jabal Tayy region (the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges) in northern Arabia during the late 2nd century CE, where they established themselves as influential pastoralists and warriors.

During the early Islamic period, the Banu Nabhan played a crucial role in the conversion of the Tayy confederation to Islam. In 630-631 CE, a delegation of fifteen Tayy chiefs led by Zayd al-Khayr of the Banu Nabhan converted to Islam and pledged allegiance to Prophet Muhammad in what became a pivotal moment for the tribe's Islamic identity. Muhammad was particularly impressed by Zayd al-Khayr, who died shortly after his conversion. However, the tribe's loyalty was tested during the Ridda Wars following Muhammad's death in 632 CE. Some members of the Banu Nabhan, including Zayd's son Muhalhil, initially joined the rebel Tulayha at Buzakha, though they were later persuaded to return to Islam through the diplomatic efforts of Adi ibn Hatim and other loyal Tayy leaders.

The Banu Nabhan continued to play significant roles throughout Islamic history as part of the broader Tayy confederation. In 1021 CE, the Banu Nabhan led by Hamad ibn Uday besieged a Khurasani pilgrim caravan at Fayd near Jabal Tayy, demonstrating their continued influence in the region. As part of the Tayy's extensive tribal network, the Banu Nabhan's influence extended across the Syrian Desert, Palestine, and northern Arabia during the medieval period. Like many specific tribal branches of the ancient confederations, the Banu Nabhan were eventually absorbed into larger tribal groupings over the centuries, with their descendants likely integrated into the broader Shammar and other Tayy successor tribes that continue to inhabit the traditional Jabal Tayy homeland.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Yemen to northern Arabia as part of the broader Tayy tribal movement in the 2nd century CE

folkLegends.title: The Naming of Tayy
folkLegends.story: According to 9th-century Muslim historian al-Tabari, Julhumah received the laqab (surname) of Ṭayyiʾ from the word ṭawā, which in Arabic means 'to plaster'. He received this name because he was said to have been 'the first to have plastered the walls of a well'.

nameEtymology: Nabhan was named after his ancestor Aswadan, who was commonly known as Nabhan, a son of Amr ibn al-Ghawth ibn Julhumah according to traditional Arab genealogists

name: Banu Nu'aym
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on genealogical descent and consensus
ancestorStory: Nu'aym ibn Hammam was the progenitor of the tribe, descending from the ancient Arab lineages of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Nu'aym is a prominent Arab tribe with historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in Oman and the UAE. They established themselves as one of the significant tribal groups in the region during the medieval period. The tribe played important roles in various political alliances and conflicts throughout Gulf history, maintaining their traditional pastoral lifestyle while adapting to changing political circumstances. Today, members of the tribe are found across multiple GCC states, with many having settled into modern occupations while maintaining tribal identity.

relations.context: Historical alliances in maritime trade and regional politics
Political alignment in Omani tribal politics

history:
The research reveals significant confusion regarding Banu Nu'aym identity, as the primary documentation focuses on the Syrian tribal confederation Nu'aym rather than confirming the Arabian Peninsula tribe mentioned in existing tribal data. The Syrian Nu'aym are described as one of the major Bedouin tribes with historical presence across Syria, particularly concentrated in the Hauran and Golan Heights regions. According to their oral traditions, they first settled in the Golan Heights area in the 15th century during Mamluk rule, having originally dwelt in northern Syria before dispersing due to conflicts with the Mawali tribe, the dominant tribe of the Syrian steppe throughout the Mamluk and early Ottoman periods.

In 16th-century Ottoman tax records, the Nu'aym were recorded as the dominant tribe among 1,122 Bedouin households in the Lajat lava field of the Hauran. The tribe was raided in the early 1770s by Sheikh Zahir al-Umar, the autonomous Arab ruler of northern Palestine. During the 18th and 19th centuries, the Nu'aym and Al Fadl tribes grazed their flocks in the Golan region. In the early 19th century, the Nu'aym of central Syria were the largest Arab tribe of the Homs Sanjak and possibly the largest in the Hama Sanjak, collectively responsible for over 25% of the taxes owed by Arab tribes in Hama.

The Nu'aym is organizationally split between three main groups: the Nu'aym of the Golan (with paramount sheikh from the Tahhan family and three subdivisions), the Nu'aym of Homs (with paramount sheikh from the Nasif family and five subdivisions), and the Nu'aym of the Ghouta countryside of Damascus (with no leading sheikh and four subdivisions). The Nu'aym of southern Syria consists of eight main branches: al-Tahhan, al-Othman, al-Dhiyab, al-Zabban, al-Madha, al-Khurfan, al-Sultan and al-Darwish. The research notes that several members of the Nu'aym currently live as Syrian expatriates in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait and the northern emirates of the United Arab Emirates.

The research does not provide clear documentation connecting these Syrian Nu'aym to the Arabian Peninsula Banu Nu'aym mentioned in existing tribal data, nor does it verify claims of Hinawi alignment or specific historical presence in Oman and the UAE. This discrepancy suggests either distinct tribal entities sharing similar names or incomplete historical documentation of migration patterns between Syria and the Arabian Peninsula.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic period migration from central Arabian Peninsula to Omani interior regions
Expansion toward coastal areas for trade opportunities and seasonal grazing

folkLegends.title: Northern Syrian Origins
folkLegends.story: According to the Nu'aym's oral tradition, the tribe had originally dwelt in northern Syria but dispersed to different areas of Syria following conflicts with the Mawali, the dominant tribe of the Syrian steppe throughout the Mamluk and early Ottoman periods.

folkLegends.title: Common Ancestor Burial Site
folkLegends.story: The village of Izz al-Din is popularly held to be the burial place of the Nu'aym's purported common ancestor, Izz al-Din al-Hamra, for whom the village was named.

folkLegends.title: Tell Abyad Origins
folkLegends.story: According to the tribe's oral traditions, the Tell Abyad region near the Turkish border, historically centered around Urfa in modern Turkey, was the point from which the rest of the tribe in Syria originally stemmed after having migrated there from Iraq.

name: Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Qays ibn Tha'laba within the Adnani tribal structure
ancestorStory: Qays ibn Tha'laba was a tribal ancestor whose descendants formed various branches across the Arabian Peninsula. The lineage traces back through the Adnani genealogical framework.
description: Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba represents a tribal branch within the broader Adnani genealogical system of pre-Islamic Arabia. This tribal grouping was part of the complex network of Arabian tribes that existed before and during the early Islamic period. Like many pre-Islamic tribal branches, they were primarily involved in nomadic pastoralism and tribal warfare. Their historical significance is preserved mainly through genealogical records rather than specific political achievements in the Gulf region.

relations.context: Both trace descent through the Adnani genealogical framework

history:
Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba represents a tribal branch within the broader Qays 'Aylan confederation, one of the major tribal groupings of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. The research reveals that within the Qays confederation structure, the Banu Sulaym tribe - descended from Khasafa through Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khasafa - was divided into three main divisions: Imru' al-Qays, Harith, and Tha'laba. This Tha'laba division of Banu Sulaym appears to represent the genealogical connection to the tribal ancestor referenced as Qays ibn Tha'laba, placing this tribal branch firmly within the Adnani genealogical framework through the Mudar-Qays lineage.

The Qays 'Aylan confederation, to which Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba belonged, originally inhabited the low-lying areas of the Tihamah along the Hejaz in western Arabia. By Muhammad's time around 570 CE, the numerous Qays branches had spread throughout central and eastern Arabia, including the Najd, Yamama, Bahrayn, and parts of Lower Mesopotamia. During the early Islamic period, Qaysi tribes initially opposed Muhammad but gradually converted to Islam by 630 CE, though many apostatized during the Ridda Wars before returning to the Muslim fold.

Following the Muslim conquests, massive Qaysi migrations occurred northward into Syria and Mesopotamia. During the Umayyad Caliphate (661-750), the Qays became one of the main political and military factions, often in rivalry with the Yamani tribes led by Banu Kalb. The Sulaym tribe, which included the Tha'laba division connected to Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba, participated in these major political developments and migrations. Qaysi tribes established themselves particularly in Jund Qinnasrin (northern Syria and Upper Mesopotamia), from where they governed on behalf of the caliphs or rebelled against them.

By the 14th century, only remnants of Qaysi tribes remained in their central Arabian ancestral lands, as huge numbers had migrated to North Africa in successive waves. The Banu Sulaym, the parent tribe containing the Tha'laba division, established themselves in Ifriqiya (central North Africa) and Fes. The power of the Qays as a unified confederation diminished with the rise of the Abbasid Caliphate, though individual Qaysi tribes remained potent forces throughout the Islamic world.

migrationPath.description: Major northward migration of Qaysi tribes including Sulaym during and after Muslim conquests, establishing settlements in Jund Qinnasrin
Successive migratory waves of Qaysi tribes to North Africa, with Banu Sulaym settling in Ifriqiya and Fes

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Aylan Epithet
folkLegends.story: Traditional Arab genealogy holds multiple theories for the origins of the 'Aylan' part of Qays 'Aylan's name. Some genealogists claim Aylan was the name of al-Nas's (Qays's) famous horse, while others assert it was his dog, his bow, a mountain where he was born, or a man who raised him.

name: Banu Quda'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation claiming descent from Quda'a ibn Ma'add, with traditional authority over constituent tribes
ancestorStory: Quda'a was said to be the son of Ma'add and brother to Nizar, though some genealogists disputed whether he was Qahtani or Adnani. The confederation formed around tribes claiming descent from his various sons.
description: Banu Quda'a was a major pre-Islamic tribal confederation that included numerous tribes across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant. They were known for their migrations and military prowess, with constituent tribes settling from southern Arabia to Syria. The confederation played significant roles in pre-Islamic politics and early Islamic conquests. Most constituent tribes eventually integrated into other formations or developed independent identities, making the confederation primarily of historical significance.

relations.context: Himyarite tribe that settled in southern part of Lakhmid Kingdom
Mahri lineage traces back through Quda'a confederation
Himyarite descendants (Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah) were defeated by Quda'a confederation at Battle of Marj Rahit in 684
Some Al Qasimi genealogies trace connections to Quda'a branches
Traditional genealogical connections through ancient Quda'a lineages

history:
Banu Quda'a was a major pre-Islamic tribal confederation spanning the Arabian Peninsula and Levant, with disputed genealogical origins - some claiming descent from Ma'add (making them Adnani/northern Arabian), others from Himyar (making them Qahtani/southern Arabian). The confederation included numerous constituent tribes, with the Banu Kalb emerging as its largest and most politically significant component in northern territories, while other major branches included the Tayy, Banu Udhra, and various smaller tribes across different regions.

The Banu Kalb, as the confederation's most prominent branch, dominated northwestern Arabia and central Syria from their base in al-Jawf and Wadi Sirhan, gradually expanding into Byzantine Syria where they served as foederati from the 4th century CE. They embraced Monophysite Christianity under Ghassanid authority and became skilled in military discipline. The Tayy branch migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in the 2nd century CE, settling in the Shammar and Salma mountain ranges (later called Jabal Tayy), where they split into two main divisions: Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila, the latter converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria during the 6th-century Fasad War.

During the Islamic conquests (630s-640s), different Quda'a tribes responded variably to Muslim expansion. The Banu Udhra converted early through a delegation to Prophet Muhammad in 630, while the Kalb initially remained neutral before converting after the Battle of Yarmouk (636). The Tayy under chiefs Adi ibn Hatim and Zayd al-Khayr converted in 629-630 and became companions of the Prophet. Under the Umayyad Caliphate (661-750), the Kalb became the military foundation of the dynasty through strategic marriages with the ruling family, while the Tayy supported Ali during the First Civil War, creating lasting tribal divisions.

By the Abbasid period (750-1258), the confederation's political cohesion had largely dissolved. The Kalb lost influence under pro-Qaysite policies and eventually allied with various movements including the Qarmatians and later the Fatimids, gradually transitioning from nomadism to settled agriculture. The Tayy expanded across the Syrian steppe and northern Arabia, with branches like the Al Fadl and Al Mira controlling vast territories from Homs to central Najd. The Banu Udhra, meanwhile, migrated to Syria, Egypt, and eventually Muslim Spain, settling in Jaén, Almería, and around Algeciras, but retained primarily historical rather than political significance after the early Islamic period.

migrationPath.description: Some Udhra clans migrated into Syria during early Islamic period
Further migration of Udhra clans from Syria into Egypt
Migration to Muslim Spain, settling in Jaen, Almeria and around Algeciras

folkLegends.title: Quda'a's Disputed Ancestry
folkLegends.story: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Quda'a was said to be the son of Ma'add and brother to Nizar, though some genealogists disputed whether he was Qahtani or Adnani. The confederation formed around tribes claiming descent from his various sons.

folkLegends.title: The Naming of Tayy
folkLegends.story: The tribe's progenitor Julhumah ibn Udad received the surname 'Tayy' or 'Tayyi' from the word ṭawā, meaning 'to plaster' in Arabic, because he was said to have been the first to have plastered the walls of a well.

folkLegends.title: Kalb's Animal-Named Ancestry
folkLegends.story: The progenitor Kalb's mother, Asma bint Duraym, was known as Umm al-Asbu (mother of wild animals) because all of her children were named after wild animals, with Kalb meaning 'dog' in Arabic.

nameEtymology: Quda'a derives from the name of the confederation's legendary progenitor. The constituent tribe Kalb means 'dog' in Arabic, while Tayy comes from ṭawā meaning 'to plaster', though this etymology is disputed among historians.

name: Banu Sa'd
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical descent from Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: Sa'd ibn Bakr was a prominent figure among the Hawazin tribal confederation. His descendants became known for their nobility and were chosen as foster families for important figures including Prophet Muhammad.
description: Banu Sa'd were a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe, part of the larger Hawazin confederation, historically based in the Najd region. They are most famously known as the foster tribe of Prophet Muhammad, with Halima al-Sa'diyya serving as his wet nurse. The tribe played significant roles in early Islamic history and later migrations across the Arabian Peninsula. Many of their descendants integrated into other tribal confederations or settled in various parts of the Gulf region.

relations.context: Muhammad's foster mother Halimah al-Sa'diyah was from Banu Sa'd, a subdivision of Hawazin

history:
The Banu Sa'd (بنو سعد) were a prominent Arabian tribe belonging to the larger Hawazin confederation, descended from Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they traced their lineage to the Adnanite branch through Adnan, who is traditionally regarded as the patriarch of the Adnanite Arabs and considered a direct descendant of the prophet Abraham (Ibrahim) through his son Ishmael (Ismāʿīl). The Islamic prophet Muhammad is said to descend from this same Adnanite lineage through the genealogical chain: Adnan > Ma'add > Nizar > Mudar > Ilyas > Mudrikah > Khuzaimah > Kinanah > al-Nadr > Malik > Fihr > Ghalib > Lu'ay > Ka'b > Murrah > Kilab > Qusai > Abd Manaf > Hashim > Abd al-Muttalib > Abd Allah > Muhammad.

The Banu Sa'd gained particular historical significance as the foster tribe of Prophet Muhammad, with Halima al-Sa'diyya (Halima Sadia) serving as his wet nurse during his early childhood years. This relationship established deep connections between the tribe and the emerging Islamic community. The tribe was historically based in the Najd region during the pre-Islamic period and was considered among the leading noble tribes of Arabia during Muhammad's era. According to Islamic sources, they were described as great warriors who fought alongside Muhammad against enemies of Islam, having close family relations with the Prophet through the foster relationship.

As part of the broader Adnanite tribal confederation, the Banu Sa'd shared the traditional Arab genealogical claims that connected them to the Ishmaelite Arabs who inhabited Northern, Western, Eastern, and Central Arabia. The Adnanites were distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of Southern Arabia, who traced their lineage to Qahtan. This genealogical distinction played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian tribal politics and continued to influence tribal identities in the Islamic period. The tribe's descendants later dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula following the Islamic conquests, with many integrating into other tribal confederations or settling in various parts of the Gulf region during subsequent migrations.

migrationPath.description: Post-Ridda Wars migration of Sa'di clans toward coastal regions following tribal fragmentation
Settlement in Omani highlands and integration with local tribal confederations
Movement of remaining Sa'di groups toward Trucial Coast oases and settlements

timelineEvents.description: Halima al-Sa'diyya of Banu Sa'd becomes wet nurse and foster mother to Prophet Muhammad

timelineEvents.title: Alliance with Prophet Muhammad
timelineEvents.description: Banu Sa'd warriors fight battles alongside Muhammad against enemies of Islam

folkLegends.title: Adnan's Prophecy of Muhammad
folkLegends.story: According to Islamic tradition, it has been said that Adnan, the patriarch of the Adnanite Arabs from whom Banu Sa'd claimed descent, foretold the coming of Muhammad and ordered his successors to follow him.

folkLegends.title: Foster Relationship with Prophet Muhammad
folkLegends.story: Halima al-Sa'diyya of the Banu Sa'd served as the wet nurse and foster mother of Prophet Muhammad during his early childhood, establishing the tribe as his foster family and creating lasting bonds between the tribe and the Prophet.

name: Banu Sahm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Meccan clan with historical religious and commercial authority in pre-Islamic Arabia
ancestorStory: Sahm ibn Murrah was a prominent ancestor of Quraysh. The clan held custodianship of divination arrows at the Kaaba in pre-Islamic Mecca.
description: Banu Sahm was a prominent clan of the Quraysh tribe based in Mecca during pre-Islamic times. They held the hereditary role of custodians of the divination arrows (azlam) at the Kaaba and were involved in Meccan trade networks. Notable members included Amr ibn al-As, the Arab general who conquered Egypt. The clan's influence diminished after the early Islamic conquests as political power shifted away from traditional Meccan tribal structures.
history: Banu Sahm was one of the prominent clans of the Quraysh tribe that controlled Mecca in pre-Islamic times. They descended from Sahm ibn Amr ibn Husays ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ayy ibn Ghalib ibn Fihr ibn Malik, making them part of the Adnani lineage through Fihr ibn Malik, the progenitor of Quraysh. The clan was closely related to Banu Jumah, as both descended from the same ancestor within the Banu Husays grouping. According to traditional Arab genealogies, Fihr ibn Malik defended Mecca from the Himyarite Kingdom around 230-240 CE, leading forces that included the ancestors of Banu Sahm. Banu Sahm held significant religious and commercial responsibilities in Meccan society, controlling the sacred divination arrows (azlam) at the Kaaba and managing the organization of offerings and vows made to the idols. They were also responsible for settling disputes and conflicts within Meccan society. The clan formed part of the al-Ahlaf (Confederates) faction alongside Banu Abd al-Dar, Banu Adi, Banu Makhzum, and Banu Jumah, in political rivalry against the al-Mutayyabun faction that included Banu Hashim and other clans descended from Abd Manaf. This factional division emerged from succession disputes between Qusayy's natural successor Abd al-Dar and his chosen successor Abd Manaf in the late 5th/early 6th century. During the early Islamic period, Banu Sahm initially opposed Muhammad's message. However, several prominent members eventually converted to Islam and played crucial roles in the early Muslim conquests. Most notably, Amr ibn al-As al-Sahmi became one of Islam's greatest military commanders, leading the conquest of Egypt and serving as its first Muslim governor. The research mentions that during a commercial incident where a Yemenite merchant was robbed by al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi, it led to the reformation of the Hilf al-Fudul alliance. Other distinguished members included Hisham ibn al-As, Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (a renowned hadith narrator), and several others who participated in the second migration to Abyssinia in 615 CE to escape Meccan persecution. The clan's traditional power structure in Mecca was gradually transformed as the Islamic state expanded and political authority shifted from tribal confederations to the new Muslim administrative system.

migrationPath.description: Several Banu Sahm members participated in the second migration to Abyssinia to escape persecution, including Khunays ibn Hudhafa, Abdullah ibn Hudhafa, Hisham ibn al-As, and others

folkLegends.title: Divine Revelation About Tribal Competition
folkLegends.story: According to some traditions, verses 1-2 of Surat al-Takathur in the Quran were revealed concerning a dispute between Banu Sahm and other Qurayshi clans over counting their dead, reflecting the competitive nature of pre-Islamic Meccan tribal politics.

name: Banu Al-Sakasek
description: No verified historical records found for Banu Al-Sakasek in Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogical sources or Gulf historical documentation. This tribal name does not appear in established references for GCC tribal structures or Arabian tribal confederations. Further genealogical research would be required to verify the existence and historical significance of this tribal designation.
history: Banu Al-Sakasek was a branch of the Kinda tribal confederation, which itself formed part of the larger Kahlan confederation originating from ancient Yemen. The Kinda initially dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, with some groups moving to Nejd where they established a vassal kingdom under Himyarite overlordship. According to the genealogical sources, Banu Al-Sakasek, alongside their tribal relatives Banu Al-Sukun, settled in and ruled parts of Hadramout in eastern Yemen, where they 'fairly ruled' the region according to the tribal records. The tribe's power structure was integrated into the broader Kindite kingdom system, which operated as a client state of the Himyarite empire until the latter's collapse in 525 AD. Following the fall of Himyar, the Kindite territories, including those controlled by Banu Al-Sakasek, came under constant pressure from Bedouin raids originating from Nejd. These sustained attacks eventually led to the destruction of Kindite political authority throughout their former domains. The surviving Kindites, presumably including remnants of Banu Al-Sakasek, were gradually absorbed into the various Najdi tribal federations that emerged in the post-Himyarite period. Some members of related Sakasik/Sakasek groups appear to have migrated northward, with the Sakasic (a Himyarite tribe) settling in northern Egypt around the 3rd century AD, where they established the town that became modern Zaqaziq. Additionally, the Lebanese town of As-Saksakiyah traces its name to the Yemeni tribe of al-Sakāsik, who reportedly settled in southeastern Lebanon in 683 CE before moving to the coastal fortifications and finally establishing themselves in the 8th century.

migrationPath.description: Kinda tribes expelled from Bahrain to East Yemen
Banu Al-Sakasek settled and ruled parts of Hadramout alongside Banu Al-Sukun
Absorption into Najdi tribal federations following destruction of Kindite authority

name: Banu Shayban
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with established genealogical records and historical presence
ancestorStory: Descended from Shayban ibn Tha'laba ibn Ukashah ibn Sa'b ibn Ali ibn Bakr ibn Wa'il. The tribe traces its lineage to the northern Arabian tribal confederation of Rabi'a.
description: Banu Shayban was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Bakr ibn Wa'il confederation. They were historically based in northeastern Arabia and lower Mesopotamia, playing significant roles in pre-Islamic conflicts and early Islamic conquests. The tribe was known for their warrior culture and participated in major battles including the famous Battle of Dhi Qar against the Sassanians. While their direct tribal structure has largely merged with other groups over time, their descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula and Iraq.

relations.context: Both tribes belong to the broader Rabi'a tribal confederation
Historical presence in lower Mesopotamia and eastern Arabia

history:
Banu Shayban was a prominent pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabian tribe descended from Shayban ibn Tha'laba ibn Ukashah ibn Sa'b ibn Ali ibn Bakr ibn Wa'il, tracing their lineage to the northern Arabian tribal confederation of Rabi'a within the Adnani genealogical line. The tribe belonged to the larger Banu Bakr confederation and was historically based in northeastern Arabia and lower Mesopotamia, following seasonal migration patterns between wintering in Jadiyya in the Najd and moving to fertile lowlands around the Euphrates for summer grazing, ranging from Upper Mesopotamia to Lower Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf shores. The tribe was celebrated for their remarkable poets, pure Arabic language, and fighting prowess, serving as the most important force within Banu Bakr when they defeated the Persians at the famous Battle of Dhi Qar.

During the pre-Islamic period, the Shayban maintained fluctuating relations with the Sasanian authorities and the Arab client kingdoms of al-Hira. Notable pre-Islamic figures included Jassas ibn Murrah al-Shaybani, whose assassination of Kulaib ibn Rabiah of the Taghlib tribe sparked the 40-year Basus War (494-534 CE), demonstrating the tribe's involvement in major inter-tribal conflicts. During Muhammad's era and the early Islamic period, the Shayban became allies of the Banu Hashim clan and played crucial roles in the Muslim conquests.

The tribe's most prominent military leader was Al-Muthanna ibn Haritha al-Shaybani, who emerged as a key commander leading initial Muslim incursions into Iraq. He coordinated tribal contingents from the vicinity of al-Hira and played crucial roles in battles including Ullais, Walaja, Buwaib (634), and the decisive Battle of al-Qadisiyyah (636). After the defeat at the Battle of the Bridge where Abu Ubayd al-Thaqafi was killed, al-Muthanna organized the retreat and preserved remnants of the Muslim army, later leading them to victory at Buwaib which helped stabilize Muslim positions in the region.

Under the Umayyad Caliphate, the Shayban remained powerful in al-Jazira province, with notable figures including Shabib ibn Yazid al-Shaybani who led a major Kharijite revolt in the 690s and al-Dahhak ibn Qays al-Shaybani who led another revolt in 745-746. The early Abbasid period marked their zenith under the family of Ma'n ibn Za'ida al-Shaybani, whose descendants Yazid ibn Mazyad and Ahmad ibn Mazyad occupied high offices. Yazid served as governor of Arminiya twice, conducting large-scale Arab colonization in Shirvan and founding the Mazyadid dynasty that ruled as Shirvanshahs until 1027. Another successful line was that of Isa ibn al-Shaykh al-Shaybani, whose son Ahmad established control over much of al-Jazira, Diyar Bakr, and Armenian borderlands before being defeated by Caliph al-Mu'tadid in 893. After the 11th century, the tribe became highly fragmented, though descendants still trace their origins to Banu Shayban in southeastern Anatolia's Diyar Bakr region, organized loosely without a central sheikh.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal migration from winter grazing in Najd to summer pastures around the Euphrates
Settlement in al-Jazira during early Islamic conquests, particularly in Diyar Bakr district

folkLegends.title: The Basus War Origin
folkLegends.story: The 40-year Basus War began when Kulaib ibn Rabiah of the Taghlib tribe shot a prized she-camel belonging to Al-Basus, an influential woman affiliated with the Banu Bakr. This action angered Jassas ibn Murrah al-Shaybani, who murdered Kulaib over this conflict, arousing the anger of the whole Taghlib tribe and starting decades of warfare between Taghlib and the Banu Shayban and Banu Bakr.

name: Banu Al-Sukun
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Yemeni tribal branch with documented pre-Islamic genealogy
ancestorStory: Al-Sukun ibn Ashras was a prominent ancestor from the Kindah confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the ancient Kahlan branch of Qahtani Arabs.
description: Banu Al-Sukun was a significant tribal branch of the Kindah confederation, originating from Yemen and part of the broader Qahtani lineage. They were prominent in pre-Islamic Arabia and played roles in early Islamic conquests and migrations. Like many ancient Arabian tribes, they dispersed across the peninsula during various migration waves, with some branches eventually settling in areas that would become part of modern GCC states. The tribe is primarily of historical significance, with their descendants integrated into larger tribal confederations over the centuries.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

history: Banu Al-Sukun was a significant tribal branch of the ancient Kindah confederation, tracing their lineage through the Qahtani Arabs via Kahlan bin Saba. According to historical sources, they were one of the main divisions of Kinda, alongside Banu Muawiyah (the ruling branch) and Banu Al-Sakasek. The tribe largely settled in Wadi Do'an east of Hadramout and did not play a major role in the main Kindite kingdom centered in Najd. Instead, they engaged in long-lasting battles with the native tribes of Hadramout, alongside Banu Al-Sakasek, eventually gaining some measure of control over parts of the region. During the early Islamic period, the Al-Jund Mosque in Taiz was established on the lands of Al-Sukun and Al-Sakasik, making it the second-oldest mosque in Yemen. Two tribes of Kinda, Al-Sukun and Al-Sakasik, fought alongside Marwan bin Al-Hakam in the crucial Battle of Marj Rahit, which solidified Marwan's rule and marked the beginning of the second phase of the Umayyad state. Al-Husayn bin Al-Numair Al-Sakuni, a leader from this tribal background, played a major role in gathering the people of Yemen in the Levant alongside Marwan ibn al-Hakam. The tribe's prominence was primarily during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, after which they became integrated into larger tribal confederations and their descendants dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula, with some branches eventually settling in areas that would become part of modern GCC states.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in eastern Hadramout region after Kinda tribal dispersal

name: Banu Sulaym
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with well-documented genealogy tracing to Adnanite lineage
ancestorStory: Sulaym ibn Mansur was a descendant of Qays Aylan from the Adnanite branch. The tribe became one of the major Arabian tribes with extensive migrations across the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Sulaym was a major Arabian tribe of Adnanite origin that played significant roles in pre-Islamic and early Islamic history. Originally based in the Najd and Hijaz regions, various branches migrated extensively across Arabia and North Africa. Some branches settled in eastern Arabia and maintained presence in areas that would later become part of modern Gulf states. The tribe is historically significant for its participation in early Islamic conquests and tribal confederations.

relations.context: Described by Ibn Khaldun as 'mother tribes' that migrated together to Egypt and the Maghreb
Al Bin Ali clan traces lineage to Banu Sulaym rather than Anizah
Both trace Adnanite origins through ancient Arabian tribal networks
Historical presence in eastern Arabian territories

history:
Banu Sulaym was a major Arabian tribe of Adnanite origin, descended from Sulaym ibn Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khasafa ibn Qays Aylan. The tribe was divided into three main divisions: Imru' al-Qays (the strongest division with branches Khufaf, Awf, and Bahz), Harith (with branches Mu'awiyah, Zafar, Rifa'a, Ka'b, and Abs), and Tha'laba (including Malik and Dhakwan branches). In pre-Islamic times, they inhabited the northern Hejaz with the Harrah volcanic field (formerly called Harrat Bani Sulaym) forming their territorial heart. The Imru' al-Qays division controlled the eastern slopes where the Bahz branch owned lucrative gold mines, while the Harith occupied the western slopes. Some Mu'awiyah branch members lived in Medina and converted to Judaism before the arrival of the Aws and Khazraj.

The Dhakwan clan maintained close ties with the Quraysh of Mecca, with members serving important roles including al-Hakim ibn Umayya as muhtasib of pre-Islamic Mecca. Initially hostile to Muhammad and fighting alongside the Quraysh at the Battle of the Trench in 627, most Sulaym converted to Islam by 630 and fought with Muhammad at the Battle of Hunayn. However, most defected during the Ridda Wars after Muhammad's death in 632. Following the Muslim conquests, Sulaym tribesmen migrated to northern Syria, the Jazira (Upper Mesopotamia), and various cities including Kufa, Basra, and throughout Khurasan, though maintaining presence in their Arabian homeland.

As members of the Qays tribal confederation, they opposed the Umayyads and suffered heavy losses at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684, with about 600 Sulaym killed. Under Dhakwan chief Umayr ibn al-Hubab, they later contributed to Umayyad defeats and engaged in territorial conflicts with the Taghlib tribe along the Khabur River until 693. In the 9th century, much of the Arabian Sulaym were absorbed by the Harb tribe from Yemen. The Fatimid caliph al-Aziz expelled the Sulaym and Banu Hilal to Upper Egypt around 975-996 for supporting the Qarmatians.

In the mid-11th century, prolonged famine in Egypt prompted their westward migration with the Hilal into Libya, where Sulaym established themselves primarily in Cyrenaica. Their descendants, known as Sa'ada, continue to dominate eastern Libya today, divided into Harabi (including Ubaydat, Bara'asa, Hasa, Derasa, Aylat Fayid) and Jabarina (including Awaqir, Magharba, Majabira, Aryibat, Baraghith) divisions. The Al Bin Ali clan of the Bani Utbah confederation traces its lineage to Banu Sulaym, distinguishing them from other Utub clans who descend from Anizah. This branch participated in the 1783 conquest of Bahrain and various military campaigns including the 1837 conquest of Mombasa.

migrationPath.description: Post-conquest migration to northern Syria and Upper Mesopotamia following Muslim conquests
Fatimid expulsion to Upper Egypt for supporting Qarmatians
Westward migration during prolonged famine, establishing in Cyrenaica while Hilal continued to central North Africa

name: Amr
legitimacyNotes: Lineage authority through descent from Tamim ibn Murr
ancestorStory: Amr was one of the sons of Tamim ibn Murr, from whom this branch of Banu Tamim traces its descent. The lineage represents one of the major subdivisions within the larger Tamimi confederation.
description: Banu Tamim Amr represents a major tribal branch within the larger Banu Tamim confederation, one of the most prominent Adnani Arab tribes. They were historically based in the Najd region of central Arabia and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. As part of the broader Tamimi tribal system, they maintained traditional Bedouin culture centered on camel herding and tribal warfare. Many descendants have integrated into modern Gulf societies, particularly in Saudi Arabia and other GCC states.

Common descent from Adnan through Tamim lineage

history: The research provided discusses Amr ibn Ma'dikarib al-Zubaidi (died 642 CE), a legendary Arabian cavalry commander from the Zubaid clan of Yemen, part of the Madhhij tribe confederation. However, this figure is entirely separate from the Banu Tamim Amr branch specified in the existing data. Amr ibn Ma'dikarib was a Qahtanite Yemeni warrior who lived in the Asir region near Abha, participated in pre-Islamic tribal warfare, converted to Islam under Prophet Muhammad, and died fighting the Sassanids at the Battle of Nahavand in 642 CE. His descendants, including sub-branches like Jubur, Obaid and Al-Uqaydat, are found mainly in Iraq, Syria and Saudi Arabia today. The research also mentions other unrelated Amr figures: Amr ibn Luhay al-Khuza'i (a pre-Islamic figure who allegedly introduced idol worship to Arabia around the 3rd-4th centuries CE) and Banu Amr bin Auf (an Arabian tribe from Quba near Medina). None of this research provides any information about the Banu Tamim Amr branch referenced in the existing data, which represents a completely different tribal lineage within the Adnani Arab confederation. The Tamimi Amr would have been based in the Najd region as part of the broader Banu Tamim tribal system, but the provided research contains no specific details about their history, migrations, or development.
