name: Dhu'l-Kala
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Himyarite royal lineage with historical sovereignty in Yemen
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor who ruled parts of ancient Yemen and held the title 'Dhu'l-Kala' meaning 'possessor of the fortress'. They were among the noble houses of the Himyarite kingdom.
description: Dhu'l-Kala was an ancient Qahtani tribe from the Himyarite nobility of Yemen, known for their royal lineage and territorial control in pre-Islamic Arabia. They were among the established ruling houses of South Arabia before Islam and participated in the early Islamic conquests. The tribe's influence was primarily centered in Yemen rather than the modern GCC states. Their historical significance lies in their role as one of the ancient Arab royal families that bridged the pre-Islamic and Islamic periods.

relations.context: Both descend from Qahtani lineage

history:
Dhu'l-Kala was an ancient Qahtani noble family from the Himyarite kingdom of Yemen, named after their ancestor who held the title 'Dhu'l-Kala' meaning 'possessor of the fortress.' They were among the most powerful aristocratic houses in pre-Islamic South Arabia, with their influence centered in the Himyarite territories of Yemen rather than the northern Arabian Peninsula. The family's prominence peaked under Dhu al-Kala Samayfa ibn Nakur al-Himyari, who led the Dhu al-Kala as the most powerful clan in Yemen during the time of Prophet Muhammad and Caliph Abu Bakr (632-634).

During the Muslim conquest of Syria in the 630s, Samayfa participated as a commander and, like many other Yemenite tribesmen, settled in Homs after its capture in 637. There he became one of the two principal leaders of the Himyar tribe alongside Dhu al-Asbah Kurayb ibn Abraha, with Samayfa being referred to by early Muslim sources as the 'king of Himyar.' According to historian Wilferd Madelung, Samayfa originally intended to settle in Damascus to establish Himyarite dominance over Syria, which conflicted with Qurayshite interests. During Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan's governorship of Syria in the 640s, Samayfa was known to act counter to Mu'awiya's orders in Homs, where the Yemenite troops followed his command rather than the official governor's.

At the Battle of Siffin in 657 during the First Fitna, Samayfa commanded the Himyarite troops in Mu'awiya's army against Caliph Ali and was killed in combat on 27 July while leading his forces against the Rabi'a contingent. His death, along with that of Ubayd Allah ibn Umar, marked the beginning of Himyarite decline in Syria. Samayfa was succeeded by his son Shurahbil, who continued as a leading Umayyad commander until his death at the Battle of Khazir in 686, after which the Himyar in Syria 'sank to military insignificance' according to Madelung.

Other members of the Dhu al-Kala family served as governors and commanders for the Umayyad caliphs, including Khalid ibn Ma'dan ibn Abi Karib who served as head of Yazid I's shurta (select troops) and later became a prominent Muslim scholar, and Imran ibn al-Nu'man who served as governor of Sind under Caliph Umar II (717-720). The family maintained their significance as part of the broader Himyarite aristocratic presence in early Islamic Syria, contributing to Homs becoming a center of South Arabian settlement, culture, and political power.

name: al-Diqeisab
description: Al-Diqeisab is a tribal name found in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about this specific tribal group is limited in widely available sources. Like many smaller tribal branches, they may represent a sub-lineage of a larger tribal confederation. Further research would be needed to establish their specific geographical distribution, historical role, and current status within the GCC region.
history: Al-Diqeisab (الدقيساب) is documented as one of the Riverine Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, inhabiting the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in an area called Dar al-Manasir. They are part of the broader Manasir tribal confederation, which constitutes one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan and should not be confused with the Manasir of the Persian Gulf region in the Arabian Peninsula. The Manasir people, including al-Diqeisab, are an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language, similar to their neighboring tribes the upstream Rubatab and downstream Shaiqiyah. According to Sudanese genealogical records, the Manasir trace their origins to multiple potential lineages with varying accounts placing their ancestor Mansur either as a descendant of al-'Abbas (the uncle of Muhammad), as Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), or as connected to other tribal lineages including the Kawahla and Shaiqi clans. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Diqeisab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. As riverine Manasir, al-Diqeisab pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop, and they are renowned for their skill in building mud houses. As of 1993, the broader Manasir population was approximately 30,000-33,000 residents. The relationship, if any, between this Sudanese al-Diqeisab clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribal groups of similar name remains undocumented in available sources.

folkLegends.title: Mansur bin Qahtan Origin
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.title: Abbasid Descent
folkLegends.story: According to the current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor Mansur belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad.

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Connection
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: Shaiqi Clan Origin
folkLegends.story: Other historians insist that they are the descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights. According to this opinion Mansur is a direct descendant of King Sabir.

name: Drisah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and consensus
description: Al Drisah is a tribal group found in parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally associated with nomadic pastoralism and have maintained their tribal identity in the modern era. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and major historical events. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in contemporary times.
history:
The Drisah are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab Arab tribal confederation that operates primarily in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab tribe is traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The confederation consists of multiple subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah, and Zahairat.

Unlike other Bani Kaab subsections that had settled by the turn of the 20th century, the Drisah maintained their nomadic lifestyle along with the Shwaihiyin subsection. The broader Bani Kaab confederation numbered approximately 7,250 people, with 1,150 remaining Bedouin at this time. This distinction between settled and nomadic sections within the confederation reflects the varied adaptations to changing economic and political conditions in the region.

In 1844, the Drisah, as part of the Bani Kaab confederation, allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. This alliance demonstrated the fluid nature of tribal politics in southeastern Arabia, where confederations formed based on immediate strategic needs. However, these alliances proved temporary, as the Bani Kaab later aligned themselves with Saudi Arabia in the 1940s under Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah, who declared to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.'

During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Drisah supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and engaged in combat against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. This conflict represented the culmination of decades of shifting tribal allegiances in the region, with the Drisah finding themselves opposed to their former Bani Yas allies. The dispute highlighted the complex interplay between traditional tribal territories, emerging oil interests, and the competing claims of regional powers in the formation of modern Gulf states.

history:
The Duru (Arabic: الدرو، singular Al Darai الدروع) is a relatively small but historically significant Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates. Despite their limited numbers, the Duru managed to involve themselves in numerous territorial conflicts throughout the Trucial States during the 20th century, particularly around the strategically important Buraimi and Liwa oases. The tribe maintained their traditional dar (territory) in these areas while engaging in complex alliances and rivalries with neighboring tribes. The Duru belonged to the Ghafiri faction and maintained persistent conflicts with other tribes, most notably the warlike Awamir who encroached upon their territory, and the Rashid tribe, who were described as maintaining 'lively tribal enmities' with the Duru.

In 1877, the Duru joined forces with the Awamir tribe to conduct raids against Dubai, while northern Bani Qitab simultaneously raided Abu Dhabi, before peace was eventually brokered. Although the Duru considered themselves dependents of Dubai and maintained good relations with the Al Bu Shamis, territorial pressures intensified in the early 20th century. During the 1920s, major fighting erupted between Bedouin tribes in the Liwa and Buraimi regions, with the Duru forming part of a southern confederation alongside the Awamir and Al Bu Shamis against the northern alliance of Manasir and Bani Yas of Dhafra.

The conflicts escalated significantly under Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan's rule of Abu Dhabi. In December 1923, Sultan marched against the Duru, prompting them to raise a force with the Awamir and Bani Qitab of Falayah that successfully defeated a party of 20 riders from Abu Dhabi, killing seven and capturing the rest. The escalating conflict saw both sides conducting retaliatory raids, with the Duru targeting Dubai in revenge for its support of Sultan's forces. Seeking external protection, the Duru, along with the Al Bu Shamis, turned to Ibn Jaluwi who was eager to extend Saudi influence among the Trucial States tribes.

This decision to seek Saudi protection contributed significantly to the territorial arguments that would later form part of the Buraimi Dispute between Saudi Arabia and the Trucial States. As portions of various tribes, including elements influenced by Duru conflicts, became Saudi citizens while others remained under British protection, the tribe's political choices had lasting geopolitical consequences that extended well beyond their limited numbers would suggest.

timelineEvents.title: Joint Duru-Awamir Raids
timelineEvents.description: Duru and Awamir tribes joined forces to raid Dubai while northern Bani Qitab raided Abu Dhabi before peace was arranged

timelineEvents.title: Bedouin Tribal Wars
timelineEvents.description: Fighting broke out in Liwa and Buraimi between northern confederation of Manasir and Bani Yas versus southern confederation of Awamir, Duru and Al Bu Shamis

timelineEvents.title: Sultan's March Against Duru
timelineEvents.description: Sheikh Sultan bin Zayed Al Nahyan marched against the Duru in December, leading to their victory over Abu Dhabi forces

timelineEvents.title: Duru Retaliatory Raid on Dubai
timelineEvents.description: Understanding that Dubai had supported Sultan, the Duru raided Dubai in revenge

timelineEvents.title: Awamir Migration to Al Hasa
timelineEvents.description: Fighting led Awamir headman Salim bin Hamad bin Rakkad to lead his people to Al Hasa, contributing to tribal schisms that affected Duru relations

name: Du'um
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership and customary authority
description: Du'um is a tribal grouping found in the Arabian Peninsula, historically engaged in traditional pastoral activities. The tribe maintains its tribal identity and customs in the modern era. Limited historical documentation makes detailed reconstruction of their specific role in Gulf regional politics difficult to establish with certainty.
history:
Du'um is documented as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid confederation consisted of eleven main branches: Al Humaid, Al Asfour, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood. The chieftainship of the confederation was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan, while Du'um maintained its position as a significant branch within this powerful tribal alliance.

Under the leadership of Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid confederation, including the Du'um branch, successfully expelled Ottoman forces from eastern Arabian cities and towns, establishing their emirate with Al-Mubarraz as the capital. The Du'um, as part of this confederation, participated in the political dominance of eastern Arabia during this period, controlling territories that extended from southern Iraq and Kuwait to al-Hasa and al-Qatif in eastern Arabia. The confederation ruled this strategically important region for over a century, controlling vital trade routes and oasis settlements.

The Bani Khalid confederation, including Du'um, maintained staunch opposition to the rising Saudi-Wahhabi alliance in Najd from the mid-18th century. When the emir of Uyayna adopted Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's ideas, the Khalidi chief ordered him to cease support, leading to Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's move to Dir'iyyah and his crucial alliance with Al Saud. The confederation's efforts to stop Saudi expansion through multiple invasions of Najd and Diriyyah ultimately failed, and the Saudis conquered the Bani Khalid domain in al-Hasa in 1793, ending their rule.

After the Ottoman restoration in 1818 following the Egyptian invasion of Arabia, members of the Al 'Uray'ir were reinstated as governors, but the Bani Khalid confederation was significantly weakened. The Du'um, along with other branches, faced encroachment from rival tribes including the Ajman, Dawasir, Subay', and Mutayr, while also suffering from internal leadership disputes. Despite forging an alliance with the 'Anizzah tribe, the confederation suffered final defeats in battles against a Mutayri-'Ajmani alliance in 1823 and against the Subay' and Saudis in 1830, permanently ending Bani Khalid rule in eastern Arabia.

timelineEvents.title: Bani Khalid Expel Ottomans
timelineEvents.description: Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid, the Bani Khalid confederation including Du'um expelled Ottoman forces from eastern Arabian cities and established their emirate

timelineEvents.title: Saudi Conquest of Al-Hasa
timelineEvents.description: The Saudis conquered the Bani Khalid domain in al-Hasa, ending the rule of the confederation including the Du'um branch

timelineEvents.title: Ottoman Restoration
timelineEvents.description: After Egyptian invasion of Arabia and Ottoman restoration, Al 'Uray'ir were reinstated as governors but Bani Khalid power was weakened

timelineEvents.title: Battle Against Mutayri-Ajmani Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Bani Khalid confederation including Du'um suffered defeat against alliance of Mutayr and Ajman tribes

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay' and Al Saud ended the rule of the Bani Khalid confederation permanently

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

name: Fad'an
legitimacyNotes: Major branch of Anizah confederation with traditional Bedouin leadership structure
ancestorStory: Descended from Fad'an ibn Rabi'ah of the Anizah tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the Adnani Arab genealogical tradition.
description: The Fad'an are a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation, historically based in northern Arabia and the Syrian desert. They were among the most powerful Bedouin tribes during the 18th and 19th centuries, controlling extensive grazing territories and trade routes. The tribe is known for their camel herding traditions and warrior culture. Today, Fad'an members are found across Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Syria, and Iraq, with some branches having settled in Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliances during Saudi state formation
Traditional rivalry over grazing territories and tribal leadership in northern Arabia

history:
The Fad'an are a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation, tracing their genealogy through the Adnani Arab lineage alongside the tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib within the large Rabiʿa branch. The Anizah tribe's existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, with two main branches recorded by early historians - one nomadic in northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and another sedentary in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. During the Ottoman era, the Anizah became masters of the oasis towns of northwestern Arabia, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, spending winter months in these areas while migrating northwards into southern Syria during summer months.

The Fad'an entered the Syrian plains in the early 19th century as part of the broader Anizah expansion into the Levant. The first Anizah tribe to enter Syria was the Hasana in the mid-18th century, establishing dominance around Hama and Homs. The Fad'an, along with their subordinate tribe the Sba'a, began challenging the established Hasana control over the plains east of Hama and Homs in the early 19th century. In 1814, large numbers of Fad'an and Sba'a forces entered the plains east of the Orontes River, prompting the Hasana to ally with local Ottoman troops against them. Despite initial resistance lasting two weeks, the Fad'an eventually retreated but continued to return periodically to raid Hasana territories west of Hama.

The Fad'an's territorial influence extended significantly during the Ottoman period, as they displaced established tribes and reshaped the tribal geography of central Syria. Following the Mawali tribe's devastating defeat in 1786 and their displacement by Anaza tribes, the Fad'an were among those granted the former Mawali rights to collect tolls from caravans traversing through Palmyra. The Hadidiyin tribe migrated from areas near Aleppo and the Euphrates valley to the Hama region in the mid-18th century specifically due to pressures from the advancing Fad'an, demonstrating their significant military capacity.

Within the broader Anizah structure, the Fad'an maintained their own sheikhs, with notable families including Ibn Ghabin (Sheikh of Dana Kahil from Al-Fadaan), Ibn Muhaid (Sheikh of Al-Fadaan), Ibn Quaishish (Sheikh of Al-Kharsa and currently sheikh of Dana Majid from Al-Fadaan), and Ibn Huraymis (Sheikh Al-Aqaqrah from Al-Fadaan). Today, settled Fad'an families are found not only in Saudi Arabia, but also in Kuwait, Jordan, Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Oman, Bahrain, and other regional states, often distinguished by retaining tribal appellations as surnames following the establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the 20th century.

migrationPath.description: Fad'an expansion into Syrian territories as part of broader Anaza tribal migrations, pressuring established tribes like the Hadidiyin

timelineEvents.title: Acquisition of Palmyra toll rights
timelineEvents.description: Following the Mawali tribe's defeat and displacement, the Fad'an were granted rights to collect tolls from caravans traversing through Palmyra, previously held by the Mawali

timelineEvents.title: Conflict with Hasana and Ottoman forces
timelineEvents.description: Large numbers of Fad'an and Siba'a entered plains east of Orontes River, fought coalition of Hasana, Rwala, and Ottoman troops for two weeks before withdrawing

name: al-Farei'ab
history:
Al-Farei'ab (الفريعاب) is a clan within the Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir), one of the Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. They inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in their homeland called Dar al-Manasir, alongside other riverine clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Hamdatiab, and al-'Amasib. The Manasir people are distinct from the Arabian Peninsula Manasir based in the United Arab Emirates, representing instead an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and Arabic as their first language.

According to current Manasir oral tradition, the tribe traces its origins to an ancestor named Mansur, though multiple genealogical theories exist. Some Manasir in Gezira claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), while some historians trace their origin to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla. Another theory suggests they descend from cousins of the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir. Critical research indicates that the current Manasir community, including al-Farei'ab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities.

As a riverain clan, al-Farei'ab pursues small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils along the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop sold on national markets. They share the material culture common to Manasir people, including skills in building mud houses (Galus) and historically floating wood from the Atbarah region. Like other Manasir, they do not have unique tribal marks (Shilukh) but copy the scarification patterns of either upstream or downstream neighboring tribes. The clan was among those affected by the construction of the Merowe Dam, which impacted all villages in Dar al-Manasir, with the Manasir population in the region totaling approximately 33,000 residents and 17,000 non-residents as of 1992-1993.

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Manasir population in Shiri Rural Council recorded as 30,000 residents with additional 17,000 non-resident members

timelineEvents.title: Merowe Dam Impact
timelineEvents.description: Manasir committee published documentation on relocation issues affecting all Dar al-Manasir villages due to dam construction

folkLegends.title: Descent from Mansur bin Qahtan
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.title: Origin from the Kawahla
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: Descendants of al-Hankab migrants
folkLegends.story: They are descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir.

name: al-Ga'al
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through shaykhs and lineage-based authority
description: Al-Ga'al is an Arabian tribe with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally involved in pastoral activities and maintain tribal social structures. The tribe has branches that have settled in various parts of the Gulf region over time. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity with maintained lineage records and traditional leadership.
history:
Al-Ga'al (الجعل) constitutes one of the riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan. The tribe forms part of the Manasir confederation, a complex amalgamation that developed over centuries through voluntary association between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab migrants, and other tribal minorities. According to documented tribal genealogies, al-Ga'al belongs to the broader Sudanese riverine Arab tribal complex centered in the region known as Dar al-Manasir.

Like other Manasir clans, al-Ga'al adopted Islam and Arabic as their primary language while maintaining their distinct tribal identity within the larger Manasir confederation structure. The tribe's integration into this confederation provided them with established systems of mutual rights, obligations, and territorial claims against neighboring tribes, while preserving their specific clan identity. The Manasir confederation includes both riverain clans like al-Ga'al and Bedouin clans, with the riverain groups pursuing small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile.

The al-Ga'al tribe exists within a broader context of Sudanese riverine tribes including the upstream Rubatab and downstream Shaiqiyah. Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including al-Ga'al, should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation throughout centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish systems of mutual respect, rights and obligations, uniting themselves in territorial and resource claims against neighboring tribes.

name: Ga'ali
legitimacyNotes: branch of larger tribal confederation with traditional pastoral territories
description: The Ga'ali are a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula, particularly known for their traditional pastoral lifestyle. They maintained trade partnerships with other tribes including the Manasir, reflecting the interconnected nature of tribal economic networks in the region. Like many smaller tribal groups, they operated within the broader framework of Arabian tribal confederations and alliances.

relations.context: Some Manasir migrate to grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in Ga'ali Country during dry season.

history: The research provided focuses entirely on the Manasir tribe of Northern Sudan, not the Ga'ali tribe of the Arabian Peninsula. The Sudanese Manasir are described as a Sunni Arab riverine tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile, with their homeland called Dar al-Manasir. They trace their origins to multiple possible ancestors including Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or descendants of the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and tribal minorities. The tribe is divided into Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah), with one riverain clan called al-Ga'al mentioned among their subdivisions. During dry seasons, some clans migrate to grazing grounds in 'Ga'ali Country' (الجعليين), suggesting historical connections between these Sudanese Manasir and a Sudanese Ga'ali group. However, this research provides no direct information about the Arabian Peninsula Ga'ali tribe that is the subject of this genealogical entry.

name: Ghafalah
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine Bedouin tribal lineage based on their traditional nomadic lifestyle and territorial claims
traditionalEconomy: selling firewood and charcoal in coastal towns, livestock (700 camels, 1,000 sheep and goats, cattle and donkeys), carrying goods across inland routes
description: The Ghafalah (singular Al Ghafli) is a Bedouin Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates. An entirely Bedouin tribe at the turn of the 20th century, they roamed the Jiri Plain inland of Ras Al Khaimah and particularly Umm Al Quwain, but did not extend their territory into the Hajar Mountains. Numbering about 500 at the turn of the 20th century, their population had dwindled to 197 by 1968. As a Ghafiri tribe, they were close to the Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and often supported them in conflict. They claimed precedence over a number of wells inland, often sharing these with the Khawatir.

relations.context: Close to the Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and often supported them in conflict
Shared wells and frequently allied, particularly in opposing oil exploration in the late 1940s
Allied together when attacked by Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan in 1843
Allied together when attacked by Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan in 1843

history:
The Ghafalah (singular Al Ghafli) is a Bedouin Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates that established themselves as a pastoral nomadic group roaming the Jiri Plain inland of Ras Al Khaimah and particularly Umm Al Quwain. Unlike many interior tribes, they did not extend their dar (roaming territory) into the Hajar Mountains or even their foothills, maintaining their nomadic lifestyle in the desert plains. As a Ghafiri tribe, the Ghafalah were closely aligned with the Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi and frequently supported them in conflicts, representing the complex tribal political alignments that characterized the region.

The tribe became embroiled in significant conflict in 1843 when they found themselves at odds with Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi, ten years after his bloody succession had triggered the 1833 flight of the Al Bu Falasah to found Dubai. Encouraged by Dubai's ruler Maktoum bin Butti bin Suhail, Khalifa launched a war against several interior tribes including the Bani Qitab, Na'im, and Ghafalah. His forces raided across the interior, looting the oasis town of Dhaid and seizing camels. The Ghafalah retaliated against Maktoum in November 1843, prompting him to attack them in turn. Though Maktoum put the Ghafalah to flight, he lost an eye in the conflict. By 1844, Khalifa had established his primacy over the interior tribes.

At the turn of the 20th century, the Ghafalah remained an entirely Bedouin tribe numbering approximately 500 people. However, their population declined dramatically to just 197 people by 1968, living across the five northern Emirates. They sustained themselves through various economic activities including selling firewood and charcoal in coastal towns, managing herds of 700 camels, 1,000 sheep and goats, as well as cattle and donkeys, and carrying goods such as dates across inland trade routes to service coastal merchants. The tribe maintained important water rights, claiming precedence over several wells in the interior, which they often shared with the Khawatir tribe.

The Ghafalah were among the tribal groups that opposed oil exploration in the interior of the Trucial States, particularly during the late 1940s. They frequently allied with the Khawatir tribe in this resistance, together frustrating the coastal Qawasim rulers' attempts to grant oil concessions and provide access to the interior regions. This opposition reflected broader tensions between the nomadic interior tribes and the increasingly modernizing coastal rulers who sought to control and exploit the resources of the hinterland.

timelineEvents.title: Conflict with Khalifa bin Shakhbut
timelineEvents.description: The Ghafalah found themselves at odds with Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi, who went to war against the Bani Qitab, Na'im and Ghafalah

timelineEvents.title: Battle with Maktoum bin Butti
timelineEvents.description: The Ghafalah hit back at Maktoum bin Butti bin Suhail in November, leading to conflict where Maktoum put them to flight but lost his eye in the process

timelineEvents.title: Khalifa's Primacy Established
timelineEvents.description: Khalifa brought together a gathering of interior tribes at Buraimi, establishing his primacy over them including the Ghafalah

timelineEvents.title: Peak Population
timelineEvents.description: At the turn of the 20th century, the Ghafalah numbered about 500 in strength as an entirely Bedouin tribe

timelineEvents.title: Opposition to Oil Exploration
timelineEvents.description: The Ghafalah were among tribes opposed to oil exploration inland of the Trucial States, particularly in the late 1940s, allying with the Khawatir

timelineEvents.title: Population Decline
timelineEvents.description: The Ghafalah population had dwindled to 197 people living in the five northern Emirates

name: Ghamid
legitimacyNotes: Documented blood lineage tracing back through Azd Shanu'ah to Qahtan, with clear genealogical records and tribal presence maintained from pre-Islamic times to present
ancestorStory: The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage back to their forefather Ghamid, who is Amr ibn Ka'b — and some say Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Ka'b — ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr, who is Shanu'ah of the Azd. There are different etymological explanations for the name, with Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i stating it derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning abundant water.
traditionalEconomy: Agriculture and trade (benefited from Arabia Felix region with more rainfall and productive fields)
description: The Ghamd (also transliterated as Ghamid) is an Azd Arab tribe of the Hejaz Region, predominantly Sunni, and considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Ghamid people are thought to be closely related to the neighboring tribe of Zahran. They have maintained their presence in their historical homeland from pre-Islamic times to the present day, and were significantly involved in early Islamic expansion and various historical conflicts.

relations.context: Al Baha is the shared homeland of both Zahran and Ghamid tribes
Closest tribes in lineage, part of Azd Shanu'ah
Closest tribes in lineage, part of Azd Shanu'ah
Border dispute and battle in Ranyah Valley, ended in Ghamid victory
Battle in Bisha in 17th century, later reconciled
Ghamid assisted Khath'am against Banu Al-Harith despite previous problems between them

history:
The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage to Ghamid (Amr ibn Ka'b ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr), who is Shanu'ah of the Azd, belonging to the Qahtani lineage. They are considered one of the oldest and largest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula, with their historical homeland in the Al-Bahah region of southwestern Hejaz. The tribe is closely related to the neighboring Zahran tribe, both being part of Azd Shanu'ah. In pre-Islamic times, when Abraha the Abyssinian marched toward Mecca to destroy the Kaaba, Ghamid confronted and defeated his cavalry forces. The tribe sent two delegations to Prophet Muhammad - first in Mecca under Abi Dhibyan's leadership, and later in Madinah during the 10th year after Hijrah, where they embraced Islam and received written guarantees of their rights.

Throughout the 17th century, Ghamid participated extensively in military campaigns and tribal conflicts. In 1638 CE, they faced an invasion by Zaid bin Mohsen which ended in reconciliation. They engaged in battles against Banu Al-Harith, supported the Sharif of Mecca in various campaigns including the famous battle of Al-Dhafa'a in 1678, and fought alongside Sharif Muhammad Al-Harith against the Dhafir tribes in Al-Qassim. During the 18th-19th centuries, they actively resisted Ottoman-Egyptian forces, notably defeating a Turkish army of twenty thousand under Abdin Bey in the Quraish Valley in 1814. They participated in numerous campaigns alongside various rulers including Imam Faisal bin Saud in 1815, where they helped achieve victory against Turkish-Egyptian forces.

The tribe maintained their presence in their ancestral homeland while many members migrated during Islamic expansions, with portions settling in Iraq, Jordan, Oman, Sudan, Egypt, UAE, and Yemen. Today, many Ghamdis have adopted the surname Al-Ghamdi and have migrated to Saudi Arabia's major metropolitan centers of Riyadh, Jeddah, and Dammam, while maintaining their tribal identity and cultural heritage. The Ghamid people are predominantly Sunni and continue to be recognized as one of the significant tribes that has maintained both its name and tribal presence from pre-Islamic times to the present day.

migrationPath.description: During Islamic expansions, many Ghamid members migrated to Iraq, Jordan, Oman, Sudan, Egypt, UAE, and Yemen while maintaining presence in ancestral homeland
Modern migration to Saudi Arabia's major metropolitan centers while maintaining tribal identity

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Turbah

timelineEvents.description: Battle between Ghamid and Ottoman-Qahtani alliance resulted in 900 Turkish deaths, 300 captured, and Ghamid capturing 4 cannons and rifles

folkLegends.title: The Name Origin According to Ibn al-Kalbi
folkLegends.story: Ibn al-Kalbi said: 'He was named Ghamid because he covered up a matter that occurred between him and his clan, so the tribal leader of Hadur called him Ghamid.'

folkLegends.title: Abraha's Defeat
folkLegends.story: When Abraha the Abyssinian passed by the lands of the Azd tribe, heading towards Mecca to destroy the Kaaba, he sent cavalry to the Azd, but Ghamid confronted them and defeated them. Abd Shams ibn Masruh al-Ghamidi composed poetry commemorating this victory.

nameEtymology: Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i stated that the name derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning 'I covered the well', which means the water in it became abundant, rejecting Ibn al-Kalbi's explanation as weak and unreliable.

name: Ghassanid
legitimacyNotes: Pre-Islamic Arab dynasty claiming descent from Azd, ruled Byzantine client kingdom
ancestorStory: Descendants of Jafnah ibn Amr from the Azd tribe who migrated from Yemen after the collapse of Marib Dam. They established a powerful kingdom in southern Syria as Byzantine clients.
description: The Ghassanids were a prominent pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled a powerful client kingdom under Byzantine patronage in southern Syria and Jordan from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. Originating from the Qahtani Azd tribe of Yemen, they migrated north and became key players in the complex geopolitics between the Byzantine and Sassanian empires. They served as buffers against rival Arab tribes and the Lakhmids, while maintaining Arab cultural identity and eventually converting to Christianity. The dynasty declined with the Islamic conquests in the 7th century, though some branches may have integrated into later tribal structures.

relations.context: They feature prominently in accounts of intertribal warfare, migrations, and alliances, including conflicts involving Rabīʿa and the Lakhmid and Ghassanid client kingdoms
Descended from Qahtani Azd tribal origins

history:
The Ghassanids were an Arab Christian tribal confederation that emerged from the Azd tribe of South Arabia in the 3rd century CE, following a legendary migration northward after the collapse of the Marib Dam. According to Arab genealogical tradition developed during the early Islamic period (7th-10th centuries), they descended from Jafna, son of Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir ibn Haritha ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn Azd, though historian Brian Ulrich notes these genealogical links are historically tenuous. Christian J. Robin's epigraphic research suggests that between the 3rd-4th centuries CE, the Ghassanids first dominated the Hejaz region around Medina before migrating north to Syria, which became their primary base of operations. Their earliest recorded appearance was in 473 CE when their chief Amorkesos signed a treaty with the Byzantine Empire acknowledging their status as foederati controlling parts of Palestine.

Establishing their capital at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, the Ghassanids became the most powerful Arab ally of the Byzantine Empire by the 6th century, serving as a buffer against rival Arab tribes and the Persian-allied Lakhmids. Under rulers like al-Harith ibn Jabalah (r. 529-569), who received the highest imperial titles from Emperor Justinian I, they controlled territory from southern Syria to the northern Hijaz and commanded all Arab confederates of the Byzantine Empire. The Ghassanids were fervent Miaphysite Christians who played crucial roles in church politics, sponsoring the ordination of Jacob Baradaeus and other bishops while maintaining extensive monastic networks. They frequently clashed with the Lakhmid kingdom in proxy wars between the Byzantine and Sassanian empires, and participated in major campaigns during the Byzantine-Sassanian War of 602-628.

The dynasty faced periodic tensions with Byzantium due to theological disputes, including a temporary dissolution of their confederation under Emperor Maurice (582-585), though they were later restored due to military necessity. Their end came with the Islamic conquests - their last phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham led Byzantine forces at the decisive Battle of Yarmuk in 636, and after briefly converting to Islam, ultimately withdrew with his followers to Byzantine Anatolia in 639. Significant Ghassanid remnants remained in Damascus and the Ghouta region, many gradually converting to Islam and serving the Umayyad caliphate as governors, commanders, and scholars. Prominent families like those of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani (d. 750s) and Abu Mushir al-Ghassani (d. 833) continued as respected Islamic scholars into the 9th century, with some Ghassanids playing key roles in supporting the Umayyad restoration during tribal summits at their old capital of Jabiyah.

folkLegends.title: The Scattering of Azd
folkLegends.story: According to the 'Scattering of Azd' story, the Azd tribe migrated northward from South Arabia after the collapse of the Marib Dam, with different groups splitting off in different directions. The Ghassan were one such group that eventually settled within the Roman limes, establishing connections with other Azdi groups including the Ansar tribes of Medina.

nameEtymology: The name derives from their ancestor Jafna according to Arab genealogical tradition, leading to their alternate designation as 'Jafnids' by medieval Arabic authors. Ancient sources record similar tribal names: Kassanitai in Ptolemy's Geography, Casani in Pliny the Elder, Gasandoi in Diodorus Siculus, and Kasandreis in Photios I of Constantinople.

name: Ghassanids
legitimacyNotes: Christian Arab dynasty that ruled as foederati under Byzantine Empire
ancestorStory: Jafnah ibn Amr led the Azd migration from Yemen to the Levant after the collapse of the Marib Dam. His descendants established the Ghassanid kingdom in southern Syria.
description: The Ghassanids were a Christian Arab dynasty of Qahtani origin that ruled a kingdom in southern Syria and Jordan from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. They migrated from Yemen as part of the Azd tribal confederation and became Byzantine foederati, serving as a buffer against Sassanian-allied Lakhmids. The dynasty reached its zenith under al-Harith ibn Jabalah in the 6th century, controlling trade routes and maintaining sophisticated court culture. They were conquered by the Islamic expansion in the 7th century, with remnants scattering across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant.

relations.context: Al Jadila branch became allied with the Ghassanids after migrating to Syria during the Fasad War
Blood relations with Yemenite dynasties that ruled Syria, enabled Tayy expansion northward

history:
The Ghassanids were a Christian Arab tribal confederation that migrated from South Arabia to the Levant in the 3rd century CE as part of the Azd tribal confederation. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Jafna ibn Amr, who led his people northward following the collapse of the Marib Dam. Christian J. Robin's epigraphic studies suggest that between the 3rd and 4th centuries CE, the Ghassanids were the main tribal confederation acting in the Hejaz, with a base possibly stationed at Medina. The period of Ghassanid domination of this region ended when they migrated northwards into Syrian territory, which would remain their main base of operations until Islamic times.

After settling in the Levant, the Ghassanids became clients of the Byzantine Empire, serving as foederati responsible for defending the eastern frontier against Bedouin raids and rival powers. Their capital was established at Jabiyah in the Golan Heights, and their authority extended via tribal alliances with other Azdi tribes all the way to the northern Hijaz as far south as Yathrib (Medina). The Ghassanids frequently fought against the Lakhmid kingdom, a rival Arab polity allied with the Sasanian Empire, and played significant roles in the Roman-Persian conflicts of late antiquity.

The Ghassanids reached their zenith under al-Harith ibn Jabalah (529-569 CE), who received the highest imperial title ever bestowed upon a foreign ruler by Emperor Justinian I and was granted authority over all Arab allies of Byzantium. They were staunch Miaphysite Christians and played a crucial role in reviving the Syrian Miaphysite Church despite Orthodox Byzantine opposition. The confederation was temporarily dissolved under Emperor Maurice (582-585 CE) into its 15 member tribes due to religious and political tensions, but was restored when Maurice realized he could not defend the eastern frontier without Ghassanid assistance.

The dynasty's power ended with the Islamic conquests, suffering decisive defeat at the Battle of Yarmuk in 636 CE under their last phylarch Jabala ibn al-Ayham. After supposedly embracing Islam, Jabala left the faith and ultimately withdrew with his tribesmen to Byzantine-held Anatolia in 639. However, significant remnants remained in Syria, residing in Damascus and the Ghouta countryside. Many gradually converted to Islam and served the Umayyad Caliphate as governors, commanders, scribes, and court officials. During the Second Muslim Civil War in 684, the Ghassan supported continued Umayyad rule and helped nominate Marwan I as caliph during a summit at their old capital of Jabiyah. Two prominent Damascene Ghassanid scholarly families, those of Yahya ibn Yahya al-Ghassani and Abu Mushir al-Ghassani, achieved prominence in early Islamic Syria as hadith scholars and preservers of Syrian administrative history.

name: Ghatafan
legitimacyNotes: Ancient pre-Islamic tribal confederation with established genealogical legitimacy through Adnanite lineage
ancestorName: Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays Aylan
ancestorStory: Ghatafan was descended from Qays Aylan through Sa'd, forming one of the major tribal confederations of northern Arabia. The tribe was known for its warrior culture and extensive grazing territories in the Najd region.
description: Ghatafan was a major pre-Islamic Adnanite tribal confederation based in the Najd region of central Arabia, particularly around the areas of Khaybar and northeastern Hijaz. They were known as fierce warriors and skilled poets, playing significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal conflicts including the famous Dahis and Ghabra wars. The confederation included several important sub-tribes and was involved in early Islamic history, with some branches initially opposing then joining the Muslim community. While historically significant in central Arabian politics, their direct influence in modern GCC states is primarily through descendant lineages that merged into other tribal structures.

relations.context: Both trace Adnanite origins through Qays Aylan lineage
Ghatafan descends from Adnan through Qays Aylan branch

history:
The Ghatafan (غطفان) were a major pre-Islamic Adnanite tribal confederation descended from Ghaṭafān ibn Saʾd ibn Qays ʿAylān, making them part of the larger Qays tribe. They inhabited the Wadi al-Rumma area of Najd between the Hejaz mountains and Jabal Shammar, with territory extending northeast of Medina. The confederation was divided into several main branches: the Banu Abs (descendants of ʿAbs ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān) in the easternmost territory, the Banu Dhubyan (from Dhubyān ibn Baghīd ibn Rayth ibn Ghaṭafān) which included the major subtribes Banu Murra, Banu Fazara, and Tha'laba, the Banu Ashja in the westernmost area, and the Anmar who inhabited the Qassim region of Najd. The Ghatafan were recognized as one of the 'Great Skulls of Arabia' - the most powerful and influential pre-Islamic tribal confederations, with descendants later found throughout Arabia and the Maghreb.

During the early Islamic period, Ghatafan played a significant role in conflicts with Muhammad's expanding Muslim community. They participated in several military engagements against the Muslims, including the Invasion of Dhi Amr in September 624 CE, the Expedition of Dhat al-Riqa, and the Invasion of Dumatul Jandal in July 626. Most notably, they allied with the Quraysh and other confederate tribes in the Battle of the Trench (627 CE), where they formed part of a coalition army estimated at 7,500-10,000 men. The Banu Nadir had successfully enlisted Ghatafan support by paying them half of their harvest from Khaybar, securing approximately 2,000 men and 300 horsemen led by Unaina bin Hasan Fazari.

The Ghatafan's most controversial involvement came during the Battle of Khaybar in 628 CE, where they had been contracted by the Banu Nadir to provide 2,000 men and 300 horsemen to defend the Jewish settlement against Muslim forces. However, during the actual battle, the Ghatafan forces failed to provide the promised reinforcements. Various accounts suggest they were either bought off by Muslim negotiators or turned back due to rumors that Muslims were attacking their own strongholds. This failure to honor their commitment to Khaybar marked a significant moment in their relations with both Jewish tribes and the emerging Muslim state.

In the 6th century, the legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab of the Banu Kalb tribe destroyed a Ghatafan sanctuary called 'Buss' that had been constructed to rival the Ka'aba in Mecca. This event demonstrates the significant religious and political influence Ghatafan wielded in pre-Islamic Arabia, as their sanctuary was considered important enough to challenge Meccan religious supremacy. According to different accounts, Zuhayr's destruction of the sanctuary was motivated either by tribal rivalry - viewing the Ghatafani sanctuary as an assertion of superiority over his own Kalb tribe - or as a favor to the Quraysh of Mecca to eliminate religious competition.

migrationPath.description: Ghatafan warriors joined Islamic conquest of Iraq under various commanders

folkLegends.title: The Sanctuary of Buss
folkLegends.story: The Ghatafan tribe constructed a religious sanctuary called 'Buss' to rival the Ka'aba in Mecca. The legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab of the Banu Kalb became enraged when he learned of this, exclaiming 'By God, that will never happen while I am alive! I will never leave Ghatafan alone while they take a haram.' He gathered his Kalbi tribesmen, raided and defeated the Ghatafan in their dwelling place, and destroyed their sanctuary. Afterward, 'he acted graciously toward Ghatafan', releasing their women and cattle.
folkLegends.source: Arab historical traditions recorded by Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani

nameEtymology: The etymology or meaning of Ghatafan is not known according to Arab genealogical sources

name: Grusha
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in northern Arabian Peninsula
description: Al Grusha (Qurosha) is a historical tribal group with roots in the northern Arabian Peninsula. They were traditionally involved in nomadic pastoralism and had connections to the broader tribal networks of the region. Historical records suggest they were part of the complex tribal movements that characterized pre-modern Arabia. Their current status as an active tribal entity in the GCC region is unclear from available historical sources.
history:
Al Grusha (Qurosha) is one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, alongside the Al Humaid, Jubur, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood clans. The Bani Khalid confederation's genealogical origins are disputed among Arab genealogists, with various theories suggesting descent from Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe, or relatives of Khalid ibn al-Walid from Banu Makhzum of Quraysh, though the direct descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid himself has been questioned due to claims that his lineage became extinct.

As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, the Grusha participated in the political dominance of Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The confederation was traditionally led by the Al Humaid clan, and under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of Al Humaid, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif in 1670, establishing their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The Grusha, like other Bani Khalid branches, were involved in the confederation's territorial control that extended from the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif oases during the 16th and 17th centuries.

The Grusha participated in the Bani Khalid's conflicts with the emerging Saudi-Wahhabi alliance in the 18th century. The confederation opposed Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab's movement and attempted to prevent Saudi expansion by ordering the emir of Uyayna to expel Ibn Abd al-Wahhab, leading to his relocation to Dir'iyyah where he joined forces with the Al Saud. The Bani Khalid's efforts to invade Najd and Diriyyah failed, and the Saudis ultimately invaded their domain in Al-Hasa, deposing the Al 'Ura'yir rulers in 1793.

Following the collapse of Bani Khalid power in the early 19th century after defeats by tribal alliances including the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, many members of the confederation, including the Grusha, faced territorial encroachment and internal leadership disputes. The confederation was no longer the potent military force it once was, and many members dispersed throughout the region or settled in Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, and other areas. The Ottomans briefly appointed a Bani Khalid governor over Al-Hasa in 1874, but this rule was short-lived.

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

name: Guzayah
legitimacyNotes: Recognized tribal lineage with traditional authority structures
description: Al-Ghaziya is a tribal group with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records exist regarding their specific territorial claims or major political activities. They appear to have been part of the broader tribal landscape of pre-modern Arabia. Their current status and activities in the modern GCC states require further historical documentation.
history: The Guzayah represents a crucial ancestral branch in Arabian tribal genealogy, serving as the direct ancestor of the major Otaibah tribe. According to 8th-century historian Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's manuscript 'The Great Ancestry', verified in 1988, Guzayah was the son of Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, placing the tribe within the broader Hawazin confederacy that traces back through Qays ʿAylān to the Adnanite lineage. Al-Kalbi's work 'The Abundance of Kinship' specifically identifies Guzayah as one of three sons of Jusham (alongside Oday and Ouseema), and notes that 'The sons of Guzayah are Juda'aa, Hami, Otaibah, and Outwara.' This genealogical position makes Guzayah the father of Otaibah, whose descendants would become one of the largest Arabian tribes. The Guzayah lineage extends back through the classical Arabian genealogical chain: Hawāzin, Manṣūr, ʿIkrima, Khasafa, Qays ʿAylān, Mudar, Nizar, Ma'add, and ultimately to Adnan of the Ishmaelites, following the Abrahamic tradition that Arabs are descendants of Abraham through his son Ishmael. The Hawazin confederacy, of which Guzayah was part, was among the major tribal groupings of pre-Islamic Arabia mentioned in the research as having descendants throughout Arabia, Libya, Algeria, Morocco, Sudan, and Iraq. While Guzayah itself appears to have been absorbed into or superseded by its descendant tribes, particularly the Otaibah, its genealogical significance remained preserved in medieval Arabic historical sources. The tribe's legacy continued through its descendants who would play major roles in Arabian Peninsula history, from pre-Islamic times through the formation of modern Saudi Arabia, as the Otaibah tribe became one of the most prominent and numerous tribes in the region.

name: Habus
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine blood lineage tribe with territorial base and distinct cultural identity, speaking Shehhi dialect with ancient Himyarite origins
ancestorStory: Their name is said to be derived from the location of their original holdings of land on the Jebel Hibs, a mountain in Ras Al Khaimah
traditionalEconomy: agriculture and trade - cultivating wheat and dates, trading agricultural products, honey, livestock and dairy products
description: The Habus is an Arab tribe primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. They are an agrarian mountain people who cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The tribe is known for speaking the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic with Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. Fiercely independent, they successfully resisted taxation attempts and maintained semi-autonomous status well into the 1960s. Their name is derived from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain.

relations.context: Frequent neighbors sharing cultural similarities and traditions, with common intermarriage especially with Al Haramsha of Bani Idaid Shihuh
Associated as fellow Hajar Mountain tribes with shared cultural similarities
Habus gradually bought up Naqbiyin territory in Wadi Naqab after the Naqbiyin fell foul of local rulers around 1695-1740
Closely allied tribe that settled the wadis and farms to the south of Wadi Bih while Shihuh inhabited mountains to the north

history: The Habus is an Arab tribe of Qahtani lineage primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. Their name derives from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain in Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe speaks the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic, which has Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE, shared with neighboring Shihuh and Dhahuriyiin tribes. As an agrarian mountain people, the Habus cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The area around Wadi Naqab was originally held by the Naqbiyin tribe, but was gradually acquired by the Habus after the Naqbiyin fell out of favor with local rulers in the pre-Qawasim period (1695-1740), with many Naqbiyin relocating to Dibba and Khor Fakkan. By 1800, Wadi Naqab was considered Habus territory. The tribe is also associated with Wadi Sal and other mountain settlements. Fiercely independent, the Habus successfully resisted taxation attempts by Sheikh Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi, ruler of Ras Al Khaimah (1921-1948), leading to a brief conflict and subsequent agreement between Habus Sheikh Hamdan bin Malik and Sultan bin Salim. A copy of their peace agreement was lodged with Sultan Qaboos of Oman upon Hamdan's death. In 1951, the Habus became involved in territorial disputes when the Wali of Bukha claimed their areas as Omani territory. They initially accepted this but later agreed to Ras Al Khaimah rule under Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi in 1955, though continued resistance into the 1960s included holding up mineral survey parties and attempting to assert independence in 1963. The tribe traded wheat, honey, goats, dairy products and firewood in Ras Al Khaimah town, purchasing metal tools, coffee, salt and clothing. They expanded their date plantations around Khatt in the 1950s-1960s through purchases and rentals. Though many mountain settlements are now abandoned, younger tribe members have returned to build summer houses, and a tribal festival established in 2010 showcases their traditions, handicrafts and perfumery.

name: Haḍramaut
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation with historical kingdoms and established genealogical lineages
ancestorStory: Haḍramaut was the son of Qahtan, the legendary ancestor of the southern Arabian tribes. The tribe established one of the most prosperous ancient kingdoms in southern Arabia.
description: Haḍramaut is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation based in the Hadhramaut valley of southern Yemen and eastern Arabia. They established one of the great pre-Islamic kingdoms, controlling the lucrative frankincense trade routes between India and the Mediterranean. The tribe produced numerous merchant families who spread throughout the Indian Ocean, establishing trading communities from East Africa to Southeast Asia. Many Hadhrami families later settled in the Gulf states, particularly in the UAE and Oman, where they remain influential in commerce.

relations.context: Conquered by Himyar c. 300 CE after Haḍramaut had previously conquered Qataban
Al Kathiri are a major branch of the Hadhrami confederation

history: Haḍramaut is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation that established one of the most powerful pre-Islamic kingdoms in southern Arabia, based in the Hadhramaut valley of what is now eastern Yemen. According to the research, the Himyarite Kingdom conquered Haḍramaut around 300 CE, indicating that the Hadhramaut kingdom was a significant independent polity that controlled substantial territory in the early centuries CE. The kingdom was later conquered by Qataban in the early 2nd century AD before ultimately falling under Himyarite control in the 4th century. The Haḍramaut region formed part of the broader South Arabian political landscape that included the kingdoms of Saba', Qataban, and Himyar, with these polities engaging in complex patterns of conquest and reconquest over several centuries. By 300 CE, when Himyar achieved final dominance over southern Arabia, Haḍramaut had been incorporated into this unified South Arabian state. The tribal confederation's territory encompassed the fertile Hadhramaut valley and surrounding areas of eastern Yemen, which provided agricultural resources and access to important trade routes. Following the Islamic conquests, descendants of the Haḍramaut tribes continued to play important roles in the broader Islamic world, particularly in trade and scholarship. The region's strategic location between the Arabian Peninsula and the Indian Ocean made it a crucial link in maritime trade networks connecting East Africa, India, and the Mediterranean world.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic period migration of Hadhrami traders to Omani ports for maritime commerce
Medieval expansion to control Persian Gulf trade routes
Modern migration establishing major merchant families in emerging Gulf trading centers

name: Hafaitat
legitimacyNotes: Branch of larger Huwaitat confederation with traditional territorial claims
ancestorStory: Hafaitat are descended from Hafeet, a branch of the larger Huwaitat tribal confederation. They trace their lineage through the Judhami branch of Quda'a.
description: The Hafaitat are a tribal branch of the larger Huwaitat confederation, traditionally based in the northern Arabian Peninsula and southern Jordan. They are known for their role in controlling trade routes between the Arabian interior and the Levant. The tribe maintained semi-nomadic lifestyle with camel herding as their primary economic activity. Today they remain active with members distributed across Saudi Arabia and Jordan.

relations.context: Hafaitat are a branch of the larger Huwaitat confederation

history: Based on the research provided, there appears to be confusion between two distinct tribal entities both named Hafaitat. The existing data conflates information from different geographical regions and tribal contexts. One reference describes Hafaitat as a branch of the Huwaitat confederation in the northern Arabian Peninsula and southern Jordan, tracing lineage through the Judhami branch of Quda'a with Qahtani roots and a pre-Islamic founding era. However, the historical section describes a completely different Hafaitat - one that is a major tribal section of the Sharqiyin tribe along the East Coast of the Trucial States (modern UAE), aligned with the Ghafiri confederation rather than having Qahtani origins. The Sharqiyin Hafaitat were one of three notable sections alongside the Yammahi and Hamudiyin, with the Al Sharqi ruling family of Fujairah deriving from this Hafaitat section. The Sharqiyin confederation, numbering around 7,000 by the early 20th century, settled extensively along the East Coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in Wadi Ham and Jiri plain, with Jabal Yibir territory historically populated mainly by the Hafaitat and Yammahi sections. The Sharqiyin Hafaitat played a central role in Fujairah's independence struggle, with Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi leading multiple insurrections against Sharjah's control beginning in 1879. After years of rebellions and conflicts, the Sharqiyin successfully declared independence from Sharjah in 1901, establishing the independent emirate of Fujairah under Hamad bin Abdullah, though British recognition only came in 1952. The Al Sharqi ruling family, descended from the Hafaitat section, has governed Fujairah continuously since 1879. The research provided does not contain sufficient information to definitively resolve which Hafaitat entity is being referenced, or whether these represent two distinct tribes sharing the same name but with different origins and geographical distributions.

name: al-Hamamir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on Qahtani lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from the ancient Himyarite kingdom of Yemen, tracing lineage to the Qahtani Arab tribes. The tribe migrated northward from Yemen in pre-Islamic times.
description: Al-Hamamir is a Qahtani tribe with ancient roots tracing back to the Himyarite kingdom of Yemen. They are part of the broader network of southern Arabian tribes that migrated northward into the Arabian Peninsula in pre-Islamic times. The tribe has maintained its tribal identity and structure throughout Islamic history, with branches found across the Arabian Peninsula. Today they remain an active tribal entity with members distributed across various Gulf states.

history: Based on the research provided, al-Hamamir (الحمامير) appears exclusively as one of the Bedouin clans within the Sudanese Manasir tribe, specifically part of the Manasir al-Badiyah (مناصير البادية) - the desert-dwelling branch of the Manasir people of Northern Sudan. The al-Hamamir clan shares grazing grounds in the Abisba' (ابسباع) and Sani (سانى) areas alongside three other Bedouin Manasir clans: al-Khabra (الخبرا), al-Muleikab (المليكاب), and al-Kagbab (الكجباب). During dry seasons, these Bedouin clans including al-Hamamir migrate to various desert territories - some moving west to Kababish tribal lands (Khala' Kabushiyah), others to Wad Hamid grazing grounds in Ga'ali country, or to Rubatab territory. The broader Manasir people, according to multiple genealogical traditions, trace their origins either to Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla lineage, or to descendants of a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts. Critical scholarship suggests the current Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities who established common genealogical recognition for mutual rights and territorial claims. The research makes no mention of any connection between this Sudanese al-Hamamir clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribe of the same name, indicating they are entirely separate entities sharing only a name.

name: Hamdan
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani confederation with established genealogical lineage and traditional leadership structure
ancestorName: Hamdan ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Awsalah ibn Rabi'ah ibn al-Khiyar ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan
ancestorStory: Descended from Hamdan ibn Malik, a prominent figure in the Qahtani lineage. The confederation formed in pre-Islamic times in the highlands of Yemen.
description: Hamdan is a major Qahtani tribal confederation historically based in the northern highlands of Yemen, particularly around Sanaa and Sa'ada. They played significant roles in early Islamic conquests and Yemen's medieval history. Some branches migrated to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula over the centuries. While primarily Yemen-based, they maintain connections with Gulf Qahtani tribes through shared lineage and historical alliances.

history: Hamdan is an ancient and prominent Arab tribal confederation with deep roots in northern Yemen, first mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions dating to the 7th century BCE. The confederation stems from the eponymous ancestor Awsala (nicknamed Hamdan), whose lineage is traced through the Qahtani line to Kahlan and ultimately to Saba. In ancient Musnad texts, Hamdan was referred to as 'Ardam Hamdan,' meaning 'the land of Hamdan,' indicating their territorial control in the northern highlands extending from Sanaa toward Marib, Najran, Saada, and the Red Sea coast. From the 4th century BCE, Hamdan kings began controlling the Kingdom of Sheba, with leadership alternating between the two main branches: Hashid (first mentioned as 'Hashdum' in 4th century BCE texts) and Bakil (referenced from 6th century BCE). Yarim Ayman of Hashid monopolized the Sabaean kingdom around the 2nd century BCE, before power shifted to Bakil in the 1st century BCE. Bakil's rule ended when the Himyarites under Dhamar Ali Yahbar I triumphed in 100 CE. During the Islamic period, Hamdan played crucial roles in early Muslim history. In 631 CE, Hamdan delegations led by poet Malik ibn Namat and prince Abu Thawr Dhu'l-Mashar met Prophet Muhammad seeking alliance. More Hamdani tribesmen converted during Ali ibn Abi Talib's expedition to Yemen in 631-632, with Muhammad appointing a Bakil tribesman as his deputy over Hamdan. The tribe remained loyal during the Ridda wars following Muhammad's death in 632. Hamdan soldiers who settled in Kufa became fervent supporters of Ali during his caliphate (656-661), contributing 12,000 men to Ali's army at the Battle of Siffin in 657 under their leader Sa'id ibn Qays al-Hamdani. This close association with Ali led Hamdan to embrace Shi'a Islam, initially following Isma'ilism before becoming supporters of Zaydism. The Banu Yam branch of Hamdan settled north of Bakil in Najran and established the Isma'ili Hamdanid dynasty at Sanaa in the 12th century, later shifting toward Zaydi acceptance. Today, Hamdan comprises over one million members across Hashid and Bakil confederations, historically known as 'the wings of the Zaydite imamate' while maintaining wide-ranging autonomy. They continue to dominate northern Yemen from Sanaa to Saada, wielding significant political influence since the 1962 revolution despite the confederation's internal diversity and periodic shifts in tribal loyalties.

name: al-Hamdatiab
history: The al-Hamdatiab are one of the riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, specifically noted in tribal records to have Shaiqi origins, distinguishing them from other Manasir sub-groups. They inhabit the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in what is collectively known as Dar al-Manasir, alongside nine other riverain clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Farei'ab, and al-'Amasib. The broader Manasir people constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan, representing an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and Arabic as their first language. Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including al-Hamdatiab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in adjoining areas. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish a system of mutual respect, rights and obligations, thereby uniting themselves in their claims for land and other resources against neighboring tribes. The riverain Manasir, including al-Hamdatiab, pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with their most important cash crop being a wide variety of dates sold on the national market. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses (Galus) and historically used to float wood from the Atbarah region. The total Manasir population was estimated at approximately 30,000-33,000 in the 1990s, though this figure encompasses all Manasir clans collectively rather than al-Hamdatiab specifically.

name: al-Hamsab
history:
The al-Hamsab (الهامزاب) are one of the Riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in their homeland of Dar al-Manasir. They are part of the broader Manasir tribal confederation, which consists of both riverine and Bedouin divisions. The Manasir people trace their origins to a historical ancestor named Mansur, though multiple genealogical accounts exist within their oral tradition regarding this lineage.

According to Manasir oral traditions documented by their own relocation committee, several competing origin stories persist: some Manasir in Gezira claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniyin, others trace their ancestry to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla tribe, while still others maintain they descend from the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab through Mansur as a descendant of King Sabir, having migrated after internal conflicts. Some contemporary oral tradition also connects Mansur to the lineage of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Prophet Muhammad.

Critical historical research suggests that the current Manasir community, including the al-Hamsab clan, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities. Early travelers documented Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and their burial customs show strong similarities to Nubian practices, indicating significant indigenous cultural continuity despite Arab genealogical claims.

The al-Hamsab, like other Riverain Manasir clans, pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop sold on national markets. They are also known for their skill in building traditional mud houses (Galus). Their population, along with all other Dar al-Manasir villages, was significantly affected by the Merowe Dam project, which necessitated relocation from their traditional homeland along the Fourth Cataract.

description: The Hammudids were a Hashemite dynasty that ruled parts of Al-Andalus (Islamic Spain) during the 11th century taifa period. They claimed descent from the Prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib and established caliphates in Málaga and Algeciras. While primarily associated with Iberian history, some branches of Hashemite families later dispersed across the Islamic world including the Arabian Peninsula. Their historical significance lies in their role during the fragmentation of the Umayyad Caliphate in Al-Andalus.

history: The Hamudiyin are a distinct section of the Sharqiyin tribe of the United Arab Emirates, historically concentrated along the eastern coast in areas including Wadi Sharm and Wadi Zikt in what is now Fujairah Emirate. As part of the broader Sharqiyin confederation, they were traditionally aligned with the Ghafiri tribal faction and were among the dominant groups along the Trucial States' eastern coastline. The Sharqiyin, including the Hamudiyin section, were originally dependencies of Sharjah but engaged in numerous rebellions throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries seeking independence. Under leaders like Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi of the Hafaitat section, they successfully resisted Sharjah's control, culminating in practical independence from 1901 and eventual British recognition of Fujairah as a separate Trucial State in 1952. The Hamudiyin subsisted primarily through agriculture, pearling, and fishing, living in barasti palm frond houses as late as the 1960s. Historical records from 1908 describe Hamudiyin settlements like Sharm with 40 houses and Dadna with 50 houses, complete with detailed accounts of their livestock and date palm cultivation along the wadis. The area of Wadi Zikt was populated mainly by the Sharqiyin tribe, corresponding to the tribal section of Hamudiyin, while the Wadi Sharm area was also mainly populated by the Sharqiyin tribe, specifically the Hamudiyin section. By 1968, a census showed 90% of the tribal population of Fujairah was Sharqiyin, making them the dominant tribal group in the emirate.
