history: The Harb tribe is an Arab tribe of Qahtanite origin tracing their lineage to Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan. The tribe dates to the 2nd century of the Islamic calendar (8th century CE), when Qahtani tribes emigrated from southern Arabia to Hejaz around 131 AH (748-749 CE) seeking water and land after conflicts with their cousins Banu Ar-Rabi'ah bin Saad. This migration sparked territorial conflicts with established Adnani Arab tribes including Juhaynah, Anazzah, Muzaynah, and Sulaym over land and water resources, with the research noting that those who remained in Arabia were largely absorbed by the Banu Harb of Yemen beginning in the 9th century. After approximately three centuries of settlement in Hejaz, the Harb tribe established dominance in the heart of Hejaz with territories surrounding the holy city of Medina. Their traditional lands extend from the Red Sea coast in Tihamah to the heart of Najd in central Saudi Arabia, and from Madinah in the north to Al Qunfudhah in the south. The tribe is organized into two main sections: Masrooh and Banu Salim, with Banu Salim further divided into Maymoon and Al-Marawihah subsections. In the 21st century, Harb has evolved into a federation of tribes and families, with many members migrating to major Saudi metropolitan centers including Riyadh, Jeddah, Al-Qassim, and Dammam. Beyond the Arabian Peninsula, significant Harb populations established themselves in Lebanon's Bekaa Valley during the 7th century, becoming one of the most prominent Arab tribes there, particularly around Baalbek and Zahle. Other branches spread to Jordan, where the Mahamid from Harb formed an emirate in Karak governorate, and to Palestine, Sudan, and Maghreb countries through various migrations and alliances.

They are historically found across various Gulf regions, particularly in areas where Hasanid families settled. The tribe has maintained their status through religious scholarship and participation in trade networks. They remain active today as part of the broader network of Hasanid descendants in the GCC region.

history: The Hasana tribe represents a branch of the Anaza tribal confederation, originating from the broader Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. The Aniza confederation's genealogical roots trace back to pre-Islamic Arabia, with the classical Arab genealogists placing them within the large Rabiʿa tribal structure. The Hasana became historically significant as the first Anaza tribe to migrate from their traditional territories in Najd to Syria, arriving in 1757 and establishing themselves in the plains east of Hama and Homs. Under their leader Muhanna al-Fadil, the Hasana drove out the long-established Mawali tribe from their traditional pasture grounds by the late 1780s, becoming the dominant Bedouin force in central Syria. The tribe gained Ottoman government sanction to collect tolls from caravans on the Palmyra trade routes and collected tribute (khuwwa) from weaker tribes and villages in the region. Despite their dominance, the Hasana faced territorial challenges in the early 19th century from other Anaza branches, particularly the Fad'an and their subordinates the Siba'a, leading to significant conflicts over control of the plains east of the Orontes River. During the French Mandate period (1920-1946), the Hasana leadership under Trad al-Milhim received substantial landholdings, with some twenty villages registered in his name during the 1930s, though this made him dependent on government support and diminished his traditional tribal authority. Following Syrian independence, the tribe continued to inhabit villages east of Homs and Hama with their headquarters in Buwaydah, adapting to the modern political system through parliamentary representation, with the al-Milhim family gaining seats in 1977 and 1994 under Hafez al-Assad's government and maintaining loyalty to the Syrian government throughout the Syrian civil war.

name: Hashid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal confederation leadership and historical territorial control in northern Yemen
ancestorStory: Descended from Hashid ibn Jusham ibn Khayran, considered one of the major tribal ancestors of the Bakil-Hashid confederation. The tribe traces its lineage to the ancient Himyarite and Sabaean kingdoms.
description: Hashid is one of Yemen's largest and most influential tribal confederations, historically based in the northern highlands around Sanaa. Together with their traditional rivals Bakil, they form the backbone of Yemen's tribal structure and have been described as 'the two wings of Yemen.' The confederation has played a crucial role in Yemeni politics throughout history, including significant influence during the Imamate period and modern republican era. While primarily Yemeni-based, some Hashid sections have historical connections to southern Arabian Peninsula regions.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani tribal origins

history:
The Hashid is one of Yemen's most ancient and influential tribal confederations, with origins tracing back over two millennia to the Sabaean period. First mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions as 'Hashdum' in the 4th century BCE, the tribe was part of the Sum'ay confederation alongside Humlan and Yarsum tribes, worshipping the god Ta'lab. According to medieval Yemeni genealogies, Hashid and their traditional rivals Bakil were sons of Jashim bin Jubran bin Nawf, descended from the ancient Hamdan tribe. The Banu Hamdan served as qayls (chiefs) of Hashid in Sabaean times and later acquired control over parts of Bakil, giving their name to the broader Hamdan confederation that included both Hashid and Bakil tribes. In the late 3rd century, the Hamdan confederation switched allegiance to the Himyarite kingdom.

The tribe's conversion to Islam came in 622 CE when Muhammad sent Ali ibn Abi Talib to Yemen after Khalid ibn al-Walid's unsuccessful mission. Unlike many Arabian tribes, the Hamdan remained loyal to Islam after Muhammad's death and did not join the ridda (apostasy) movement. They maintained allegiance to Ali ibn Abi Talib throughout his conflicts with the Umayyads, and by the 10th century had invited Imam al-Hadi Yahya bin al-Hussain to establish Zaidi rule in northern Yemen at Sa'da in 893-897 CE. This established the Hashid and Bakil as the 'two wings' of the Zaidi imamate, a relationship that would define their political role for centuries. Throughout Islamic history, Hashid warriors participated in major conquests and battles, with prominent figures like Saeed bin Qais Al-Hamdani carrying the Hamdan banner in the battles of the Camel and Siffin.

The confederation played crucial roles in Yemen's medieval politics, supporting various Zaidi imams while maintaining significant autonomy in their northern highland territories around Sanaa. In modern times, the Hashid confederation was dominated for decades by the powerful al-Ahmar family, particularly Sheikh Abdullah ibn Husayn al-Ahmar who formed a crucial alliance with President Ali Abdullah Saleh from 1978 until his death in 2007. This alliance made al-Ahmar Yemen's second most powerful figure as Speaker of Parliament. However, following the Arab Spring in 2011, his sons led the Hashid in an insurgency against Saleh, playing an essential role in the revolution that forced the president to step down in 2012. The subsequent conflict with the Houthis and loss of Saudi support led to internal divisions within the confederation, with many Hashid tribes reaching separate peace agreements or switching sides, marking a significant decline in their unified political influence.

name: Hawamil
legitimacyNotes: No information found about Hawamil tribe in the provided text about Hawaii, the U.S. state
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: <UNKNOWN>
history:
The Hawamil are documented as one of the constituent sections of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, appearing in contemporary records as Al Hameli/Hawamil (الهوامل/الهاملي), with the family name Al Hameli representing them in modern UAE society. According to historical sources cited by British colonial administrators, the Hawamil were reported to have originally been part of the Al Ali tribe before their incorporation into the Bani Yas confederation, though the specific circumstances and timing of this integration are not documented in available sources.

As part of the broader Bani Yas confederation, the Hawamil participated in the tribal movement from the Najd region of central Arabia to the coastal regions of what is now the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Yas, including the Hawamil section, initially settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan. Following the discovery of fresh water on Abu Dhabi island in 1793, the confederation migrated there, with the town of Abu Dhabi being established in 1795.

The Hawamil, as part of the Bani Yas confederation, experienced the demographic changes that affected the broader tribal group throughout the twentieth century. The total Bani Yas population, which Lorimer estimated at around 12,000 in the early 1900s, declined to approximately 8,000 by the early 1950s and further to 5,884 by the 1968 Abu Dhabi census. This decline was attributed to emigration during the late 1950s when many families moved to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia seeking employment in oil and construction sectors.

Contemporary leading members of the Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi identify more than 20 sections as forming part of the confederation, with the Hawamil being recognized as one of these constituent groups. The historical accounts of the confederation's composition have varied among different observers, reflecting shifting political relationships as associated tribes sometimes sought full integration under the authority of the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, while changing regional dynamics at other times encouraged assertions of autonomy.

name: Hawazin
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Adnani confederation with documented pre-Islamic nobility and descent from Hawazin ibn Mansur
ancestorStory: Hawazin was descended from Mansur ibn Ikrima, establishing one of the major Adnani tribal confederations. The tribe became renowned for their eloquent Arabic speech and fierce independence.
description: Hawazin was one of the most powerful pre-Islamic tribal confederations of central Arabia, centered in the Najd and eastern Hijaz regions. They were famous for their linguistic prowess and provided wet nurses to noble families, including the Prophet Muhammad's nurse Halima. The confederation was decisively defeated by Muslim forces at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE, after which many branches scattered and integrated into other tribal groups. Several modern Gulf tribes trace partial descent from Hawazin sub-tribes.

history: The Hawazin was a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation originally based in western Najd and around Ta'if in the Hejaz, forming part of the larger Qays tribal group. According to traditional genealogy, they descended from Hawazin ibn Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khasafa ibn Qays Aylan ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them Adnanite Arabs. The confederation consisted of subtribes including Banu Sa'd, Banu Jusham (known collectively as 'Ujz Hawazin or 'the rear of Hawazin'), and the powerful Banu Thaqif and Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, which were often counted separately. Beginning around 550 CE, the Hawazin became vassals of the Banu 'Abs of Ghatafan under chieftain Zuhayr ibn Jadhima, but discontinued tribute after his death, leading to sporadic conflicts with Ghatafan. During the late 6th-century Fijar War, the Hawazin fought alongside much of the Qays confederation against the Quraysh and Kinana tribes over four years, achieving several victories including at Nakhla, Shamta, and Ukaz. The Hawazin had minimal contact with the Prophet Muhammad until 630 CE, though his wet nurse Halima bint Abu Dhu'ayb came from their Banu Sa'd subtribe. After Muhammad's conquest of Mecca, Malik ibn Awf of Banu Nasr mobilized Hawazin and Thaqif forces, but they were decisively defeated at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE. Many Hawazin were captured and their property seized, though Muhammad reconciled with them by returning captives and recognizing Malik's chieftainship after his conversion. Following Muhammad's death in 632, the Hawazin joined the Ridda Wars against the early Muslim state but eventually returned to Islam. After their defeat, many branches scattered and migrated to various regions including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia. Modern tribes including the Otaibah of Saudi Arabia claim descent from Hawazin lineages, with the Otaibah specifically tracing ancestry through Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin according to Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century genealogical work.

migrationPath.description: Post-Ridda Wars dispersal of Hawazin clans to Syria and surrounding regions
Migration during early Islamic conquests, with Hawazin participating in conquest and settlement of Mesopotamia

name: Himyar
legitimacyNotes: Originally a confederation of tribes that formed a unified kingdom; ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe after independence from Qataban
traditionalEconomy: international trade in frankincense, myrrh, and ivory; hub linking Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa
description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

relations.context: In the pre-Islamic era, Mudar was usually politically dominated by Himyar, until the collapse of the latter

history:
Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity that dominated the southern highlands of Yemen from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe, with their capital at the ancient city of Zafar near modern Sanaa. The kingdom was fundamentally a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Pliny the Elder described it as one of 'the richest nations in the world.' The Himyarites systematically conquered neighboring realms, taking Saba' around 25 BCE (definitively around 280 CE), Qataban around 200 CE, and Haḍramaut around 300 CE, thereby uniting southern Arabia for the first time.

The kingdom underwent dramatic religious transformations that defined its later history. Originally practicing South Arabian polytheism with deities like Wadd, ʿAthtar, 'Amm, and Almaqah, the Himyarites converted to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin (c. 375–400 CE), making it a unique Jewish kingdom in Arabia. This conversion was possibly motivated by a desire to distance themselves from the Christianizing Roman Empire. The Jewish period reached its climax under Dhu Nuwas, who persecuted Christians in Najran, leading to the Aksumite invasion of 530 CE that ended Jewish rule. Christianity then became the official religion under Aksumite rule until the Sasanian conquest in 575-578 CE.

After the rise of Islam, Himyarite noble families played crucial roles in early Islamic history. The aristocratic families of Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah led South Arabian contingents in the conquest of Homs in 638, making it a center of South Arabian settlement and political power. They supported Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan against Caliph Ali in the First Muslim Civil War (656–661). However, their influence waned with their defeat at the Battle of Marj Rahit against the Quda'a confederation and Marwan I in 684, and practically diminished with the death of their leader at the Battle of Khazir in 686. Nonetheless, members of these families continued to play important roles through the remainder of Umayyad rule as governors, commanders, scholars, and pietists.

In Yemen's tribal genealogy, Himyar represents one of the four major tribal federations that stabilized after Islam, alongside Madhhaj, Kinda, and Hamdan. According to genealogical tradition, Himyar was the eldest son of Saba and brother of Kahlan, though ancient Sabaean texts refer to them as 'sons of a paternal uncle' connected to the greatest god of the ancient Qataban kingdom rather than Sheba. The Himyarites originally seized 'Dahsum,' the land of the Yafa and Al-Ma'afer tribes, and fought prolonged wars with Saba until completely overthrowing it around 275 CE. Their tribal confederation included groups like Zaid Al-Jamhur, Banu Quda'a, and Sakasik, with many tribes throughout Yemen and beyond claiming Himyarite ancestry. Some Himyarite families later established dynasties including the Yufirid Dynasty (847–997), Mahdid Dynasty (1159–1174), and various regional emirates.

migrationPath.description: Himyarite noble families, led by Dhu'l-Kala and Dhu Asbah, migrated to Syria as part of Muslim conquest armies and established themselves as the dominant South Arabian tribes in Homs
Himyarite contingents including Abraha ibn Sabbah and Abu Shamir participated in the Muslim conquest of Egypt and helped plan the new city of Fustat

folkLegends.title: The Red Robe Origin
folkLegends.story: According to genealogical tradition, the name Himyar derives from their ancestor being called Himyar because he wore a red robe, though his real name was 'Al-Arnaj.'

folkLegends.title: Abu Karib's Conversion to Judaism
folkLegends.story: King Abu Karib (r. 390–420) was said to be the first Jewish convert after a military expedition to Yathrib (Medina). When he fell ill during a siege, two Jewish scholars named Ka'b and As'ad restored him to health and convinced him to lift the siege and convert to Judaism along with his army. He brought the scholars back to his capital where they convinced the population to also convert.

nameEtymology: According to genealogical tradition, the name derives from their ancestor wearing a red robe (himyar meaning red), though his real name was said to be 'Al-Arnaj.' However, ancient texts suggest Himyar was an ancient ruling family name rather than deriving from an individual ancestor.

name: Hourani
legitimacyNotes: Local clan status based on settlement and integration in Gulf communities
description: The Hourani is a small clan found in various Gulf communities, likely deriving their name from the Hauran region of southern Syria. They appear to be descendants of migrants or settlers who integrated into existing tribal structures in the Arabian Peninsula. Their presence in the Gulf is documented but limited, with members scattered across different emirates and regions. They maintain clan identity while being integrated into larger tribal confederations.
history: The Hourani clan derives its name from the Hauran region of southern Syria, with documented origins tracing to the Arab al-Halqiyyin tribe. According to historical records of the prominent Al-Hourani family, they moved from the town of Jasim in the southern Hawran region to Hama in central Syria around the 1510s, establishing their distinctive surname Al-Hourani based on their regional origin. The family claimed descent from Muhammad through genealogical records displayed in Hama's museum, though such genealogical claims require careful verification. The research indicates connections between Hourani Bedouins and the broader Mahamid tribal confederation, with the Hourani listed as a branch that emerged from the Mahamid's migrations from the Arabian Peninsula through the Levant. Syrian sources specifically identify Hourani Bedouins in the Daraa and Quneitra governorates, noting their close relationships with Jordanian tribes. The clan appears to represent descendants of migrants who integrated into existing tribal structures throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf regions, maintaining clan identity while becoming part of larger tribal confederations. Their presence in Gulf communities reflects the broader pattern of Levantine migration to the region, though their numbers remain limited and scattered across different emirates. The Mahamid confederation itself traces origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb tribe, with the majority originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, before spreading across the Arabian Peninsula and Maghreb regions.

migrationPath.description: Al-Halqiyyin tribal migration from southern Hawran region to central Syria

name: Hudhayl
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented genealogy tracing to pre-Islamic era
ancestorStory: Hudhayl ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar, eponymous ancestor of the tribe. The tribe descended from this figure and became one of the prominent tribes of the Hijaz.
description: Hudhayl was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe based in the mountainous regions between Mecca and Medina in the Hijaz. They were known for their poetry, archery skills, and as fierce warriors who controlled strategic routes in central Arabia. The tribe played a significant role during the early Islamic conquests and produced notable poets and scholars. While historically powerful in the Arabian Peninsula, their direct influence in the modern GCC states is limited compared to other tribal groups.

relations.context: The Bakr sub-tribe was at war with the Hudhayl, resulting in casualties
Historical movement patterns from Arabian Peninsula to Gulf region

history: Hudhayl (بنو هذيل) was a major pre-Islamic Arabian tribe descended from Hudhayl ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar, making them part of the Adnani lineage through their common ancestor with the Kinana tribe. According to Ibn Ishaq, their genealogical line traces back through Mudar, Nizar, Ma'ad, and Adnan to Ishmael and Abraham. Hudhayl was the brother of Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah, whose son Kinana became the progenitor of Banu Kinana, establishing their close relationship to the ancestors of Prophet Muhammad through this shared Mudrikah lineage. The tribe inhabited the mountainous regions between Mecca and Medina in the Hijaz and became renowned throughout Arabian history for their talented poets, skilled archers, and fierce warriors who controlled strategic routes in central Arabia. During the pre-Islamic period, specific sub-groups of the tribe worshipped the god Suwa' according to Islamic sources, reflecting the polytheistic religious practices common in the region before Islam. The tribe produced notable companions of the Prophet, including Abdullah ibn Masud, as well as distinguished poets like Abū Dhuʾayb al-Hudhalī who was contemporary with Muhammad. In 930 CE, during the infamous Qarmatian Sack of Mecca, Banu Hudhayl demonstrated their martial prowess and loyalty to Islamic sacred sites when they ambushed the retreating Qarmatian forces who had desecrated the Kaaba and stolen the Black Stone. The Hudhayl warriors successfully liberated many prisoners and recovered much of the stolen loot before the Qarmatians managed to escape back to Bahrain, as part of a larger ongoing conflict between the tribe and the radical Ismaili sect. This action exemplified their role as defenders of the holy sites and their continued importance in early Islamic history. Today, the descendants of Banu Hudhayl are found primarily in Saudi Arabia, with populations also present in Tunisia, Jordan, and Egypt, maintaining their historical reputation for scholarship and cultural contributions.

migrationPath.description: Small groups of Hudhayli warriors settled in Basra during Islamic conquests

name: Huwala
legitimacyNotes: Persian Gulf Arab merchants and traders with historical maritime and commercial authority
description: The Huwala are Arab tribes who migrated across the Persian Gulf between the Arabian Peninsula and Persian coast, particularly active in trade and pearling. They established communities on both sides of the Gulf, with significant presence in what is now UAE, Bahrain, and southern Iran. Historically they were prominent maritime merchants and pearl traders, often maintaining dual loyalties and commercial networks across Gulf waters. Many Huwala families remain influential in modern Gulf states, particularly in business and trade sectors.

relations.context: Conflicts over control of Bahrain, Huwala served Persian interests against Utub expansion
Historical maritime and trade competition in lower Gulf
Intermarriage and commercial relationships in Bahrain

history:
The Huwala are Arabian tribal confederations who migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Persian Gulf coast of Iran beginning in the 13th-14th centuries, with continued migrations through the 19th century. They settled primarily in Iran's Hormozgan and Fars provinces, particularly around Bandar Abbas, Qishm, and the mainland near Bandar Lengeh. Unlike most Persians who are Twelver Shia, the Huwala follow Sunni Islam like most Peninsular Arabs, maintaining their Arabian identity despite centuries of residence on the Persian coast. The term specifically refers to actual Arab Huwala tribes including the Qawasem, Hammadi, Al Nasur, Obaidli, and Bani Tamim - not to be confused with Sunni Larestani Achomi families who sometimes adopt Huwala identity.

In the 18th century, the Arab Al Qasimi tribal confederation took control of southern Iranian coasts and islands around Bandar Lengeh. In 1779 the Iranian Zand dynasty recognized a Qasimi as local ruler (farmandar) of Bandar Lengeh. The Qasimis remained in control until 1887 when they were defeated by the British in their 'anti-piracy' campaign. Contemporary historians like Niebuhr and Lorimer documented that true Huwala Arabs lived in the specific Shibkoh region from Bandar Kanj in the south to Bandar Kangan in the north. Most Huwala re-migrated back to the Arabian Peninsula between the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with the imposition of restrictive economic policies by Reza Shah in the 1930s - including centralization, conscription, and forced unveiling of women - leading to the final exodus of most remaining Huwala back to Arabia.

According to Saudi historian Jalal Al-Haroon, there are two types of Huwalas: the first consists of the original Arabs who migrated to Southern Iran from Coastal Eastern Arabia during the 16th and 17th centuries, such as the Bani Hammad, Qawasim, Obaidli, and Al-Haram, now referred to as Arab Fāris or Arab al-Sāḥil. The second type refers to the indigenous people of Southern Iran who resided under the rule of the aforementioned tribes and later migrated back with them to Arabia during the 20th century. Today, many Huwala families remain influential in modern Gulf states, particularly in business and trade sectors, with notable presence in the UAE, Bahrain, and Qatar where they are collectively known as one of the major ethnic groups.

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Huwala Name
folkLegends.story: The meaning of the word 'Huwala' remains unclear, and many Gulf historians continue to debate its origins and significance. Contrary to popular belief, there is very little evidence to support the claim that it means 'to change over.' It appears that the Huwala was a tribal confederation formed in Coastal Oman, similar to the Al-Utub confederation, who were at times their arch rivals. However, it appears that the term was abandoned shortly thereafter, which explains its disappearance in the oral tradition of the Huwalas themselves.

nameEtymology: The meaning of 'Huwala' (Arabic: الهولة, plural of Huwali هولي) remains unclear and debated among Gulf historians. Contrary to popular belief, there is little evidence supporting claims it means 'to change over.' Portuguese sources define Huwala as 'migrant Arabs.' The term may have originated as a tribal confederation in Coastal Oman but was later abandoned, explaining its disappearance from Huwala oral tradition.

name: Huwayla
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
description: Huwayla is a minor Arabian tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe appears in some genealogical records but lacks extensive documentation regarding their specific territorial holdings, political influence, or economic activities. Their current status and activities in the modern GCC states remain unclear due to limited historical sources.
history:
The Huwayla tribe is documented as a sub-tribe of the Qawasim (Al Qasimi) clan, with their most significant historical role being the establishment of their rule in Sharjah around 1727. According to historical records, the Huwayla tribe of the Qawasim clan established itself in Sharjah circa 1727, declaring the emirate independent and marking a crucial political development in the Trucial Coast region. This establishment transformed Sharjah into one of the wealthiest towns in the region, building upon a settlement that had existed for over 5000 years prior to Huwayla control.

The Huwayla's governance was formalized through the broader Al Qasimi dynasty structure, which has ruled Sharjah since the 18th century. Their political authority was internationally recognized when Sheikh Sultan I signed the General Maritime Treaty with Britain on 8 January 1820, accepting protectorate status to prevent Ottoman Turkish expansion into the region. Like neighboring emirates including Ajman, Dubai, Ras Al Khaimah, and Umm Al Quwain, Sharjah under Huwayla-Qasimi rule was recognized as a salute state by the British, albeit of the lowest class with 3-gun honors, reflecting its strategic importance on the maritime route to India.

The Huwayla tribe's rule continued through the Al Qasimi dynastic line into the 19th and 20th centuries, maintaining control over Sharjah's political and economic affairs. Their governance extended inland to areas like Wasit Suburb by the early 20th century, though the territory between the city and Al Dhaid remained under the control of interior tribes. The Huwayla-Qasimi leadership successfully navigated the transition from British protectorate status to full independence, with Sharjah joining the United Arab Emirates on 2 December 1971 as a founding member.

migrationPath.description: Huwayla tribe of the Qawasim clan established itself in Sharjah and declared independence

name: Ilyās
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical authority as ancestor of major Adnani tribal groups
ancestorStory: Ilyās ibn Muḍar is the legendary ancestor of several major Arab tribal confederations. He is considered the progenitor of tribes including Hawazin, Ghatafan, and other northern Arabian groups.
description: Ilyās ibn Muḍar represents a major genealogical branch within the Adnani Arab lineage system. As an ancestral figure, Ilyās is credited as the progenitor of several significant tribal confederations that spread across the Arabian Peninsula. His descendants include the Hawazin and Ghatafan tribal groups, among others, who played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian history. While Ilyās himself is a genealogical construct, the tribal groups claiming descent from him have had historical presence in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including regions that are now part of the modern GCC states.

relations.context: Ilyās is descended from Adnan in the genealogical system

history:
Ilyās ibn Muḍar represents a pivotal genealogical figure within the Adnani Arab lineage system, serving as the progenitor of several major tribal confederations. According to Arabian genealogical tradition, he was the son of Muḍar ibn Nizār and descended from the patriarch Adnan, making him a fifth-generation descendant of Adnan through the Muḍarī branch. His full lineage is recorded as Ilyās ibn Muḍar ibn Nizār ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan, establishing him as an ancestor of the Islamic prophet Muhammad through the Quraysh tribe.

As a pre-Islamic Arabian tribal chief, Ilyās ibn Muḍar ruled over the Hijaz region and maintained significant religious authority over the Kaaba in Mecca. He was notably the first to establish the custom of slaughtering animals on the premises of the Kaaba and rediscovered the Maqam Ibrahim, which he repaired and placed in a corner of the sacred structure. His rule was marked by significant religious reforms, as he promoted a monotheistic form of religion that was incompatible with pre-Islamic Arabian polytheism, openly denouncing idol worship and leading his people to reject polytheism temporarily.

Ilyās's family structure established several important tribal lineages. His wife was named Khindif, and through her he had three sons: Amir, Amr, and Umayr, whose names were later changed to Mudrikah, Tabikha, and Qam'ah respectively. Through his son Mudrikah, Ilyās became the ancestor of the Banū Hudhayl tribe. His brother Qays Aylan became the progenitor of the Qays tribal confederation, establishing parallel lineages that would play significant roles in Arabian tribal politics.

The religious reforms initiated by Ilyās ibn Muḍar had lasting but temporary effects on pre-Islamic Arabia. His monotheistic influence created a period of reduced polytheism until the arrival of 'Amr ibn Luhay, who reintroduced idol worship. Ironically, it was Ilyās's own grandson, Khuzayma ibn Mudrika, who would be the first to set up the idol of Hubal, which the Arabs worshipped for generations until the rise of Islam in the 7th century CE. This demonstrates the cyclical nature of religious influence in pre-Islamic Arabian society and the challenges of maintaining monotheistic practices in a predominantly polytheistic environment.

folkLegends.title: The Monotheistic Reformer
folkLegends.story: Ilyās ibn Muḍar openly denounced idol worship and led his people to accept monotheism, creating a period of religious reform in pre-Islamic Arabia. His reforms were so influential that they temporarily eradicated polytheism among his followers until later generations reverted to idol worship.

folkLegends.title: Guardian of the Kaaba
folkLegends.story: Ilyās maintained the Kaaba in Mecca and was the first to start the custom of slaughtering animals on its premises. He also rediscovered the Maqam Ibrahim, repaired it, and placed it in a corner of the Kaaba.

nameEtymology: The name Ilyās has also been rendered as al-Yas in some sources, though the research does not provide specific etymology for the name's meaning or linguistic origin.

name: Jadis
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with legendary status in pre-Islamic genealogy
ancestorName: Jadis ibn Lud ibn Sam ibn Nuh
ancestorStory: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Jadis was descended from Lud, son of Sam (Shem), son of Noah. They were considered one of the ancient Arab tribes that inhabited the Arabian Peninsula in pre-Islamic times.
description: Jadis was an ancient Arabian tribe mentioned in pre-Islamic genealogical traditions and early Islamic historical accounts. They were traditionally placed among the early Qahtani Arab tribes that inhabited parts of the Arabian Peninsula in antiquity. The tribe is primarily known through genealogical literature rather than detailed historical records. They are considered extinct as a distinct tribal entity, having disappeared or been absorbed into other tribal groups before the Islamic period.
history:
Jadis was an ancient Arabian tribe that, according to Arab genealogical tradition, descended from either Lud or Aram, both sons of Shem (Sam), son of Noah, though the 11th-century historian Miskawayh stated that both Jadis and their neighbors Tasm were sons of Lud. The tribe was classified among the al-Arab al-Ba'ida (extinct Arabian tribes) and belonged to the Qahtani lineage of South Arabian tribes. They settled in the region of Al-Yamama in the Arabian Peninsula alongside the Tasm tribe, where they built palaces and fortresses and created an independent kingdom and civilization that flourished from approximately 110 BCE until their extinction in the 6th century CE.

By the 6th century CE, both Jadis and Tasm were ruled by King Imliq of Tasm, who had implemented oppressive policies including droit du seigneur, deflowering Jadis virgins before their marriage, and controlling trade routes. The Jadis eventually rebelled against this tyranny and assassinated King Imliq during a special performance, where several men from the Jadis revealed hidden swords from the sand and killed him. This act of rebellion would ultimately lead to their destruction.

Despite constant warnings from Zarqa al-Yamama, a legendary blue-eyed woman of their own tribe who possessed exceptional eyesight and could reportedly see enemies from a week's distance, the Jadis ignored the threat of incoming Himyarite armies. The Himyarite forces entered Al-Yamama and completely exterminated the unprepared Jadis tribe, killing all adults and enslaving the children in retaliation for the assassination of King Imliq, who was an ally of Himyar. This genocide marked the complete end of Jadis as a distinct tribal entity.

After their extinction, the palaces and fortresses that Jadis had built in Al-Yamama were taken over and used by the Banu Hanifa tribe. The tribe's legacy survived primarily in Arabian oral traditions and genealogical literature rather than detailed historical records. Their story was later adapted into the 2005 Jordanian television series 'Akher Ayyam Al Yamama' (The Last Days of Al Yamama), demonstrating the enduring cultural memory of these ancient Arabian peoples.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Al-Yamama region alongside Tasm tribe by 110 BCE

timelineEvents.title: Settlement in Al-Yamama
timelineEvents.description: Tasm and Jadis tribes already settled in Al-Yamama region, establishing independent kingdom

timelineEvents.title: Rule under King Imliq
timelineEvents.description: Tasm prevails over Jadis, both ruled by King Imliq who implements droit du seigneur policy

timelineEvents.title: Assassination of King Imliq

timelineEvents.title: Himyarite Invasion and Extinction
timelineEvents.description: Himyarite armies completely wipe out Jadis tribe in retaliation, adults killed and children enslaved

folkLegends.title: The Legend of Zarqa al-Yamama
folkLegends.story: Zarqa al-Yamama was a legendary blue-eyed woman from the Jadis tribe who possessed exceptional intuition, sharp eyesight, and the ability to predict events before they occurred. She was believed to have the ability to see riders from the distance of one week. When enemies of her tribe decided to hide behind trees which they carried to evade her gaze, Zarqa noticed the moving trees and alerted her tribe that the trees were moving towards them. However, members of her tribe thought she was going mad and chose to ignore her warning. The troops of Hassan al-Himyari eventually reached her tribe and killed every man in the camp, then they tore out Zarqa's eyes and crucified her.

folkLegends.title: Migration from Hejaz Legend
folkLegends.story: According to legend, the Banu Hanifa tribe moved to al-Yamama from the Hejaz after the region's original inhabitants, the extinct people of Tasm and Jadis, were decimated by war.

name: Jawabir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and customary authority
description: The Jawabir are a tribal group found in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are present in several Gulf states as part of the broader tribal structure of the region. Like many smaller tribal branches, they maintain traditional social organization while adapting to modern Gulf society. Their historical activities and current status vary by location within the GCC states.
history:
The Jawabir are one of three subsections of the Dhawahir tribe, along with the Daramikah and Bani Saad. According to historical records from the turn of the 20th century, the Dhawahir tribe numbered approximately 4,500 people and held traditional dominance over the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain region. The Jawabir specifically settled in Al Ain, while the Daramikah populated Hili, Mutared and Qattara, and the Bani Saad lived in Jimi. The tribe followed a semi-nomadic lifestyle, staying in their villages during the summer date season to tend their date groves and moving throughout the Trucial States during winter months.

The Jawabir, like other Dhawahir subsections, maintained large flocks of sheep, herded camels, and engaged in charcoal trading from the fertile woodlands of the oasis, which were sustained by an effective falaj irrigation system. They had a long-standing alliance with the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi and the Bani Yas confederation, which proved crucial to their political survival in the region's complex tribal dynamics.

This relationship became severely strained in the late 19th century when Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') began systematically purchasing date groves and water rights predominantly from the Dhawahir. The changing ownership patterns throughout the oasis led to rebellion, and in 1877 Zayed went to war with the Dhawahir. After a month-long conflict, he successfully captured their main settlement 'Ain Dhawahir (now simply Al Ain) and built a fort to establish his dominion, appointing a Dhawahir member as his headman. This marked the end of Dhawahir autonomous control over their traditional territories.

Contemporary records also reference a separate group called Jawabir that acquired territory in Samawah during the 19th century Ottoman redistribution of Khaza'il lands in Iraq. These Jawabir were among the tribes that took territory in Samawah as part of the Ottoman Empire's strategy to diminish Khaza'il power by introducing rival tribes to their lands. However, the connection between the UAE Jawabir of the Dhawahir and the Iraqi Jawabir remains unclear and requires further research to establish any genealogical or historical links.

Expansion eastward toward the Gulf coast seeking new grazing lands and trade opportunities
Settlement along the lower Gulf coast, adapting to pearling economy

timelineEvents.title: Dhawahir Rebellion and Defeat
timelineEvents.description: Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan went to war with the Dhawahir tribe, including the Jawabir subsection, after they rebelled against his land acquisition policies. After a month-long conflict, Zayed captured 'Ain Dhawahir (Al Ain)

timelineEvents.title: Consolidation of Al Nahyan Control
timelineEvents.description: Zayed bin Khalifa marched on the oasis again with support from Dubai, using 30 horsemen and 300 camel riders to quell opposition and take the Dhawahir's main settlement, establishing a wali

name: Juboor
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal legitimacy through established lineage and territorial presence
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its descent to an ancestor named Jabr, from whom they derive their name. They are considered part of the larger tribal confederation with historical presence in Iraq and Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al-Juboor is an Arab tribe with historical presence primarily in Iraq and parts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally known as a nomadic pastoral tribe with branches that migrated across various regions. The tribe has maintained its traditional structure and identity, with some sections settling in Gulf states. They remain active today with members distributed across Iraq, Saudi Arabia, and other Arab countries.

relations.context: Both tribes migrated across similar routes in Arabian Peninsula
Historical cooperation in Iraqi tribal confederations

history:
The Juboor tribe is historically connected to the Jabrids (Banu Jabr), an Arab dynasty that ruled extensive territories in eastern Arabia from the early 15th to early 16th centuries. The Jabrids were of Banu Uqayl origin, an ancient Arab tribe belonging to the Banu Ka'b branch of the larger Banu 'Amir confederation. The most prominent Jabrid ruler was Ajwad ibn Zamil, who died in 1496 and was described by contemporaries as being 'of tribe of Banu Uqayl origin' and as 'the king of al-Ahsa and Qatif and the leader of the people of Najd.' The Jabrid dynasty was established when Ajwad's elder brother deposed and killed the last Jarwanid ruler in Qatif in the early 15th century.

At their height, the Jabrids controlled the entire Arabian coast on the Persian Gulf, including the islands of Bahrain, and regularly led expeditions into central Arabia and Oman. They had significant influence in Oman, appointing Ibadhi Imams and receiving annual tribute. Following Ajwad's death, his kingdom was divided among descendants, with Migrin ibn Zamil inheriting al-Hasa, Qatif, and Bahrain. The Jabrid state was known for its strong support of Maliki law and traditional Islamic science, with Sultan Ajwad founding many schools across Eastern Arabia and Oman, and patronizing Nabati poetry.

The Jabrid kingdom began to decline after Migrin ibn Zamil fell in battle in Bahrain in 1521 during a failed attempt to repel Portuguese invasion. The kingdom collapsed soon afterwards on the mainland, though one branch remained active in Oman for nearly three centuries. The ultimate fate of the Jabrids remains uncertain among historians. Some believe they migrated to Iraq, while others suggest they are identical with the 'Jubur' section of the Bani Khalid tribe, which eventually took control of the eastern Arabian region after the Jabrids.

In contemporary times, the Juboor maintain a presence in Iraq, with the tribe being among those present in the town of Sulaiman Bek in Saladin Governorate, alongside other Arab tribes including Al-Nuaim, Bani Az, Albu Salih, Albu Sabah, and Al-Bayat. This suggests the continuation of the tribal structure and identity in modern Iraq, consistent with the historical pattern of Jabrid/Juboor presence in the region.

migrationPath.description: Bani Khalid, including Juboor branch, established control over Eastern Arabia under Barrak ibn Ghurayr
After defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, many Bani Khalid members including Juboor dispersed to Iraq, Kuwait, and other Arab countries

timelineEvents.description: First Saudi State conquered Bani Khalid domain in Al-Hasa, deposing Al 'Ura'yir rulers

timelineEvents.title: Defeat by Tribal Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Bani Khalid defeated in battle by alliance led by Mutayr and Ajman tribes

timelineEvents.title: Final Military Defeat
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay' and Al Saud ended Bani Khalid rule, leading to dispersal across Arab regions

name: Jubur
legitimacyNotes: Established through tribal confederacy and historical settlement patterns in eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The Jubur trace their lineage to Jabir, from whom the tribal name derives. They are considered part of the larger Zubayd tribal confederation.
description: The Jubur are a major tribal confederation primarily based in eastern Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and parts of Iraq. They are part of the larger Zubayd tribal group and have historically been involved in pastoral nomadism and inter-regional trade. The tribe played significant roles in the political dynamics of the northern Arabian Peninsula during the 18th and 19th centuries. Today they remain active with members distributed across several GCC states, particularly in Kuwait and eastern Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Historical alliances in Iraq and eastern Arabia
Competition over grazing lands and trade routes in eastern Arabia

history:
The Jubur (Arabic: الجبور) are an Iraqi Arab tribe that forms part of the larger Zubayd tribal confederation, which belongs to the Madhhij group. They trace their lineage to the legendary pre-Islamic warrior Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi (died 642 CE), a cavalry commander who converted to Islam and served in major battles including Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, and Nahavand under the Rashidun Caliphate. Along with the Azza, Dulaim, Janabi and Obaidi federations, the Jubur are sub-groups of the Zubaydi tribe, one of the prominent Arabian tribal groups of Iraq.

A significant portion of the Jubur tribe settled in Hawija and Kirkuk in the eighteenth century, establishing themselves in northern Iraq. The tribe was originally entirely Sunni Muslim until the 19th century, when some members began converting to Shia Islam, particularly in the mid-Euphrates region of southern Iraq. This religious transformation reflects the broader sectarian dynamics that have shaped Iraqi tribal society over the centuries.

During the Armenian genocide of 1915, the Jubur tribe demonstrated notable humanitarian action by sheltering Armenian deportees in the al-Jazira desert, providing refuge to those being deported by the Ottoman Empire. This action illustrates the tribe's historical role in regional humanitarian affairs during times of crisis.

In modern times, the Jubur have played significant roles in Iraqi military and political affairs. Since 2014, the tribe has actively fought against ISIS, participating in operations to retake Tikrit and other Iraqi cities during the War in Iraq (2013–17). Some Jubur members joined the Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF), the Iranian-backed paramilitary umbrella group that became part of the Iraqi Armed Forces. Following the liberation of Qayyarah in 2016, members of the Jubur tribe from Qayyarah and surrounding villages formed the Popular Mobilization Forces' 39th Regiment, nicknamed 'Commandos of al-Jubur' or 'Lions of the Tigris,' which subsequently participated in the Battle of Mosul (2016–2017). Contemporary tribal leaders include Ahmed Abdullah al-Jubouri, who has served as Governor of Salah ad-Din Governorate, and Najim Abdullah al-Jubouri, a senior military officer known for his role as Governor of Nineveh and for leading military operations during the liberation of Mosul from ISIS.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Hawija and Kirkuk regions during the 18th century

folkLegends.title: Descent from Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi
folkLegends.story: The Jubur claim descent from the legendary pre-Islamic warrior Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi, who died in 642 CE. This warrior was renowned as a cavalry commander who converted to Islam and served in major battles including Yarmuk, al-Qadisiyyah, and Nahavand under the Rashidun Caliphate.

nameEtymology: The tribal name Jubur (الجبور) derives from Jabir, according to tribal ancestral traditions

name: Junb
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani tribal legitimacy through descent from Junb ibn Sa'd al-Ashira
ancestorStory: Junb is descended from Sa'd al-Ashira, a major branch of the Qahtani tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the ancient South Arabian kingdoms.
description: Junb is a Qahtani tribe historically based in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Yemen and Najran regions. They are part of the larger Sa'd al-Ashira tribal confederation and have maintained their tribal identity through centuries. The tribe has branches that have migrated to various parts of the Arabian Peninsula over time. Today, members of Junb can be found across the Arabian Peninsula, including in Saudi Arabia's southern regions.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani origins through Sa'd al-Ashira

history: Junb is one of the three main tribes that compose the Qahtan tribal confederation, alongside Sanhan and Rufaida. The Qahtan confederation represents a major Qahtanite Arab lineage tracing back to ancient South Arabian origins, distinct from the northern Adnanite Arabs. According to traditional Arab genealogy, the Qahtanites are considered by some Arab traditions to be the original Arabs, originating from modern-day Hadhramaut in Yemen. The term 'Qahtan' appears in multiple Ancient South Arabian inscriptions found in Yemen, establishing its historical presence in the region. The Qahtanite Arabs are traditionally divided into two main sub-groups: Himyar (representing the settled Arabs of the south) and Kahlan (representing their nomadic kinsmen), with various tribal divisions stemming from these lineages. Historically, the Qahtan confederation, including Junb, settled in Al-Quwai'iyah region of Najd and expanded its influence throughout southern Najd during the period of the second Saudi state under Ibn Saud's rule. Several historians describe how Qahtan became one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a period before eventually returning to their original homeland in the south. This migration pattern reflects the broader movement of Qahtanite tribes between their southern Arabian origins and various territories across the peninsula. Today, members of Junb and the broader Qahtan confederation are distributed across multiple countries including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates, maintaining their tribal identity while adapting to modern nation-state boundaries.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Junb warriors and families northward following Islamic conquest participation, establishing new settlements in Najd
Expansion into Asir region during medieval period, establishing mountain settlements and trading posts

timelineEvents.title: Tribal Formation
timelineEvents.description: Establishment of Junb as distinct tribal entity within Sa'd al-Ashira confederation in Yemen highlands

timelineEvents.title: Islamic Conversion

timelineEvents.title: Iraq Campaign Participation

timelineEvents.title: Ottoman Integration
timelineEvents.description: Junb territories incorporated into Ottoman administrative system, tribe maintains local governance under imperial oversight

name: Jusham
legitimacyNotes: descent from Hilal ibn Amir lineage
ancestorName: Jusham ibn Muawiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: Jusham was a branch of the Hilalian confederation that participated in the great Bedouin migrations. They trace their lineage through the Hawazin tribal group to Mudar.
description: Jusham was a tribal branch of the larger Hilalian confederation, part of the Banu Hilal migration that moved westward across North Africa in the 11th century. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who participated in the great Bedouin tribal movements. As part of the Hilalian tribes, they were primarily active in the western Arabian Peninsula and North African regions rather than the eastern Arabian Gulf. Their historical significance relates more to the broader Arab tribal migrations than to specific GCC regional politics.

relations.context: both trace Adnani Hawazin ancestry

history:
The Banu Jusham (Arabic: بنو جشم) were a large Arabian sub-tribe descended from Jusham ibn Muawiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin, making them part of the larger Hawazin confederation within the Qays Aylan tribal group. As Adnanite Arabs tracing their lineage through Mudar, they were pastoral nomads who inhabited the steppes between Mecca and Medina during the pre-Islamic era. The tribe was frequently engaged in conflicts with rival tribes including the Banu Fazara and participated in the major inter-tribal warfare of their time.

During the late 6th century, the Banu Jusham joined other Hawazin tribes in the Fijar War against the Quraysh and Kinana confederation. This conflict arose after the murder of 'Urwa ibn al-Rahhal of the Banu 'Amir by al-Barrad ibn Qays al-Damri during a sacred season, leading to multiple battles over four years. The Hawazin, including Jusham, achieved several victories against their Qurayshi opponents at locations like Shamta and Ukaz before the war eventually concluded with a negotiated peace.

The Banu Jusham had minimal contact with Prophet Muhammad until 630 CE when they were defeated alongside other Hawazin tribes at the Battle of Hunayn. Following this defeat, they reconciled with the Muslim state under Muhammad's leadership. However, after Muhammad's death in 632, the Jusham joined other Hawazin tribes in the Ridda Wars, discontinuing tribute payments to Medina and fighting against the early Islamic state before ultimately returning to the Muslim fold.

The tribe later became part of the great Hilalian migration westward across North Africa in the 11th century. According to historical sources, the Banu Jusham formed one of the six major divisions of the Banu Hilal confederation during this migration period. After their defeat by the Almohads, they were settled alongside the Riyah tribe in regions of present-day western Morocco by the Almohad ruler Yaqub al-Mansur, with the Jusham specifically establishing themselves in the Tamasna region. Additionally, genealogical sources indicate that Jusham also formed one of the largest divisions within the Banu Taghlib's al-Araqim group, where they were known as one of 'al-Rawkan' (the two horns) alongside the Malik division due to their significant size and military strength. Through various tribal genealogies and migrations, the Jusham name became connected to multiple Arabian tribal branches, reflecting the complex intertribal relationships that characterized Arabian Peninsula tribal history.

Participation in the great Hilalian Bedouin migration westward
Settlement by Almohad ruler Yaqub al-Mansur after defeat

name: Juwarin
description: The Juwarin were a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula with documented alliance connections to the Al Bu Falah. Limited historical records make definitive statements about their origins, territorial base, and specific role in Gulf politics difficult to establish. Their alliance with the Al Bu Falah suggests some involvement in the broader tribal dynamics of the region. Current status and activities remain unclear from available historical sources.

relations.context: Allied during seasonal migrations and nomadic movements

history:
The Juwarin (Al Juwarin) were a tribal group that formed part of the Al Ajwad division within the larger Al-Muntafiq tribal confederation of southern Iraq and Kuwait. As documented in historical records, the Juwarin were among the constituent tribes of this major confederation that traced its genealogy to the Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. The Al-Muntafiq confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and later held control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. The confederation's tribal structure was complex and heterogeneous, with the leadership traditionally held by the Al-Saadun family (Sharifs from Mecca), while constituent tribes like the Juwarin had varied origins within the broader Arabian tribal framework.

During the Ottoman era, the Juwarin, along with other Muntafiq tribes, gradually transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq. The broader Muntafiq confederation converted to Shia Islam from the late eighteenth century onwards, though religious composition varied among constituent tribes. Historical accounts from the early 20th century describe the Juwarin as semi-nomadic bedouins who would migrate seasonally alongside other Muntafiq tribes including the Albu Salih, Shuraifat, and Al Aunan.

These migrations followed traditional routes through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait, reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trading purposes before returning north to their tribal territories. The Juwarin would travel with well-armed tribal groups during autumn migrations to northeastern Arabia, encompassing southern Iraq and northern and western Kuwaiti territories. They would engage in trade with local villages and towns in Kuwait before returning north for the summer season. By the 1940s and early 1950s, the majority of nomadic elements within the broader Muntafiq confederation, including the Juwarin, had settled in countries and regions that were part of or close to the traditional Muntafiq territories.

The Juwarin's documented alliance connections beyond the immediate Muntafiq confederation indicate their involvement in broader Gulf tribal dynamics and political relationships. Like other Muntafiq constituent tribes, many Juwarin today are likely distributed across Iraq, Kuwait, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, with some members potentially among the stateless 'bedoon' populations in Kuwait who belong to the Muntafiq tribal confederation.

migrationPath.description: Migration to Iraq during Islamic conquests as part of Al-Muntafiq confederation
Seasonal nomadic migrations through Wadi Batin valley to Arabian Gulf coast for trading

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Shaiba
