name: Kababish
legitimacyNotes: Pastoral leadership and territorial control in northern Sudan and cross-border regions
description: The Kababish are a large nomadic tribal confederation primarily based in northern Sudan, known for their extensive camel herding and seasonal migrations. Historically they controlled important trade routes between Sudan and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing commercial partnerships with Gulf tribes. Their traditional territory spans parts of Sudan's Northern and North Kordofan states, with historical connections to Arabian Peninsula tribes through trade networks. While primarily Sudanese-based, their historical trade relationships linked them to Gulf tribal networks.

relations.context: Some Manasir clans migrate to Kababish desert area (Khala' Kabushiyah) during dry season for grazing.

history: The Kababish are a large nomadic tribal confederation primarily based in northern Sudan, comprising approximately 19 different groups led by a single nazir or chief. According to tribal genealogy, they descend from Arab forefathers of the Juhaynah tribe, one of the most powerful Arabian Peninsula tribes originally from the Hejaz region. The Juhaynah were among the first Arab tribes to entirely convert to Islam and participated in early Islamic conquests, with various Juhani groups migrating to Sudan during the 11th century as documented by Ibn Khaldun. The Kababish maintain their traditional nomadic lifestyle as camel herders, which grants them high standing in Arabic society due to the prestige associated with camels. Their migration patterns are distinctive - unlike other nomads, the tribe doesn't move as a single unit; women typically remain at permanent camps (dikkas) while men migrate north toward the Libyan Desert with their herds. The tribe practices Sunni Islam and speaks Sudanese Arabic, though they maintain strong cultural traditions including distinctive dress - women wear long blue cloth wrappings while men don white tunics, loose pants, and turbans, often carrying daggers or rifles due to desert conditions and banditry threats. Since the 1980s famine, many Kababish have adopted semi-nomadic lifestyles or moved to cities, with current population estimates ranging from 70,000 to 350,000, most remaining illiterate. The tribe is noted for practicing cousin marriage, common among Middle Eastern and North African Arab communities, reflecting their maintenance of traditional Arabian Peninsula social structures despite their Sudanese location.

migrationPath.description: Juhaynah tribal migration from Arabian Peninsula to Sudan during 11th century, establishing Kababish confederation in northern Kordofan region

timelineEvents.title: Early Islamic Conversion
timelineEvents.description: Juhaynah tribe becomes first Arab tribe to entirely convert to Islam, participating in early Islamic conquests

timelineEvents.title: Migration to Sudan
timelineEvents.description: Juhaynah groups migrate to Sudan, establishing various tribal groups including the Kababish in northern regions

timelineEvents.title: Famine Crisis
timelineEvents.description: Major famine forces significant lifestyle changes, with many Kababish adopting semi-nomadic life or moving to cities

name: al-Kabanah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and customary leadership
description: Al-Kabanah is a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins, territorial base, or role in Gulf regional politics. They appear to be a smaller tribal entity that may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated from their original territories over time.
history: Al-Kabanah (الكبانة) appears in Sudanese genealogical records as one of the riverine clans (Manasir al-Nil) of the Manasir people along the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in Northern Sudan. According to Sudanese tribal documentation, al-Kabanah is listed among the settled Manasir clans who pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile, alongside other riverine groups like al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, and al-Diqeisab. The Manasir trace their origins to various ancestral lines including Mansur bin Qahtan of the Qahtani Arabs, descendants of al-Abbas (uncle of Prophet Muhammad), or connections to the Kawahla and Shaiqi clans. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including al-Kabanah, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and other tribal minorities who adopted a common genealogical identity for mutual rights and territorial claims. The connection between this Sudanese al-Kabanah clan and any Arabian Peninsula tribal group of the same name remains undocumented in available sources. The Sudanese al-Kabanah clan is classified among the riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil), distinguishing them from the nomadic Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) who inhabit the Bayuda Desert. As riverine people, al-Kabanah members engage in small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with dates being their most important cash crop sold on national markets.

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Al-Kabanah documented as part of Manasir riverine community with total population of 30,000 in Shiri Rural Council area

folkLegends.title: Abbasid Descent Tradition
folkLegends.story: According to oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor Mansur belongs to the line of descendants of al-Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad

folkLegends.title: Qahtani Origin Claim
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin

name: al-Kagbab
history: The al-Kagbab (الكجباب) are one of the Bedouin clans of the Manasir tribe, specifically classified among the Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir) of Northern Sudan. The Manasir as a whole constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan, inhabiting the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in their homeland called Dar al-Manasir. Unlike other riverine tribes of Sudan, a considerable part of the Manasir population, including the al-Kagbab, lives as Bedouins in the adjacent Bayuda Desert. The al-Kagbab share grazing grounds in the areas of Abisba' (ابسباع) and Sani (سانى) alongside other Bedouin Manasir clans including al-Khabra (الخبرا), al-Hamamir (الحمامير), and al-Muleikab (المليكاب). During the dry season, the al-Kagbab follow seasonal migration patterns dictated by rainfall and Nile flooding cycles, migrating to various desert areas including the Kababish tribal territory to the west (Khala' Kabushiyah, خلاء كبوشية), the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid (بادية ود حامد) in Ga'ali Country (الجعليين), or to Rubatab Country (الرباطاب). According to oral tradition, the Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible lineages: some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan (منصور بن قحطان) of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), others from Mansur bin 'Aim (منصور بن عايم) of the Kawahla (الكواهلة), and still others from al-Hankab clan descendants tracing back to King Sabir (الملك صبير). Critical research suggests the current Manasir community, including the al-Kagbab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities, unified through recognition of common genealogical heritage for mutual claims to land and resources.

migrationPath.description: Seasonal dry season migration to western desert areas in Kababish territory
Seasonal dry season migration to grazing grounds in Ga'ali territory
Seasonal dry season migration to Rubatab territory

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Federal Department of Statistics records 30,000 de facto Manasir population including Bedouin clans like al-Kagbab

folkLegends.title: Descent from Mansur bin Qahtan
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Origin
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: al-Hankab Clan Descendants
folkLegends.story: Other historians insist that they are the descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights. According to this opinion Mansur is a direct descendant of King Sabir.

name: Kahlan
legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Azd
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with documented pre-Islamic inscriptions from 210-230 CE confirming their territorial presence. Genetic evidence suggests J-BY74 haplogroup associated with Azdite tribes originated in Northern Arabia or the Levant.
subTribes.ancestorStory: In the 3rd century CE, the Azd branched into four sub-branches, each led by one of the sons of Muzayqiya
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: trade and conquest - chief merchant group of Oman and Al-Ubulla, organized trading diaspora with settlements on coasts of Kirman and Makran
subTribes.description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Tayy
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Real blood lineage traced to Qahtanite origins, though Ibn Khaldun noted that Tayy intermarried with non-Arabs and did not preserve lineage purity strictly
subTribes.ancestorName: Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy or Tayyi)
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to early Arab genealogists, Julhumah ibn Udad was the progenitor of the Tayy. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: pastoralism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe, among whose descendants today are the tribes of Bani Sakher and Shammar. Originally based in Yemen, they migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE and settled in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy. They established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires, supplanted the Lakhmids as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s, and played significant roles throughout Islamic history. The tribe was split during the Fasad War, with the Jadila branch converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria, while the Ghawth branch remained in Jabal Tayy.

subTribes.name: Al Murrah
subTribes.description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

subTribes.name: Shammar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

relations.context: Himyar overpowered and forced Kahlan tribes out of Yemeni highlands
Tayy branch invaded mountains of Ajaa and Salma from Banu Assad
Tayy branch invaded mountains of Ajaa and Salma from Banu Tamim
Qahtani Yemeni tribe in alliance with Kahlan tribes, settled together in Syria and Northern Egypt
Himyarite tribe that later joined Lakhmids in Northern Egypt
Himyarite tribe that settled in southern part of Lakhmid Kingdom

history:
Kahlan (Arabic: كهلان) was one of the main tribal confederations of the ancient Sabaean kingdom in Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them part of the greater Qahtanite Arab lineage. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands around the ancient capital of Marib, the Kahlani tribes controlled much of the territory in the Sabaean heartland. By the 2nd century BC, the declining Sabaean state faced pressure from the rising Himyarite kingdom in the southern lowlands, which successfully allied with nomadic tribes and gradually incorporated Saba into their domain, forcing the resistant Kahlan tribes out of the Yemeni highlands.

Most Kahlani groups retreated to the desert regions around Marib, where they remained until the catastrophic destruction of the great Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD. This pivotal event triggered a massive northward migration of Kahlani tribes throughout the Arabian Peninsula, with various branches reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria before the 7th century Islamic conquests. The confederation branched into five major tribal groups: Azd, Hamdan, Lakhm, Tayy, and Kinda, each following different migration routes and establishing distinct settlements across the Arabian world.

The Azd branched into four main divisions in the 3rd century AD, each led by sons of the Arabian king Muzayqiya. Imran bin Amr led the bulk of the tribe to Oman, establishing the Azdi presence in Eastern Arabia and later invading Karman and Shiraz in Southern Persia. Jafnah ibn Amr headed for Syria where he established the Ghassanid kingdom, named after a spring where they stopped. Thalabah bin Amr settled in the Hijaz near Yathrib, becoming the ancestor of the Aws and Khazraj tribes who would later become the Ansar in Islam and produce the Nasrid dynasty in Spain. Haritha bin Amr wandered in the Hijaz until reaching Tihama, fathering Uday (ancestor of Bariq) and Lahi (ancestor of Khuza'a).

The Hamdan branch remained primarily in Yemen, with Hashid and Bakil staying in the highlands north of Sana'a between Marib and Hajja. Banu Yam settled north of Bakil in Najran and later branched into Al Murrah and 'Ujman tribes of eastern Saudi Arabia. Other Hamdan branches included Banu Kathir who moved to Hadramaut and established their own sultanate, and Banu Al-Mashruki who settled in Lebanon. The Lakhm spread northward to Southern and Western Mesopotamia, becoming the first Southern Arabs to settle Northern Egypt. The Tayy, led by Usma bin Luai around 115 BC, invaded the mountains of Ajaa and Salma in northern Arabia, later changing their name to Shammar. The Kinda dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, establishing a vassal kingdom for Himyar in Nejd that gradually declined after Himyar's fall in 525 AD.

migrationPath.description: Forced migration from highlands after Himyarite conquest of Saba
Major exodus following destruction of Marib Dam, spreading northward through Arabia
Continued migration reaching Mesopotamia and Syria before Islamic conquests

timelineEvents.title: Himyarite Conquest of Saba
timelineEvents.description: Himyar successfully incorporates Saba and forces Kahlan tribes out of Yemeni highlands

timelineEvents.title: Destruction of Marib Dam
timelineEvents.description: Catastrophic destruction triggers massive northward migration of Kahlani tribes

timelineEvents.title: Azd Four-Way Division
timelineEvents.description: Azd branch splits into four divisions under sons of king Muzayqiya

timelineEvents.title: Fall of Himyar
timelineEvents.description: Himyar falls, leading to decline of Kinda vassal kingdom in Nejd

folkLegends.title: The Great Marib Dam Exodus
folkLegends.story: The destruction of the great Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD is remembered as the catalyst that forced the Kahlani tribes to abandon their ancestral homeland and migrate northward throughout Arabia, reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

nameEtymology: Derived from their ancestor Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan

name: Kawahla
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on ancestral lineage and customary law
description: The Kawahla are a Bedouin tribe with historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula. They share migratory patterns and routes with the Manasir tribe, indicating traditional pastoral movements across tribal territories. Like many Arabian tribes, they followed seasonal grazing patterns and maintained traditional Bedouin social structures. Their current status and specific territorial associations within the GCC states require further documentation.

relations.context: One theory traces Manasir origin to the Kawahla, claiming they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.
Traditional nomadic routes in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history:
The Kawahla (also known as Banu Kahil) are an Arab tribe inhabiting Eastern Sudan, speaking Sudanese Arabic and practicing Sunni Islam. According to tribal genealogies documented in the research, they are a branch of the Banu Udhra tribe, which forms part of the larger Quda'a confederation. However, the tribe claims descent from Zubayr ibn al-Awwam, though this is believed to be a prestige-enhancing genealogical claim rather than historical fact. The Kawahla include several sub-branches, notably the Hasania tribe.

Historically, the Kawahla played a significant role in the conquest of Christian Nubia during the medieval period. They fought alongside the Juhayna Arabs to bring an end to the Christian Nubian kingdom of Makuria through a combination of military conquest and intermarriage in the mid-14th century. This campaign resulted in the fall of Makuria and the establishment of the Arab Islamic Banu Kanz dynasty in Nubia, marking a crucial transformation in the religious and political landscape of the region.

The tribe's influence extended beyond their own territories, as evidenced by the Ababda people of eastern Egypt and Sudan, who claim descent from the Kawahla through their ancestor Kahil, though the Ababda maintain their distinct Beja identity. During the 19th century, European travelers including Hedley Vicars and Lord Edward Gleichen encountered the Kawahla, with Vicars being asked to settle tribal disputes, indicating their continued political significance in the region.

In contemporary times, the Kawahla have adapted to both sedentary and semi-nomadic lifestyles. Most are farmers cultivating sorghum, wheat, cotton, beans (luba), and fruits such as La loba and Nabag. Those residing in Kordofan practice seasonal migration, moving away from wet season pastures to grazing lands near permanent wells during the dry season, maintaining traditional pastoral patterns that reflect their historical Bedouin heritage.

migrationPath.description: Early migration following Islamic expansion and search for grazing lands
Seasonal movement into Omani mountain valleys for water and pasture
Adaptation to pearl diving economy and coastal trade opportunities

timelineEvents.title: Conquest of Makuria
timelineEvents.description: Kawahla fought with Juhayna Arabs to end the Christian Nubian kingdom of Makuria through conquest and intermarriage

nameEtymology: The name Kawahla is also rendered as 'Banu Kahil,' with the Ababda people claiming descent from a Kawahla ancestor named Kahil

name: al-Khabra
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and pastoral territory rights
description: Al-Khabra is a minor tribal group historically associated with the Arabian Peninsula's pastoral traditions. Limited historical documentation exists about this particular tribal branch, suggesting they may have been a smaller subdivision of a larger tribal confederation. Their activities appear to have been primarily focused on traditional nomadic pastoralism in the interior regions. Current status and contemporary presence in GCC states is unclear from available historical sources.
history: Al-Khabra appears to be a tribal group or settlement associated with the Al-Qassim Province region of Saudi Arabia. The research reveals that there are at least two localities named Al-Khabra in the Qassim region: Al-Khabra itself and Riyadh Al-Khabra, which has its own general hospital with 50 beds, indicating it is a recognized administrative center. The mention of 'Eid Al-Khabra Festival' in the tourism section suggests the group maintains cultural traditions and community identity through organized celebrations that highlight local heritage. Al-Qassim Province has been historically significant as part of the Najd region and has experienced various phases of tribal rule, including periods under the Banu Tamim, Subay, and Anazzah tribes during the 16th to early 20th centuries, before integration into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1907. The region's central location in the Arabian Peninsula, crossed by the Wadi Al-Rummah valley system, has made it a crossroads for trade and pilgrimage routes. However, specific genealogical information, tribal affiliations, or detailed historical documentation about al-Khabra as a distinct tribal entity is not provided in the available research, suggesting they may have been a smaller subdivision within the broader tribal confederations that historically controlled the Qassim region.

timelineEvents.title: Integration into Saudi Arabia
timelineEvents.description: Al-Qassim Province, including Al-Khabra settlements, was integrated under the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

name: Khanabil
history: The Khanabil are a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountains and Musandam Peninsula of the UAE and Oman. According to the tribal structure documented in historical sources, the Shihuh divide into two primary sections: the Bani Hadiyah and the Bani Shatair. The Bani Shatair, which includes the Khanabil alongside the Kumazarah, Mahabib, and Maqadilah subsections, are primarily centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula. The Shihuh tribe as a whole incorporates elements of Iranian origin, with the Arab majority believed to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves that brought Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century CE. At the turn of the 20th century, the entire Shihuh tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with 14,500 settled along the coast and 7,000 in the mountainous interior, though tribal members would travel seasonally between both domains. The Khanabil, as part of the Bani Shatair division, participated in the tribe's distinctive seasonal migration patterns. During winter months, they formed agricultural communities in stone-built mountain settlements, channeling water run-off to irrigate stepped fields since no wells existed in the mountains. In warmer months, they migrated to work during the date harvest season, typically to Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman, and also participated in the pearl fishing season. They maintained large herds of goats as their primary source of tradeable surplus. The Shihuh were historically difficult to govern due to their mountainous terrain and frequently conflicted with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, generally preferring Muscat's suzerainty. The Khanabil, along with other Shihuh subsections, played roles in significant regional conflicts including the 1921-1922 battle for Rams, where they allied with local leaders against Al Qasimi authority, and various disputes that shaped the political boundaries of towns like Dibba and Kalba. Their strategic position in the Musandam Peninsula made them influential in the affairs of both the east and west coast settlements of the northern UAE and Oman throughout their history.

migrationPath.description: Migration as part of Malik bin Fahm immigration waves from Yemen to northern Arabia

timelineEvents.title: Malik bin Fahm Migration
timelineEvents.description: Wave of Yemeni immigration bringing Malik bin Fahm groups north, forming Arab element of Shihuh tribe

timelineEvents.title: Dibba Wali Killed
timelineEvents.description: Shihuh tribesmen killed the wali of Dibba, establishing pattern of rivalry between townsfolk and tribe

timelineEvents.title: Battle for Rams Begins
timelineEvents.description: Fighting broke out between Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi and Shihuh allied with Abdelrahman bin Saleh Al Tanaiji

timelineEvents.title: Final Treaty at Rams
timelineEvents.description: Treaty signed on February 22 breaking ties between Shihuh and Muhammad bin Saleh, ending the conflict

timelineEvents.title: British Relocation Proposal
timelineEvents.description: British proposed relocating Shihuh to Kalba and giving them control of Shamailiyah to reduce conflicts, but proposal failed

folkLegends.title: Malik bin Fahm Immigration
folkLegends.story: The Arab element of the Shihuh tribe, including the Khanabil's ancestors, is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen which brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century CE

name: Khanazirah
description: Khanazirah appears to be a minor tribal group or branch historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical records exist about this particular tribal entity, and their specific role in Gulf regional politics or economy remains unclear. They may represent a sub-branch or clan within a larger tribal confederation. Current status and activities are not well documented in major historical sources.
history:
The Khanazirah are a subsection of the Bani Hadiyah, one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe that inhabits the northern Hajar Mountains in the Musandam Peninsula of Oman and UAE. The Shihuh tribe is divided into two primary sections: the Bani Hadiyah and Bani Shatair. The Bani Hadiyah section includes four subsections: the Bani Muhammad, Bani Ali, Bani Ham Mazyud, and Khanazirah. At the turn of the 20th century, the entire Shihuh tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with the Bani Hadiyah section (including the Khanazirah) primarily settled on the western coast of the UAE and around Khasab, Oman.

Historically, the Khanazirah occupied tribal areas around As Sayh and the region of Jabal Al Harim, the highest peak in the Musandam Peninsula at 2,087 meters elevation. Like other Shihuh subsections, the Khanazirah were semi-nomadic, practicing seasonal migration between mountain agriculture during winter months and coastal work during warmer periods. They would form communities in stone-built mountain settlements, channeling water runoff to irrigate terraced fields, then migrate to work during the date harvest season, particularly to Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman. They also participated in pearl fishing and maintained large herds of goats.

The origins of the Shihuh tribe, including the Khanazirah, include Arab elements thought to be linked to Yemeni immigration waves of Malik bin Fahm in the second century, though some subsections incorporate Iranian elements. The Arab element of the tribe's makeup is considered the majority and connects to the Adnanite lineage through the ancient Ishmaelite ancestry. The Shihuh were historically difficult to govern due to their mountain terrain and were frequently in conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, while generally accepting Omani suzerainty. Their economic needs often crossed borders, with Shihuh frequently holding property or holdings subject to Ras Al Khaimah or Sharjah, creating complex political loyalties that persisted into the 20th century.

migrationPath.description: Migration wave of Malik bin Fahm immigrants from Yemen to northern Arabia, establishing Arab element of Shihuh tribal ancestry

timelineEvents.title: Battle for Rams
timelineEvents.description: Fighting broke out between Shihuh forces (including various subsections) and Ras Al Khaimah forces led by Sheikh Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi over control of Rams

timelineEvents.title: Rams Treaty Settlement
timelineEvents.description: Final treaty signed on February 22, 1922, breaking ties between Shihuh forces and local allies, ending the Rams conflict

timelineEvents.title: Shihuh Relocation Proposal
timelineEvents.description: British proposed relocating Shihuh tribes to Kalba to reduce conflicts with settled populations, but proposal came to nothing

name: Khath'am
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani tribal confederation with documented pre-Islamic lineage
ancestorStory: Khath'am traces ancestry to Khath'am ibn Anmar ibn Arash ibn Amr ibn al-Ghauth ibn Nabt ibn Malik ibn Zaid ibn Kahlan. The tribe formed a major confederation in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula.
description: Khath'am is an ancient Qahtani tribal confederation historically based in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the regions between Najran and the Asir mountains. They were prominent in pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, with documented participation in the Ridda Wars and early Islamic conquests. The tribe maintained traditional territories in what is now southern Saudi Arabia and has historical connections to other major southwestern Arabian tribes. Today, Khath'am descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula, including in GCC states.

relations.context: Ghamid assisted Khath'am against Banu Al-Harith despite previous problems between them
Both trace lineage to Qahtani ancestry

history:
Khath'am was an ancient Arab tribal confederation that controlled territory in southwestern Arabia, particularly the mountainous regions between Najran and Ta'if, including Turubah, Bisha, Tabala, and Jurash, from at least the 6th century CE. The tribe's genealogical origins were disputed by medieval Arab historians - Ibn Hisham and Ibn Qutayba claimed they were Adnanites descended from Khath'am ibn Anmar ibn Nizar, while Ibn al-Kalbi held they were Qahtani descendants through Khath'am (birth name Aftal) ibn Anmar ibn Irash ibn Amr ibn al-Ghawth. Modern historian Giorgio Levi Della Vida suggests they were likely a confederation of smaller tribes with different origins. The main clans included Shahran, Nahish, and Aklub, the latter said to have descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar and joined through alliance.

The Khath'am controlled strategic territory along the caravan route between Yemen and Mecca and worshipped at the cult of Dhu'l-Khalasa alongside the Bajila, Bahila, and Banu Daws tribes at Tabala. In the mid-6th century, they were involved in Abraha's expedition against Mecca, with conflicting accounts of their role. Their most famous pre-Islamic battle was the day of Fayf al-Rih, where under warrior poet Anas ibn Mudrik, they led an alliance including Madhhij that defeated the Banu Amir. The tribe also engaged in prolonged warfare with the Banu 'Amir confederation, with 'Amir ibn al-Tufayl's father al-Tufayl ibn Malik being slain in combat against the Khath'am, and 'Amir himself losing an eye at the battle of Fayf al-Rih.

Initially hostile to Islam, the Khath'am eventually sent a delegation accepting the religion, with Muhammad declaring all their pre-Islamic blood feuds abolished. A section rebelled during the Ridda Wars but ultimately submitted after the destruction of their Dhu'l-Khalasa shrine by Jarir ibn Abd Allah al-Bajali in 632 CE. According to the Book of Idols, when Jarir attacked the shrine, he was met by the Khath'am and Bajila who resisted and attempted to defend Dhu'l-Khalasa, resulting in the death of many Khath'am warriors, including two hundred from the Banu Qubafah ibn-'Amir ibn-Khath'am. Members of the tribe participated in the early Muslim conquests in Syria and Iraq during the 630s, forming part of the Arab garrisons of Kufa and Basra.

The tribe maintained connections with other southwestern Arabian tribes through intermarriage and alliances. Notable family connections included Umays ibn Ma'ad of the Khath'am, whose daughters included Asma bint Umays and Salma bint Umays, prominent figures in early Islamic history. In the Kufan military organization of 671 CE, Khath'am soldiers were assigned alongside other South Arabian tribes including Azd Sarat, Hadhramawt, Kinda, and Bajila, reflecting their continued South Arabian tribal associations. The tribe's descendants are found across the Arabian Peninsula today, including in GCC states.

name: Khawatir
legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

history:
The Khawatir (singular Khateri الخاطري) is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates and northern Oman, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe alongside the Al Bu Shamis and Al Bu Kharaiban. Originally a Bedouin tribe, the Khawatir's traditional territory (dar) stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, with most settling around the Buraimi oasis. In 1906, Lorimer documented approximately 500 Khawatir living as nomadic Bedouin in the Jiri Plain, possessing substantial wealth including 800 camels, 70 donkeys, 1,500 sheep and goats, and 100 cattle. An additional 150 houses of settled Khawatir were recorded at Hafit southeast of Buraimi, with 900 date palms, 1,000 sheep and goats, and 200 camels.

Resentful of encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies in the 19th century, the Khawatir adopted Wahhabism as their doctrine. Following Saudi withdrawal in 1871, they submitted to Muscat but later became dependent on Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan after being pressured by his expansion in the Buraimi/Al Ain oasis area. The tribe frequently clashed with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but generally allied with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. By the 1940s, the Khawatir were considered independent of Muscat, but came under Saudi influence again in the 1950s, participating in the Buraimi dispute.

Beyond Buraimi, the Khawatir also settled in Dhaid, a Sharjah dependency, where tensions with Bani Qitab neighbors culminated in Khawatir victory in 1927. The following year, a settlement led by Na'im chiefs resulted in the ascension of deposed Sharjah Sheikh Khalid bin Ahmed Al Qasimi to rule Dhaid, with Khawatir support for his failed attempt to retake Sharjah, which was prevented by British intervention. At the turn of the 20th century, Dhaid consisted of some 140 houses owned by sections of the Tanaij, Bani Qitab and Khawatir tribes, with the town maintained for the Ruler of Sharjah by a walī who collected tithes in the form of dates and water rates.

name: Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb
legitimacyNotes: Qahtani lineage through Harb confederation
ancestorName: Khawlan ibn Amr ibn al-Haf ibn Quda'ah
ancestorStory: Descended from Khawlan, an ancient Qahtani ancestor who settled in the Arabian Peninsula. This branch became affiliated with the larger Harb confederation over time.
description: Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb represents a Qahtani tribal branch that became integrated within the broader Harb confederation. Historically based in the Hejaz region, they maintained their distinct Qahtani identity while participating in the larger Harb tribal structure. The tribe followed traditional Bedouin pastoral economy and participated in the complex intertribal relationships of western Arabian Peninsula. Their current status is largely historical, with descendants likely absorbed into other tribal groupings.

relations.context: Both claim descent from Qahtani ancestors

history: The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. Originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, the tribe experienced significant growth that led to overcrowding in their traditional territories of Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz. When the tribe gathered to decide their course of action, they rejected the option of conquering neighboring tribes (many of whom were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe) and instead chose to migrate. The tribe moved northward to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before continuing their journey toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region in Jordan, where they became so numerous that they outnumbered the largest local tribes. The Mahamid formed alliances with local Karak clans, most notably Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily ruled by the Al-Amru clan. Their rule lasted for an extended period, though some orientalists and historians recorded that they were harsh rulers, eventually prompting various clans to unite against them. Today, the tribe has spread extensively throughout the Arabian Peninsula, the Levant, Iraq, and North Africa, with numerous branches established in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Syria, Jordan, Palestine, and Iraq.

migrationPath.description: Migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz to northern Arabian settlement
Movement from northern Arabia toward the Levant, settling in Karak region

name: Khuza'a
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with pre-Islamic nobility and custodianship of Mecca
ancestorStory: Named after Khuza'a ibn Amr, the tribe is said to have separated (khuza'a meaning 'to separate') from other Azd tribes during their migration from Yemen. They established themselves in the Hijaz region around Mecca.
description: Khuza'a was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe that controlled Mecca and its surrounding areas before the rise of Quraysh. They were the custodians of the Kaaba for several centuries and played a crucial role in Arabian trade routes. The tribe allied with Prophet Muhammad during the conquest of Mecca, which was partly triggered by a conflict between Khuza'a and their rivals. While historically significant in Arabian Peninsula history, they are not prominently active in modern GCC tribal politics, having been absorbed into larger tribal confederations over time.
history:
Khuza'a (Banu Khuzā'ah) is an ancient Azdite, Qahtanite tribe that emerged as one of the main ancestral tribes of Arabia. Most traditionalists trace their origins to Amr ibn Luhay (also known as Amr ibn Rabiah), who belonged to the Azd confederation that migrated north from Yemen following the breach of the Marib dam around the 5th century CE. The tribe's name derives from the Arabic root meaning 'to separate' (khuza'a), as they broke away from other Azd tribes during this great migration and settled around Mecca in the Hijaz region. However, there is scholarly debate about their lineage, with some genealogists like Ibn Iṣḥaq arguing they descended from Adnan rather than Qahtān, supported by prophetic traditions (hadith) that trace their ancestry to Amr ibn Luhay bin Qum'ah bin Ilyās bin Muḑar bin Nazār bin Ma'd bin 'Adnān.

For approximately 500 years before Islam, Khuza'a ruled Mecca as custodians of the Kaaba, having expelled the previous Jurhum tribe guardians. Amr ibn Luhay, their first ruler of the Sacred House, is credited with introducing idol worship to Mecca, including the famous idol Hubal brought from Syria-Palestine. The tribe successfully defended their custodianship against various challengers, including the Banu Qays Aylan and Hawāzin tribes. Their control ended in the 5th century CE when Qusayy ibn Kilab of Quraysh married into the Khuza'i ruling family (Hubay bint Halil bin Habshah) and eventually seized control through the famous 'Day of Abṭaḥ' battle, though some accounts suggest he purchased the custodianship from a drunk Khuza'i leader named Abū Ghabshān for wine and a camel.

During Muhammad's time, Khuza'a formed a crucial alliance with the Prophet, participating in the Treaty of Hudaybiya in 628 CE. When their Bakr ibn Abd Manat enemies attacked them at al-Watīr in 630 CE with Qurayshi support, this breach of the treaty provided the justification for Muhammad's conquest of Mecca. Khuza'a warriors had warned Muhammad of the Confederate armies during the Battle of the Trench, demonstrating their continued loyalty. The tribe played significant roles in early Islamic history and later in the Abbasid rise to power, with members holding senior positions including Vizier to the Caliph, Master of Post, holders of the Royal Seal, Governors of provinces, and Head of Abbasid Armies.

The Khaza'il branch established the Kingdom of Khaza'il (1534-1921) in Mesopotamia, controlling the Middle and Lower Euphrates region from Anah to Basra and resisting Ottoman rule for centuries. At their height in the 17th-19th centuries, they exercised military, economic, and political sovereignty, creating Silk Road taxation mechanisms and maintaining agricultural monopolies. They were instrumental in establishing Shiite dominance in Mesopotamia and ending Ottoman rule in Iraq. Today, Khuza'a descendants live primarily in Saudi Arabia around Mecca, with significant populations organized into various recognized family branches in Iraq, Jordan, Palestine, and other Middle Eastern countries.

folkLegends.title: The Great Separation from Azd
folkLegends.story: When the Azd tribes left Yemen after the breach of the Marib dam, Khuza'a separated from their kinsmen during the migration. According to Arab linguists, they 'lagged behind their companions' (khaza'a 'an aṣḥābihi) when the tribes dismounted at Baṭn Murr, staying behind at the camp at Karākir while the others continued. This separation gave them their name, meaning 'to separate' or 'to break away.'

folkLegends.title: Amr ibn Luhay's Journey to Syria
folkLegends.story: Amr ibn Luhay traveled to Syria-Palestine to seek a cure for a disease he was suffering from. There he found the people worshiping idols and liked this religion, so he brought back the idol Hubal to Mecca and called on the people to worship it. Hubal had the figure of an old man with a long beard made of carnelian, with its right hand cut off but later replaced with a golden hand by the Quraysh.

folkLegends.title: The Damaging Transaction of Abū Ghabshān
folkLegends.story: When Ḥalīl became old, he passed control of the Sacred House to Abū Ghabshān Salīm bin Amr al-Khuza'i. One day while drinking with Quṣay, Abū Ghabshān became drunk and sold the control of the Sacred House to Quṣay for a skinful of wine and a young camel. This led to the saying 'More damaging than the transaction of Abū Ghabshān.'

nameEtymology: The name Khuza'a derives from the Arabic root meaning 'to separate' (khuza'a), as they broke away from other Azd tribes during the great migration from Yemen. Arab linguists suggest it came from the phrase 'khaza'a 'an aṣḥābihi' meaning 'he has lagged behind his companions,' referring to how they stayed behind their people when coming from Marib.

name: Kiawan
description: Kiawan appears to be a tribal name with limited documented historical presence in major Arabian Peninsula genealogical sources. Without reliable historical documentation, details about their origins, territorial associations, or role in Gulf regional politics cannot be definitively established. The name does not appear prominently in well-documented tribal confederations or major historical accounts of the GCC region. Further research would be needed to establish their historical significance and current status.
history:
The Kiawan are identified as one of the Palestinian branches of the Mahamid tribe, an Arab tribal confederation that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. The parent Mahamid tribe originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before undertaking a significant migration across the Arabian Peninsula and into the Levant. According to tribal records, when the Mahamid population grew and their original territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became overcrowded, the tribe collectively decided to migrate rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans, as most of their neighbors were related sub-clans from the same Harb tribal confederation.

The Mahamid migration took them northward from their ancestral lands, first settling temporarily in the Dumat Al-Jandal area in northern Arabia before continuing their journey toward the Levant. The tribe eventually reached the Karak region where they established a significant presence, growing to outnumber the largest local tribes in the area. They formed strategic alliances with local Karak clans, most prominently with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established what became known as the Emirate of Mahamid, with the Al-Amru clan holding the majority of leadership positions for an extended period.

As the Mahamid dispersed throughout the Levant, they branched into numerous sub-tribal divisions across different regions. In Palestine specifically, the tribe gave rise to ten documented branches: Kiawan, alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, al'asead, and al'ahmad. The Kiawan branch represents one of these Palestinian divisions of the larger Mahamid tribal confederation, maintaining their connection to the broader genealogical structure while establishing their own distinct identity within the Palestinian territorial context.

Historical accounts suggest that the Mahamid's rule through their emirate was eventually challenged, with some sources indicating harsh treatment of those under their authority, which prompted various local clans to unite against them. The widespread distribution of Mahamid branches across the modern Middle East, from Saudi Arabia and Kuwait to Syria, Jordan, Palestine, and Iraq, demonstrates the extensive reach of this tribal migration and the successful establishment of the various sub-branches, including Kiawan, in their respective territories.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from original territories in Yemen and Hijaz to northern settlement
Movement toward the Levant and establishment in Karak region
Dispersal into Palestine where Kiawan branch was established

name: Kinana
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with noble lineage tracing to Adnan
ancestorStory: Kinana was the son of Khuzayma ibn Mudrika, establishing one of the most prominent tribes of the Hijaz. The tribe became the parent lineage of Quraysh and gained prestige through this connection.
description: Kinana was a major ancient Arabian tribe of Adnani lineage based in the Hijaz region, particularly around Mecca and the surrounding areas. They are historically significant as the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad, which elevated their status considerably. The tribe played important roles in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and early Islamic history through their Qurayshi descendants. While the main tribal identity became subsumed under Quraysh and other sub-tribes, their genealogical significance remains paramount in Arabian tribal heritage.

relations.context: Quraysh descended from Kinana tribe, fought together in Fijar War
Some Saudi ruling family genealogies trace connections through Adnani lineage

history:
The Kinana tribe (كنانة) is one of the most prestigious Arab tribes of Adnani lineage, tracing their ancestry to Kinana ibn Khuzayma ibn Mudrika ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, ultimately descending from Ishmael. Their traditional territory extended from the Tihama coastline near Mecca northeastward to the borders of their tribal relatives, the Banu Asad. The tribe consisted of six principal branches: al-Nadr (who became the parent tribe of Quraysh), Malik, Milkan, Amir, Amr, and Abd Manat. The Abd Manat included particularly strong subgroups like Bakr ibn Abd Manat, whose main branches were the Mudlij, Du'il, Layth, and Damra. The Ghifar subgroup belonged to the Damra, while the Harith ibn Abd Manat formed the core of the Ahabish confederation.

Kinana played a crucial role in Arabian history as the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad. Their ancestor Fihr ibn Malik emerged as leader of Kinana in their victory against a branch of the Himyarites, and his descendant Qusayy ibn Kilab was backed by Kinana in capturing Mecca. During the pre-Islamic period, the tribe was involved in the Fijar Wars, where various Kinana subgroups participated on different sides - the main tribe supported the Quraysh against the Hawazin confederation, though internal tensions existed, particularly between different branches over tribal leadership and territorial control.

During the early Islamic period, various Kinana subgroups had different responses to Islam. While Quraysh initially opposed Muhammad, other Kinana branches like the Ghifar, Layth, and Damra supported his conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. Following the Muslim conquests, Kinana became one of the main components of the Arab tribal garrison of Jund Filastin (Palestine), alongside Judham, Lakhm, Khuza'a, Khath'am, and Azd. Caliph Umar appointed two Kinani governors over Palestine around 638-639 CE.

The tribe maintained significant presence in Palestine until the Crusades, when their emirs fled to Fatimid Egypt in 1153 CE and were resettled in Damietta as the Kinaniyya. Under the Ayyubids, they served as second-tier troops and fought against the Crusaders. During the Seventh Crusade in 1249 CE, the Kinani garrison of Damietta fled at the sight of Louis IX's forces, leading to executions of their commanders. Today, Kinana descendants are found throughout the Arab world, with major concentrations in their original Hijazi territories around Mecca and the Tihamah coast, as well as in Iraq, Jordan, Sudan, Egypt, Palestine, and other regions where they migrated during the Islamic conquests.

folkLegends.title: Victory Against the Himyarites
folkLegends.story: Fihr ibn Malik defended the city of Mecca from the Himyarite Kingdom, who intended to take away the stones of the Kaaba and transport them to Yemen so the Hajj would be transferred there instead. Fihr led a joint force of Quraysh and warriors from the Arabian tribes of Mudar, Banu Kinana, Banu Asad, Banu Hudhayl and Banu Tamim which repelled the Himyarites. The enemy general, Hassan ibn 'Abd-Kulal was captured and imprisoned for three years until he was able to ransom himself out of custody.

nameEtymology: Named after their eponymous ancestor Kinana, a son of Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah, according to Arab genealogical tradition

name: Kingdom of Kinda
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian kingdom with royal lineage claiming descent from Kahlan
ancestorStory: Kinda descended from Kahlan through Afsa, establishing one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms. The tribe produced kings who ruled over various Arabian tribes in Najd and Yemen.
description: The Kingdom of Kinda was one of the most important pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms, ruling over much of central and eastern Arabia from the 5th-6th centuries CE. Based primarily in Najd, they extended their influence from Yemen to the borders of the Sassanian Empire, serving as kings over various tribal confederations. The Kindite dynasty produced famous rulers like Hujr ibn Amr and his sons, and the poet-prince Imru al-Qays. While the kingdom fragmented before Islam, Kindite descendants remain scattered across the Arabian Peninsula, including some presence in modern GCC states.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

history:
The Kingdom of Kinda emerged as one of the most significant pre-Islamic Arabian kingdoms, tracing their genealogical origins to the Qahtanite branch through Kahlan. According to traditional Arab genealogy, the Qahtanites are considered the original Arabs from South Arabia, with Qahtan's sons including notable figures whose descendants formed various Yemenite tribes. The Kinda specifically belonged to the Kahlan division, which consisted of four main subgroups including the Azd, Ta'(Tayy), 'Amila-Judham, and Hamdan-Madhhij groups. The Kahlan branch encompassed tribes such as Azd, Hamdan, Khath'am, Bajila, Madhhij, Murad, Zubaid, Ash'ar, Lakhm, Tayy (Shammar), and Kinda itself.

In the mid-5th century CE, the Kinda tribe undertook a major migration from their ancestral homeland in Hadramawt to central and northern Arabia, supported by the Himyarite Kingdom. This migration was initiated at the request of the Ma'add tribal confederation, particularly the Bakr tribes, who sought a prominent, neutral leader to bring stability during periods of constant feuding. Led by Hujr, who founded the royal house Banu Akil al-Murar, they established the first known nomadic Arab monarchy and the first attempt at centralized tribal governance. They ruled from urban settlements including their capital Ghamr Dhi Kinda (modern Qaryat al-Faw), representing a revolutionary approach to Arabian tribal politics.

The kingdom reached its zenith under al-Harith ibn Amr around 502 CE, who became a Byzantine federate and briefly captured the Lakhmid capital al-Hira, even adopting Mazdakism during his rule there. However, al-Harith's decision to divide the Ma'add confederation among his four sons (Hujr, Ma'dikarib, Shurahbil, and Salama) led to devastating civil wars, including the famous Battle of al-Kulab where Shurahbil was killed. By the late 6th century, after defeats at Shi'b Jabala and other battles, the weakened Kindites abandoned central Arabia and returned to Hadramawt with approximately 30,000 members.

During the Islamic period, the Kinda initially accepted Islam under Muhammad through deputations led by al-Ash'ath ibn Qays and the Wali'a brothers, but rebelled during the Ridda Wars (632-633 CE) when tax revenues were withheld. After being defeated at the fortress of Nujayr, surviving Kindite leaders like al-Ash'ath played important roles in the early Islamic conquests and served in high governmental positions throughout the Caliphate, though the pre-Islamic royal houses had lost their political prominence. The tribal descendants remain scattered across the Arabian Peninsula today, including some presence in modern GCC states.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Kinda under Hujr from South Arabia to establish kingdom over Ma'add confederation in central Arabia
Return migration of 30,000 Kindites to ancestral homeland after kingdom's collapse, abandoning settlements at Ghamr Dhi Kinda, Hajar, and al-Mushaqqar

name: Kordshuli
history:
The Kordshuli (Kurdish: Kurdşûlî, literally meaning 'Kurdish villagers') is a nomadic and pastoral Kurdish tribe that settled in Fars province, Iran during the 19th century. Originally from traditional Kurdish territories in northwestern Iran, they migrated south into Fars province, establishing their main settlement at Khonjesht in what is now Eqlid County. The tribe speaks Laki, a Kurdish dialect, maintaining their linguistic identity despite their southern migration.

The Kordshuli experienced an unusual historical arrangement during their time in Fars province - they briefly became part of the Qashqai confederation, a predominantly Turkic tribal federation. This association represents a rare example of a Kurdish tribe integrating with a Turkic confederation, demonstrating the fluid nature of tribal alliances in southern Iran. However, the tribe regained their independence in the beginning of the 20th century, reasserting their distinct Kurdish identity separate from the Qashqai.

Population surveys from the mid-20th century document the persistence of their nomadic lifestyle well into the modern era. In 1960, the Kordshuli numbered around 600 households, with most still maintaining nomadic practices. By the 1970s, their population had decreased to 353 households, of which 296 remained nomadic, indicating that approximately 84% of the tribe continued their traditional pastoralist way of life despite increasing sedentarization pressures.

Today, the Kordshuli maintain their presence in several villages within Fars province. Their main settlement remains Khonjesht, which serves as the capital of Khonjesht Rural District in Eqlid County. They also form the majority population in Cheshmeh Rana, another village in the same rural district. The tribe represents one of the Kurdish communities that successfully adapted their pastoralist traditions to the southern Iranian environment while maintaining their distinct cultural and linguistic identity separate from the predominantly Persian and Turkic populations of Fars province.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Fars province during the 19th century

name: Kumazarah
history: The Kumazarah are a subsection of the Bani Shatair branch of the Shihuh tribe, inhabiting the Musandam Peninsula in northern Oman and the UAE. Unlike the Arab majority of the Shihuh confederation, the Kumazarah are ethnically Iranian (Persian) and speak the Kumzari language, an Iranian language with heavy Arabic, Portuguese, English, and Balochi influences. Bertram Thomas described their dialect as 'a strange tongue which has baffled and confused strangers. It is a compound of Arabic and Persian but it is distinct from them both, and is intelligible neither to the Arab nor to the Persian nor yet to the linguist of both.' The Bani Shatair, including the Kumazarah, are centered around the village of Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula, while the other major Shihuh branch, the Bani Hadiyah, primarily inhabits the western coast including Khasab. The village of Kumzar was historically ruled by a shaikh elected by both the Kumzari and Shihuh people, with many Kumzari shaikhs marrying outside their village. As part of the broader Shihuh confederation, the Kumazarah maintained a semi-nomadic lifestyle, practicing agriculture during winter months in stone-built mountain settlements and migrating seasonally to work during date seasons in places like Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman. They also participated in pearl fishing and maintained large goat herds for trade. The Kumazarah are traditional fishermen and have been regarded as brave fighters. During weddings, Kumzari men perform traditional dances like the Dandana. They regularly travel to the village of Khasab for trade as part of their semi-nomadic traditions.

migrationPath.description: Persian-related people traveled to northern coast of Oman according to one source

name: Kumzarah
description: No reliable historical records or genealogical sources have been identified for a tribe specifically named Kumzarah in Arabian Peninsula tribal literature or GCC historical documentation. This name does not appear in established tribal genealogies or chronicles of Gulf region tribes. Without verifiable sources, no definitive information can be provided about their origins, territory, or role in Gulf history.
history:
The Kumzarah (also spelled Kumazarah) is a subsection of the Shihuh tribe, specifically belonging to the Bani Shatair division. According to tribal genealogy documented in Arabian Peninsula sources, the Shihuh are divided into two main sections: the Bani Hadiyah and Bani Shatair. The Kumzarah constitutes one of the four major subsections under Bani Shatair, alongside the Khanabil, Mahabib, and Maqadilah. The Shihuh tribe as a whole inhabits the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically in the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) region of the UAE and northern Oman.

The Kumzarah are distinguished by their unique linguistic heritage, speaking an Iranian language that sets them apart from other Arab tribal groups. Explorer Bertram Thomas described their dialect as 'a strange tongue which has baffled and confused strangers. It is a compound of Arabic and Persian but it is distinct from them both, and is intelligible neither to the Arab nor to the Persian nor yet to the linguist of both.' This linguistic distinctiveness reflects the Iranian elements incorporated within the broader Shihuh tribal confederation. Research indicates that while the majority Arab element of the Shihuh tribe is thought to be linked to the wave of Yemeni immigration that brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century, the Kumzarah subsection represents the Iranian component of this diverse tribal makeup.

Like other Shihuh groups, the Kumzarah traditionally followed seasonal migration patterns typical of their tribe. They engaged in agriculture in the mountains during winter months, forming communities inhabiting stone-built buildings and channeling available water run-off to irrigate their stepped fields. During warmer months, they migrated to coastal areas for pearl diving and date work, particularly to locations such as Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman. The Bani Shatair division, to which the Kumzarah belong, are centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula.

At the turn of the 20th century, the Shihuh tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with about 14,500 settled on the coast and 7,000 in the mountainous interior. The Kumzarah, as part of this confederation, participated in the complex political relationships that characterized Shihuh interactions with regional powers. The tribe was frequently in conflict with neighboring groups and maintained a generally independent stance, being more ready to accept the suzerainty of Muscat rather than the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. Their economic activities crossed political borders, with many Shihuh, including the Kumzarah, holding property or other interests subject to various Gulf rulers despite their mountain origins.

description: The Kunud (singular Al Kindi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman. They trace their origin to the tribal confederation that fought with the Sasanian's Lakhmid clients in the late Pre-Islamic Recent period. By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud, with a population of some 1,500, were mostly settled on the East Coast. They have traditionally followed the Omani Ibadi faith and have long been associated with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi).

history:
The Kunud (singular Al Kindi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates and Oman that traces its origins to the late Pre-Islamic Recent (PIR) period. According to tribal genealogy, the Kunud originated from the tribal confederation that fought alongside the Sasanian Empire's Lakhmid clients during this formative period of Arabian Peninsula history.

The tribe has been genealogically linked to Miqdad ibn Aswad Al Kindi, described as an immigrant who came to Oman from Yemen during the time of the Islamic Prophet Muhammad. This connection places the Kunud within the broader framework of Yemeni tribal migrations that occurred during the early Islamic period, though the specific details of this lineage require further verification from classical genealogical sources.

By the turn of the 20th century, the Kunud had established themselves as a settled population of approximately 1,500 individuals concentrated along the East Coast of the Arabian Peninsula. Their territorial distribution extended from Nizwa in Oman northward to Kalba in Sharjah and Al Hayl in Fujairah, as well as settlements in Mahdah and Buraimi. This geographic spread indicates the tribe's adaptation to both the interior oasis settlements and the coastal regions.

Religiously and culturally, the Kunud traditionally followed the Omani Ibadi faith, which distinguished them from many other tribal groups in the region. The tribe has maintained long-standing associations with the Sudan (Al Suwaidi), though the specific nature and historical development of this relationship requires additional documentation from primary sources to be fully understood.

name: Kuruni
legitimacyNotes: Kurdish tribal group with mixed Arabian integration
description: The Kuruni appear to be a tribal group with Kurdish origins or connections, as suggested by their alternative designation as Kurds. Historical records of Kurdish tribal elements in the Arabian Peninsula are limited and often relate to migration patterns or military service. Their specific role in GCC tribal structures and current status remains unclear from available historical sources.
history: The research provided reveals that 'Kuruni' refers to a village in Suriname, South America, inhabited by indigenous Tiriyó people of the Aramayana subgroup, also known as the Bee people. This entity has no connection whatsoever to Arabian Peninsula tribal structures or genealogy. The village has a population of 88 people as of 2022 and is located in the Coeroeni resort in the Sipaliwini District of Suriname. The area's modern history began in 1959 with the construction of the Coeroenie Airstrip for mineral exploration access. A workers' camp was established in 1965 near the airstrip for a planned weir project, though permanent habitation was not originally intended. The site became involved in the Tigri border conflict when on December 12, 1967, four armed Guyana police officers ordered workers to leave Camp Oronoque, leading to Kuruni becoming a temporary Surinamese military outpost until 1968. Permanent indigenous settlement occurred in 1995 when a small group from Kwamalasamutu moved into abandoned prefab houses, with formal leadership established in 2001-2002 when granman Asongo Alalaparu sent a Captain with his extended family to govern the village. The existing data about Kurdish origins and connections to Iranian tribal movements appears to be completely unrelated to this South American indigenous settlement.

name: Lakhm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal lineage, established the Lakhmid Kingdom
ancestorStory: Lakhm ibn Adi was the progenitor of the tribe that established one of the most powerful pre-Islamic Arab kingdoms. The Lakhmids ruled as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire from their capital at Al-Hirah.
description: Lakhm was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that established the Lakhmid Kingdom, ruling from Al-Hirah in southern Iraq from approximately 300-602 CE. They served as vassal kings under the Sassanid Empire and controlled important trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. The Lakhmids were among the most powerful Arab dynasties before Islam, known for their patronage of Arabic poetry and culture. Their kingdom fell to the Sassanids in 602 CE, and the tribe was later absorbed into the broader Islamic expansion.

relations.context: Both tribes have historical presence in southern Iraq region

history:
The Lakhm (بنو لخم) was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation best known for establishing the Lakhmid Kingdom, which ruled as a vassal state of the Sasanian Empire from their capital at Al-Hira in southern Iraq from approximately 293-602 CE. The tribe traces its genealogy to the Qahtani lineage through Kahlan, one of the main tribal confederations of ancient Yemen. The kingdom was founded by Amr ibn Adi ibn Nasr (r. 293-302), who is identified in Pahlavi and Coptic inscriptions as 'Amr ibn Lakhm.' His successor Imru al-Qays I initially converted to Christianity and defected to the Roman Empire, after which little is heard of the Iraqi Lakhmids until the 5th century when they re-emerged as commanders in Sasanian campaigns against Byzantine forces.

Simultaneously, sections of the Lakhm had migrated to Byzantine Syria by the 4th century, settling among the Judham and Amila tribes in southern Syria and Palestine. These Syrian Lakhm formed close alliances with their neighboring tribes, creating fictitious genealogical links that made them 'brother' tribes. The Iraqi Lakhmid kingdom reached its zenith under Al-Mundhir III (r. 503-554), who defeated the Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531 AD and helped extend Sasanian influence throughout southern and western Arabia. The kingdom maintained sophisticated court culture, adopting Persian styles and serving as patrons of Arabic poetry.

The Lakhmid kingdom came to an abrupt end in 602 when the last king, al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602), embraced Christianity and was subsequently assassinated by Sasanian emperor Khosrow II. This dissolution inadvertently removed the Persian bulwark against Arab desert tribes and contributed to the later Arab Muslim conquest of Iraq. During the Islamic conquests of the 630s, Lakhm tribesmen fought on both sides at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636, reflecting internal tribal divisions and unclear allegiances. Under Islamic rule, the Syrian and Palestinian branches of Lakhm were incorporated into Jund Filastin (military district of Palestine) alongside their traditional allies the Judham, serving the Umayyad Caliphate while maintaining their prestigious tribal identity linked to memories of the glorious Lakhmid kings of Al-Hira.

Throughout the early Islamic period, the Lakhm are almost always mentioned together with the Judham as a single unit in historical sources. They fought together under the same banner at major battles including Siffin in 657, supporting the Umayyad cause. Notable Lakhm figures included members of the Banu al-Dar clan, such as the famous Tamim al-Dari, who converted to Islam and received grants of land in southern Palestine from the Prophet Muhammad. The tribe's prestige continued into later centuries due to 'its archaic flavour, the glorious memories which it recalled' of the Lakhmid kings, with notable figures as late as the 9th and 10th centuries claiming descent from the tribe.

They controlled key trade routes and served as intermediaries between the Persian Empire and Arabian Peninsula tribes. The dynasty reached its peak under rulers like al-Mundhir III and al-Nu'man III, wielding significant influence over Arabian tribal affairs. Their kingdom was dissolved by the Sassanids in 602 CE and later conquered by Muslim Arabs during the Islamic expansion.

history:
The Lakhmids were an Arab dynasty of the Banu Lakhm tribe that ruled a vassal kingdom of the Sasanian Empire from the late 3rd to early 7th centuries CE, with their capital at al-Hira in southern Iraq. Founded by Amr ibn Adi ibn Nasr around 293-302 CE, who is identified in Pahlavi and Coptic inscriptions as 'Amr ibn Lakhm,' the kingdom served as a crucial buffer state protecting Persia's western frontier from nomadic Arab incursions. The Banu Lakhm belonged to the Qahtanite lineage, tracing their ancestry to the southern Arabian tribes that had migrated northward in pre-Islamic times. After an early period where Amr's successor Imru al-Qays I defected to Rome and converted to Christianity, little is heard of the Lakhmids until the 5th century when they re-emerged as powerful commanders in Sasanian campaigns against the Byzantines and rulers over the Arab tribes of northern Arabia.

The dynasty reached its apex under al-Mundhir III (r. 503-554), who defeated the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531, forced the Romans to pay tribute, and helped extend Sasanian influence across southern and western Arabia during his fifty-year reign. The Lakhmids were marked by their role as intermediaries between the Persian Empire and Arabian Peninsula tribes, controlling key trade routes and wielding significant influence over Arabian tribal affairs. The kingdom's decline began after al-Mundhir III's death, culminating when the last Lakhmid king, al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602), converted to Christianity and was subsequently deposed and executed by Sasanian emperor Khosrow II in 602.

Branches of the Lakhm tribe had also established themselves in Byzantine Syria from at least the 4th century, as evidenced by the Namara inscription dated to 328 CE. These Syrian Lakhm dwelt in the southern parts of the region near the tribes of Judham and Amila, forming close alliances and fictitious genealogical links that made them 'brother' tribes. During the Muslim conquest of Syria in the 630s, Lakhm tribesmen fought on both Byzantine and Muslim sides due to pre-existing tribal divisions and rivalries. The tribe's unclear allegiance during the conquest is reflected in Caliph Umar's order to exclude them from shares in war spoils around 638, though they were eventually incorporated into the Muslim polity.

Throughout the early Islamic period, the Lakhm of Syria and Palestine were almost invariably grouped with the Judham tribe, fighting together under the same banner in major conflicts including the Battle of Siffin in 657 and later Umayyad military campaigns. Along with the Judham and other tribes like Kinana and Azd Sarat, the Lakhm formed part of the Arab tribal soldiery of Jund Filastin (military district of Palestine) during the Umayyad period. Despite their integration with other tribes, their nisba (epithet) continued to carry prestige due to 'its archaic flavour, the glorious memories which it recalled' of the kings of al-Hira, with notable figures as late as the 9th and 10th centuries continuing to claim descent from the tribe.

description: The Lakhmids were a pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of al-Hirah in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries. They served as vassals to the Sassanian Empire, controlling trade routes and acting as a buffer against Byzantine influence and nomadic Arab tribes. The dynasty reached its peak under kings like al-Mundhir III and al-Nu'man III, extending influence across much of eastern Arabia including areas that would become part of modern Gulf states. They were conquered by the Rashidun Caliphate in 602 CE, ending their rule before the rise of the modern Gulf political structures.

history:
The Lakhmids were a pre-Islamic Arab dynasty that ruled the kingdom of al-Hirah in southern Mesopotamia from the late 3rd century until 602 CE. The dynasty was governed by the Nasrid ruling house of the Banu Lakhm tribe, which traced its lineage to the Qahtani confederation through Kahlan ibn Saba. The kingdom was founded by Amr ibn Adi (r. 293-302 CE), who appears in inscriptions as 'Amr ibn Lakhm' and established al-Hirah as the capital and political base. The Lakhmids functioned as a crucial client state of the Sassanian Empire, serving as a buffer between Persian and Roman spheres of influence and defending Persia's western frontier against nomadic Arab tribal incursions.

The kingdom emerged after the collapse of the Kingdom of Hatra and Palmyrene Empire, filling the resulting power vacuum in the region. Initially pagan, the Lakhmids gradually adopted Christianity, with Christianity being introduced to al-Hirah in the 4th century and the city having a bishop by 410 CE. The dynasty reached its zenith under al-Mundhir III ibn al-Nu'man (r. 505-554 CE), who reigned for approximately fifty years and achieved significant military victories, including defeating the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids at the Battle of Callinicum in 531 CE. During this period, the Lakhmids projected Sassanian hegemony across eastern Arabia, extending their influence into regions that would later become modern Gulf states.

The kingdom maintained sophisticated Persian-influenced court culture while serving as a counterbalance to the Ghassanids, the main Arab client kingdom allied with the Romans. Al-Hirah became a major center for trade and missionary activity, acting as a gateway for Christian missions to both the Sassanian world and the desert Arabs. The last Lakhmid king, al-Nu'man III ibn al-Mundhir (r. 580-602 CE), converted to Christianity, reportedly influenced by Simeon Jabara, the bishop of al-Hirah. However, this conversion was viewed unfavorably by the Sassanians, who made efforts to reconvert him.

The dynasty came to an abrupt end in 602 CE when Sassanian emperor Khosrow II deposed and executed al-Nu'man III, bringing Lakhmid territories under direct Persian control. This dissolution proved strategically disastrous for the Sassanians, as it removed their crucial Arab buffer state and inadvertently paved the way for the Arab Muslim conquest of Iraq in the 630s under Khalid ibn al-Walid. The fall of the Lakhmids was followed by the Banu Bakr's victory over the Sassanians at the Battle of Dhi Qar and ultimately contributed to the decisive Battle of Qadisiyya in 636 and the Muslim conquest of Persia. Lakhmid descendants later established the Abbadid dynasty in 11th-century al-Andalus, while other branches settled across Syria, Palestine, and Egypt during the Islamic conquests.
