folkLegends.title: The Architect's Sacrifice at Khawarnaq Palace
folkLegends.story: According to later tradition, the legendary Byzantine architect Cenmar was recruited by the Lakhmid court to design the construction of the famous Khawarnaq Palace near al-Hirah, built by Al-Nu'man I to accommodate the young Sassanian prince Bahram V. After completing the magnificent design, Cenmar was killed to prevent him from designing a similar castle for another person.

nameEtymology: The name 'Lakhmid' derives from the Banu Lakhm tribe, appearing in the late 3rd century Paikuli inscription which refers to 'Amr of the Lakhm' as one of the vassals of the Sassanian Empire. However, historians note there is no evidence that any connection between Nasrid leaders and Lakhm that existed in the 3rd century was still present in the 6th century.

name: Madhhij
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Qahtani confederation with established genealogical legitimacy through Madhhij ibn Adad
ancestorStory: Madhhij was the son of Adad ibn Zayd ibn Yashjub ibn Urayb ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba. The tribe descended from this eponymous ancestor and became one of the major Qahtani confederations.
description: Madhhij was one of the major ancient Qahtani tribal confederations of southern Arabia, historically based in Yemen and parts of Najran. The confederation played a significant role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and early Islamic conquests, with various branches migrating throughout the Arabian Peninsula. Many of their sub-tribes integrated into other tribal structures over time, with some branches settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states. While the original confederation structure no longer exists as a unified entity, genealogical connections to Madhhij remain important in Arabian tribal identity.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage
Al Kathiri claim descent from Madhhij confederation

history:
Madhhij was one of the major ancient Qahtanite Arab tribal confederations that originated in southern Arabia, specifically Yemen and parts of Najran. The confederation descended from the eponymous ancestor Madhhij, son of Adad ibn Zayd ibn Yashjub ibn Urayb ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba, establishing their clear Qahtanite lineage. Al-Hamdani's 10th-century work 'Description of the Arabian Peninsula' cited Madhhij thirty times, documenting their major branches including Nukha, Zubaid, Ruha, and Hada - the latter renowned as the best archers among the Arabs. The tribe is documented in early historical sources, including the Namara inscription of 328 CE, which records that the Nasrid king of al-Hira, Imru al-Qays bin Amr, launched raids against Madhhij territories reaching Najran.

The confederation produced notable historical figures such as Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib al-Zubaidi al-Madhhaji, the legendary Yemeni knight-king who later converted to Islam, and Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakha'i, a close companion of Prophet Muhammad and military commander under Ali ibn Abi Talib. Before Islam, Madhhij maintained their own religious practices, including bringing a tribal idol to the annual pilgrimage at the Kaaba with specific ritual chants. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic conquests, particularly in campaigns against the Persian and Byzantine empires, with Prophet Muhammad reportedly stating that many people of Paradise would come from Madhhaj, praising their noble qualities of honor, bravery, justice, and wisdom.

After the establishment of Kufa as a garrison city, many Madhhij members, including Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib, settled there. The confederation later fought against the Qarmatians in Yemen under leaders like Abul Ashira and Malik ibn Marara al-Rahawi. While the original confederation structure no longer exists as a unified entity, genealogical connections to Madhhij remain important in Arabian tribal identity. Al-Hamdani's commentaries indicate that descendants still lived in the same regions he documented over 1100 years ago, demonstrating the enduring geographical continuity of Madhhij settlement patterns in Yemen and parts of Najran. Various branches of the confederation integrated into other tribal structures over time, with some branches settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states.

migrationPath.description: Settlement in Kufa garrison city after Islamic conquests, including chief Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib

timelineEvents.title: Settlement in Kufa
timelineEvents.description: Madhhij members including Amr ibn Ma'adi Yakrib settle in newly established garrison city of Kufa

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Siffin
timelineEvents.description: Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakha'i of Madhhij serves as military leader under Ali ibn Abi Talib

folkLegends.title: The Tribal Idol at Pre-Islamic Pilgrimage
folkLegends.story: Before Islam, Madhhij had its own idol that they used to bring in the yearly pilgrimage to Kaaba. They used to make Talbiya specific to Madhhij for that idol, encircling the Kaaba several times and pleading their Madhhij Talbiya to Allah to let that idol be put around the Kaaba. The Arabs inherited the Kaaba from Ibrahim who named it Beit Allah (the house of God), but in later ages they started worshipping idols and brought them to the Kaaba to bless their idols by God.

name: Mahabib
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership based on lineage and consensus
description: The Mahabib are a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula region. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are primarily associated with nomadic pastoral activities. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and major historical events. They continue to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern era.
history: The Mahabib are a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe (Arabic: الشحوح, al-Shiḥuḥ) that inhabits the Musandam Peninsula and northern Hajar Mountains. The Shihuh tribe splits into Bani Hadiyah and Bani Shatair, with the Bani Shatair further divided into the Khanabil, Kumazarah, Mahabib, and Maqadilah subsections. The Mahabib historically occupied specific tribal territories in the mountainous regions of what is now the UAE-Oman border area, particularly around Wadi Rahabah and Wadi Halhal in the Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah. Like other Shihuh subsections, the Mahabib maintained a semi-nomadic lifestyle, practicing seasonal agriculture in stone-built mountain settlements during winter months and migrating to coastal areas for date harvests and pearl diving during warmer periods. The Arab element of the Shihuh, including the Mahabib, is believed to trace back to Yemeni immigration waves led by Malik bin Fahm groups in the second century. The tribe maintained large goat herds and practiced terraced agriculture using mountain water runoff. In recent years, the traditional Mahabib tribal areas around Wadi Rahabah have been significantly impacted by intensive open-pit mining operations from the neighboring Stevin Rock quarry, owned by the Emirate of Ras Al Khaimah, which has practically devastated much of their ancestral territory.

timelineEvents.title: Yemeni Migration Wave
timelineEvents.description: Arab element of Shihuh tribe, including Mahabib ancestors, believed to have arrived with Malik bin Fahm immigration from Yemen

name: Mahamid
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from Harb tribe through Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah lineage, established political emirate in Karak region
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage
description: The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb. Originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, they spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Maghreb. They are known for their frequent travels, emigration and instability. When their population grew and their area of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to decide their future. They rejected conquering neighboring tribes (many being from Harb) and instead chose to migrate. They established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region after moving through Dumat Al-Jandal and the Levant, where they became one of the largest tribes and allied with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba.

relations.context: Mahamid traces origins to Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, neighboring tribes were clans and sub-clans from Harb
Allied with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba and other Karak clans when they established their emirate
The Mahameed alliance with Al-Huwaitat
The Mahameed alliance with Bani Abbad
The Mahameed alliance with Bani Hamida
The Mahameed alliance with Zoubi

history:
The Mahamid are an Arab tribe that traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb lineage, part of the greater Qahtani tribal confederation through the genealogical line of Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan. Originally residing in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina, they became known for their frequent travels and migrations throughout the Arabian Peninsula. According to their tribal biography, when their population grew and their traditional areas in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz became too narrow, tribal elders gathered to decide their future. They rejected the suggestion to conquer neighboring tribes, as many were fellow Harb clans with whom they shared kinship through their fifth grandfather, and instead chose migration as their path forward.

The Mahamid moved north through Dumat Al-Jandal, where they settled for a period before continuing toward the Levant. They eventually reached and settled in the Karak region of Jordan, where their numbers were so substantial that they outnumbered the largest existing tribes in the region. There they formed alliances with local clans, most prominently with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba, and established what became known as the Emirate of Mahamid, with the Al-Amru clan providing the majority of rulers during this period. Their emirate lasted for an extended time, though some orientalist and historical sources suggest their rule was eventually opposed by different clans who united against them, allegedly due to harsh governance that prompted this resistance.

As part of the larger Harb tribal federation, the Mahamid are classified under the Banu Salim section, specifically within the Maymoon subsection, where they appear as 'Al-Mahamid (Al-Mihmadi)' in tribal genealogies. Today, the tribe has spread across multiple countries throughout the Arabian Peninsula, the Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa. In Sudan, they form part of the Northern Rizeigat confederation, with the Um Jalul clan exercising tribal leadership - notably through figures like Musa Hilal, whose Um Jalul clan has played a significant role in Sudanese tribal politics and the Darfur region.

The modern Mahamid maintain extensive geographical distribution with distinct branches: in Saudi Arabia they include the alrtuei, almatiei, almadhakir, almuhabida, and alrathea clans; in Jordan they branched into Al Abdul Dayem, Al-Shaleikh, Al-Bahri, and Ayal Abdo, forming various tribal alliances with Al-Huwaitat, Bani Abbad, Bani Hamida, and Zoubi; in Palestine they include the kiawan, alkhudur, alsawalima, alhamamada, almusaeada, alhasasana, aljeayisa, dar alearabi, al'asead, and al'ahmad branches. They also maintain presence in Syria (where the Hourani branched from them), Kuwait, Iraq, and various Arab African countries, preserving their identity as part of the Harb tribal confederation while adapting to diverse political and geographical contexts across the Arab world.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration from ancestral lands in Yemen and Hijaz region northward to Dumat Al-Jandal
Continued migration from Dumat Al-Jandal toward the Levant, settling in Karak region

timelineEvents.description: Qahtani tribes including Harb ancestors emigrated from southern Arabian Peninsula to Hejaz

timelineEvents.title: Tribal Council Decision
timelineEvents.description: Mahamid tribal elders gathered to decide future due to population growth, chose migration over conquest

timelineEvents.title: Establishment of Emirate of Mahamid
timelineEvents.description: Founded emirate in Karak region, becoming one of largest tribes with alliances to local clans

timelineEvents.title: Alliance Formation
timelineEvents.description: Formed alliances with Al-Omaro Bani Uqba and other Karak clans during emirate period

folkLegends.title: The Tribal Council Migration Decision
folkLegends.story: When the Mahamid tribe enlarged and their area of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to take opinions about what they need to do. Some suggested conquering the neighboring tribes, but this suggestion was rejected because most of those in the neighborhood were clans and sub-clans from the Harb tribe, and they were all united by the fifth grandfather. Instead, they settled their opinion on leaving and migration.

folkLegends.title: The Journey to Dumat Al-Jandal and the Levant
folkLegends.story: The last thing known of their cousins is that they went to the Dumat Al-Jandal area in the north, settled there for a period of time, and then moved towards the Levant until they reached the Karak region where they were of such a large number that they outnumbered the largest tribes in the region.

name: Mahashir
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabia
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Mahshar, an ancestor connected to the broader Qahtani tribal confederation. They established themselves in the eastern regions of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Mahashir are a tribal branch with historical presence in eastern Arabia, particularly in areas that are now part of Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally involved in pastoralism and participated in the complex tribal politics of the region. The tribe maintained connections with other Qahtani tribes and played a role in the social and economic networks of the Gulf region. They continue to exist as part of the broader tribal structure in modern Gulf states.

relations.context: Both tribes share Qahtani origins and historical presence in eastern Arabia
Historical cooperation in eastern Arabian tribal affairs

history:
The Mahashir constitute one of the main branches of the powerful Bani Khalid tribal confederation that dominated Eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. According to historical sources, the Mahashir were listed among the principal branches of Bani Khalid, which included the Al Humaid, Al Asfour, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and the Mahashir & Nahood. While the chieftainship of the confederation was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan, the Mahashir represented a significant tribal division within this broader structure that controlled the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif oases during the 16th and 17th centuries.

The genealogical origins of the Mahashir, like other Bani Khalid branches, remain disputed among Arab genealogists. Various claims link the confederation to Khalid ibn al-Walid of Banu Makhzum from Quraysh, to Banu Rabi'ah bin Amir bin Sa'sa'a from Hawazin, or to Banu Ghazia from Tayy. Some genealogists question whether they descend from Khalid ibn al-Walid himself or from his relatives, with historical texts noting the possible extinction of Khalid's direct lineage and inheritance passing to his relative Ayoub bin Salama bin Abdullah bin Al-Walid.

Under the leadership of Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid confederation, including the Mahashir, successfully expelled Ottoman forces from Eastern Arabia and established their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The confederation's domain extended across the deserts of al-Hasa and al-Qatif, with the Mahashir participating in the complex tribal politics and economic networks of the Gulf region. Like other Bani Khalid branches, the Mahashir likely adopted Shi'ite Islam over time, creating lasting animosity with the Wahhabi movement and the House of Saud.

The decline of Bani Khalid power began with Saudi invasions starting in 1793, when the Saudis invaded al-Hasa and deposed the ruling Al 'Ura'yir. Following temporary Ottoman restoration after 1818, the confederation faced defeats to tribal alliances including battles with the Mutayr and Ajman tribes in 1823, and final battles with the Subay' and Saudis in 1830 that ended their rule. Many Mahashir members, along with other Bani Khalid branches, subsequently dispersed across the Gulf region, settling in modern-day Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, UAE, Qatar, and Iraq, where their descendants continue to maintain tribal identity within the broader Gulf Arab social structure.

migrationPath.description: Early migration eastward as part of Bani Khalid confederation to dominate Eastern Arabian oases
Dispersal across Gulf region following defeat and end of Bani Khalid rule

timelineEvents.title: Bani Khalid Emirate Established

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat and End of Rule
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay' and Al Saud brings Bani Khalid rule to a close, ending Mahashir political influence in Eastern Arabia

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari Eggs
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

name: Mahris
legitimacyNotes: Well-documented lineage tracing back to Quda'a through Mahra ibn Haydan. Genetic studies show minimal admixture with >85.9% Arabian ancestry, supporting their claimed Arabian lineage.
ancestorStory: Descended from Mahra ibn Haydan ibn Amr ibn al-Hafi Quda'a ibn Malik ibn Amr ibn Murra ibn Zayd ibn Malik ibn Ḥimyar, who was from Quda'a
peakPowerEra: early Islamic period through Mahra Sultanate (lasting five centuries until 1967)
description: The Mahris are an Arab ethnographic group primarily inhabiting South Arabia, especially in the Al-Mahra Governorate in Yemen and the island of Socotra. They are descended from Mahra bin Haydan and are known for their unique Mahri language, a Modern South Arabian language. They played significant roles in early Islamic conquests of North Africa and Spain, and maintained the Mahra Sultanate for five centuries until 1967. They are famous for their Mahri camels and have distinctive genetic markers showing minimal admixture with >85.9% Arabian ancestry.

relations.context: Mahri lineage traces back through Quda'a confederation

history: The Mahris are an ancient Arab tribe descended from Mahra ibn Haydan ibn Amr ibn al-Hafi Quda'a ibn Malik ibn Amr ibn Murra ibn Zayd ibn Malik ibn Ḥimyar, tracing their lineage to the Qahtani branch of Arabian genealogy. They are primarily concentrated in the Al-Mahra Governorate of eastern Yemen and the island of Socotra, with populations also found in Dhofar Governorate of Oman, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE. The Mahris are distinguished by their preservation of the Mehri language, a Modern South Arabian language closely related to Soqotri and Shehri, with approximately 115,200 Mahri speakers globally as of recent estimates. During the early Islamic period, a large delegation from Mahra led by Mahri bin Al-Abid visited Prophet Muhammad, who presented Islam to them and they embraced the faith. The Prophet wrote a letter granting them protection and declaring they shall not be harmed. The Mahris played a crucial role in early Islamic military history, participating in the conquest of North Africa and Spain under the command of Amr ibn al-As. They were granted lands in the newly conquered territories, including the region of Jabal Yashkar east of Al-Askar and later the Mahra quarter (Khittat Mahra) in Fustat, the new capital of Egypt established in 641 CE. Their commander Abd al-sallam ibn Habira al-Mahri was promoted to lead the conquest of Libya and became the first Muslim leader of Libya. The Mahris maintained political autonomy through the Mahra Sultanate, which lasted for five centuries until its abolition in 1967 when it was absorbed by the People's Republic of South Yemen. The sultanate came under British protection in 1886 as part of the Aden Protectorate. The Mahris are renowned for their distinctive Mahri camels, famous for their speed, agility, and endurance, which were integral to their military success during the Islamic conquests and became known as Mehari camels in North Africa. Genetically, the Mahris show minimal admixture with over 85.9% Arabian ancestry, and on Socotra, around 71.4% carry the basal haplogroup J*(xJ1,J2). Christianity was introduced to Socotra in the 5th century, with Cosmas Indicopleustes reporting a 'multitude of Christians' around 518 AD, and vestigial Christianity may have survived until the 16th century according to Portuguese sources.

migrationPath.description: Mahri forces granted lands in Fustat after participating in Islamic conquest of Egypt, establishing the Mahra quarter
Abd al-sallam ibn Habira al-Mahri led Muslim army in conquest of Libya, defeating Byzantine forces

folkLegends.title: Descendants of the ʿĀd Kingdom
folkLegends.story: The Mehri people are traditionally considered descendants of the ʿĀd Kingdom and blood relatives of the Thamud. According to Islamic genealogies, the forefather of the Mehri people was Ya'rub, the son of Qahtan, grandson of the Islamic prophet Hud, and ancestor of the Himyarite, Qataban and Sabaean kingdoms. Ya'rub (or, by alternate accounts, Ya'rub's son), is sometimes credited with the invention of the Arabic language.

nameEtymology: Named after Mahra bin Haydan, their ancestral founder according to tribal genealogy

name: Makatim
legitimacyNotes: Traditional merchant family authority through trade networks and commercial influence
description: The Makatim are a prominent merchant family historically based in Kuwait and other Gulf ports. They were involved in the traditional Gulf economy of pearling, trade, and maritime commerce. The family established trading networks across the Arabian Gulf and Indian Ocean routes. They remain active in modern Gulf business communities, particularly in Kuwait.

relations.context: Traditional merchant relationship with Kuwait's ruling family

history: The Makatim are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab Arab tribe, which has origins in the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and areas of the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin, the Makatim had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a population of some 7,250 people, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin. The Bani Kaab tribe, including the Makatim subsection, became involved in the complex political dynamics of southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, their allegiances shifted over time - by the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had aligned with the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. The research also indicates there is a residential area called Khudra al-Makatim (Green of the Makatim) in the Wilayat of Al-Khaburah in Oman's North Al Batinah Governorate, with approximately 300 inhabitants as of 2010, suggesting continued Makatim presence in their traditional territories.

migrationPath.description: Migration from interior Arabia to Kuwait to establish maritime trading operations

name: Manai'i
legitimacyNotes: Established through genealogical descent and traditional tribal recognition in Qatar and eastern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Manee, from whom they derive their tribal name. They are considered part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabian origin.
description: The Manai'i are a Qahtani tribe historically based in Qatar and parts of the eastern Arabian Peninsula. They have been involved in traditional Gulf activities including pearling, fishing, and maritime trade. The tribe has maintained its presence in modern Qatar where members continue to play roles in contemporary society. They have historical trade partnerships with other tribes including the Dahaminah.

relations.context: Co-habited in settlements along Wadi Qor at turn of 19th century
Both tribes present in Qatar and UAE regions

history: The Manai'i are a Qahtani tribe that traces its lineage to an ancestor named Manee, from whom they derive their tribal name. They are part of the broader Qahtani tribal confederation of southern Arabian origin and have been historically based in Qatar and parts of the eastern Arabian Peninsula. At the turn of the 19th century, members of the Manai'i tribe were documented as co-habiting with approximately 150 members of the Dahaminah tribe in settlements along the Wadi Qor area, which encompasses parts of what is now Ras Al Khaimah and Fujairah in the UAE. The tribe has been traditionally involved in Gulf maritime activities including pearling, fishing, and trade, maintaining historical partnerships with other tribes including the Dahaminah. The Manai'i have maintained their presence in modern Qatar where tribal members continue to participate in contemporary society.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic period migration from Najd toward the Arabian Gulf coast as part of broader Qahtani tribal movements
Settlement in Qatar region to participate in emerging maritime trade and pearling economy

timelineEvents.title: Manai'i Settlement in Wadi Qor
timelineEvents.description: Manai'i tribal members documented as co-habiting with Dahaminah tribe in settlements along Wadi Qor

name: Manasir
legitimacyNotes: Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation throughout the centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in the adjoining areas. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish a system of mutual respect, rights and obligations.
ancestorStory: According to the current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor is called Mansur and belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad. Multiple explanations are offered: some claim their apical forefather is Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin; others trace their origin to the Kawahla saying they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim; still others insist they are descendants of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir.
traditionalEconomy: The riverain Manasir pursue small scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile. Their most important cash crop is a wide variety of dates. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses and used to float wood from the region of Atbarah. The Bedouin portion herds goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys.
description: The Manasir constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. They inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile and call their homeland Dar al-Manasir. Similar to their neighbouring tribes, the upstream Rubatab and the downstream Shaiqiyah, the Manasir are an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language. Unlike other riverine tribes of Sudan, a considerable part of their population lives as Bedouins in the adjacent Bayuda Desert. The nomadic life of herding their stock of goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys is however limited for many to the rainy season, coinciding with the annual inundation of the Nile.

relations.context: Allied in Abu Dhabi
By the 1920s, the Awamir fought alongside the Manasir, Duru and Bani Qitab in conflicts around Buraimi and Abu Dhabi town.
Involved in tribal fighting and raids in the 1920s
Close relationship, fought together against Wahhabis
Under the Bani Yas, they were considered a subsection of the Manasir

history: The Manasir represent one of the most significant tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula, with two distinct branches that are not to be confused: the Sudanese Manasir of Northern Sudan and the Al Manasir of the United Arab Emirates. The UAE Manasir, long closely associated with Abu Dhabi's ruling Bani Yas, were traditionally one of the most important tribes in the emirate of Abu Dhabi. Unlike their settled counterparts the Bani Yas, the Manasir were entirely nomadic Bedouin who roamed between Buraimi and Qatar, from the Persian Gulf coast to Liwa. They were fiercely independent and did not necessarily consider themselves dependents of the Sheikh of Abu Dhabi, though they cooperated closely with the Bani Yas. The tribe consisted of several subsections including the Al Bu Mundir (Kaabara, Mani, Marashid, Matawaah, Midahima), Al Bu Rahamah (Al Bu Khail, Tarsif, Tararifah and Wabran), Al Bu Shaar (Ghawainam, Rashaiyid and Al Bu Thuwaibit), and Al Rabeea. At the turn of the 20th century, some 1,400 Manasir roamed this tribal area, growing to about 4,000 by the 1950s, with 2,800 living in Abu Dhabi emirate. They subsisted through date farming, pearling, camel breeding, and operating camel trains for trade between oases and coastal towns. The Manasir were exempted from paying the customary date tax to the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, reflecting their independent status. They played crucial military roles, frequently forming important parts of Abu Dhabi's fighting force alongside the Bani Yas in conflicts throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries, including the 1834 attack on Sharjah, the 1848 expulsion of Wahhabis from Buraimi, and the 1870 Battle of Dhank. However, they also acted as kingmakers, notably in 1928 when Baluchis and Manasir carried out a coup that killed Sheikh Saqr bin Zayed Al Nahyan, paving the way for Sheikh Shakbut bin Sultan Al Nahyan's rule. As traditional revenue sources declined in the late 1920s with the pearl trade collapse, many Manasir turned to working for oil companies like ARAMCO and Qatar Petroleum, though they typically worked only long enough to meet immediate needs before returning to their pastoral lifestyle.

migrationPath.from: Desert regions between Buraimi and Qatar
migrationPath.description: Seasonal summer migration to Liwa for date harvest, maintaining areesh palm frond houses
Winter migration pattern, with some sections wintering in Semeih and Abu Dhabi

name: Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir)
legitimacyNotes: Bedouin branch of Manasir with traditional tribal authority based on lineage and desert expertise
ancestorStory: The Bedouin Manasir are part of the larger Manasir tribal confederation, maintaining the traditional nomadic lifestyle and desert customs of their ancestors.
description: The Manasir al-Badiyah are the Bedouin branch of the larger Manasir tribal confederation, traditionally based in the desert regions between Abu Dhabi and Oman. They maintained nomadic pastoralist traditions longer than their settled relatives, specializing in camel herding and desert trade routes. The tribe has historical presence in the Liwa oasis region and the Empty Quarter, with traditional grazing rights across tribal territories. Today they are integrated into UAE society while maintaining cultural traditions and tribal identity.

relations.context: Historical alliance with ruling family of Abu Dhabi
Shared desert territories and grazing routes
Traditional marriages between Bedouin tribes

history:
The Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir) are the nomadic branch of the larger Manasir tribal confederation of Northern Sudan, distinct from their riverine counterparts along the Nile. According to oral traditions recorded in tribal genealogies, the Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible ancestors within the Qahtani lineage. Some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arab Qahtaniin lineage, while others trace lineage to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, or to descendants of a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts. Critical research suggests the current Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities who established common genealogical recognition for mutual rights and territorial claims.

The Bedouin branch (Manasir al-Badiyah) is organized into four main clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab, who traditionally shared the grazing grounds of Abisba' and Sani. These nomadic clans maintained a pastoralist lifestyle centered on herding goats, sheep and camels in the Bayuda Desert adjacent to the Nile. Their nomadic life was typically limited to the rainy season, coinciding with the annual inundation of the Nile, when they would move their livestock to desert valleys for grazing.

During dry seasons, the Manasir al-Badiyah clans engaged in seasonal migrations to various territories according to traditional grazing agreements. Some clans migrated west to the Kababish territory (Khala' Kabushiyah), others moved to the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in Ga'ali country, or to Rubatab territories. This system of seasonal movement allowed them to maintain their herds while respecting territorial boundaries of neighboring tribes. Early travelers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and similarities with Nubian burial customs can still be observed, reflecting the complex cultural amalgamation that formed the tribe.

Like other Sudanese tribes, the Manasir do not have unique tribal marks (Shilukh), but copy the scarification patterns of either their upstream or downstream neighboring tribes. The current division between Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) reflects their adaptation to different ecological niches along the Nile and in the adjacent desert regions, with the Bedouin branch maintaining longer adherence to traditional nomadic practices.

description: The Manasir al-Nil are a branch of the broader Manasir tribal confederation that settled along the Nile River in Sudan. They represent a Sudanese riverain community that maintains genealogical connections to the Arabian Peninsula Manasir tribes. While geographically distant from the GCC, they share common ancestry with UAE-based Manasir tribes and maintain cultural and lineage ties to the broader Manasir tribal network.
history:
The Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir) are a Sudanese Arab riverine tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Northern Sudan, calling their homeland Dar al-Manasir. This fertile river oasis extends approximately 130 kilometers along the Nile between the upstream Rubatab tribe near Abu Hamed and the downstream Shaiqiyah tribe past Birti village. The tribe represents an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and speaks Arabic as their first language, while maintaining a dual lifestyle that combines settled riverine agriculture with seasonal nomadic pastoralism in the adjacent Bayuda Desert.

The current Manasir community formed through voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish mutual rights and obligations, uniting themselves in land claims against neighboring tribes. Earlier travelers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and strong similarities of their burial customs with Nubians can still be observed.

The tribe is organizationally divided into two main branches: Riverain Manasir (Manasir al-Nil) comprising ten clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Farei'ab, al-Hamdatiab, and al-'Amasib; and Bedouin Manasir (Manasir al-Badiyah) consisting of four clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, al-Muleikab, and al-Kagbab who share grazing grounds in the Bayuda Desert. During dry seasons, some clans migrate to the desert area of the Kababish tribe to the west, others to the grazing grounds of Wad Hamid in the Ga'ali Country or to the Rubatab Country.

The riverain Manasir pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with dates as their primary cash crop sold on the national market. They are renowned for their skill in building mud houses (Galus) and historically floated wood from the Atbarah region. The 1993 population was estimated at 30,000 residents with an additional 17,000 non-resident Manasir, though these figures remain vague regarding Bedouin populations. The community faced displacement due to the Merowe Dam project flooding their ancestral homeland.

name: Maqadilah
history: The Maqadilah is a subsection of the Bani Shatair, one of the two main divisions of the Shihuh tribe that inhabits the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) region of the UAE and Oman. The Bani Shatair division, which includes the Maqadilah along with the Khanabil, Kumazarah, and Mahabib subsections, is centered around Kumzar at the tip of the Musandam Peninsula, while the other main Shihuh division, the Bani Hadiyah, is mostly found on the western coast including Khasab, Oman. The Arab element of the Shihuh tribe, which forms the majority and includes the Maqadilah, is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen that brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century. At the turn of the 20th century, the entire Shihuh tribe numbered approximately 21,500 people, with some 14,500 settled on the coast and 7,000 inhabiting the mountainous interior, though tribal members would travel seasonally between both domains. The Shihuh, including the Maqadilah subsection, practiced a distinctive seasonal lifestyle that differed markedly from desert Bedouin culture. During winter months, they formed agricultural communities in stone-built mountain settlements, channeling water run-off to irrigate stepped fields, while in warmer months they migrated mainly to work during the date season at locations like Khasab, Dibba, and the Batinah coast of Oman, also participating in pearl fishing seasons. They maintained large herds of goats as their primary source of trade surplus. The Shihuh were historically difficult to govern and fiercely maintained their independence, frequently coming into conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah while generally being more ready to accept the suzerainty of Muscat. Their economic needs crossed borders, with Shihuh often holding property subject to different rulers, and they played significant roles as kingmakers in settlements like Kalba and in shaping the complex territorial arrangements of towns like Dibba.

name: Marar

The text primarily focuses on other entities that share the name 'Marar', including a Hindu caste in Kerala, India, and various individuals with the surname Marar.
history:
The Marar (singular Al Marri) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that forms a subsection of the Bani Yas confederation. The tribe traditionally follows the Hinawi alignment and practices Maliki Sunni Islam. They became part of the tribal confederation brought together by Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') of Abu Dhabi in the late 19th century, indicating their incorporation into the broader political structure of what would become the UAE.

In 1890, under Zayed's leadership, the Marar joined with the Manasir tribe to conduct a series of raids on the coast between Abu Dhabi and Dubai, successfully capturing 400 camels and 100 horses. This demonstrates their active role in the inter-tribal conflicts and raiding economy that characterized the region during this period. The Marar had established settlements in Dubai, but their relationship with the ruling Al Maktoum family became strained.

A significant migration occurred in 1891 when approximately 400 Marar men, feeling ill-treated by Sheikh Rashid bin Maktoum of Dubai, ended the pearling season by sailing their boats to Sharjah and settling there permanently. This mass migration created a diplomatic crisis that required three years of bitter negotiations, arbitrated by the British Resident, to settle the resulting financial claims and counter-claims between Dubai and Sharjah.

By the turn of the 19th century, the Marar had become distributed across multiple emirates within what is now the UAE. They maintained 200 houses in the Layyah area of Sharjah, 40 houses in Abu Dhabi, and 30 in Dubai. The tribe was divided into distinct socio-economic groups: the Bedouin branch consisted of about 70 families in Liwa who were closely related to the Mazari, while the urban Marar were traditionally pearl divers, reflecting the dual economy of the region.

Inter-tribal tensions continued into the 20th century, with conflicts arising between the Marar and Sudan tribes in Sharjah in 1927. This led to another migration when 150 Marar members left Sharjah to settle in Deira, Dubai, where an area still bears their name (transliterated as Al Murar or Al Marar). The tribe maintained significant political connections through strategic marriage alliances with UAE ruling families, including Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum's mother and wife, both of whom were Marar, demonstrating their continued importance in the political fabric of the emerging UAE state structure.

description: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe settled the western area of Rams, specifically the Bani Humud neighbourhood, between Rams and Hulaylah. They lived in traditional khaimahs - small stone-walled houses with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs. The Maraziq population left the area by the early 1950s and are now dispersed throughout the UAE.

history: The Maraziq (singular Al Marzooqi or Al Marzouqi) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that became traditionally associated with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe is believed to originally have migrated from Arabia, though the specific origins and timeframe of this migration are not documented in available sources. Upon settling in the Rams area, the Maraziq took the name of the district they inhabited, which had originally been populated by Iranians. The tribe established themselves in the western area of Rams, specifically in what became known as the Bani Humud neighbourhood or freej, located between Rams and Hulaylah. They shared this settlement area with another tribe, the Tunaij. The Maraziq lived in traditional khaimahs - small stone-walled houses topped with gabled barasti (palm-frond) roofs, which were characteristic of the late Islamic township of Rams. By the early 1950s, the Maraziq population had left the Rams area entirely and became dispersed throughout the UAE. Notably, the Bani Humud area of Rams continued to be known as the Marzouqi neighbourhood even after the Maraziq families had departed, indicating their lasting association with the location.

name: Mariandyni
history:
The Mariandyni were an ancient tribe of Thracian origin located in northeastern Bithynia in ancient Anatolia (modern-day Turkey), not in the Arabian Peninsula. They inhabited the region between the Sangarius and Billaeus Rivers, east of the Thyni and Bithyni tribes. According to ancient sources, their territory was called Mariandynia, with Pliny referencing a 'Sinus Mariandynus' (Mariandynian Gulf) along their coast. The tribe's ethnic origins remain disputed among ancient historians - Strabo believed they were a branch of the Bithynians, while Herodotus distinguished them from other Thracian groups and noted their armor resembled Paphlagonian rather than Bithynian equipment.

The chief city in their territory was Heraclea Pontica, whose inhabitants eventually reduced the Mariandyni to a state of collective serfdom similar to the Cretan Mnoae or Thessalian Penestae. According to modern researcher John Hind, the Mariandyni may have initially ceded coastal territory to Heracleot colonists peacefully, seeking protection from the Bebrykes and Paphlagones. Over time, the Herakleots acquired the Lycus Valley and locked the Mariandyni into agricultural villages as a dependent people, also subject to impressment as fleet rowers.

In the early 6th century BC, they remained independent, paying tribute directly to Lydian king Croesus and warring with Heraclea. Under Persian rule following Cyrus the Great's conquest of Lydia in 546 BC, the Mariandyni formed part of the third Persian satrapy. The Persian Empire included diverse peoples from across its vast territories, with the Mariandyni listed among the various ethnicities that served in Persian armies alongside Persians, Medes, Assyrians, and many other subject peoples.

The tribe is connected to Greek mythology through Mariandynus, their eponymous hero, and figures like Titias, who was said to be either a son of Zeus or the eldest son of Mariandynus. Titias was eventually deified by the Mariandynians and had a city named after him. His sons included Priolaus, Lycus and Mariandynus, with some sources naming Bormus instead of Priolaus. The Mariandyni were known for their mourning songs performed in memory of fallen heroes, particularly after Priolaus was killed in battle against the Bebrycians, which the Mariandyni won with assistance from Heracles.

name: Mawali
legitimacyNotes: Islamic legal status as non-Arab Muslims with tribal protection
description: Mawali refers to non-Arab Muslims who converted to Islam and became clients of Arab tribes, particularly during the early Islamic expansion period. In the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, they included Persians, other non-Arab peoples, and freed slaves who gained protection and social status through tribal affiliation. They played important roles in early Islamic administration, scholarship, and commerce. As a social category rather than a tribe, the mawali system largely dissolved over time as Islamic society evolved and tribal distinctions became less rigid.
history: The Mawali tribe represents a distinct Arab tribal confederation based in northwestern and central Syria, primarily in the regions of Idlib and Hama. The tribe's origins are obscure, though they were reputedly descended from a large troop of former slaves or branched off from the Beshar tribe, another group originally composed of former slaves. By the 16th century, they had evolved into a heterogeneous grouping combining semi-nomadic sheepherders and camel-raising nomads of different origins. The Mawali derived their prestige from their leading family, the Al Fadl (later known as Al Abu Risha), whose chiefs had been formally recognized as amir al-arab (commander of the Bedouin) of the Syrian steppe since the Ayyubid period in the 13th century. When the Ottomans conquered Syria in 1516, they initially sought to force the tribe's submission through multiple military campaigns. Unable to bring the Mawali to heel, the Ottoman government co-opted them in 1574, granting their emir a ceremonial feather and stipend of 6,000 Venetian ducats, after which the Al Fadl emirs became known as Abu Risha ('father of the plume'). The Mawali controlled territory spanning from Hama and Homs in the west to the Palmyra oasis in the east and the Euphrates River valley in the north, giving them control over key trade routes between Baghdad and Damascus and between Aleppo and Iran. Their strongholds included the fortified town of Salamiyah. The Mawali's dominance began to waver in the mid-17th century, and by the late 18th century, they faced mass migrations of Anaza Bedouin into the Syrian steppe. In the 1770s, regular conflicts with the Hassana tribe gradually pushed the Mawali out of their traditional desert abodes into the Jabal Zawiya and Jabal al-A'la highlands around Hama. Their failed 1786 revolt under emir Muhammad al-Khurfan, which included raids on villages around Hama, resulted in a devastating Ottoman counterattack that massacred hundreds of tribesmen and sealed their loss of dominant position in the Syrian steppe. Despite their diminished capacity, Mawali sheikhs continued to receive official titles and policing duties from the government, particularly the hereditary office of serkeder urban (chief of the Bedouin) of Hama, held by the Khurfan family of the Masharifa clan. During the French Mandatory period (1920-1946), the Mawali had become primarily sheepherding pastoralists with significantly shorter seasonal migrations compared to camel-raising tribes. They joined the Syrian nationalist cause, participating in the Great Syrian Revolt of 1925-1927 and engaging in frequent warfare with the pro-French Hadidiyin tribe throughout the 1920s and early 1930s. By the mid-20th century, the tribe had fully settled, inhabiting around sixty villages in the Idlib and Hama governorates. During the Syrian Civil War beginning in 2012, the Mawali were among the first Arab tribes to rebel against the government, forming the Mawali Brigade under the Free Syrian Army and fighting in confrontations around Maarat al-Nu'man, Hama, Aleppo, and Raqqa under the leadership of Sheikh Jamal al-Shayish.

name: Mazari
legitimacyNotes: The name 'Mazari' appears to be used by multiple unrelated tribal groups, suggesting it may be a toponymic designation rather than indicating true blood kinship between these groups.
description: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups rather than a single unified tribe. The text identifies at least two separate Mazari tribes: an Emirati Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates and a Baloch tribe settled in Rajanpur and Sindh, Pakistan. These appear to be separate entities that share only the name 'Mazari', likely derived from different etymological origins.
history: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups that share only the name 'Mazari' but have completely different origins and histories. The most prominent is the Baloch Mazari tribe, one of the oldest Baloch tribes whose name derives from the Balochi word 'mazar' meaning 'Tiger'. This tribe consists of two main branches: the Balachani of Baloch origin and the Kurd of Kurdish origin. The Mazari conquered an area known as Tuman Mazari, encompassing parts of present-day Sindh's Kashmore District, Punjab's Rojhan Tehsil, and surrounding areas, with their stronghold at Rojhan-Mazari in Pakistan's Rajanpur District. During British rule, the Mazari reached their golden era when their territory extended from Bhong to the Sulaiman Mountains and from Kashmore to Giamul, covering approximately 8,000 square kilometers. The tribe fought significant battles against the Sikh Empire, including the 1836 attack on Mithankot led by Sardar Karam Khan under Chief Mir Bahram Khan. Peace was achieved in 1838 when Mir Bahram Khan visited Lahore with 12,000 tribesmen and was received by Maharaja Ranjit Singh at Lahore Fort. Notable leaders included Sir Nawab Imam Buksh Khan Mazari, who received British honors for his loyalty and services on the western frontier. His descendants continued as tribal chiefs, with the most recent being Mir Balakh Sher Mazari (1928-2022), the 22nd Sardar and 7th Mir of the Mazaris, educated at Aitchison College and Oxford University. His brother Sherbaz Khan Mazari became a prominent Pakistani politician, serving as caretaker Prime Minister in 1993. Separately, the Emirati Mazari (Al Mazrouei) are an Arab Bedouin tribe that forms part of the Bani Yas confederation. According to the research, they are considered part of the Bani Yas tribal confederation originally from Bani Tamim lineage, settling primarily in Abu Dhabi and the Liwa Oasis where they owned date gardens and engaged in pearl diving and pastoralism. The 1968 Abu Dhabi census recorded 1,287 members of the Mazari subsection among the Bani Yas population of 5,884. The Emirati Mazari supported Zayed bin Khalifa against Wahhabi incursions in the 19th century and later played important roles in UAE politics and oil industry development.

name: Mazari (Baloch)
legitimacyNotes: Baloch tribal identity with traditional leadership structure
ancestorStory: The Mazari trace their lineage to Mazar, a prominent figure among the Baloch tribes. They are part of the larger Baloch tribal confederation that migrated from Balochistan to the Arabian Peninsula.
description: The Mazari are a Baloch tribal group that forms part of the broader Baloch diaspora in the Arabian Peninsula. Historically, they migrated from the Balochistan region and settled in various parts of the Gulf states, particularly maintaining their distinct Baloch cultural identity. Like other Baloch tribes in the region, they have traditionally been involved in pastoralism and have integrated into the social fabric of Gulf societies while preserving their tribal structure. They remain an active community within the larger Baloch population in the GCC states.
history:
The Mazari are a Baloch tribe of Pakistan whose name derives from the Balochi word 'mazar' meaning 'tiger.' They are one of the oldest Baloch tribes, consisting of two primary branches: the Balachani of Baloch origin and the Kurd of Kurdish origin. Their stronghold is Rojhan-Mazari in Rajanpur District, Punjab, near the borders of Balochistan, Sindh and Punjab. The area they conquered became known as Tuman Mazari, encompassing most of Kashmore District in Sindh, Chatha Baksha, Mazari District, and all of Tehsil Rojhan in Punjab.

Under British rule, the Mazari chief controlled territory from Bhong to the Sulaiman Mountains and from Kashmore to Giamul, covering approximately 8,000 square kilometers. The tribe engaged in significant warfare against the Sikh Empire from 1836-1838. In 1836, Mithankot was attacked by Mazari forces under Sardar Karam Khan, brother of Chief Mir Bahram Khan, in response to threats from Maharaja Kharak Singh. The Sikhs retaliated by burning Rojhan under Diwan Sawan Mal Chopra, though the Mazari evacuated in time and continued guerrilla warfare from the Sulaiman Mountains. The conflict ended in 1838 when Mir Bahram Khan visited Lahore with 12,000 tribesmen, meeting Maharaja Ranjit Singh at Lahore Fort and establishing peace.

The British colonial period saw the rise of prominent Mazari leaders including Sir Nawab Imam Buksh Khan Mazari, who became the second chief and was knighted for his services, particularly in managing tribal affairs and assisting with the western frontier. His descendants continued this legacy, with the tribe producing notable political figures in modern Pakistan including Sherbaz Khan Mazari, who served as caretaker Prime Minister. The Mazari speak Sulimanki Balochi and maintain their traditional tribal structure with the chief holding the title of Mir and Tumandar. The tribe consists of 4 large sub-clans and 60 small sub-tribes, with the Mazari Chief family belonging to the Balachani Clan.

name: Mazari (Emirati)
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical claims and settlement patterns in UAE territories
description: The Mazari are a tribal group found within the United Arab Emirates, representing one of the smaller tribal entities in the federation. They are primarily settled in specific regions of the UAE and maintain traditional tribal social structures. Like many UAE tribes, they have adapted to the modern state system while preserving their tribal identity and customs.
history:
The Mazari (singular: Mazrouei) are an Emirati tribal group that forms part of the Bani Yas confederation, historically representing one of the most significant Bedouin components of this tribal alliance. According to tribal traditions, the Mazari claim descent from the Bani Tamim tribe, an Adnanite lineage tracing back to the northern Arabian tribal system. The tribe had already embraced Islam prior to their arrival in what is now the UAE, though the specific period of their migration remains unrecorded in available sources.

Historically, the Mazari were primarily concentrated in the Liwa Oasis, which served as their traditional homeland and where they established themselves as the principal Bedouin section of the Bani Yas confederation. British records from the early 20th century documented their substantial presence, with 142 families recorded in 1951 and 151 families in 1954, totaling approximately 315 houses at the turn of the 20th century according to J.G. Lorimer's surveys. Beyond Liwa, significant Mazari populations were also documented in Al Khan (Sharjah) with around 300 individuals, and in the Adhen and Asimah areas with approximately 500 members.

Originally nomadic herdsmen, the Mazari underwent a significant economic transformation during the early 20th century when declining camel values forced them to transition from pastoralism to settled agriculture in the oasis regions. Despite this shift to sedentary life, they maintained seasonal pastoralism and participated actively in the pearl diving industry, operating boats launched from Bandar Rudaim. The UK Memorial identified them as the principal property owners among the six Bani Yas tribes in their region, indicating their substantial economic influence within the confederation.

The Mazari played crucial military roles during 19th-century regional conflicts, contributing significant manpower to support Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa against Wahhabi incursions. In 1848, when Sheikh Saeed bin Tahnun Al Nahyan repelled Wahhabi forces from Buraimi Oasis, the Mazari, alongside the Manasir tribe, positioned themselves south of Abu Dhabi to intercept any relieving forces from Nejd. However, early 20th-century tribal conflicts in the Dhafra region created internal divisions within the Mazari, splitting them between those seeking Saudi protection and others who migrated to Abu Dhabi and its surrounding islands. In the modern UAE, the Mazari have maintained prominent positions in government and industry, with notable figures including Yousef bin Omar Al-Mazrui (federal petroleum minister until 1994 and ADNOC chairman) and Ahmad bin Fadhil Al-Mazrui (wali of Liwa until 1955).

name: Mazrui
description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

relations.context: Opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar in the 18th century

history:
The Mazrui are an Arab tribal confederation with documented presence across multiple regions of the Arabian Peninsula and East Africa. The research reveals distinct but related Mazrui lineages: the East African branch that established political control over parts of Kenya from the 18th-20th centuries, and the UAE branch that forms a major subsection of the Bani Yas confederation. The East African Mazrui, originating from Oman, governed Mombasa and other coastal areas throughout the 18th century, maintaining opposition to the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled Zanzibar. They demonstrated their resistance by allying with Portuguese forces on at least one occasion to attack Stone Town, showing their willingness to align with European powers against rival Arab dynasties.

The UAE Mazari branch represents a separate but potentially related lineage within the broader Mazrui tribal network. According to the Amir of the Mazari at Adhen and Ghayl, this branch traces its origins to the Bani Tamim tribe and had already embraced Islam prior to settling in the UAE region at an unspecified period. Originally belonging to the Ghafiri faction and following the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence, the Abu Dhabi Mazari generally came to adhere to the Maliki school. The Liwa Oasis served as their primary homeland, where they owned significant date gardens and practiced seasonal pastoralism, moving with their camel herds to grazing areas in Dhafra or Khatam during winter months.

Conflicts in the 19th and early 20th centuries significantly impacted Mazari tribal unity and territorial control. An outbreak of hostilities between Bedouin tribes in the Dhafra region created divisions within the Mazari, with some groups seeking Saudi protection while others migrated to Abu Dhabi and its islands. After Sheikh Hamdan bin Zayed Al Nahyan arranged a truce, the Mazari returned to Dhafra, but continued fighting allowed Abdelaziz Ibn Saud of Saudi Arabia to increase his influence over the tribes and exact zakat taxation from them. These shifting allegiances and internal schisms later formed part of the Saudi claims that led to the Buraimi Dispute.

In the modern UAE, the Mazari have achieved significant political and economic prominence within Abu Dhabi's government structure. From the 1960s through the 1990s, members of the Mazari, alongside the Suwdan, held numerous senior positions within the Abu Dhabi emirate and at the federal level. The ruler's wife is a member of the family, and several individuals currently occupy senior roles, including positions as chairman of ADNOC, members of the Abu Dhabi Executive Council, and directors of various authorities and development councils. The East African Mazrui similarly played important roles in resistance movements, actively opposing British colonial rule alongside the Kikuyu and Kamba peoples when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century.

Migration from Liwa region to Ghayl during economic hardships of the 1940s

name: Misaid
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and local consensus
description: Al Misaid is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula with presence in Gulf regions. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins and traditional territories. They maintain tribal identity and kinship structures typical of Arabian Peninsula tribes. Their current status and activities within the GCC context require further historical verification.
history: Based on the existing data, the Misaid are documented as a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribal confederation, which has historical presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab were traditionally associated with territories around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, as well as areas east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, the Misaid had adopted a settled lifestyle, unlike some other nomadic Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin, becoming part of a total Bani Kaab population of approximately 7,250 members. The tribe's political allegiances shifted significantly during the 19th and 20th centuries, reflecting the fluid nature of tribal alliances in southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they participated alongside other Bani Kaab sections in an alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas confederation, successfully driving Wahhabi forces from the Buraimi region. However, by the 1940s, tribal loyalties had transformed dramatically when the Bani Kaab, under their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah, declared allegiance to Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Misaid, as part of the broader Bani Kaab structure, supported Saudi claims to the region and actively opposed British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. This involvement in regional geopolitical conflicts positioned the Misaid within the larger struggles that ultimately shaped modern Gulf state boundaries and tribal allegiances in the UAE-Oman border regions.

name: Miyadilah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage and pastoral leadership
description: The Miyadilah are a small Arabian tribal group with limited documented historical presence in the Gulf region. Their exact origins, territorial base, and historical significance remain poorly documented in major tribal genealogical sources. They appear to have been associated with pastoral activities in the Arabian Peninsula but lack prominent historical records of major political or economic influence in the GCC states.
history: The Miyadilah are documented as a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, which has significant presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab, of which Miyadilah forms part, are associated with the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and territories in the Emirates east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, the Miyadilah had settled along with most other Bani Kaab subsections, distinguishing them from the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin branches. The broader Bani Kaab confederation, including the Miyadilah, became involved in significant regional conflicts and alliances. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nayhan of the Bani Yas to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. By the 1940s, their allegiances had shifted, with the tribe supporting Saudi interests during the period when oil concessions became a major regional issue. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Bani Kaab, presumably including the Miyadilah subsection, supported the Saudi incursion and were involved in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

name: Miyalisah
history: The Miyalisah are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, which has historical roots in the area around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and in areas of the UAE east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin who remained nomadic, the Miyalisah had settled by the turn of the 20th century. As part of the Bani Kaab confederation, they became involved in the complex political alliances of southeastern Arabia. In 1844, they allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nayhan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, by the mid-20th century, the Bani Kaab including the Miyalisah had shifted their allegiance to support Saudi Arabia. During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, they supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

name: Mizahamiyin
ancestorStory: Descended from Muzahim, though specific genealogical details vary in tribal accounts.
description: The Mizahamiyin are a tribal group whose historical presence and exact lineage remain poorly documented in available sources. They appear to be connected to the broader tribal networks of the Arabian Peninsula, though their specific role in Gulf politics and economy is not well established in mainstream historical accounts. Their current status and territorial associations require further genealogical research to establish definitively.
history: The Mizahamiyin are a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation present in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab, including the Mizahamiyin, are traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions of the UAE east of Buraimi, including the Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Unlike some other Bani Kaab subsections such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin who remained nomadic, the Mizahamiyin had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a broader settlement pattern that saw most Bani Kaab subsections become sedentary. By 1844, the Bani Kaab tribe, including the Mizahamiyin, had allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a confederation that drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. However, allegiances in this region were notably fluid over time. By the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had shifted their alignment to the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah confirming to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab supported the Saudi incursion and were involved in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies, demonstrating their continued alignment with Saudi interests in this critical territorial dispute.

name: Mudar
legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Quraysh
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
subTribes.description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

history:
Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Mudar traced descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing it among the foundational ancestors of the 'northern Arabs' (Adnanites) as distinct from the southern Qahtanite tribes. The confederation divided into two major branches through Mudar's sons: Ilyās (also called al-Yās) and ʿAylān (Qays ʿAylān), from whom descended influential tribes including Kināna, Quraysh, Tamīm, Hudhayl, and Asad.

The earliest historical evidence for Mudar appears in South Arabian inscriptions. The al-῾Irāfa 1 inscription (c. 440 CE) from Zafar, capital of Himyar, records a Mudarite chief traveling to Mas'al Jumh to meet the Himyarite king, indicating Mudar was then under Himyarite hegemony. The Maʾsal 2 inscription (521 CE) mentions Mudar providing reinforcements to a Himyarite expedition into Sasanian territory under Banu Tha'labah leadership. During this period, Mudar tribes occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions.

Mudar rose to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, who migrated to Syria. At various times they were under the suzerainty of southern Arabian powers, particularly the Kingdom of Kinda whose rulers bore the title 'king of Maʿadd' or 'king of Mudar and Rabīʿa'. Following the decline of Himyarite authority and collapse of Kindite rule, Mudar tribes emerged as autonomous actors in central Arabia. During the later sixth century, sections of Mudar expanded into northern Arabia and Mesopotamia, where the name Diyār Muḍar came to designate a distinct tribal territory.

With the rise of Islam, many Mudar tribes entered the Muslim polity, though not simultaneously or uniformly. The Quraysh, descended from the Mudarite Kināna branch, became particularly significant as the tribe of Prophet Muhammad. In early Islamic historiography, 'Mudar' increasingly functioned as a political label within the broader tribal dualism opposing northern Arabs (Mudar and Rabīʿa) to southern Yemeni (Qahtanite) tribal groupings, a division that became especially prominent after the first Islamic civil wars. Together with Rabīʿa, Mudar formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd, representing a major political and cultural bloc in Arabian tribal politics.

folkLegends.title: Division of Nizār's Inheritance
folkLegends.story: According to genealogical narratives, when Nizār died, his sons divided their heritage, resulting in Mudar gaining the epithet 'the Red' (al-ḥamrāʿ) because he received his father's red tent while his brothers Rabīʿa, Anmar, and Iyad received other portions of the inheritance.

name: Mudhar
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Mudhar ibn Nizar, one of the primary divisions of Adnanite Arabs
ancestorStory: Mudhar was one of the sons of Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan, representing one of the major tribal confederations of northern Arabia. The Mudharis became one of the two main divisions of Adnanite tribes.
description: Mudhar was one of the great tribal confederations of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, comprising numerous tribes descended from Mudhar ibn Nizar. They were primarily based in the Najd and northern Arabian Peninsula, representing the northern Adnanite Arab division in opposition to the Rabiah confederation. The Mudhari tribes included major groups like Quraish, Tamim, and Qays, who played crucial roles in early Islamic conquests and later Arab expansion. While the confederation as a political entity is historical, many of its constituent tribes remain active throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region.

relations.context: The two main branches of North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes
Mudhar descended from Adnan through Nizar

history: Mudhar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia, tracing descent from Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan. Together with Rabi'a, it formed one of the two largest groupings of northern (Adnanite) Arabs, as opposed to the southern Qahtanite tribes. The confederation occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions in pre-Islamic Arabia. The earliest epigraphic mention of Mudhar appears in the al-'Irafa 1 inscription (c. 440 CE) discovered at Zafar, capital of the Himyarite Kingdom, where a Mudhari chief is recorded traveling to Mas'al Jumh to meet the Himyarite king, indicating Mudhar was under Himyarite hegemony at the time. A second inscription, Ma'sal 2 (June 521 CE), mentions Mudhari reinforcements led by the Banu Tha'labah supporting a Himyarite expedition into Sasanian territory. According to genealogical tradition, Mudhar divided into two principal branches: Ilyas (or al-Yas) and Aylan (Qays Aylan), from which descended influential tribes including Kinana, Quraysh, Tamim, Hudhayl, and Asad. Following the decline of Himyarite authority and collapse of Kindite rule, Mudhar tribes emerged as autonomous actors in central Arabia. During the later sixth century, sections of Mudhar expanded into northern Arabia and Mesopotamia, where the region became known as Diyar Mudhar. The confederation maintained close associations with Mecca and its sanctuary, with several religious offices connected to the pilgrimage attributed to Mudhari families. With the rise of Islam, Mudhar tribes entered the Muslim polity at different times, and 'Mudhar' increasingly functioned as a political label in the tribal dualism opposing northern Arabs (Mudhar) to southern Arabs (Yemen/Qahtan). This division became especially prominent during the Qays-Yaman wars, including major conflicts in Palestine and Transjordan between 793-796 CE, where Mudhari tribes led by Amir ibn Umara al-Murri fought against Yamani confederations and their Abbasid allies. During the Abbasid era, many members of Banu Hanifa and related Mudhari tribesmen migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, where they dominated the gold-mines of Wadi Allaqi near Aswan, eventually forming the Banu Kanz dynasty that ruled the Egyptian-Nubian frontier region.
