name: al-Muleikab
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on ancestral lineage and established settlement patterns
description: Al-Muleikab is a tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to establish their specific origins, traditional territories, or detailed genealogical connections. They appear to maintain their tribal identity in the modern era, though their current distribution and activities require further research to establish definitively.
history: The existing data indicates that al-Muleikab is documented as one of the Bedouin clans (Manasir al-Badiyah) of the Manasir tribe in Northern Sudan, specifically inhabiting the Bayuda Desert adjacent to the Fourth Cataract of the Nile. According to tribal genealogy, they belong to the nomadic division of the Manasir people and share grazing grounds of Abisba' and Sani with three other Bedouin Manasir clans: al-Khabra, al-Hamamir, and al-Kagbab. The Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible lineages according to oral tradition - some claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), others from Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla, and still others from al-Hankab clan descendants of King Sabir. However, the provided research materials do not contain any additional information about al-Muleikab specifically, focusing instead on broader Arab history, general tribal structures, and other Arab groups. Critical research suggests the Manasir, including al-Muleikab, represent a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, Arab emigrants, and recruited tribal minorities. During the dry season, al-Muleikab and other Bedouin Manasir clans migrate to various grazing areas including the Kababish territory to the west, Wad Hamid grazing grounds in Ga'ali country, or Rubatab territory, following traditional nomadic patterns of herding goats, sheep and camels. The current research materials do not provide specific information about their Arabian Peninsula origins, migrations to Sudan, historical events, or detailed genealogical connections beyond what is already documented.

timelineEvents.title: Population Census

name: Mutair (Ikhwan forces)
legitimacyNotes: Part of the Ikhwan religious-military movement under Ibn Saud's leadership
ancestorStory: Descends from Mutayr ibn Ghutayf of the Ghatafan tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through the Hawazin branch of Qays Aylan.
description: Mutair is a major Najdi Bedouin tribe that became one of the most prominent members of the Ikhwan movement in early 20th century Saudi Arabia. Historically based in central Najd, they played a crucial role in Ibn Saud's military campaigns to unify Saudi Arabia between 1910-1930. The tribe later rebelled against Ibn Saud in the famous Ikhwan Revolt (1927-1930) before being subdued. Today, Mutair remains one of Saudi Arabia's largest and most influential tribes, with members across the Gulf states.

history:
The Mutayr (Muṭayr) is a major Arab tribe with genealogical origins traced to Ghatafan, descendants of Ishmael according to 15th-century historian Ahmad al-Qalqashandi. The tribe's original homelands were in the northern Hejaz near Medina, but in the 17th century they undertook a large-scale eastward migration into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes including Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. This migration brought them into rivalry with the Utaybah tribe, who had also recently moved into central Arabia from Hejaz. By the early 20th century, Mutayr had become the dominant nomadic tribe of the Al-Qaseem region, making them strategically important in the conflict between the Al Saud and Al Rashid clans.

Under the leadership of Faisal Al-Dewish (Al-Duwaish), Mutayr played a pivotal role in the Ikhwan movement that would shape modern Saudi Arabia. In 1912, Ibn Saud began settling nomadic tribes in agricultural villages called hijras, where they were indoctrinated into puritanical Wahhabi Islam and transformed into religious warriors known as the Ikhwan. The most important Mutayri settlement was al-Artawiyya at the northern edge of the Dahna desert. Al-Dewish enthusiastically led Mutayr's participation in the Ikhwan, providing Ibn Saud with crucial military support including the 1924 conquest of Hejaz.

Tensions arose when Ibn Saud, having completed his territorial expansion by 1925, forbade further raiding into British-protected territories of Iraq, Transjordan, and Kuwait. In late 1927, Mutayr tribesmen launched unauthorized cross-border raids, particularly targeting an Iraqi police fort at Busaiya which was part of their traditional grazing grounds. This defiance escalated into the famous Ikhwan Revolt (1927-1930), as Al-Dewish and other Ikhwan leaders accused Ibn Saud of religious laxity and abandoning true Wahhabi principles.

The revolt culminated in the Battle of Sabilla on March 29-31, 1929, where Ibn Saud's modernized forces equipped with machine guns and vehicles decisively defeated the traditional camel-mounted Ikhwan warriors, killing approximately 500 rebels. Al-Dewish fled to Kuwait in October 1929 but was detained by the British and handed over to Ibn Saud. He died in Riyadh prison on October 3, 1931, reportedly from a heart condition. The rebellion was finally suppressed on January 10, 1930, and surviving Ikhwan were incorporated into regular Saudi units, later forming the foundation of the Saudi Arabian National Guard. Today, Mutayr remains one of Saudi Arabia's largest tribes, with members settled across the kingdom and Kuwait.

migrationPath.description: Large-scale eastward migration from Hejaz highlands into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes

name: Mutayr
legitimacyNotes: The tribal lineage appears to be authentic blood lineage supported by DNA testing showing shared haplogroups and common ancestry among members. The tribe has embraced genetic genealogy to study their genealogy using modern technologies.
ancestorStory: According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

history: The Mutayr tribe is a major Arab tribal confederation with origins in the northern Hejaz near Medina, belonging to Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays Aylan ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. According to genealogist Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), Mutayr descends from Ghatafan, who are considered descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA testing has confirmed that male Mutayr participants share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-chromosome lineage from the J1 haplogroup network. The tribe's name 'Mutayr' derives from the al-Mutarinah section and means 'abundant in rain' or 'generous.' The tribe is organized into three main branches: Bani Abdullah (the largest, comprising over half the tribe), Al-Ulwa (also known as Alwa), and Braih (Birayh), each divided into numerous clans. During the 17th century, Mutayr underwent a major eastward migration from their original Hejaz highlands into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes like Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. This migration established Mutayr as the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem region. The tribe played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under the leadership of Faisal Al-Dewish (Al-Duwaish). In 1912, Ibn Saud undertook to settle nomadic tribes in newly created villages called hijras, indoctrinating them into puritanical Islam and creating the Ikhwan brotherhood. Mutayr's most important settlement was al-Artawiyya at the northern edge of the Dahna desert. Under Al-Dewish's leadership, Mutayr provided crucial military support to Ibn Saud, participating in the 1920 attack on Kuwait at al-Jahra (repelled under British pressure) and the 1924 conquest of Hejaz. However, Al-Dewish later led an Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud, claiming the king had abandoned true faith by refraining from attacking European-ruled Iraq and Syria. Ibn Saud defeated the rebels at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929, after which Al-Dewish fled to British-controlled Iraq but was handed back and imprisoned until his death. According to Bedouin oral tradition, Mutayr women are credited with creating the battoulah (Gulf burqa), a metallic-looking face mask worn by married women in the Persian Gulf region, around 1870 CE when a Mutayr woman used it to escape an unwanted marriage proposal. Originally nomadic Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in Saudi Arabian cities and towns, establishing up to 400 villages across the country, particularly in Riyadh, Medina, and the central region, with significant populations also in Kuwait.

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Battoulah Face Mask
folkLegends.story: According to Bedouin oral tradition, around 1870 CE a Mutayr woman created the battoulah (Gulf burqa) to escape an unwanted marriage proposal. A father was preparing to marry his daughter off to a man she did not desire. Unable to refuse openly, the girl decided to create a solution. She cut a piece of black cloth with uneven eyeholes to conceal her face, attaching it securely. To complement the look, she added a loose white cloth around her legs for additional coverage. When she appeared in front of her potential suitor, her unusual appearance shocked him, and the marriage proposal was rejected. This invention gained popularity within the Mutayr tribe and later spread to other tribes in the Arabian Peninsula.

nameEtymology: The tribe's name 'Mutayr' derives from the al-Mutarinah section and means 'abundant in rain' or 'generous'

name: Al-'Ulwa
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority through lineage connection to Mutayr confederation
description: Al-'Ulwa is a major branch of the Mutayr tribal confederation, one of the largest Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally based in the Najd region of central Saudi Arabia and have historically been involved in nomadic pastoralism and tribal warfare. The tribe played significant roles in various historical conflicts and migrations throughout the Arabian Peninsula. Today they remain an active tribal group within Saudi Arabia with members also found in other Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliance during Saudi state formation

history:
Al-'Ulwa is one of the three major branches of the Mutayr tribal confederation, alongside Banu Abdullah and Braih. According to genealogical sources including Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA studies have confirmed that Mutayr members share a common ancestor and predominantly belong to the J1 haplogroup network. Al-'Ulwa is internally divided into seven main clans: Al-Muhahah, Al-Jiblan, Al-'Awanah, Al-Rukhman, Al-Mala'bah, Al-Sahabah, and Al-Bara'sah.

Mutayr's original homelands were the highlands of northern Hejaz near Medina. At some point in the 17th century, the tribe began a large-scale migration eastwards into central Arabia, displacing many other Bedouin tribes in the area, such as Harb and Anizzah who were forced to move northwards. This migration brought them into rivalry with Utaybah, who inhabited roughly the same areas as Mutayr in central Arabia. Because Mutayr were the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem, which was the main bone of contention between the clans of Al Saud and Al Rashid vying for control of central Arabia in the early 20th century, they came to play an important role in the history of Arabia during that era.

The tribe was led by Faisal Al-Dewish, who frequently changed sides in the conflict between the two Saudi Arabian leaders. In 1912, Abdul Aziz Ibn Saud undertook to settle the nomads in newly created villages (hijras), where the bedouins were indoctrinated into a puritanical form of Islam and became warriors for Ibn Saud's cause as the Ikhwan. Faisal Al-Dewish, who was from the Al-'Ulwa branch, led the Ikhwan movement enthusiastically, providing Ibn Saud with crucial military support. The most important Mutayri settlement was al-Artawiyya, at the northern edge of the Dahna desert.

In 1920 Al-Dewish led an attack by the Ikhwan on Kuwait at al-Jahra, and later joined other sections of the Ikhwan in the conquest of the Hejaz on behalf of Ibn Saud in 1924. However, Al-Dewish and other Ikhwan leaders subsequently led a rebellion against Ibn Saud, seeking to take over the newly conquered provinces for themselves and claiming that Ibn Saud had abandoned the true faith. Ibn Saud defeated the rebels at the Battle of Sabilla in northeastern Nejd, and Al-Dewish sought refuge with the British in Iraq before being handed over to Ibn Saud. He was imprisoned and died not long afterwards. Today, nearly all members of the tribe are settled in the cities and towns of Saudi Arabia, making up to 400 villages across the country, with large populations also in Kuwait.

ancestorStory: Descended from Abdullah, an ancestor within the Mutayr tribal confederation. The branch maintains its distinct identity while being part of the larger Mutayr structure.
description: Bani Abdullah is a prominent branch of the Mutayr tribal confederation, one of the major Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally based in Najd and have historically practiced nomadic pastoralism across central and eastern Arabia. As part of Mutayr, they participated in the major tribal movements and conflicts of the 18th and 19th centuries, including interactions with the Saudi state. Today they maintain their tribal identity while many members have settled in modern GCC states, particularly Saudi Arabia and Kuwait.

history: The research provided contains no information about Bani Abdullah of the Mutayr tribal confederation. While the documents extensively cover other Arabian tribes including Bani Shaiba (custodians of the Kaaba from Quraysh), Bani Kaab (from Oman and UAE), various Yemeni tribal confederations like Hamdan, Kinda, Madhhaj, and Himyar, and early Islamic migration patterns, there is no mention of Bani Abdullah as a branch of Mutayr anywhere in the source material. The research confirms the existence of major tribal structures throughout the Arabian Peninsula and provides detailed genealogical information for numerous tribes, but does not contain any specific historical, genealogical, or geographical information about this particular tribal branch. Without direct research evidence about Bani Abdullah's origins, migrations, conflicts, territorial control, or historical development within the Mutayr confederation, no verified historical narrative can be constructed beyond acknowledging the absence of documentation in these sources.

name: Braih
legitimacyNotes: Recognized sub-tribe within the Mutayr confederation with traditional grazing and territorial rights
ancestorStory: Braih is considered one of the founding branches of the Mutayr tribal confederation. The lineage traces back to the early divisions within Mutayr that established the major sub-tribal units.
description: Braih is a sub-tribe of the larger Mutayr confederation, one of the major Bedouin tribal groups of central Arabia. Historically based in the Najd region of Saudi Arabia, they participated in the traditional nomadic pastoral economy and tribal raids. As part of Mutayr, they played a role in the early Saudi state formations and the unification of Saudi Arabia. The tribe remains active today with members settled across Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states.

relations.context: Historical alliance as part of Mutayr's support for Saudi unification

history: Braih (also spelled Birayh) is one of the three main divisions of the Mutayr tribal confederation, alongside Bani Abdullah and 'Ulwa. According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan, who are descendants of Ishmael. Modern DNA testing has confirmed that Mutayr members share the same haplogroup with most Y-chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network, supporting their common ancestry. Mutayr's original homelands were in the highlands of northern Hejaz near Medina, but in the 17th century the tribe began a large-scale eastward migration into central Arabia, displacing other Bedouin tribes such as Harb and Anizzah who were forced northward. As part of this migration, Braih became established in the Najd region where they developed a rivalry with the 'Utaybah tribe who had also moved into central Arabia from Hejaz. Because Mutayr dominated the Al-Qaseem region, which was contested between the Al Saud and Al Rashid clans in the early 20th century, the Braih and other Mutayr divisions played crucial roles in Arabian politics. Under Faisal Al-Dewish's leadership, they joined Ibn Saud's Ikhwan movement in 1912, settling in villages like al-Artawiyya and becoming warriors for the Saudi cause. The Braih participated in major military campaigns including the 1906 Battle of Rawdat Muhanna where Mutayr warriors killed Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, and the 1924 conquest of Hejaz. However, Al-Dewish later led an Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud, which was defeated at the 1929 Battle of Sabilla. Today, the historically nomadic Braih are largely settled across Saudi Arabia's cities and towns, with significant populations also in Kuwait.

name: Mysians
history: The Mysians (Ancient Greek: Μυσοί) were an ancient Indo-European people of northwestern Anatolia who lived in the region of Mysia, in what is now modern-day Turkey. According to the research, they were part of a larger group called the Western Mushki, which also included the Phrygians. The Mysians are classified as 'Coastal Phrygians' in contrast to the inland Phrygians, and they may have been part of the hypothetical Armeno-Phrygian linguistic group that migrated into Anatolia during the Bronze Age collapse around 1200 BCE. The research indicates that the Mysians, along with the Phrygians, may have descended from the Mushki people who entered Asia Minor during this period of widespread migration and upheaval. They were closely related to their neighbors the Phrygians, sharing similar cultural and possibly linguistic characteristics. The name appears in ancient sources in connection with the Trojan War era, where Mysians are mentioned as allies of Troy. During the Roman period, the name 'Moesi' was adopted by Roman administrators to refer to Balkan peoples, specifically chosen because of the historical connection between the Anatolian Mysians and the Trojan-era Dardanians, both of whom lived in proximity to each other in ancient Anatolia. This administrative naming was part of Roman efforts to create ideological connections between their Trojan origin myths and contemporary Balkan peoples.

migrationPath.description: Possible migration as part of the Mushki/Western Mushki movement into Asia Minor during the Bronze Age collapse, though some scholars dispute this migration theory

timelineEvents.title: Bronze Age Collapse Migration
timelineEvents.description: Possible entry into Asia Minor as part of the broader Mushki migration during the Bronze Age collapse period

nameEtymology: The name Mysians derives from their settlement in the region of Mysia in northwestern Anatolia. The Roman period saw the name adapted as 'Moesi' for administrative purposes in the Balkans, chosen specifically because the historical Mysians lived close to the Trojan-era Dardanians in Anatolia.

name: Nahood
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
description: Al-Nahood is a tribal name mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation is limited. Like many smaller Arabian tribes, they were traditionally engaged in nomadic pastoralism across the desert regions. Their current status and specific historical role in Gulf region politics remains unclear due to limited documented presence in major historical events.
history:
The Nahood are documented as one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, appearing alongside other major clans including Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir. The Bani Khalid confederation traditionally claims descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, the companion of Muhammad and esteemed general, though Arab genealogists have questioned this claim and suggested alternative lineages including descent from Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, or from Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe. The chieftainship of the Bani Khalid has traditionally been held by the Al Humaid clan, not the Nahood.

As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, the Nahood participated in the rise to power in eastern Arabia during the 15th-18th centuries. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid clan in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces from Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions and established their emirate with the capital at Al-Mubarraz. The confederation's territory extended from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south, wielding significant political influence over Najd in central Arabia. The Bani Khalid maintained ties with tribal members who had settled in Nejd and cultivated clients among Najdi town rulers.

The Nahood, as part of the Bani Khalid confederation, became staunch enemies of the rising Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. When the emir of Uyayna adopted the ideas of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the Khalidi chief ordered him to cease support and expel Ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who subsequently moved to Dir'iyyah and joined forces with the Al Saud. The Bani Khalid attempted to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to stop Saudi expansion but failed. The Saudis eventually invaded the Bani Khalid domain in al-Hasa and deposed the ruling Al 'Ura'yir in 1789, though sources also mention 1793 as the date of conquest.

Following the Ottoman reinstatement of Bani Khalid rulers in 1818 after overthrowing the Al Saud, the confederation faced new challenges. The Bani Khalid were no longer the potent military force they once were, with tribes such as the Ajman, Dawasir, Subay' and Mutayr encroaching on their desert territories. Internal quarrels over leadership further weakened them. Despite forging an alliance with the 'Anizzah tribe, they were eventually defeated by tribal alliances along with the reestablished Al Saud. A decisive battle with an alliance led by the Mutayr and Ajman tribes in 1823, followed by another battle with the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, ended Bani Khalid rule. Many members of the confederation, including likely Nahood families, subsequently dispersed to various regions including Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, and other parts of Saudi Arabia, where descendants continue to reside today.

migrationPath.to: Iraq, Kuwait, Bahrain, Palestine, Lebanon, other parts of Saudi Arabia
migrationPath.description: Dispersal following military defeats and end of Bani Khalid rule, with confederation members including likely Nahood families settling across various regions

timelineEvents.description: Ottomans conquered al-Hasa and al-Qatif, reinstating members of Al 'Uray'ir as rulers after overthrowing Al Saud

timelineEvents.title: Battle with Mutayr-Ajman Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Decisive defeat of Bani Khalid by alliance led by Mutayr and Ajman tribes

timelineEvents.title: Final Defeat by Subay'-Saudi Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Battle with Subay' and Al Saud alliance ended Bani Khalid rule permanently

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Desert Bustard
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

name: Naqbiyin
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with clear territorial associations and historical presence in the region dating back at least 300 years in some locations.
description: The Naqbiyin is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that has been influential in the tribal politics of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates. By the early 20th century, they had settled throughout the region including Khor Fakkan, Kalba, Dibba, and Fahlain, with a population of approximately 1,800 people primarily engaged in agriculture. They are known for their strategic settlements and close relationship with the Qawasim rulers.

relations.context: Habus gradually bought up Naqbiyin territory in Wadi Naqab after the Naqbiyin fell foul of local rulers around 1695-1740
The Naqbiyin have traditionally been considered close to the Qawasim rulers and were given strategic lands by the Al Qasimi as protection against the Sharqiyin
The Naqbiyin were frequently in conflict with the Sharqiyin on the East coast in the Shamailiyah area
Both tribes settled together in Khatt village

history: The Naqbiyin (النقبي, singular Al Naqbi) is a UAE tribe that derives its name from the Wadi Naqab area of Ras Al Khaimah, where they were originally concentrated. The tribe dispersed from this agriculturally rich wadi after disputes with pre-Al Qawasim local rulers, likely between 1695-1740, with many members relocating to settlements in Sharjah emirate including Dibba, Khor Fakkan, and Kalba. By around 1800, their former territory in Wadi Naqab had been acquired by the Habus tribe. The Naqbiyin established strategic settlements throughout the region, including Khatt, Daftah in Wadi Ham, and Fahlain on the Jiri plain. In 1903, Lorimer documented Fahlain as consisting of 60 Naqbiyin houses with 2,000 date palms, primarily engaged in agriculture. The tribe maintained close relations with the Qawasim rulers, with their settlements in Khatt and Fahlain forming part of Hassan bin Rahma's sheikhdom when he signed the 1820 General Maritime Treaty with the British. On the east coast (Shamaliyah), the Naqbiyin were frequently in conflict with the neighboring Sharqiyin tribe but played influential roles in tribal politics. They were considered the 'powers behind the throne' when Kalba gained independence from Sharjah in 1937 under Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi. The Al Qasimi rulers strategically granted them lands as protection against the Sharqiyin, recognizing their presence in Khor Fakkan even before the Portuguese sack of 1506. The tribe's settlement in Khatt reportedly occurred over a 300-year period, and the Al Naqbi Tower still stands in the village today. By the early 20th century, their total settled population across the region numbered approximately 1,800 people. The Naqbiyin were also present in the Wadi Madha area alongside the Madhahana, Bani Hamid, Bani Sa'ad, and Dahwahir tribes.

migrationPath.description: Dispersal from agriculturally rich wadi after disputes with pre-Al Qawasim rulers

nameEtymology: The tribe derives its name from the Wadi Naqab area of Ras Al Khaimah, from which they dispersed

name: Nawaljiyin
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established family lines
description: The Nawaljiyin are a tribal group mentioned in Arabian Peninsula genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about their specific origins, territorial base, and role in Gulf history remains limited in widely available sources. Further research in local archives and oral traditions would be needed to establish their precise historical significance and current status.
history:
The Nawaljiyin are a subsection of the Bani Kaab (بني كعب), an Arab tribe with historical presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab are traditionally associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and regions of the UAE east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The tribe comprises multiple subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat. The Nawaljiyin distinguished themselves from the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections by settling permanently by the turn of the 20th century, forming part of a population of approximately 7,250 people, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin.

By 1844, the Bani Kaab, including the Nawaljiyin, had formed a tribal confederation with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas and other interior tribes of southeastern Arabia. This alliance successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi, demonstrating the strategic importance of the Bani Kaab in regional politics. The tribe maintained rivalries with the Na'im and Bani Qitab during this period, with the Bani Kaab pushing sections of the Na'im northward as far as the Jiri plain.

In the fluid political landscape of southeastern Arabia, the Bani Kaab's alliances shifted significantly over the following century. By the 1940s, the tribe had aligned with Saudi Arabia, with their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' Despite historical rivalries, the Bani Kaab and Na'im temporarily cooperated in the 1940s when oil concessions became a prospect, opposing the oil companies, Trucial Sheikhs' rule, the Sultan of Muscat, and British authority alike.

During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Nawaljiyin, as part of the Bani Kaab confederation, supported the Saudi incursion led by Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis. The tribe participated in armed resistance against British-backed forces, specifically fighting against the Trucial Oman Levies during this contentious period that would ultimately reshape the political boundaries of the region.

timelineEvents.title: Alliance with Bani Yas
timelineEvents.description: Bani Kaab allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in confederation to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi

timelineEvents.title: Settlement Period
timelineEvents.description: Nawaljiyin among the settled sections of Bani Kaab by turn of 20th century

timelineEvents.title: Saudi Alliance
timelineEvents.description: Bani Kaab shifted alliance to Saudi Arabia, with Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring territories as Saudi

timelineEvents.title: Buraimi Dispute
timelineEvents.description: Bani Kaab supported Saudi incursion and fought against Trucial Oman Levies

name: Northern Bani Qitab
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage authority through Qitab ancestry
ancestorStory: Northern branch descended from Qitab, distinguished from southern Bani Qitab settlements through geographic and political divisions.
description: Northern Bani Qitab represents the northern territorial branch of the broader Bani Qitab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula. They were traditionally distinguished from their southern counterparts through geographic settlement patterns and pastoral territories. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin lifestyle through camel and sheep herding across northern Arabian territories. Their historical significance lies primarily in their role within the broader Qitab tribal structure rather than as independent political actors.

relations.context: Both tribes shared northern Arabian pastoral territories

history: The Northern Bani Qitab represents the nomadic northern territorial branch of the broader Bani Qitab tribal confederation, distinguished from the settled southern section through geographic settlement patterns and lifestyle. At the turn of the 19th century, the tribe consisted of some 2,100 nomadic Bedouin (of whom 600 were fighting men) and 2,700 settled people. The Northern branch's dar (district) stretched from south of the Buraimi oasis to the eastern foothills of the Hajar Mountains, the Jiri plain north of Sharjah, and the fertile area around Sharjah's inland oasis town of Dhaid. The Northern Bani Qitab mostly settled in the inland towns of Dhaid and Al Falayah. The tribe was highly influential in Trucial States politics, often involved in disputes between rulers. In 1905, they built a fort at Wadi Hatta and harassed caravans passing to the Omani Batina coast, leading to conflict with the Na'im tribe and intervention by Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan. They maintained strong loyalty to Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla of Umm Al Qawain, and when he died suddenly of pneumonia in 1922, the Bani Qitab under Sheikh Muhammed bin Ali Al Huwaidan were instrumental in ensuring the smooth succession of Sheikh Abdulla bin Rashid Al Mualla II, providing 100 men to secure the town. Throughout the 1920s-1940s, they remained involved in tribal conflicts across the interior, fighting in Buraimi and participating in raids with various tribes including the Awamir, Duru, Manasir and Bani Yas. The tribe supported Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi in his various political struggles, playing a key role in his accession as Ruler of Kalba in 1937. Effectively controlling the interior of the Northern emirates, they were courted by various rulers for oil concession access, maintaining a decisive force of 200-250 armed, mounted Bedouin even into the 1940s. The singular form Al Ketbi became a common family name in the Northern UAE, with notable members including Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi (third wife of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan) and various political figures and analysts.

migrationPath.description: Original migration from Yemen following Qahtani tribal movements northward into central Arabia
Temporary movement north during Islamic conquests to participate in campaigns in Iraq and Syria
Displacement during Qarmatian conflicts, seeking refuge in Mesopotamian borderlands

timelineEvents.description: Bani Qitab built a fort at Wadi Hatta and started harassing caravans, leading to conflict with Na'im tribe

timelineEvents.title: Umm Al Qawain Succession Support

timelineEvents.title: Support for Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi

timelineEvents.title: Interior Peace Negotiations
timelineEvents.description: Rulers of Abu Dhabi, Dubai and Sharjah met to broker peace after tribal fighting involving Bani Qitab across the interior

timelineEvents.title: Sharjah-Ajman War Participation
timelineEvents.description: Joined with Sultan in Sharjah's war with Ajman due to influence of Abdulrahman of Heerah

timelineEvents.title: Kalba Ruler Installation
timelineEvents.description: Played key role in Khalid bin Ahmad Al Qasimi's accession as Ruler of Kalba

name: Obaidli
legitimacyNotes: Established merchant and pearling family with traditional leadership in coastal communities
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Obaid Allah, an ancestor who settled in the Gulf coast region. The name reflects their descent from this progenitor.
description: The Obaidli are a tribal group historically present in the Gulf coastal regions, particularly in areas that are now part of the UAE and Qatar. They were traditionally involved in pearling, fishing, and coastal trade activities. The tribe has maintained its presence in Gulf society and continues to be recognized in the modern GCC states. They represent one of the smaller but established tribal entities in the region's complex social fabric.

relations.context: Both tribes settled in similar Gulf coastal areas

history: The Obaidli are documented as one of the authentic Arab Huwala tribes, part of a tribal confederation that migrated from the Arabian Peninsula to the Iranian coast of the Persian Gulf between the 13th-19th centuries. According to historical sources, they were among the 'Arabs of the Eastern Coast' (عرب الساحل الشرقي) who settled primarily in the Shibkoh region of southern Iran, spanning from Bandar Kanj to Bandar Kangan. The Obaidli, alongside the Qawasem, Hammadi, Al Nasur, and Bani Tamim, formed the core of the actual Arab Huwala confederation, distinct from the Achomi Persian populations of the same region. They maintained their Sunni Islamic faith and Arabic tribal identity while residing under various Persian dynasties. During the 18th-19th centuries, they participated in the maritime trade networks of the Persian Gulf, particularly during the period when the Al Qasimi confederation controlled much of the southern Iranian coast around Bandar Lengeh. The restrictive economic policies imposed by Reza Shah Pahlavi in the 1930s, along with centralization efforts and social reforms, prompted most Huwala Arabs including the Obaidli to migrate back to the Arabian Peninsula. They settled across various Gulf states, particularly in the UAE and Qatar, where they integrated into the emerging modern state structures while maintaining their distinct tribal identity and heritage as authentic Arabs of Persian Gulf maritime tradition.

migrationPath.description: Initial migration as part of Huwala confederation to Iranian coast
Return migration due to Reza Shah's restrictive policies

timelineEvents.title: Qasimi Recognition
timelineEvents.description: Iranian Zand dynasty recognized Qasimi rule over Bandar Lengeh region where Huwala tribes including Obaidli were established

timelineEvents.title: British Anti-Piracy Campaign
timelineEvents.description: Qasimis defeated by British, affecting Huwala tribal territories in southern Iran

timelineEvents.title: Mass Huwala Migration
timelineEvents.description: Reza Shah's centralization policies and economic restrictions trigger return migration of Huwala Arabs to Arabian Peninsula

name: Otaibah
legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

relations.context: Otaibah is descended from and represents the largest remaining alliance of the Hawazin confederation
Conflicts over pastures in central Najd during 18th century, with Otaibah eventually superseding Anazzah
Mutayr aided by Qahtan fought against Anazzah in 18th century, later superseded by Otaibah
Centuries-long struggle in Najd and Hejaz, with conflicts in 1881-1882 when Otaibah plundered Harb camps
Muhammad's foster mother Halimah al-Sa'diyah was from Banu Sa'd, a subdivision of Hawazin

history:
The Otaibah (عتيبة) is one of the largest Arabian tribes, descended from the pre-Islamic Hawazin confederation and tracing their lineage through the Adnani branch to Ishmael. According to 8th-century genealogist Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, the name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. The tribe's genealogical chain extends through Hawāzin, Manṣūr, ʿIkrima, Khasafa, Qays ʿAylān, Mudar, Nizar, Ma'add to Adnan. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance under the Otaibah banner.

During the Ottoman period, the tribe engaged in the centuries-long struggle for control of Najd and Hejaz. In 1470, they were attacked by Sharif Muhammad ibn Barakat in eastern Ta'if. By the 18th century, the Otaibah had superseded the Mutayr and Qahtan tribes to become the dominant force in central Najd. During the 19th century, they were involved in multiple conflicts including supporting Ibrahim Mohammed Ali against Abdullah bin Saud in 1816, and engaging in warfare with Ibn Rashid throughout the 1870s-1880s. In 1872, Otaibah chief Muslit bin Rubayan attacked western settlements of Riyadh, leading to retaliatory raids by Saud bin Faisal.

In the early 20th century, under leaders Sultan ibn Bjad and Eqab bin Mohaya, the Otaibah joined the Ikhwan movement and were instrumental in Ibn Saud's conquest of Al-Hasa, Ha'il, Al-Baha, Jizan, Asir, Ta'if, Mecca, and Jeddah. However, the tribe split during the 1928 Ikhwan revolt - Sultan ibn Bjad led the rebellious Barka branch while Eqab bin Mohaya and Omar bin Rubayan's Roug faction remained loyal to King Abdul Aziz. The rebellion was crushed at the Battle of Sabilla on 29 March 1929. The tribe established 19 Ikhwan settlements including Al-Ghata'at, Al-Sajir, and Sinam.

In 1979, Otaibah member Juhayman al-Otaybi led the seizure of the Grand Mosque of Mecca with 500+ militants, holding it for two weeks before being defeated and executed. Today the tribe is divided into three main branches: Barga (برقا), Rog (روق) including Al-Hafah (الحفاه), and Banu Saad (بنو سعد), with many members serving in Saudi Arabia's armed forces. The tribe remains one of the most significant in the Arabian Peninsula, maintaining its historical territories in Najd and Hejaz.

migrationPath.description: After most Hawazin clans departed to other lands, remaining groups consolidated in central Najd under Otaibah leadership

folkLegends.title: The Banner Origin
folkLegends.story: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin, and the clans of Hawazin all united under one of his descendants in early times during the first centuries.

folkLegends.title: Ma'add's Divine Protection
folkLegends.story: According to Ibn Kathir's 14th-century work, God commanded the prophet Jeremiah to take Ma'add son of Adnan away from the conflict with Nebuchadnezzar II, declaring 'For I, the almighty God, will bring forth from him a generous prophet, and the last among prophets.' Jeremiah carried Ma'add to the Levant where he grew among the sons of Israel, and Baruch the scribe preserved his genealogy.
folkLegends.source: Ibn Kathir's The Beginning and the End

nameEtymology: According to 8th-century genealogist Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, the name 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin, deriving from the man Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin

name: Qahtan (Qahtanite lineage)
legitimacyNotes: Genealogical legitimacy as descendants of Qahtan, the legendary ancestor of southern Arabian tribes
ancestorStory: Identified by some with the biblical Joktan
description: Southern Arabs / Pure Arabs / al-Arab al-Ariba. Origin: South Arabia (Yemen, Hadhramaut).

subTribes.name: Himyar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally a confederation of tribes that formed a unified kingdom; ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe after independence from Qataban
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: international trade in frankincense, myrrh, and ivory; hub linking Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa
subTribes.description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

subTribes.name: Kahlan
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
subTribes.ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
subTribes.description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Qahtan (tribe)
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a major tribal confederation with documented historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation structure with three main constituent tribes (Sanhan, Junb, Rufaida) suggests a political alliance rather than pure blood lineage.
subTribes.description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

relations.context: Direct descendant tribe maintaining the Qahtanite name and identity
Claims Qahtanite descent through the Saba lineage
Traditional genealogical division between Qahtanite (southern) and Adnanite (northern) Arab lineages

history:
The Qahtanites (Arabic: قَحْطَانِيون), also known as Banu Qahtan or al-Arab al-Ariba (the Pure Arabs), are the southern Arabian tribes originating from modern-day Hadhramaut and Yemen. The term 'Qahtan' is mentioned in multiple Ancient South Arabian inscriptions found in Yemen, and some Arab traditions believe that the Qahtanites are the original Arabs. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descend from a semi-legendary ancestral figure named Qahtan and his 24 sons, who are considered the progenitors of Yemen and controllers of the Arabian Peninsula. The genealogists disagree about Qahtan's exact pedigree - some trace him back to Ishmael through a lineage including al-Hamaysa, Tayman, and Nabt, while others equate him with the biblical Yoqtan (Joktan) son of Eber, and still others connect him to Jokshan, son of Abraham and Keturah.

The Qahtanite people are traditionally divided into two major sub-groups: Himyar (the settled Arabs of the south) and Kahlan (their nomadic kinsmen). Among Qahtan's sons are noteworthy figures like A'zaal (believed to have been the original name of Sanaa), Hadhramaut, Jurhum, and Ya'rub, whose son Yashjub is the father of Saba. All Yemenite tribes trace their ancestry back to Saba through either of his two sons, Himyar or Kahlan. The Kahlan division consists of four main subgroups: the Ta or Tayy, the Azd group which invaded Oman, the Amila-Judham group of Palestine, and the Hamdan-Madhhij group who mostly remain in Yemen. Major Kahlan tribes include Azd (encompassing Aus, Khazraj, Bariq, Ghassan, Khuza'a and Daws), Hamdan, Khath'am, Bajila, Madhhij, Murad, Zubaid, Ash'ar, Lakhm, Tayy (Shammar), and Kinda.

Pre-Islamic migrations saw various Qahtanite groups moving northward from their Yemen homeland. The Ghassanids (ca. 250 CE) represented the last major non-Islamic Semitic migration northward out of Yemen, initially settling in the Hauran region and eventually spreading to Palestine and Jordan, briefly securing governorship of Syria away from the Nabataeans. Following the rise of Islam in the 7th century, the Qahtanites became heavily involved in the Arab conquests, migrating to newly conquered territories and intermingling with local populations across the Levant, Mesopotamia, Egypt, and North Africa.

During the Umayyad era, a significant blood feud erupted between the Qahtanites and the northern Adnanite tribes of Qays, known as the Qays-Yaman rivalry, which continued in various forms and degrees until the 19th century. This rivalry fundamentally shaped Arabian tribal politics for over a millennium. The traditional distinction between northern Adnanites and southern Qahtanites became a major organizing principle in Arab society, with the Qahtanites often identified with the 'Yaman' faction in this enduring political divide. Modern historiography suggests this genealogical system may have been systematized during the Umayyad period to organize tribal affiliations and political loyalties.

Qahtanite participation in the Muslim conquest of Syria
Large-scale Qahtanite migration to Iraq following Muslim conquest
Qahtanite tribal migrations to Egypt under the Umayyad Caliphate
Banu Hilal and other Qahtanite tribes sent by Fatimids to North Africa

folkLegends.story: Arab tradition maintains that a semi-legendary ancestral figure named Qahtan and his 24 sons are the progenitors of Yemen who controlled the Arabian Peninsula

folkLegends.title: Biblical Ancestry of Qahtan
folkLegends.story: Some genealogists equate Qahtan with the biblical Yoqtan (Joktan) son of Eber, while others connect him to Jokshan, son of Abraham and Keturah

folkLegends.title: Ishmael Learning Arabic from Jurhum
folkLegends.story: Jurhum, a son of Qahtan, formed the second Jurhum tribe from which Ishmael is traditionally believed to have learned Arabic

nameEtymology: The term 'Qahtan' is mentioned in multiple Ancient South Arabian inscriptions found in Yemen. The name al-Arab al-Ariba means 'the Pure Arabs' in Arabic, referring to their status as original Arabs according to tradition.

name: Qahtan (tribe)
legitimacyNotes: This is a major tribal confederation with documented historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation structure with three main constituent tribes (Sanhan, Junb, Rufaida) suggests a political alliance rather than pure blood lineage.
description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

subTribes.name: Bani Hajer
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Claimed Qahtanite lineage through Abida tribe, with documented leadership succession and migration patterns
subTribes.ancestorStory: Al Hawajer are a Qahtanite tribe from Abida, they resided near Sarat Abida also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub
subTribes.description: Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, are an influential Qahtanite tribe in the Arabian Peninsula. Originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub), they are known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia. They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

history:
Qahtān is an ancient Arab tribal confederation representing one of the two major divisions of Arab peoples, tracing its lineage to Qahtani origins as opposed to the Adnaniyah northern Arabs. According to genealogical tradition, Qahtan was among the earliest Arabs, with his descendants considered the authentic ancient Arabs of southern Arabia. The confederation is composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida, with the tribe's original homeland in Yemen from where they later expanded northward. The research indicates that according to Arab genealogical beliefs, Arabs divide themselves into 'Arab 'Ariba' (true Arabs) who descend from their greatest ancestor Qahtan, 'Arab Ba'ida' (extinct Arabs) mentioned in the Quran as having perished due to their disbelief, and 'Arab Musta'riba' (Arabized Arabs) whose greatest ancestor was Adnan.

During the medieval period, the Qahtan tribe achieved significant prominence in the Najd region, particularly settling in Al-Quwai'iyah and expanding their influence throughout southern Najd. However, when Najd came under the rule of Ibn Saud during the second Saudi state, the tribe eventually returned to its original homeland in the south. Several historians describe this period as when Qahtan became one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a time before departing for their ancestral lands. The confederation demonstrates complex genealogical connections within the broader Qahtani tribal system, with the research noting that the Harb tribe traces its lineage as Harb ibn Saad ibn Saad ibn Khawlan ibn Amr ibn Qadha'ah ibn Himyar ibn Qahtan.

The Ya'rubids of Oman, who ruled between 1624 and 1742, traced their descent from Ya'arab bin Kahtan, whom some date to about 800 BC, demonstrating the far-reaching influence of Qahtani lineages. The family originated in Yemen and belonged to the Ghafiri faction before establishing their rule in Oman. Today, members of the Qahtan confederation and its sub-tribes are distributed across the Arabian Peninsula, including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe has produced numerous notable figures in modern times, including politicians, athletes, academics, and religious leaders, maintaining their historical significance in contemporary Arabian Peninsula society.

migrationPath.description: Qahtan tribe settled in Al-Quwai'iyah region of Najd and expanded influence in southern Najd
Returned to original homeland in the south after Najd came under Ibn Saud's rule during the second Saudi state

folkLegends.title: Qahtan as Ancestor of Southern Arabs
folkLegends.story: According to genealogical tradition, Qahtan was among the earliest Arabs, with his descendants considered the authentic ancient Arabs of southern Arabia, as opposed to the northern Arabized Arabs. Arab genealogical beliefs hold that Arabs divide themselves into 'Arab 'Ariba' (true Arabs) who descend from their greatest ancestor Qahtan.

folkLegends.title: Ya'arab bin Kahtan Origin
folkLegends.story: The Al-Ya'ariba trace their descent from Ya'arab bin Kahtan, whom some date to about 800 BC. The family originated in Yemen and belonged to the Ghafiri faction.

name: Qataban
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian kingdom and tribal confederation based on control of frankincense trade routes
ancestorStory: Qataban was one of the four major kingdoms of ancient South Arabia, tracing lineage to Qahtanite origins. The tribe established a powerful kingdom controlling crucial incense trade routes between the 8th century BCE and 3rd century CE.
description: Qataban was one of the four major ancient South Arabian kingdoms, controlling territory in what is now southern Yemen and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia. They dominated the frankincense trade routes from the 8th century BCE to 3rd century CE, with their capital at Timna. The kingdom declined due to changing trade patterns and pressure from Himyarite expansion. While historically significant to Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy, they have limited direct relevance to modern GCC tribal structures, existing primarily as an ancestral reference point for various Qahtanite lineages.

relations.context: Initially integrated into Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE; later conquered Qataban in c. 200 CE
Qataban represents one of the major ancient Qahtanite tribal kingdoms

history: Qataban (Qatabanian: 𐩤𐩩𐩨𐩬, QTBN) was an ancient Yemenite kingdom in South Arabia that existed from the early 1st millennium BCE to the late 1st or 2nd centuries CE. It was one of the six ancient South Arabian kingdoms alongside Sabaʾ, Maʿīn, Ḥaḍramawt, Ḥimyar and Awsān. The kingdom was centered around the Wādī Bayhān with its capital at Timnaʿ, controlling territory in what is now southern Yemen and parts of modern-day Saudi Arabia. At its maximum extent, Qatabān's territory extended from the Bāb al-Mandab in the southwest to the Ṣayhad desert to the north, and the western limits of Ḥaḍramawt to the east. The earliest human occupation in the region dates to around the 20th century BCE consisting of a Neolithic population, with the earliest settlements from the 11th to 10th centuries BCE. Qatabān developed into a centralized state centered around Timnaʿ by the late 7th or early 6th century BCE, initially ruled by joint kings including Hawfiʿamm Yuhanʿim son of Sumhuʿalay Watar and Yadʿʾab son of Ḏamarʿali. In the late 7th century BCE, Qatabān initially allied with neighboring Ḥaḍramawt under Sabaean king Karibʾil Watar, but hostilities soon broke out and Qatabān came under Sabaean control during the 6th century BCE. Qatabān regained independence in the late 5th century BCE and embarked on successful expansionist policies, conquering Maʿīn and challenging Sabaean supremacy by the 3rd century BCE. The kingdom's rulers adopted prestigious titles including mukarrib ('unifier') and malik ('king'), reflecting their regional hegemony. However, Qatabān began declining in the 2nd century BCE when the Ḥimyarite tribal confederation seceded around 110 BCE and joined Sabaʾ to form the kingdom of Sabaʾ and Ḏū-Raydān. The kingdom's economy was based on irrigation agriculture, myrrh production, and most importantly, control of the lucrative frankincense trade routes connecting South Asia with the Mediterranean world. The kingdom finally ended in the late 1st century CE when Ḥaḍramawt and Ḥimyar divided and annexed its territories, marking the conclusion of over seven centuries of Qatabanian political independence.

migrationPath.description: Several waves of Semitic-speaking immigrants from the Levant and Mesopotamia arrived into South Arabia, bringing cultural elements including pottery, eventually giving rise to ancient South Arabian culture including Qatabān

nameEtymology: The name appears in ancient Qatabanian script as 𐩤𐩩𐩨𐩬 (QTBN), romanized as Qatabān. Greco-Roman sources recorded variations: Strabo referred to it as Kattabania (Κατταβανία) and Pliny the Elder called the Qatabānians the 'Gebbanitae'

name: Qawasem
legitimacyNotes: Maritime power and traditional ruling family with religious scholarship heritage
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi trace their lineage to Qasim ibn Raziq, who established the family's presence in the region. They became prominent through their maritime activities and religious scholarship in the Gulf.
description: The Al Qasimi (Qawasem) are a prominent Arabian dynasty that has ruled Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah in the UAE since the 18th century. Historically based along the Trucial Coast, they were major maritime powers controlling key trade routes in the Persian Gulf and engaging in both commerce and naval warfare. The British initially viewed them as pirates, leading to several military campaigns, but they later became key allies through the Trucial States system. Today they remain the ruling families of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates.

relations.context: Same tribal confederation, Al Qasimi is modern designation
Historical competition for territorial control in Trucial States
Modern UAE federation partners as ruling families

history: The Al Qasimi (Qawasem) trace their origins to Hashemite lineage, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. The Qawasim tribe are classified as Huwala - Arabian tribes who migrated to Iran in the 13th-14th centuries, maintaining connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia. By the 18th century, they had established control over both sides of the Persian Gulf, ruling from Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah on the Arabian coast while maintaining dominion over Lengeh, Qishm, and Bandar Abbas on the Iranian coast. In 1779, the Iranian Zand dynasty formally recognized a Qasimi as farmandar (local ruler) of Bandar Lengeh. The Qawasem became strong adherents of the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and allied themselves with the Emirate of Diriyah. This alliance, combined with their control of Gulf trade routes, brought them into conflict with the British-allied Omani Empire and eventually with Britain itself. The British branded them as 'pirates,' leading to two major military campaigns - the Persian Gulf campaign of 1809 and a more decisive campaign in 1819 under William Keir Grant. The 1820 General Maritime Treaty established peace, transforming the 'Pirate Coast' into the Trucial States. The Qawasem lost their Iranian territories in 1887 when defeated by the British, retreating to focus on their Arabian domains. Today, the Al Qasimi continue to rule Sharjah under Sultan bin Muhammad Al-Qasimi and Ras Al Khaimah under Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, making them one of the longest reigning royal families in the Arabian Peninsula.

migrationPath.description: Initial Huwala migration to Iranian coast, establishing presence in Hormozgan province
Formal Qasimi control recognized over Bandar Lengeh by Zand dynasty
Retreat from Iranian domains after British defeat, consolidating power in Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah

name: Qays Aylan
legitimacyNotes: Major Adnanite lineage branch with extensive sub-tribal divisions
ancestorStory: Qays ibn Aylan was a major Adnanite ancestor whose descendants formed numerous influential tribes. His lineage produced many prominent Arabian tribes that spread across the peninsula.
description: Key Adnanite branch

relations.context: Direct descent from Adnanite lineage

history:
Qays Aylan was a major Arab tribal confederation that branched from the Mudar group, with their progenitor traditionally identified as Qays ibn Aylan. According to Ibn Khaldun, 'Qays Aylan' was actually the epithet of al-Nas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them part of the Adnanite or 'North Arabian' lineage. The confederation comprised three main branches: Khasafa (including Hawazin, Sulaym, and Muharib), Sa'd (including Ghatafan with its Abs and Dhubyan sub-tribes, and A'sur with Ghani and Bahila), and Amr (including Adwan and Fahm). By the 7th century, the descendants of Qays were so numerous that the term Qaysi came to refer to all North Arabians.

Originally inhabiting the low-lying areas of the Tihamah along the western Arabian Hejaz, by Muhammad's time (ca. 570 CE) the Qaysi tribes had spread throughout central and eastern Arabia, parts of Lower Mesopotamia, and regions around Medina and Mecca. Initially hostile to Islam, tribes like the Ghatafan and Sulaym fought against the Muslims at Medina between 622-629. However, by 630 CE most had converted, though many apostatized during the Ridda Wars before rejoining Islam after their defeat at the Battle of Buzakha.

During the Umayyad period (661-750), the Qays formed one of the main political factions opposing the Yamani tribes led by Banu Kalb. This rivalry had both political and ethnic dimensions, as Qays represented 'northern Arabs' while Yaman were 'southern Arabs.' The Qays suffered a severe defeat at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684, leading to years of revenge-driven conflicts. Under leaders like Zufar ibn al-Harith al-Kilabi, they resisted from strongholds in al-Qarqisiya and Ras al-Ayn until submitting to Umayyad authority in 691.

The Qays reached their political peak under Marwan II, who relocated the Umayyad capital to Harran in Qaysi territory. However, their power collapsed after the Battle of the Zab in 750, where they fought as Marwan's principal military force against the victorious Abbasids. Following this defeat, massive numbers of Qaysi tribesmen migrated to North Africa and al-Andalus, including the Banu Sulaym in Ifriqiya, Fazara in Cyrenaica, Banu Ashja' in Algeria and Morocco, and Banu Hilal in various North African regions. By the 14th century, only remnants remained in their central Arabian homeland.

migrationPath.description: Pre-Islamic expansion from original Tihamah homeland to Najd, Yamama, and Bahrayn
Migration during Muslim conquests, particularly to Jund Qinnasrin and northern regions

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Aylan Epithet
folkLegends.story: Various theories exist about the origins of the 'Aylan' part of Qays Aylan's name. Some traditions hold that Aylan was the name of al-Nas's famous horse, while others claim it was his dog, his bow, a mountain where he was born, or a man who raised him.

nameEtymology: The full name Qays Aylan derives from their progenitor Qays, with various disputed theories about 'Aylan' including it being the name of a horse, dog, bow, mountain, or foster father. Ibn Khaldun asserts 'Qays Aylan' was the epithet of al-Nas ibn Mudar.

name: Qubaisat
legitimacyNotes: Established sub-tribe of the larger Bani Yas confederation
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: Al Qubaisi (Al Qubaisiat) are a sub-tribe of Bani Yas, of Abu Dhabi, closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. The Al Qubaisi family share close relations with the leading royal family.

relations.context: Al Qubaisi are a sub-tribe of Bani Yas and closely associated with them

history:
The Qubaisat (Al Kubaisat/Al Qubaisi) are documented as a section of the Bani Yas tribe with a complex history of secession and territorial disputes in the 19th century Gulf region. Historical records show that members of this tribal section migrated to and settled at Khor Al Adaid on three separate occasions during the 19th century: 1835, 1849, and 1869. The first major secession occurred in 1836 when Al-Kubaisat, under Sheikh Khadim-bin-Nahman, sought to avoid consequences of certain piratical activities and established themselves at Khor Al Adaid, breaking allegiance with Abu Dhabi. This initial settlement was violently suppressed in 1837 when the Sheikh of Abu Dhabi, with British approval, sent troops that killed 50 inhabitants and dismantled the settlement's houses and fortifications.

The most significant and lasting secession occurred in 1869 under Sheikh Buttye-bin-Khadim, when the Qubaisat again left Abu Dhabi to resettle at Khor Al Adaid. This colony grew to approximately 200 Bani Yas tribespeople who owned 30 pearling ships and built defensive fortifications including a small fort with two towers. Sheikh Buttye-bin-Khadim asserted territorial claims extending from Ras-al-Hala to Sir Bani Yas island, declaring this territory as 'the ancient home of the Al-Kubaisat.' The settlement became entangled in Ottoman-British imperial rivalry when Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani offered the Ottoman flag to the Qubaisat, though Sheikh Buttye-bin-Khadim initially refused, claiming to be under British protection while occasionally hoisting the Ottoman flag on Fridays.

The Khor Al Adaid settlement became a focal point of regional conflict and diplomatic tension throughout the 1870s. The area was used as a base for piratical activities, though British investigations determined that while pirates operated from Qubaisat ports, the tribal leadership was too weak to prevent this rather than actively supporting piracy. Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi repeatedly requested British permission to reassert authority over the settlement, leading to unsuccessful reconciliation attempts between the two sheikhs. The situation deteriorated when continued piracy and the refusal to release captured sailors from Al Wakrah prompted British support for Abu Dhabi's claims.

The final destruction of the Qubaisat settlement at Khor Al Adaid came in 1879 when Sheikh Zayed, supported by British naval forces with 70 boats and accompanied by an English war steamer, launched a massive invasion that resulted in the village being pillaged and destroyed, with all boats carried off and inhabitants fled. This military action created significant Ottoman diplomatic protest, as they claimed Khor Al Adaid as Turkish territory. Following this destruction, attempts were made by Sheikh Jassim to resettle members of the Kubaisat tribe from Al Wakrah back to Khor Al Adaid in 1891, with about 40 tribal members expressing willingness to return provided one of Jassim's sons served as their sheikh, though these resettlement plans were ultimately abandoned. The territorial dispute over Khor Al Adaid contributed to broader regional conflicts and remained a point of contention between Qatar, Abu Dhabi, and later the UAE well into the modern era.

migrationPath.description: First secession under Sheikh Khadim-bin-Nahman to avoid consequences of piratical activities, ended with violent suppression
Second secession followed by compulsory return

name: Quraysh
legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

relations.context: Quraysh descended from Kinana tribe, fought together in Fijar War
Muhammad negotiated with them and Banu Khazraj to mediate conflict during Hijrah
Muhammad negotiated with them and Banu Aws to mediate conflict during Hijrah
Allies of Muhammad, conflict with Quraysh client clans broke Treaty of al-Hudaybiya
Fought against Quraysh and Kinana in the Fijar War over control of trade routes
Main trade rivals of Quraysh, fought in Fijar War, eventually Quraysh gained control over Ta'if's trade
Alliance formed through Al-Jadara family connections and intermarriage

history: The Quraysh are an ancient Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam, tracing their lineage to Adnan the Ishmaelite through their progenitor Fihr ibn Malik. According to traditional genealogical sources, Fihr belonged to the Kinana tribe with the full lineage: Fihr ibn Malik ibn al-Nadr ibn Kinana ibn Khuzayma ibn Mudrika ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan. Fihr led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen, though the sanctuary remained under control of the Yemeni Khuza'a tribe. His descendants lived scattered among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them around the late 5th century CE and established Quraysh dominance over Mecca. Qusayy divided the clans between Quraysh al-Bitah (those settled around the Kaaba) and Quraysh al-Zawahir (those in the outskirts), assigning religious and administrative duties among his sons. The tribe was organized into ten main clans, most notably including Banu Hashim, Banu Umayya, Banu Taym, Banu Makhzum, and others, with succession disputes between Abd al-Dar and Abd Manaf leading to factional divisions known as al-Ahlaf ('the Confederates') and al-Mutayyabun ('the Perfumed'). By the 6th century, the Quraysh had become wealthy merchants controlling trade routes between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean, running summer caravans to Gaza, Damascus and Syria, and winter caravans to Yemen, while also establishing political and economic alliances with Bedouin tribes to protect their trade networks. When Muhammad began preaching Islam in Mecca around 610 CE, the Quraysh initially showed little concern but their opposition grew as he challenged prevalent polytheism, leading to the Muslim-Quraysh War (624-630 CE) after Muhammad's migration to Medina in 622 CE. Major battles included Badr (624 CE) where the Quraysh suffered significant losses including the death of Abu Jahl, Uhud (625 CE) where they achieved a tactical victory under Abu Sufyan, and the Battle of the Trench (627 CE) where a Quraysh-led confederation of 10,000 men failed to capture Medina. The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah in 628 CE established a ten-year truce, but was broken when Qurayshi allies attacked the Banu Khuza'ah, leading to Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE with 10,000 men, after which the Quraysh converted to Islam. Following Muhammad's death in 632 CE, Islamic leadership traditionally remained with the Quraysh, as seen with the Rashidun caliphs, and later the Umayyad and Abbasid dynasties, with the tribe's organizational and commercial skills proving crucial in the early Islamic state's administration and the subsequent Islamic conquests.

migrationPath.description: Qusayy ibn Kilab gathered scattered descendants of Fihr ibn Malik and unified them in Mecca, establishing Quraysh control over the city and the Kaaba

folkLegends.story: Fihr ibn Malik led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against attacking tribes from Yemen, establishing the Quraysh connection to the sacred sanctuary

folkLegends.title: Qusayy's Unification
folkLegends.story: Qusayy ibn Kilab gathered his scattered kinsmen and took control of the Kaaba, unifying the descendants of Fihr and establishing Quraysh control over Mecca

nameEtymology: Sources differ on the etymology of Quraysh. One theory holds it was the diminutive form of qirsh (shark). Arab genealogist Hisham ibn al-Kalbi asserted the name stemmed from taqarrush, meaning 'a coming together' or 'association'. Another theory traces it to eponymous ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab, who gathered his kinsmen together.

name: Qurra
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Hilal confederation with recognized lineage
ancestorStory: Qurra was a son of Hilal ibn Amir, from whom this branch of the Banu Hilal takes its name. The tribe migrated westward with other Hilali branches during the great Arab migrations.
description: The Qurra were a branch of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin. They participated in the great Hilali migrations westward across North Africa in the 10th-11th centuries. As part of the larger Hilal tribal movement, they were primarily Bedouin pastoralists who played a significant role in the Arabization of the Maghreb. Their direct presence in the modern Gulf region is limited, as most Qurra branches settled in western territories during the medieval migrations.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Hawazin tribal confederation

history:
The Qurra represents a distinct branch of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin tribal group within the broader Adnani lineage. As part of the great Hilali tribal confederation, the Qurra were among the most notable sub-tribes alongside the Athbaj, Riyah, Jusham, Zughba, and Adi during the massive Arab migrations of the 10th-11th centuries CE. Historical sources estimate that approximately one million Arab nomads participated in these westward migrations to the Maghreb, fundamentally transforming the demographic and cultural landscape of North Africa.

The Qurra, as Bedouin pastoralists, played a significant role in the Arabization of the Maghreb following the Fatimid decision to send the Banu Hilal confederation westward in 1050 CE. Prior to this major migration, some Qurra factions had already established presence in North African territories - notably, a faction of Zughba settled in Tripoli with Banu Qurra during 1037-1038 CE, indicating early Qurra presence in the region before the main Hilali movement. In the 12th century, the Qurra were documented as occupying territories stretching across North Africa, with the Zughba tribe (their close allies) controlling areas from Tlemcen in the west to Algiers in the east.

The tribal structure of the Qurra followed the traditional Bedouin confederation model, maintaining their Adnani Arab lineage and pastoral lifestyle throughout their migrations. Unlike some other tribal groups that established centralized settlements, the Qurra integrated into the broader tribal confederations of North Africa rather than founding independent territorial states. Their migration pattern followed the classic westward movement of the Hilali tribes, moving from their original Arabian territories across Egypt and into the Maghreb, where they became part of the complex tribal politics of medieval North Africa.

The Qurra's historical trajectory demonstrates the broader pattern of Arab tribal expansion and settlement in North Africa during the medieval period. As part of the Banu Hilal confederation, they maintained their tribal identity and genealogical connections while adapting to new geographical and political environments. Their limited presence in the modern Gulf region reflects the permanent nature of their westward settlement, as most Qurra branches established themselves in the territories they conquered during the 11th century migrations, contributing to the lasting Arabization of the western Maghreb alongside other Hilali tribes.
