name: Rabīʿa
legitimacyNotes: Claimed descent from Rabīʿa ibn Nizār, major Adnanite tribal confederation
ancestorStory: Rabīʿa was one of the sons of Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān, representing one of the major divisions of northern Arabian tribes. The Rabīʿa confederation emerged from his lineage and became one of the most significant tribal groupings in pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Rabīʿa was one of the major Adnanite tribal confederations of pre-Islamic Arabia, claiming descent from Rabīʿa ibn Nizār. The confederation included prominent tribes such as Bakr ibn Wāʾil and ʿAbd al-Qays, and controlled territories across northeastern Arabia and parts of Iraq. They were known for their conflicts with their Mudari cousins and played significant roles in pre-Islamic tribal politics and early Islamic conquests. While the confederation as a unified entity is historical, many of its constituent tribal branches continue to exist across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region today.

relations.context: Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd
ʿAbd al-Qays was a major constituent tribe of the Rabīʿa confederation

history:
Rabīʿa ibn Nizār represents one of the two main branches of North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes alongside Mudhar, tracing descent from Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. According to classical Arab genealogists, the confederation encompassed major tribal branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Bakr ibn Wa'il (with sub-tribes Banu Hanifa, Banu Shayban, Banu Qays ibn Tha'laba), Taghlib, and al-Nammir ibn Qasit. Like other Adnanite Arabs, legendary accounts place their original homelands in the Tihamah region of western Arabia, from which they migrated northwards and eastwards across the Arabian Peninsula and Mesopotamia.

Geographically, Abd al-Qays settled in Eastern Arabia including modern Bahrain and were largely sedentary, while Bakr's territories stretched from al-Yamama (around modern Riyadh) to northwestern Mesopotamia. Taghlib inhabited the eastern banks of the Euphrates, and Anizah divided between sedentary sections in southern Yamama and nomadic groups further north. Many Rabīʿa tribes, particularly Abd al-Qays, Taghlib, al-Nammir, and sections of Bakr, were predominantly Christian before Islam, with Taghlib maintaining Christian identity for some time after the Muslim conquests. Anazah and Bakr worshiped an idol called al-Sa'eer in pre-Islamic times.

During the 7th century, Mu'awiya settled Rabīʿa tribes in Diyar Rabi'a, the easternmost province of Jazira (Upper Mesopotamia), centered around Mosul and encompassing the upper Khabur River region and both shores of the Tigris from Jazirat ibn Umar to Tikrit. The confederation participated in early Islamic conquests and played significant roles during the Rashidun and Umayyad periods. The region of Diyar Bakr and later the city of Diyarbakır in southern Turkey take their names from Banu Bakr, a major Rabīʿa sub-tribe.

During the Abbasid era, members of Bani Hanifa and related Bakr tribesmen migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, dominating gold mines near Aswan and intermarrying with local Beja peoples, giving rise to tribes like the Banu Kanz. The region came under various dynastic controls including the native Hamdanid dynasty in the 10th century, followed by Buyids and Uqaylids until the Seljuq conquest. Modern descendants of Rabīʿa tribes continue to exist across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, with several royal families including Al Saud, Al Khalifa, and Al Sabah tracing ancestry to various branches of the confederation, though some lineage claims are disputed among genealogists.

name: Rab'ia tribe
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Adnanite tribal confederation with documented pre-Islamic prominence
ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar was one of the sons of Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. The tribe descended from him became one of the major northern Arabian tribal confederations.
description: Rabi'a was one of the major ancient Arabian tribal confederations, descended from Rabi'a ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. They were prominent in northern and eastern Arabia during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. The confederation included several important sub-tribes that played significant roles in Arabian history. Many of their branches later settled in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, including areas that are now part of the GCC states, though the original confederation structure dissolved over time.

history: The research provided focuses on Rabi'a ibn Mani' al-Muraydi, the second emir of Diriyah beginning in 1463, rather than the ancient Rabi'a tribal confederation. This Rabi'a was the son of Mani', who founded the emirate of Diriyah in Najd. According to the sources, Mani' originally lived with his relatives in the eastern Arabian Peninsula in a place called al-Diriyah near al-Qatif (distinct from the later Diriyah that became the Saudi capital). In 1447, Mani' corresponded with a relative named Ibn Dara'a who was influential in al-Yamama, and they reached an agreement whereby Mani' would migrate with his family from eastern Arabia to Wadi Hanifa. Ibn Dara'a sold him land in Ghasibah and al-Mulibid, which were abandoned and uninhabited territories that Mani' transformed into a habitable settlement, attracting migration from his tribe and creating the new city of al-Diriyah. Upon Mani's death on 14 August 1463, his son Rabi'a succeeded to the throne and continued the expansion of the emirate. During Rabi'a's reign, numerous villages were founded, the emirate's territories grew remarkably, and the population of Diriyah multiplied significantly. Rabi'a sought to expand his domain by conquering parts of al-Yazid's lands and the populations of al-Naima and al-Wasil. However, the growth of the emirate led to factional interests and power struggles. A faction headed by Rabi'a's own son Musa eventually seized control through a coup, with Musa attempting to kill his father whom he viewed as a threat to his own interests. Though badly wounded, Rabi'a managed to escape with a group of supporters and sought refuge with Hamad ibn Hassan ibn Tuq, the emir of al-Uyaynah, who provided him shelter and protection. The historian Uthman ibn Bishr recorded that Rabi'a became famous in Najd as his kingdom expanded and he fought against the Yazid clan, before his son Musa's rebellion and attempted patricide forced him into exile.

migrationPath.description: Mani' ibn Rabi'a al-Muraydi migrated with his family from eastern Arabia to establish the settlement that became al-Diriyah after reaching agreement with Ibn Dara'a

name: Rabi'ah
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.
traditionalEconomy: Mixed - bedouin pastoralism and sedentary agriculture, with some branches being mostly sedentary while others remained nomadic
description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

history: Rabi'ah ibn Nizar represents one of the two main patriarchal branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, alongside Mudhar. According to classical Arab genealogists, Rabi'ah's original homeland was in the Tihamah region of western Arabia, from which the tribe migrated northwards and eastwards across the Arabian Peninsula and into Mesopotamia. The tribe's major branches include Abd al-Qays (who settled in Eastern Arabia including modern Bahrain and were mostly sedentary), Banu Bakr (whose lands stretched from al-Yamama around modern Riyadh to northwestern Mesopotamia), Anizah (divided between sedentary sections in southern Yamama and bedouin sections further north), Taghlib (residing on the eastern banks of the Euphrates), and al-Nammir ibn Qasit. Many of these branches, including Abd al-Qays, Taghlib, al-Nammir, and sections of Bakr, were predominantly Christian before Islam, with Taghlib remaining Christian for some time after the Islamic conquests. During the Abbasid era, significant numbers of Bani Hanifa and related Bakr tribesmen migrated from al-Yamama to southern Egypt, particularly the Wadi Allaqi region near Aswan, where they dominated gold mining operations and intermarried with local Beja peoples. These migrants collectively identified as 'Rabi'a' in Egypt and became ancestors of the Banu Kanz (Kunooz) tribe, named after their Fatimid-era leader Kanz al-Dawlah of Bani Hanifa. The Rabi'ah legacy continues through numerous royal dynasties including the Al Saud of Saudi Arabia, Al Khalifa of Bahrain, Al Sabah of Kuwait, and the Hamdanid, Uyunid, and Dulafid dynasties of medieval times.

name: Ramzan (Qatar section)
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority within larger Na'im confederation structure
description: The Ramzan are a sub-tribal section of the larger Na'im tribe with presence in Qatar. As part of the Na'im confederation, they share the broader tribal identity and Adnani lineage. The Qatar section represents families who settled in the peninsula, contributing to Qatar's tribal demographic structure. They maintain traditional tribal customs while adapting to modern Qatari society.

relations.context: Both are sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation

history:
The Ramzan are a sub-tribal section of the larger Na'im tribe, one of the most significant traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribes in the Gulf region. As part of the Na'im confederation, they share the broader tribal identity and Adnani lineage. According to J.G. Lorimer's Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf published in 1904, the Na'im were described as 'a Bedouin tribe who grazed their cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah in 1873.' The tribe were reported as being one of the most powerful tribes in Qatar in an 1890 British government report, with 60 or 70 of the tribe's branch in Qatar having a hereditary attachment to the Bahraini emir.

In 1937, a critical dispute over Zubarah took place between Bahrain and Qatar that fundamentally affected the Ramzan section's distinct identity. The Na'im in Qatar split into two sections during this conflict: the Ramzan, who supported the Qatari emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani, and the main section Al Jabr, who had pledged allegiance to the ruler of Bahrain. This political division marked the Ramzan's emergence as a distinct identity within the broader Na'im confederation, differentiating them from their tribal kinsmen who maintained loyalty to Bahrain.

Hostilities escalated when an armed force sent by Abdullah Al Thani, numbering in the hundreds, confronted around 60 armed men of the Na'im who were encamped inside Ath Thaqab Fort. The conflict resulted in casualties and the capture of the fort, with Abdullah Al Thani's forces occupying the villages of Ath Thaqab, Freiha, Al-ʽArish and Al Khuwayr. As a result of this conflict, about 1,000 members of the Na'im tribe went into exile in Bahrain, with some returning after the Second World War. The Ramzan section, having chosen to support Qatar, remained in the peninsula and became integrated into Qatar's tribal demographic structure.

As part of the Na'im's traditional territory, the Ramzan shared in the tribe's historical presence around the Buraimi oasis and their role as one of the numerically strongest tribes in the region, with the combined Na'im population estimated at approximately 13,000 at the beginning of the twentieth century. The Qatar section represents families who settled in the peninsula, contributing to Qatar's tribal demographic structure while maintaining traditional tribal customs and adapting to modern Qatari society. Their connection to geographical features is evidenced by places like Waab Bin Ramzan, a prominent depression near Al Thakhira that was named after 'a man of the Al Ramzan tribe who is said to have died there long ago.'

migrationPath.description: Na'im tribe including Ramzan section grazing cattle on pastures surrounding Zubarah

folkLegends.title: The Man of Waab Bin Ramzan
folkLegends.story: A prominent depression near Al Thakhira earned its name from a man of the Al Ramzan tribe who is said to have died there long ago. The term waab refers to a low-lying, expansive, and moisture-retaining area where rainwater would accumulate for several weeks, creating a temporary wetland that later became fertile ground for various wild plants and grasses.

name: Rashid
legitimacyNotes: No tribal information is provided in the source text. The name 'Rashid' appears only as a disambiguation term referring to various places, people, and concepts, but not as a tribal entity.
description: The text provided does not contain information about a tribal entity called 'Rashid'. The source material appears to be a disambiguation page listing various meanings of the name 'Rashid/Rachid/Rasheed' (meaning 'rightly guided' in Arabic), followed by biographical information about Rashida Tlaib, a Palestinian-American politician. No tribal lineage, structure, or traditional Arabian tribal characteristics are mentioned.
history: The Rashid tribe represents multiple distinct tribal entities across the Arabian Peninsula, each with separate origins and histories. The most prominent is the Rashid tribe of the UAE, documented as a small Bedouin tribe numbering approximately 300 members according to Thesiger. This tribe had extensive migration patterns stretching from Buraimi to the Abu Dhabi coast and as far south as Hadhramaut, centered in the Dhafrah area west of Liwa. They belonged to the Hinawi tribal confederation and were frequently involved in raiding activities, maintaining particular enmities with the Ghafiri-aligned Saar and Duru tribes. Thesiger described them as 'among the most authentic of the Bedu, the least affected by the outside world.' The tribe was renowned for breeding hardy camels and, unlike many Trucial States tribes, depended entirely on animal husbandry rather than date cultivation or settlement. A separate Rashid entity was the House of Rashid (Al Rashid), a Shammar dynasty that ruled the Emirate of Ha'il from 1836-1921. Founded by Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid, this dynasty became the most formidable enemies of the House of Saud in Najd. Centered in Ha'il, a city that derived wealth from Hajj pilgrimage routes and commerce, the Rashidi emirs cooperated closely with the Ottoman Empire. The dynasty faced recurring succession disputes and violent internal conflicts, with six Rashidi leaders dying violently in the late 19th century. In 1890, Al Rashid occupied Riyadh and defeated the Saudi tribes, forcing them into exile. However, by 1921, Ha'il was captured by Abdulaziz Al Saud, ending Rashidi rule. Some family members went into voluntary exile to Iraq, Pakistan, Oman, Kuwait, and the UAE.

name: Remeithi
legitimacyNotes: No tribal information found for 'Remeithi' in the provided text. The text contains biographical information about unrelated individuals and medical conditions, but no Arabian Gulf tribal data.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: <UNKNOWN>
history:
The Al Remeithi (الرميثات/الرميثي) are documented as one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation in the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Yas originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, and settled in the Liwa Oasis around 1700 under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan. As part of this confederation, the Remeithi were among approximately 20 tribes that formed the Bani Yas Coalition, which extended from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar.

The confederation's total population was estimated by J.C. Lorimer at around 12,000 in the early 20th century, with about 10,000 settled members distributed between Liwa Oasis, Abu Dhabi town, Dubai, and other coastal and inland settlements, plus approximately 2,000 nomadic members. By the 1950s, the Bani Yas population had declined to approximately 8,000, and the first Abu Dhabi census in 1968 recorded 5,884 Bani Yas members within the emirate, though subsequent censuses in 1971 and 1975-1976 did not record tribal affiliation.

The Remeithi, like other Bani Yas sections, experienced significant population decline during the late 1950s due to emigration for employment opportunities in Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia, particularly in the oil and construction sectors. This migration occurred as oil exploration in Abu Dhabi became increasingly mechanized and local labor demand declined. Large-scale return migration occurred only after the emirate's development accelerated following the accession of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan.

As members of the Bani Yas confederation, the Remeithi were part of a complex tribal structure that varied in composition over time. Contemporary leading members of the Bani Yas identify more than 20 sections as forming part of the confederation, reflecting shifting political relationships as associated tribes sometimes sought full integration under the authority of the Ruler of Abu Dhabi, while changing regional dynamics at other times encouraged assertions of autonomy.

name: Riyah
legitimacyNotes: Established Hilalian lineage with documented genealogy
ancestorStory: Descended from Riyah, son of Hilal ibn Amir, as part of the great Hilalian tribal confederation. The tribe traces its origins to the broader Hilalian migration patterns.
description: Riyah is a branch of the larger Hilalian tribal confederation, descended from the Adnani lineage through Hilal ibn Amir. Historically known as part of the great Hilalian migrations that spread across North Africa and parts of Arabia in the medieval period. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin pastoral lifestyle and tribal organization. Their current status in the Gulf region is largely historical, with descendants having integrated into various modern tribal structures.

relations.context: Both tribes share Adnani origins and historical nomadic patterns

history:
The Riyah (Arabic: رياح) is one of the most powerful sub-tribes of Banu Hilal, tracing their genealogy through the Adnani lineage to Riyah, son of Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. Originally from the Najd region of central Arabian Peninsula, the Riyah were among the major tribal divisions of Banu Hilal, alongside the Athbaj and Zughba. During the pre-Islamic period, their history was intertwined with other Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a tribes in the ayyam al-arab (battle days of the Arabs). The tribe likely accepted Islam after Muhammad's victory at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE, but did not participate in the Ridda Wars following the Prophet's death.

In the early 8th century, some Riyah migrated from Najd to Egypt, later moving to Iraq and the Levant in the 9th century. The most significant migration occurred in the 11th century when the Fatimid Caliphate dispatched the Banu Hilal confederation, including the Riyah, to punish the rebellious Zirid dynasty of Ifriqiya. At the time of this great migration, their chief was Munis ibn Yahya of the Mirdas family. The Riyah were part of the estimated 200,000 to 1 million Arab nomads who transformed North Africa's demographic and cultural landscape through this massive population movement.

By Ibn Khaldun's era (1332-1406), the Riyah had established themselves primarily in eastern Algeria around Constantine and northern Tunisia. The tribe was internally organized into several sub-fractions: Mohammed, Mirdass, Sinber, and Dhouaouda. The Riyah demonstrated significant military prowess throughout the medieval period, notably in 1217 when their warriors reinforced the Almohad army against the Marinids, killing their leader Abd al-Haqq I. Ibn Abi Zar recorded this victory and identified the Riyah Arabs as the strongest tribe in the Maghreb at that time.

However, the tribe's resistance to Almohad authority led to their devastating defeat at the Battle of Sétif on April 27, 1153. Under the leadership of Mahrez ibn Ziyad, a faction of the Riyah opposed Abd al-Mu'min's demands to participate in campaigns against Christian Spain. The battle resulted in Mahrez's death, his head displayed on a pike, and the subsequent dispersal of his tribal section. The Almohads captured 80,000 Riyah tents and relocated the survivors to Morocco, where they were resettled along with other Hilalian tribes like the Zughba and Khult. This forced relocation contributed to the rural Arabization of the western Maghreb. The Riyah played a crucial role in spreading Bedouin Arabic culture and nomadic lifestyle across North Africa, fundamentally altering the region's linguistic and cultural identity, with their descendants becoming integrated into various North African tribal structures.

name: Rubatab
legitimacyNotes: Riverine Nubian tribe with historical presence in northern Sudan
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Rabat, the Rubatab are part of the Ja'alin confederation of tribes. They trace their lineage through Arab ancestry mixed with indigenous Nubian populations.
description: The Rubatab are a Sudanese tribe primarily based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, part of the broader Ja'alin tribal confederation. They are not traditionally associated with the Arabian Peninsula or Gulf region, though some members may have migrated to Gulf states in modern times. Their historical territory centers around the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile. The tribe has limited direct relevance to GCC tribal politics or history.

relations.context: Neighboring upstream riverine tribe with similar culture and lifestyle. Some Manasir clans migrate to Rubatab Country during dry season.

history: The Rubatab people (Arabic: الرباطاب, ar-rubāṭāb) are a Sudanese Arab tribe inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile River in northern Sudan, specifically within the Abu Hamad District stretching north of Berber until Abu Hamad. With a population of approximately 60,000 as of 2012, they constitute a sub-group of the larger Ja'alin tribal confederation, one of the three prominent Sudanese Arab tribes in northern Sudan. The Rubatab maintain their tribal homeland in what is described as poor and barren rocky lands south of Abu Hamad, bordering the Ababda people, Bishari tribe, and Manasir tribe. Like their neighboring riverine tribes including the mid-stream Manasir and downstream Shaiqiyah, the Rubatab are classified as an Arab tribe of Northeastern Sudan who speak an archaic form of Arabic as their mother tongue. However, scholars have classified the Ja'alin confederation, including the Rubatab, as 'Afro-Arab hybrid' peoples representing a mixture of indigenous Africans and Arabs who came to Sudan between the 9th and 14th centuries. Earlier Western scholars described this ethnic composition using various terms including 'bastardized Arab blood, paganized Islam, and creolized Arabic.' The tribe maintains traditional riverine culture along the Nile with documented customs from 1918 including specific inheritance practices where property is shared among heirs and each family maintains genealogical records. Their social structure includes distinctive marriage customs where girls typically marry paternal cousins, elaborate wedding ceremonies featuring communal feasts with dancing and the singing of praise songs called Sumar, and complex dowry arrangements involving both land (mahr) and money (tu'ma). Religious practices blend Islamic beliefs with local traditions including veneration of various walis (saints) and belief in houris described as white-skinned beings with long flowing hair who live in underwater Nile villages.

timelineEvents.title: Arab-Nubian Integration Period

folkLegends.title: The Five Brothers and the Division of Land
folkLegends.story: According to oral tradition, all the land between Khartoum and Dongola was once owned by a sheikh who had five sons. Before dying, he pleaded with them to avoid quarrels. When dividing their inheritance, the eldest son Robat called his brothers together and said 'I am the eldest and I will set you an example that we may all live together in peace as our father wished. I will take first choice and will choose the rocky lands south of Abu Hamed.' This explains why the Rubatab occupy what is considered the worst, most barren land in the district.

nameEtymology: Named after their ancestor Robat (also spelled Rabat), who according to local legend was the eldest son in the folk tale of the five brothers' land division

name: Rufaida
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and descent from Qahtani origins
ancestorStory: Descended from the ancient South Arabian Qahtani tribes. The tribe traces its lineage through various branches that settled in different parts of the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Al-Rufaida is a Qahtani Arab tribe with ancient South Arabian origins that has branches distributed across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula. Historically, they were involved in the traditional Bedouin lifestyle of pastoralism and participated in the tribal confederations of pre-Islamic Arabia. The tribe has maintained its tribal identity through genealogical preservation and continues to exist in modern times with members found in several Gulf states. Their historical significance lies in their participation in the broader tapestry of Arabian tribal politics and migration patterns.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani origins

history:
According to the research, there is significant confusion between two entirely different entities: the historical figure Rufaida Al-Aslamia and the modern Rufaida tribal branch. Rufaida Al-Aslamia (born approximately 620 AD) was an Arab medical practitioner recognized as the first female Muslim nurse in Islam. She belonged to the Bani Aslem tribe of the Khazraj tribal confederation in Medina and was among the Ansar who supported Prophet Muhammad. She was born into a family with strong medical ties, as her father Sa'ad Al Aslamy was a physician under whom she initially obtained clinical experience.

Rufaida Al-Aslamia's historical significance lies in her pioneering role in Islamic medicine and nursing. She practiced her skills in field hospitals during battles, with Muhammad ordering all casualties to be carried to her tent for treatment. She participated in the battles of Khandaq and Khaibar, leading groups of volunteer nurses. Her contributions were so valued that Muhammad assigned her a share of war bounty equivalent to that of soldiers who had fought, in recognition of her medical and nursing work. She also trained a group of women companions as nurses, establishing the foundation for female medical practice in early Islam.

The research explicitly states that there is no established genealogical connection between the historical Rufaida Al-Aslamia of the Bani Aslem tribe and the modern Rufaida branch of the Qahtan tribal confederation. The modern Rufaida appears to be one of the three main branches of the Qahtan tribal confederation, alongside Sanhan and Junb, with ancient South Arabian Qahtani origins. However, the research provides no specific historical details about this tribal branch's genealogy, migrations, or territorial control beyond its association with the broader Qahtan confederation.

The broader Qahtan confederation, to which the modern Rufaida belongs, has members distributed across Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. Historically, the Qahtan tribe settled in the Najd region known as Al-Quwai'iyah and expanded its influence in southern Najd during the era of the second Saudi state under Ibn Saud's rule, becoming one of the powerful tribes of Najd for a period before returning to their original homeland in the south.

timelineEvents.title: Rufaida Al-Aslamia's Birth
timelineEvents.description: Birth of Rufaida Al-Aslamia, recognized as the first female Muslim nurse, from whom the tribal name may derive

nameEtymology: The research suggests the tribal name may derive from the historical figure Rufaida Al-Aslamia, but explicitly states that no direct genealogical connection has been established between the historical figure and the modern tribal branch

name: Rulers of Ras Al Khaimah
legitimacyNotes: hereditary ruling family established through tribal confederation and British recognition
ancestorStory: The Al Qasimi dynasty traces its origins to Qasim bin Rashid who established their rule in the 18th century. The family became prominent maritime rulers controlling both Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah before the territories were divided.
description: The ruling family of Ras Al Khaimah, part of the Al Qasimi dynasty that historically controlled significant portions of the Arabian Gulf coast. Based in Ras Al Khaimah emirate in the northern UAE, they were prominent maritime traders and rulers who controlled shipping routes in the Gulf. The family split from their Sharjah cousins in the 19th century, establishing separate emirates. Today they remain the hereditary rulers of Ras Al Khaimah emirate as part of the United Arab Emirates federation.

relations.context: The relationship was often fractious, with villages seceding several times before 1921
both branches of the Al Qasimi dynasty
part of the broader Qawasim tribal confederation

history: The Al Qasimi dynasty that rules Ras Al Khaimah traces its origins to the Qawasim tribe, which are of Huwala origin with Hashemite lineage, descending from Husayn ibn Ali through their eponymous ancestor Al Qasim bin Idris bin Ja'far al-Zaki. Their ancestors migrated and maintained connections between the Arabian Peninsula and Persia, particularly maintaining rulership over the town of Lengeh on the coast of Hormozgan province until its annexation by Iran in 1887. The first recorded ruler was Sheikh Rahma Al Qasimi (1708-1731), followed by his son Sheikh Matar bin Butti Al Qasimi (1731-1747). The dynasty established itself as a significant maritime power in the early 18th century, with Sheikh Rahma bin Matar Al Qasimi described by the Dutch as the 'Emir of Julfar' and one of the richest Arab merchants. During the 18th century, the Qawasim embraced the Muwahhidun (Wahhabi) movement led by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and became close allies of the Emirate of Diriyah. By the early 19th century, they emerged as a maritime power controlling territories on both sides of the Persian Gulf, including Ras Al Khaimah, Sharjah, Qishm, Bandar Abbas, and Lingeh. Their control of trade routes led to conflicts with the British-allied Omani Empire and eventually with Britain itself, resulting in the British labeling them as 'pirates' and the southern Gulf coast as the 'Pirate Coast'. The British mounted two major military campaigns against them in 1809 and 1819. The 1819 campaign was particularly devastating, with Major-General William Keir Grant leading 3,000 soldiers in the bombardment and capture of Ras Al Khaimah and the siege of Dhayah Fort, where the last Al Qasimi resistance surrendered on December 22, 1819. This led to the General Maritime Treaty of 1820, signed by Sheikh Sultan Bin Saqr Al Qasimi, establishing British protectorate over the Trucial States. In 1869, Ras Al Khaimah became fully independent from Sharjah under Sheikh Humaid bin Abdullah Al Qasimi, though it was re-incorporated into Sharjah from 1900 to 1921. Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi ruled from 1948 to 2010, initially declining to join the UAE upon its formation in 1971 due to disputes over the Iranian seizure of Abu Musa and the Tunb islands. However, on February 10, 1972, under his leadership, Ras Al Khaimah became the seventh emirate to join the United Arab Emirates. The current ruler, Sheikh Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, has governed since 2010, with Mohammed bin Saud Al Qasimi as the appointed heir presumptive.

migrationPath.description: Qawasim tribe established themselves in Ras Al Khaimah, maintaining connections with Persian territories including Lengeh

name: Rumaithat
legitimacyNotes: No tribal entity named 'Rumaithat' is described in the provided text. The text discusses individuals and places with similar names but no tribal structure.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: Based on the provided text, there is no information about a tribe called 'Rumaithat'. The text contains information about three distinct entities: 1) Rumaitha Al Busaidi - a contemporary Omani activist and scientist, 2) Rumaithiya - a residential area in Kuwait, and 3) Rumaythah ibn Abi Numayy - a historical Emir of Mecca from the 14th century. None of these appear to be references to a tribal entity called 'Rumaithat'.
history: The Rumaithat (Al Remeithi) are one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia. As part of the broader Bani Yas, they descend from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a, along with the Awamir tribe. The Bani Yas confederation, including the Rumaithat, settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 in present-day Abu Dhabi territory under the leadership of the Al Bu Falah clan. The Rumaithat were among the sections that formed part of this powerful confederation that would come to dominate much of what is now the United Arab Emirates. As members of the Bani Yas, they were part of the migration to Abu Dhabi island in 1793-1795 following the discovery of fresh water, and the subsequent establishment of Abu Dhabi town as the seat of the emirate. The Rumaithat, like other Bani Yas sections, experienced population decline through the mid-20th century due to emigration to Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia for employment opportunities, particularly in oil and construction sectors during the late 1950s, before experiencing return migration following the accession of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and the emirate's oil-driven development.

name: Ruwallah
legitimacyNotes: Major branch of Anizzah confederation with recognized leadership through traditional sheikhs
ancestorStory: Descended from Rwailah, son of Rabiah of the Anizzah tribal confederation. The tribe emerged as a distinct branch that became one of the most powerful Bedouin groups in northern Arabia.
description: The Ruwallah (Rwala) are one of the largest and most prestigious branches of the Anizzah tribal confederation, traditionally ranging across northern Saudi Arabia, Jordan, Syria, and Iraq. Historically known as one of the most powerful Bedouin tribes, they controlled vast territories and trade routes in the Syrian desert and northern Arabian Peninsula. The tribe is famous for their camel breeding, warrior traditions, and complex political alliances. Today, members of the Ruwallah are found across the GCC states, particularly in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, though many have settled while maintaining tribal identity.

relations.context: Historical alliances and conflicts during Saudi state formation
Traditional competition for territory and resources in northern Arabia

history:
The Ruwallah (Arabic: الرولة, Rwala) emerged as a distinct tribal branch sometime in the 16th century or shortly thereafter, belonging to the Dhana Maslam branch of the large Anizah tribal confederation. According to classical Arab genealogists, the Anizah tribe, including the Ruwallah, belongs to the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib, tracing their lineage to the northern Arabian (Adnani) genealogical tradition rather than the southern Arabian (Qahtani) line. The tribe was traditionally centered in the region of al-Jawf and Sirhan Valley in northern Arabia, with their tribal territories extending southwards to al-Qasim and northwards to Damascus before the demarcation of Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century.

The Ruwallah developed into one of the most powerful branches of the Anizah confederation, becoming so large and influential that they practically developed into an independent tribe. They were known for controlling vast territories and trade routes in the Syrian desert and northern Arabian Peninsula, engaging in the traditional Bedouin practices of camel breeding, warrior traditions, and complex political alliances. The tribe engaged in battles with other branches of Anizah and became arch-enemies of the large Shammar tribe, who inhabited roughly the same area and dominated Nejd in the late 19th century after temporarily deposing the Al Saud.

The leadership of the Ruwallah rests with the House of Sha'lan (Al Sha'lan), whose most notable leader was Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan (1847-1942), who ruled the tribe between 1904 and 1942. Nuri secured his rule by killing his brother Sattam and another brother, and became known as Emir of Jawf after occupying the Al Jawf region in 1909 from Al Rashid forces. Initially allied with the Ottomans until his exile to Spain in 1910, he later joined the Arab Revolt against the Ottoman Empire in 1917 under the influence of T.E. Lawrence and Emir Faisal. His forces were among the Arab units that entered Damascus on October 1, 1918.

The Ruwallah were notably progressive in adopting modern technology, with Al Shalaan owning multiple vehicles and creating a fleet of three 'armoured cars' with machine guns in 1926. The tribe developed significant political connections through strategic marriages with the Al Saud family - Nuri's granddaughters married multiple Saudi princes including Abdullah bin Abdulaziz. The Ruwallah consist of five major branches: Al-Kwakbah, Al-Doughman, Al-Murre'eth, Al-Frrejah, and Al-Ga'a'gaah. With the establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century, the traditional Bedouin lifestyle was severely impacted, and most tribe members have settled into sedentary or urban life in Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Syria, while maintaining their tribal identity.

migrationPath.description: Early expansion northward from central Arabian homeland into Syrian desert regions
Establishment of control over al-Jawf oasis and surrounding territories

name: Saba'
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Yemeni kingdom and dynasty with claimed descent from biblical/Quranic Queen of Sheba
ancestorName: Saba ibn Yashjub ibn Yarub ibn Qahtan
ancestorStory: Saba is considered the progenitor of the Sabaean people and the founder of the ancient kingdom of Saba in Yemen. According to Arab genealogical tradition, he was a descendant of Qahtan through Yashjub ibn Yarub.
description: Saba was an ancient Arabian kingdom and dynasty based in Yemen, famous for the legendary Queen of Sheba mentioned in biblical and Quranic accounts. The Sabaean kingdom controlled important trade routes between Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean, accumulating wealth through incense and spice trade. Their capital was at Marib, known for its great dam and advanced irrigation systems. While historically centered in Yemen, Sabaean influence extended throughout southern Arabia and their genealogical legacy is claimed by various Qahtani tribes across the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Himyar conquered Saba' multiple times, first in c. 25 BCE, with political fortunes changing frequently until final conquest around 280 CE
Saba is considered ancestor of Qahtani tribal confederation

history:
Saba' (Sheba) was an ancient South Arabian kingdom that existed in Yemen from approximately 1000-800 BCE until 275 CE, representing one of the foundational civilizations of the Arabian Peninsula. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Saba is considered the progenitor of the Sabaean people and descended from Qahtan through Yashjub ibn Yarub, making it a foundational Qahtani lineage. The kingdom's heartland was located around Marib and Sirwah, with Marib serving as its primary capital and featuring the famous Marib Dam mentioned in the Quran. The Sabaeans spoke Sabaic, a variety of Old South Arabian, and left behind extensive epigraphic records including over 6,500 Sabaic inscriptions out of 12,000 total Ancient South Arabian inscriptions.

The first major phase of Sabaean civilization lasted from the 8th-1st centuries BCE, reaching its height under rulers called 'mukarrib' (federator). The kingdom achieved its greatest territorial extent under Karib'il Watar, who through protracted warfare extended Sabaean territory from Najran in the north to the Gulf of Aden in the southwest, and eastward to the western foothills of the Hadhramaut plateau. This territorial range would not be seen again until the Himyarite Kingdom over 1,100 years later. The Sabaeans controlled crucial trade routes for frankincense and myrrh, accumulating enormous wealth through the spice trade with Africa, Arabia, and the Mediterranean, with trading partners extending from Khindanu and the Middle Euphrates to Gaza and later Petra.

After declining due to Roman expansion disrupting trade routes, Saba was first annexed by Himyar around the 1st century BCE, but re-emerged from the 1st-3rd centuries CE with a secondary capital established at Sanaa. During this second period, power dynamics had shifted from oasis cities like Marib to highland tribes, and the remarkable Ghumdan Palace was built at Sanaa. The Sabaeans also established a significant presence in the Horn of Africa, founding the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia around 800 BCE and maintaining colonies along the Somali coast. The kingdom was permanently conquered by the Himyarites around 275 CE, after which Saba lost its royal status and reverted to a normal tribe limited to the citizens of Marib.

The Sabaeans practiced a theocratic monarchy centered around their national god Almaqah, with four other deities also worshipped: Athtar, Haubas, Dhat-Himyam, and Dhat-Badan. Their religious and cultural influence extended far beyond their political borders through trade networks and conquest. Modern Qahtani tribes across the Arabian Peninsula trace their genealogical heritage to this ancient Sabaean legacy, and the kingdom's memory is preserved in both biblical and Quranic accounts of the Queen of Sheba's visit to King Solomon, though historians consider these accounts legendary. The collapse of traditional South Arabian civilization occurred in the 6th century CE due to multiple factors including political upheaval, the Plague of Justinian, severe droughts, and the breakdown of the Marib Dam system.

migrationPath.description: Sabaean migration across the Red Sea to establish the kingdom of D'mt in modern-day Ethiopia, building temples to Almaqah and spreading Sabaean culture

folkLegends.title: Queen of Sheba and Solomon
folkLegends.story: The biblical and Quranic accounts describe the legendary Queen of Sheba (known as Bilqis in Islamic tradition and Makeda in Ethiopian tradition) visiting King Solomon of Israel, engaging in trade involving aromatics and gold. This story reflects the kingdom's legendary wealth and international reputation, though it is considered legendary by historians as early first-millennium BCE epigraphic sources show no evidence of diplomatic missions or women rulers.

folkLegends.story: According to the Jewish historian Josephus, Sheba was the home of Princess Tharbis, a Cushite who is said to have been the wife of Moses before he married Zipporah.

nameEtymology: The name Saba' carries great prestige among South Arabians and Abyssinians, as it was widely considered to be the birthplace of South Arabian civilization as a whole. The tribal identity was expressed as 'sons of Almaqah' (their national god), reflecting the theocratic nature of Sabaean society.

name: Sabaeans
legitimacyNotes: Ancient South Arabian kingdom with divine kingship and control of incense trade routes
ancestorName: Saba ibn Yashjub ibn Yarub ibn Qahtan
ancestorStory: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Saba was descended from Qahtan and became the progenitor of the great South Arabian civilization. The kingdom is also associated with the biblical Queen of Sheba.
description: The Sabaeans were an ancient South Arabian civilization centered in present-day Yemen, famous for their advanced irrigation systems and control of the lucrative incense trade. They established one of the most powerful kingdoms in pre-Islamic Arabia, with their capital at Ma'rib and the famous dam that sustained their agricultural economy. Their trade networks extended throughout the Arabian Peninsula and connected to the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean routes. While the kingdom declined by the 6th century CE, Sabaean tribal descendants dispersed throughout Arabia and are considered ancestors of many Qahtani tribes in the modern GCC states.

relations.context: The Azd bordered Sabaeans in modern-day Yemen
Sabaeans are considered major ancestors of Qahtani tribal confederation
Kathiri claim descent from ancient South Arabian Sabaean origins

history:
The Sabaeans were an ancient South Arabian civilization that emerged around 1200-1000 BCE in the highlands of Yemen, centered at Ma'rib with their capital and the famous dam that sustained their agricultural economy. According to Arab genealogical tradition, they descended from Saba, son of Qahtan, making them part of the Qahtani Arab lineage and considered ancestors of many modern Qahtani tribes. The kingdom experienced two distinct periods: the first Sabaean kingdom (8th-1st centuries BCE) dominated by rulers called 'mukarrib' (federators) who established hegemony over neighboring South Arabian kingdoms, and a second resurgent kingdom (1st-3rd centuries CE) with rulers using the title 'malik' (kings).

During their imperial peak under Karib'il Watar (8th-7th centuries BCE), the Sabaeans controlled vast territories extending from Najran in the north to the Gulf of Aden, dominating the lucrative frankincense and myrrh trade routes that connected the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean networks. Their advanced irrigation system centered on the Ma'rib Dam supported a 10,000-hectare oasis and sustained their economy for over a millennium. The Sabaeans also established significant presence in the Horn of Africa, founding the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia around 800 BCE and maintaining trade networks along the East African coast.

The first Sabaean kingdom declined due to Roman economic pressure and territorial losses to neighboring kingdoms like Ma'in, Qataban, and ultimately Himyar, which annexed them around the 1st century BCE. After briefly re-emerging around 100 CE with Sanaa as a secondary capital, the Sabaeans were permanently conquered by the Himyarites around 275 CE. Following the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the 6th century CE, the event known as 'Sil al-Aram' (the Great Flood) caused massive population dispersal throughout Arabia. Sabaean tribal descendants migrated both internally within Yemen and externally to various regions, becoming ancestors to numerous Qahtani tribes found throughout the modern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the GCC states where their lineage is still recognized today.

migrationPath.description: Following the final Himyarite conquest and the eventual collapse of the Ma'rib Dam, Sabaean populations dispersed throughout Arabia in what became known as the Sil al-Aram migration
