timelineEvents.description: First stone inscriptions appear and Sabaean leaders adopt the title 'mukarrib' (federator), marking the beginning of their imperial expansion phase

timelineEvents.title: Conquests of Karib'il Watar
timelineEvents.description: Karib'il Watar conducts eight major military campaigns, conquering Awsan, Qataban, Hadhramaut, and extending Sabaean territory from Najran to the Gulf of Aden

timelineEvents.title: Construction of Almaqah Temple at Sirwah
timelineEvents.description: Yada'il Darih fundamentally remodels the Great Temple of Almaqah at Sirwah, creating monumental inscriptions recording the achievements of Sabaean rulers

timelineEvents.title: Founding of D'mt Kingdom
timelineEvents.description: Sabaean populations establish the kingdom of D'mt in Ethiopia with capital at Yeha, building temples to Almaqah and spreading Sabaean religious and cultural practices

timelineEvents.title: Completion of Ma'rib Dam
timelineEvents.description: Full closure of the Ma'rib Dam system is accomplished, enabling irrigation of the entire 10,000-hectare oasis and supporting the kingdom's agricultural base

timelineEvents.title: Roman Invasion Attempt
timelineEvents.description: Governor Aelius Gallus leads Roman expedition against Saba, besieging Ma'rib but forced to withdraw due to heat exhaustion, though Roman trade disruption continues

timelineEvents.title: First Kingdom Falls to Himyar
timelineEvents.description: Economically weakened by Roman disruption of trade routes, the first Sabaean kingdom is annexed by the expanding Himyarite Kingdom

timelineEvents.title: Second Sabaean Kingdom Emerges
timelineEvents.description: After Himyarite collapse, Saba re-emerges as independent kingdom with Sanaa as secondary capital alongside Ma'rib, focusing on highland rather than oasis power

timelineEvents.title: Final Himyarite Conquest
timelineEvents.description: After prolonged civil war between Yemeni dynasties, the late Himyarite Kingdom permanently conquers Saba, ending Sabaean royal status and reducing them to tribal level

timelineEvents.title: Ma'rib Dam Collapse
timelineEvents.description: Political authority weakens and dam maintenance fails, leading to final breach and temporary abandonment of the Ma'rib oasis, triggering major population dispersal

folkLegends.title: Queen of Sheba and King Solomon
folkLegends.story: According to biblical and Quranic tradition, the Queen of Sheba (known as Bilqis in Islamic tradition and Makeda in Ethiopian tradition) visited King Solomon of Israel, engaging in trade involving aromatics and gold. The story appears in both the Hebrew Bible and the Quran, though historians consider the account legendary as early first-millennium BCE epigraphic sources show no evidence of diplomatic missions or women rulers in Saba during that period.

folkLegends.title: Sil al-Aram (The Great Flood)
folkLegends.story: The catastrophic collapse of the Ma'rib Dam caused widespread flooding that destroyed surrounding villages, cities, and farms, forcing the Sabaean population to migrate throughout Arabia. This event, mentioned in the Quran, led to the dispersal of Sabaean tribes across the Arabian Peninsula, with many becoming ancestors of modern tribal groups.

nameEtymology: The name 'Saba' appears to derive from the ancient South Arabian kingdom centered at Ma'rib. Among South Arabians and Abyssinians, Sheba's name carried prestige as it was widely considered the birthplace of South Arabian civilization. The term appears in various forms across ancient sources including Sabaic inscriptions and external accounts.

name: Sakasic

relations.context: Himyarite tribe that later joined Lakhmids in Northern Egypt

history:
The Sakasic (also spelled Sakasek) were a Himyarite tribe that emerged during the period of Himyarite dominance in southern Arabia. According to the research, they were specifically identified as one of the branches of Banu Al-Sakasek under the Kinda tribal confederation, which was part of the larger Qahtani lineage tracing back to Qahtan. The Kinda tribes, including the Sakasic, initially dwelt in Bahrain but were expelled to East Yemen, where some groups moved to Nejd and established a powerful government that served as a vassal kingdom for Himyar.

The Sakasic, alongside Banu Al-Sukun, fairly ruled parts of Hadramout in eastern Yemen during their time in the Arabian Peninsula. As a Himyarite tribe, they were part of the southern Arabian confederation that eventually conquered and incorporated the declining Sabaean kingdom by the 2nd century BC. They were contemporaries of other major Himyarite and Qahtani tribal groups including the Lakhmids and Banu Judham, with whom they would later share territories in Egypt.

Around the 3rd century AD, coinciding with the destruction of the Marib Dam and the subsequent northward migrations of many Yemeni tribes, the Sakasic migrated from their homeland in Yemen to northern Egypt. This migration was part of a larger movement of southern Arabian tribes, including the Lakhmids and Banu Judham, who were among the first Southern Arabs to settle in Northern Egypt. The Sakasic established themselves in the ancient town of Bubastis in Egypt, which they renamed Zaqaziq after their tribal name.

The Sakasic settlement became so significant that Zaqaziq not only became the modern name of the town but also gave its name to one of Egypt's provinces, demonstrating the lasting impact of their settlement in the region. Like other Kinda tribes, the Sakasic gradually declined after the fall of Himyar in 525 AD, but their legacy in Egypt through the naming of Zaqaziq and its province represents one of the most enduring examples of Arabian tribal influence in North Africa.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Yemen to northern Egypt around 3rd century AD, settling in ancient Bubastis

timelineEvents.title: Himyarite Incorporation of Saba
timelineEvents.description: Sakasic as part of Himyarite confederation conquered and incorporated the declining Sabaean kingdom

timelineEvents.title: Settlement of Bubastis
timelineEvents.description: Sakasic tribe settled in ancient town of Bubastis in Egypt, renaming it Zaqaziq after their tribal name

timelineEvents.title: Fall of Himyar
timelineEvents.description: Decline of Sakasic influence following the fall of the Himyarite kingdom

nameEtymology: The modern Egyptian city and province of Zaqaziq derives its name from the tribal name Sakasic, demonstrating the linguistic evolution from the original Arabian tribal designation to its current form

name: Salalat
description: The Salalat were a tribal group mentioned in historical Arabian Peninsula records. Limited historical documentation exists about their specific origins, territorial base, or major role in Gulf regional politics. They appear to have been a smaller tribal entity that may have been absorbed into larger confederations or migrated from their original territories.
history:
The Salalat are documented as one of the subsections of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation with territories around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and areas of the UAE east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. The Bani Kaab confederation comprised multiple subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat. Unlike some nomadic sections of the confederation such as the Drisah and Shwaihiyin, the Salalat had settled by the turn of the 20th century as part of a total Bani Kaab population of approximately 7,250 members, of whom 1,150 were Bedouin.

In 1844, the Salalat, as part of the broader Bani Kaab confederation, participated in a significant tribal alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas. This coalition successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi, demonstrating the Salalat's integration into the wider political dynamics of southeastern Arabia. The Bani Kaab, including the Salalat, were rivals to the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes and had pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain.

A century later, the political alignments had shifted dramatically. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab confederation, including the Salalat, supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis. The tribal sheikh at the time, Obaid bin Jumah, reportedly told the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province that 'Our territories are yours.' The Salalat participated in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies during this period, demonstrating their continued role in the complex tribal politics of the Buraimi region.

The historical record suggests the Salalat maintained their settled lifestyle in the traditional Bani Kaab territories spanning the border regions between modern-day Oman and the UAE. Their participation in major political events from the 1840s through the 1950s indicates their continued importance within the Bani Kaab confederation, despite being one of the smaller settled sections rather than a major nomadic division.

migrationPath.description: Northern migration of some Salalat clans seeking new grazing lands and trade opportunities

timelineEvents.title: Alliance Against Wahhabis
timelineEvents.description: Salalat as part of Bani Kaab allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi

timelineEvents.title: Settlement Period
timelineEvents.description: Salalat had settled as part of Bani Kaab confederation by turn of 20th century

timelineEvents.title: Buraimi Dispute Support
timelineEvents.description: Participated in Saudi-supported resistance against British forces during Buraimi Dispute

name: Salihids
legitimacyNotes: Claimed legitimacy through Ismaili religious authority and military conquest
ancestorStory: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Sulayhi established the dynasty in the 11th century as an Ismaili ruler in Yemen. The dynasty claimed descent from noble Arab lineage and religious authority through Fatimid connections.
description: The Salihids (Sulayhids) were an Ismaili Shia dynasty that ruled parts of Yemen from 1047 to 1138 CE. Based in the Yemeni highlands with their capital at Sana'a, they controlled important trade routes and agricultural regions. The dynasty was notable for the prominent role of Queen Arwa al-Sulayhi, who ruled effectively for several decades. Their influence extended into parts of the Arabian Peninsula but they are primarily significant to Yemeni rather than Gulf history, eventually being overthrown by the Ayyubids.
history:
The Salihids (Arabic: بنو سليح, Banū Salīḥ) were a dominant Arab foederati tribe that served the Byzantine Empire in the 5th century CE, succeeding the Tanukhids and preceding the Ghassanids as Byzantium's chief Arab allies. According to genealogical sources, they stemmed from the Quda'a tribal confederation, with their lineage traced as Salīḥ ibn Ḥulwān ibn ʿImran ibn al-Ḥafī ibn Quḍāʿa. The tribe's royal house was known as the Zokomids (Ḍajaʿima), founded by Zokomos (Ḥamāṭa) who converted to Christianity along with his subjects after a monk prophesied the birth of his son. Originally concentrated in the Wadi Sirhan and Balqa regions of modern Jordan, they were charged with collecting taxes from Bedouins within the Limes Arabicus and protecting the Byzantine frontier from raiders.

The most notable Salihid phylarch was Dawud (David), a Christian king who initially engaged in military campaigns but later embraced religious life, earning the nickname 'al-Lathiq' (the bedraggled) for carrying water and mortar during the construction of his monastery, Dayr Dawud, between Resafa and Ithriya in northern Syria. His piety weakened his military position, leading to his death in battle at the hands of Tha'laba ibn Amir of Banu Kalb and Mu'awiya ibn Hujayr of Banu Namir between al-Qurnatayn and Mount Harib. The Salihids' decline accelerated after their participation in Emperor Leo I's failed 468 CE expedition against the Vandals in North Africa, which decimated their forces.

The tribe was ultimately defeated by the Ghassanids around 502 CE, becoming subordinate federate tribes. The killing of key Salihid leaders including Dawud, his cousin Ziyad (who died at the battle of al-Baradān against the Kinda), and tax collector Sabīṭ ibn al-Mundhir (slain by Ghassanid chief Jidʿ ibn ʿAmr) contributed to their downfall. During the Islamic conquest of the Levant in the 630s, Christian Salihids fought alongside other Arab federate tribes against Muslim forces at Dumat al-Jandal and Ziza, refusing conversion offers at Qinnasrin in 638. The tribe largely dispersed throughout Syria and Iraq, joining the Quda'a confederation during the Umayyad period, with their staunch Christianity rendering them isolated in the Islamic era unlike other Arab tribes whose members continued to flourish.

migrationPath.description: Early migration from northern Arabian valley into Byzantine frontier regions, possibly allied with Palmyrene Empire
Expansion northward as Byzantine foederati, establishing presence around Resafa and Ithriya
Dispersal after Islamic conquest, integration into Quda'a confederation during Umayyad period

timelineEvents.title: Rise to Prominence
timelineEvents.description: Salihids became Byzantium's chief Arab ally following the decline of the Tanukhids after their failed revolt

timelineEvents.title: Zokomos Conversion
timelineEvents.description: Zokomos converts to Christianity with his subjects after monk's prophecy, founding the Zokomid ruling house

timelineEvents.title: Vandalic Expedition Disaster
timelineEvents.description: Large Salihid contingent decimated in Emperor Leo I's failed expedition against Vandals in North Africa

timelineEvents.title: Death of Dawud
timelineEvents.description: King Dawud killed in battle by Tha'laba ibn Amir and Mu'awiya ibn Hujayr between al-Qurnatayn and Mount Harib

timelineEvents.title: Battle of al-Baradān
timelineEvents.description: Salihid phylarch Ziyad killed in battle against Kinda tribe led by Hujr ibn Harith

timelineEvents.title: Ghassanid Supremacy
timelineEvents.description: Salihids defeated by Ghassanids, becoming subordinate federate tribes under Byzantine authority

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Dumat al-Jandal
timelineEvents.description: Christian Salihids fight alongside other Arab federate tribes against Muslim forces under Iyad ibn Ghanm

timelineEvents.title: Refusal at Qinnasrin
timelineEvents.description: Orthodox Christian Salihids refuse Abu Ubayda's offer to convert to Islam at military encampment

folkLegends.title: The Prophetic Birth of Zokomos' Son
folkLegends.story: According to Sozomen, Zokomos became a phylarch under the Byzantines and converted to Christianity along with 'all his subjects' after 'a certain monk of great celebrity' prophesied the birth of his son on condition of embracing the Christian faith. The son born was named Amr, whose name signifies a good omen, and he may have been the prophesied child who founded a branch of the Zokomid household.

nameEtymology: The actual name of the tribe's eponymous progenitor Salīḥ, according to the Jamharat, was ʿAmr

name: Sanhan
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Yemeni tribal authority with historical presence in southern Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Sanhan traces its lineage to Sanhan bin Yazid, considered one of the ancient Qahtani tribal ancestors. The tribe has maintained its identity and territorial base in Yemen for centuries.
description: Sanhan is a Qahtani Arab tribe historically based in the highlands of Yemen, particularly in the Sanaa region. They are known for their agricultural settlements and traditional tribal governance structures. The tribe has maintained its presence in Yemen throughout various political changes and continues to be active in tribal affairs. While primarily Yemen-based, some branches have historical connections to broader Arabian Peninsula tribal networks.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage

history: Sanhan is a Qahtani Arab tribe with deep historical roots in the highlands southeast of Sanaa, Yemen. Ancient Sabaean inscriptions dating to the 7th century BCE consistently mention Sanhan alongside Khawlan, indicating their longstanding presence in the region. The research indicates that Sanhan maintained close relationships with neighboring Khawlan tribes and was part of broader Qahtani genealogical networks. Ancient Sabaean texts reference military campaigns in Wadi Dafa where Sanhan resided alongside Dadan tribes, and this location remains inhabited by Sanhan today. The tribe gained significant political prominence through Ali Abdullah Saleh, who was born in 1947 in Beit al-Ahmar village in Sanhan territory, approximately 20 kilometers southeast of Sanaa. Despite not being from a traditionally powerful sheikhly family, Saleh rose through military ranks after joining the Royal Armed Forces in 1958 and participating in the 1962 Nasserist coup that established the Yemen Arab Republic. After becoming president in 1978 following Ahmad al-Ghashmi's assassination, Saleh created an extensive patronage system centered on his Sanhan tribe. Approximately 70% of command positions in Yemen's security services and army were filled by Sanhan members, making the tribe the backbone of his three-decade rule. The tribe's political influence extended through Saleh's extended family network, with his brothers, sons, and nephews occupying key government positions. Even after Saleh's death in 2017 during fighting with Houthi forces in Sanaa, Sanhan remains an influential tribal group in Yemen's complex political landscape.

timelineEvents.title: First Historical Mention
timelineEvents.description: Sanhan tribe first mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions alongside Khawlan tribe, indicating established presence in Yemen highlands

timelineEvents.title: Birth of Ali Abdullah Saleh
timelineEvents.description: Ali Abdullah Saleh born in Beit al-Ahmar village in Sanhan territory, 20km southeast of Sanaa

timelineEvents.title: Nasserist Coup Participation
timelineEvents.description: Saleh participates in military coup overthrowing Yemeni monarchy, establishing Yemen Arab Republic

timelineEvents.title: Saleh Becomes President
timelineEvents.description: Ali Abdullah Saleh elected president of Yemen Arab Republic after al-Ghashmi's assassination, beginning Sanhan political dominance

timelineEvents.title: Patronage System Establishment
timelineEvents.description: Saleh consolidates power by placing 70% of military command positions with Sanhan tribe members

timelineEvents.title: Yemeni Unification
timelineEvents.description: Saleh becomes president of unified Yemen, extending Sanhan influence over entire country

timelineEvents.title: Death of Ali Abdullah Saleh
timelineEvents.description: Saleh killed by Houthi sniper during battle in Sanaa, ending decades of Sanhan political dominance

name: Sawalim
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on lineage and consensus
description: The Sawalim are a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula. Like many smaller tribal branches, they maintain traditional pastoral economic activities and social structures. Limited historical documentation makes it difficult to trace their specific origins or major historical roles in Gulf region politics.
history:
The research provides evidence of Sawalim presence in two distinct geographical regions: the Levant and the Arabian Peninsula. In the early Ottoman period, the Sawalim appear as a Bedouin tribal group active in the political conflicts of the Levant. Historical records document their involvement in the revolt of Janbirdi al-Ghazali against the Ottoman Empire in 1521, where they allied with other tribal groups including the Banu Ata and Banu Atiyya in the Gaza and Ramla areas, opposing the Turabay dynasty and other tribes loyal to Ottoman rule.

In the Arabian Peninsula, the Sawalim are documented as a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribal confederation. This branch of the Sawalim established territorial control in areas around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions of the UAE east of Buraimi, including the strategic valleys of Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. By the turn of the 20th century, these Sawalim had undergone a significant social transformation, transitioning from their traditional nomadic lifestyle to settled communities as part of a larger Bani Kaab population estimated at approximately 7,250 people.

Contemporary settlement patterns show continued Sawalim presence in Oman, with Falij al-Sawalim in the Wilayat of Al Khaburah in North Al Batinah Governorate recording 14 residents as of 2010. Additionally, the research indicates a connection to Upper Egypt, where a village called El Sawalim exists within the jurisdiction of Tahta in the Sohag Governorate, suggesting either historical migration patterns or shared tribal nomenclature across these regions. The relationship between these geographically dispersed Sawalim communities requires further documentation to establish definitive genealogical connections.

migrationPath.to: settled areas in Oman and UAE
migrationPath.description: Transition from nomadic to settled life as part of Bani Kaab confederation by the turn of the 20th century

timelineEvents.title: Support for Janbirdi Revolt
timelineEvents.description: Sawalim tribal forces allied with Janbirdi al-Ghazali during his rebellion against Ottoman rule, alongside Banu Ata and Banu Atiyya tribes

timelineEvents.title: Transition to Settled Life
timelineEvents.description: Sawalim subsection of Bani Kaab completed transition from nomadic to settled lifestyle in UAE-Oman border region

name: Sba'a
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal confederation recognized by customary law and historical precedent
description: Al-Sba'a (The Seven) was a historical tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, likely consisting of seven allied tribes or tribal sections. The confederation appears in early Arabic poetry and historical accounts as part of the complex tribal alliances of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia. Like many ancient confederations, the specific composition and territories of Al-Sba'a are not well-documented in surviving historical sources. The name suggests a formal alliance structure based on the significant number seven in Arabian tribal tradition.
history:
The Sba'a (Al-Sba'a, "The Seven") is a sub-tribe of the great Anizah confederation, part of the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The Anizah confederation, including the Sba'a, has pre-Islamic origins and was among the tribes that supported Ali ibn Abi Talib at the Battle of Siffin, contributing 4,000 armored warriors. The Sba'a is organized into several sub-sections, including Al-Abedah led by Ibn Hudayb, Al-Rasaleen led by Ibn Aida, and the Mezrab section from which came Sheikh Medjuel el Mezrab.

In the mid-18th to early 19th centuries, the Sba'a entered the central Syrian plains as part of the broader Anizah migration northward from the Arabian Peninsula. They often conflicted with the Hasana tribe for territorial control over the Syrian plains. The Sba'a established themselves in the Syrian desert regions, with their traditional territories extending into areas around Salamiyah, where they formed loose alliances with Ismaili settlers in the mid-19th century to provide mutual aid against other Bedouin groups.

The tribe was led by various sheikhs, most notably Ibn Murshid as Sheikh of Sba'a, with sub-sections maintaining their own leadership structure. A significant political moment occurred in June 1930 when Sheikh Rakan bin Mershed of the Sba'a signed a peace treaty with Nuri bin Hazaa Al Shalaan, the powerful chief of the Ruwallah tribe, ending conflicts between these two Anizah sub-tribes that had been competing for influence in the Syrian desert.

The Sba'a's connection to broader regional politics was demonstrated through the marriage of Jane Digby, an English aristocrat, to Sheikh Medjuel el Mezrab of the Mezrab section of the Sba'a in the 1850s. This union, which lasted 28 years until Digby's death in 1881, highlighted the tribe's integration into the complex social fabric of 19th-century Syria. Digby adopted Arab dress, learned Arabic, and lived both in Damascus and in the nomadic desert lifestyle, spending half of each year in goat-hair tents with the tribe.

migrationPath.description: Sba'a entered central Syrian plains as part of Anizah confederation migration

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Siffin participation
timelineEvents.description: Anizah confederation including Sba'a supported Ali ibn Abi Talib with 4,000 armored warriors

timelineEvents.title: Entry into Syrian plains
timelineEvents.description: Sba'a entered central Syrian plains, often conflicting with Hasana tribe

timelineEvents.title: Alliance with Ismaili settlers
timelineEvents.description: Sba'a formed loose alliance with Ismaili settlers around Salamiyah

timelineEvents.title: Jane Digby marriage
timelineEvents.description: English aristocrat Jane Digby married Sheikh Medjuel el Mezrab of Mezrab section

timelineEvents.title: Peace treaty with Ruwallah
timelineEvents.description: Sheikh Rakan bin Mershed signed peace treaty with Nuri Al Shalaan of Ruwallah

nameEtymology: Al-Sba'a means 'The Seven' in Arabic, suggesting the tribe was originally a confederation of seven allied tribes or tribal sections, reflecting the significance of the number seven in Arabian tribal tradition

name: Shaiqiyah
legitimacyNotes: Riverain Sudanese Arab tribe with claimed Ja'ali lineage
ancestorStory: Descended from Shaiq, claimed to be part of the Ja'ali tribal confederation of northern Sudan. The tribe traces its origins to Arab migrants who settled along the Nile in medieval times.
description: The Shaiqiyah are a Sudanese Arab tribe historically based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, particularly in the Dongola region. They were known as skilled horsemen and warriors who controlled river trade routes and resisted Egyptian expansion in the early 19th century. The tribe has limited direct connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal politics but maintains cultural and genealogical ties to broader Arab tribal networks. Their alliance with the Manasir reflects historical connections between Sudanese and Gulf Arab tribal confederations.

relations.context: Neighboring downstream riverine tribe with similar culture. One theory suggests Manasir descended from Shaiqi clan al-Hankab.

history: The Shaiqiyah (also rendered Shaigiya, Shawayga or Shaykia) are an Arabized Nubian tribe primarily based along the Nile River in northern Sudan, particularly in the Dongola region and Dar al-Shayqiya stretching from Korti to the fourth Nile cataract. Despite claims to descent from a Hejazi Arab named Shaig, allegedly a descendant of Abbas (uncle of Prophet Muhammad) who came from the Arabian Peninsula in the 7th century, they have been classified by scholars as Arabised Nubians who originated in the 15th century as a hybrid of various tribes settled in the area. By the early 20th century, tribal nobles themselves denied Arab origins, stating they were indigenous to Sudan. The tribe was historically bilingual in Arabic and Dongolawi (a Nubian language) until the late 19th century, with complete Arabization occurring by the early 20th century. The Shaiqiyah emerged as an independent political entity after the Makurian civil war of 1365, initially as subjects of the Funj Sultanate before breaking free around 1690 by defeating the Abdelab governor. They became renowned as fierce horsemen and warriors who controlled river trade routes and conducted raids as far as Wadi Halfa in the north and Shendi in the south. Their military prowess was noted by early 19th century travelers like James Bruce and John Lewis Burckhardt, who described them as perfectly independent people fighting on horseback in coats of mail with lances, targets, and sabres. The tribe's independence ended in 1820 when they were defeated by the Turco-Egyptian forces at the battle of Korti under Muhammad Ali's conquest, despite initial resistance led by Mihera Bint Abboud. Following their defeat, they were incorporated into Turkish ranks and used during the suppression of the Ja'alin revolt in 1822, receiving Ja'alin lands between Shendi and Khartoum as reward for their service. During the Mahdist period (1880s-1898), the tribe suffered severe persecution, with many leaders executed, reducing them to a few hundred families by the reconquest. They later recovered and served prominently in the Egyptian Army and Sudan Defence Force, with General Ibrahim Abboud becoming Sudan's president in 1964.

migrationPath.description: Migration from more southern regions to present homeland as noted by James Bruce

timelineEvents.title: Independence from Makuria
timelineEvents.description: Dar Shaigiya became independent after Makurian civil war

timelineEvents.title: First Historical Mention
timelineEvents.description: Italian visitor to Upper Egypt remarks on pyramids in country of the 'Xiogeia'

timelineEvents.title: Break from Funj Sultanate
timelineEvents.description: Shaiqiyah defeated Abdelab governor and became only independent tribe in region

timelineEvents.title: Challenge by Mamelukes
timelineEvents.description: Challenged at Dongola by Mamelukes but continued to dominate considerable part of Nubia

timelineEvents.title: Battle of Korti
timelineEvents.description: Defeated by Turkish/Egyptian forces after refusing to submit, retreated southwards

timelineEvents.title: Suppression of Ja'alin Revolt
timelineEvents.description: Used by Turks to suppress Ja'alin revolt, demonstrated brutality and received Ja'alin lands as reward

timelineEvents.title: Surrender to Mahdi
timelineEvents.description: Saleh Bey and 1,400 men surrendered to Mahdi's forces, leading to tribal proscription

folkLegends.title: Descent from Shaiq ibn Abbas
folkLegends.story: The tribe claims descent from Shaiq, allegedly a descendant of Abbas (uncle of Prophet Muhammad) who came from the Arabian Peninsula in the 7th century

nameEtymology: Named after their claimed ancestor Shaiq, though this origin is disputed by scholars who consider them indigenous Arabized Nubians

name: Shammar
legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

relations.context: The Ruwallah became the arch-enemy of the large tribe of Shammar, who inhabited roughly the same area and dominated Nejd in the late 19th century. A 19th century oral poetic epic tells of rivalry between heroes from both tribes.
Shammar is the largest branch descended from Tayy tribe
Blood relations with Yemenite dynasties that ruled Syria, enabled Tayy expansion northward
Blood relations with Yemenite dynasties that ruled Iraq, enabled Tayy expansion to al-Hirah

history:
The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy tribe, which migrated from Yemen to the northern Arabian Peninsula in the second century CE. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia during their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE. These mountains subsequently became known as Jabal Shammar from the 14th century, when the tribe first appeared in literature under the name Shammar. The tribe consists of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah, with the historical seat of leadership in Hāʾil.

The Shammar ruled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from 1836 under the Al Rashid dynasty, reaching peak power around the 1850s when they controlled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to Syria and Upper Mesopotamia. Two clans succeeded each other in ruling Hāʾil in the 19th century: first the Al Ali, then the Al Rashid. During the civil war that tore apart the Second Saudi State, the Al Rashid emirs intervened and gradually took control of much of the Saudi realm, finally capturing Riyadh in 1895 and expelling the Saudi leaders to Kuwait. The Bedouin Shammari tribesmen provided the majority of Al Rashid's military support.

In the 17th century, large sections of Shammar under Al Jarba leadership migrated to Iraq, reaching as far as the northern city of Mosul. The Shammar became one of Iraq's largest tribes with over 1.5 million members, divided into two geographical subsections: the northern branch known as Shammar al-Jarba (mainly Sunni), and the southern branch Shammar Toga (which converted to Shia Islam around the 19th century). The Saudi conquest of Hāʾil in 1921 ended the Rashidi emirate, with many Shammar subsequently fleeing to Syria and Iraq. Despite modernization, the Shammar maintain strong tribal identity across Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Syria, and Jordan, with notable political influence including Ghazi al-Yawar becoming Iraq's interim president in 2004.

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Name Shammar
folkLegends.story: In the Namara inscription dating from 328 CE, the name 'Shammar' is believed to refer to a city in Yemen, though it may refer to the city where the Himyarite King Shammar Yahri'sh lived in Radda District, located about 100 kilometres from Dhamar. Since King Shammar Yahri'sh ruled during the last decade of the third century, it could be referring to the city he lived in or one named after him.

folkLegends.title: The First Chiefs of Shammar
folkLegends.story: Oral tradition mentions that the first chiefs of the Shammar tribe, Arar and Omair, were of the 'Abda family of Dhaigham, who ruled Shammar from Jabal Shammar.

nameEtymology: The name 'Shammar' may derive from the Himyarite King Shammar Yahri'sh who ruled in the last decade of the third century, or from a city in Yemen where he lived, as referenced in the Namara inscription of 328 CE. The mountains of Ajā and Salma came to be known as 'Jabal Shammar' (Shammar's Mountain) from the 14th century.

name: Shammar al-Jarba
legitimacyNotes: Traditional leadership through the Al Jarba sheikhs, historically recognized authority over northern Arabian territories
ancestorStory: The Al Jarba trace their lineage to Jarba ibn Muhammad, who established the family's prominence within the Shammar confederation. They became the paramount sheikhs of Shammar through political acumen and tribal alliances.
description: The Shammar al-Jarba are a prominent branch of the larger Shammar tribal confederation, serving as the traditional paramount sheikhs of Shammar. They are historically based in northern Arabia, particularly the Najd and extending into Iraq and Syria. The Al Jarba family has maintained political leadership over various Shammar sections for centuries, playing significant roles in Arabian politics and tribal alliances. While their main territories lie outside the GCC, they have historical connections and presence in northern Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Both are major branches of the broader Shammar confederation
Fellow Shammar tribal section under traditional Al Jarba leadership

history: The Shammar al-Jarba represent the paramount sheikhly branch of the larger Shammar tribal confederation, tracing their lineage to Jarba ibn Muhammad who established their prominence within Shammar. The broader Shammar confederation originated as part of the Tayy tribe, which migrated from Yemen to the mountains of Aja and Salma in northern Arabia around 115 CE under Usma bin Luai. These mountains were initially called Jabal Tayy, later renamed Jabal Shammar in the 14th century when the tribe adopted the Shammar name. The Al Jarba clan emerged from the Al Sultah section of the Al Aslam branch of Shammar, becoming the traditional ruling family of the confederation. In the late 18th century, around 1791-1798, a massive exodus of Shammar occurred under Al Jarba leadership following conflicts with the expanding Saudi-Wahhabi state. Sheikh Muslit bin Mutlag Al-Jarba was killed in 1791 in the Battle of Aladwa against Ibn Saud, prompting his successor Mutlag Al-Jarba to lead thousands of Shammar families to safety in southern Iraq. This migration established the Shammar al-Jarba as the dominant tribal force in the Mesopotamian region between the Tigris and Euphrates, reaching as far north as Mosul. The Al Jarba subsequently became one of Iraq's most powerful tribes, owning vast tracts of land and supporting the Hashemite monarchy. After 2003, Ghazi al-Yawar from the Al Jarba clan was chosen as Iraq's interim president. The family also established significant presence in Syria, where they continue to lead Syrian Shammar under Sheikh Humaydi Daham al-Hadi al-Jarba (1936-2022). The Al Jarba have maintained their traditional role as paramount sheikhs of Shammar across borders, with contemporary leadership including the current Sheikh of Sheikhs whose uncle is Ghazi al-Yawar. The family has historically allied with Ottoman authorities against Wahhabi expansion and later played significant roles in modern Middle Eastern politics, maintaining their influence through strategic alliances and tribal leadership across Iraq, Syria, and their ancestral lands in northern Saudi Arabia.

name: Shammar Al-Sayeh
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Shammar confederation with established genealogical lineage
ancestorStory: Al-Sayeh is considered a notable ancestor within the Shammar tribal genealogy. The branch derives its name and identity from this progenitor within the broader Shammar confederation.
description: Shammar Al-Sayeh is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the major Arabian tribal groups. Historically based in the northern Arabian Peninsula, they participated in the traditional nomadic lifestyle and tribal movements across the region. As part of the Shammar confederation, they have historical connections to various Gulf regions through migration and tribal alliances. The branch maintains its distinct identity while being part of the broader Shammar tribal structure.

relations.context: Both branches of the larger Shammar confederation
Fellow Shammar tribal branches with common ancestry

history: The Shammar Al-Sayeh represents a tribal branch within the larger Shammar confederation, which traces its origins to the Tayy tribe that migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in 115 CE under the leadership of Usma bin Luai. The Tayy conquered the mountains of Ajā and Salma from the Banu Assad and Banu Tamim, with these territories later becoming known as Jabal Shammar in the 14th century. The broader Shammar confederation consists of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah. Research specifically identifies Shammar Al-Sayeh as 'a tribal confederation of tribes from Shammar' that maintained independence from the Al-Jarba authority that led major Shammar migrations to Iraq. While the main Shammar confederation became centered in Ha'il and later established the Emirate of Jabal Shammar under the Al Rashid dynasty, the Al-Sayeh branch appears to have maintained its distinct identity within the broader tribal structure. The research indicates that during various historical periods, different Shammar groups, including Al-Sayeh, migrated to Iraq and other regions, with some settling in areas like Najaf where they formed communities alongside other Shammar groups such as Al-Zuhairy and Al-Towej. The Al-Sayeh branch, like other Shammar divisions, would have participated in the complex tribal politics and conflicts that characterized Arabian Peninsula history, including the long rivalry with the Anazah confederation and the various Saudi-Rashidi wars of the 18th and 19th centuries.

name: Shammar Toga
legitimacyNotes: Branch of the larger Shammar confederation with traditional pastoral rights
ancestorStory: Toga is considered the eponymous ancestor of this branch of Shammar. The lineage traces back through the broader Shammar tribal genealogy.
description: Shammar Toga is a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the major Arabian tribal groups. They are traditionally nomadic pastoralists who have historically ranged across northern Arabia and into Iraq. As part of the broader Shammar tribal system, they maintain traditional social structures and continue to be present in modern Gulf states. The tribe follows the typical Shammar patterns of seasonal migration and livestock herding.

relations.context: Both are branches of the greater Shammar confederation
Sister branches within Shammar tribal structure

history: Shammar Toga is a Shia branch of the larger Shammar tribal confederation that emerged from the broader Shammar tribal system. According to Wikipedia sources, the Shammar are a Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy tribe, which migrated into northern Arabia from Yemen in the second century CE. The Shammar confederation consists of three main branches: Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah, with Shammar Toga being a geographical rather than genealogical subdivision. In the 17th century, a large section of the Shammar left their traditional homeland of Jabal Shammar under the leadership of the Al Jarba clan and settled in Iraq, reaching as far north as Mosul. The Shammar became divided into two geographical branches in Iraq: the northern branch known as Shammar al-Jarba, which remained primarily Sunni, and the southern branch, Shammar Toga, which converted to Shia Islam around the 19th century after settling in southern Iraq. This religious conversion occurred during the period when many originally Sunni tribes living along the Euphrates and Tigris converted to Shia Islam, often through interaction with Shia missionaries from Najaf and Karbala who operated with relative freedom during Ottoman rule. The conversion was part of a broader pattern in which tribes adopted sedentary agricultural life in the hinterlands of the Shia shrine cities and frequently traded and interacted with their residents.

name: Shihuh
legitimacyNotes: The tribe incorporates elements of Iranian origin with the Kumazarah subsection speaking an Iranian language. The majority Arab element is linked to Yemeni immigration in the second century.
ancestorStory: The Arab element of the tribe's make-up is thought to be linked to the wave of immigration from Yemen, which brought groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants north in the second century.
traditionalEconomy: Agricultural communities in winter months with terraced mountain fields, seasonal migration for date harvests and pearl fishing, goat herding for trade
description: The Shihuh is an Arab tribe living in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman, inhabiting the northern part of the Hajar Mountain range, specifically in the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula). The tribe has long been influential in the affairs of both the east and west coast settlements of the northern UAE and Oman and has fiercely maintained both its identity and independence. They are essentially agricultural during winter months in mountain communities, migrating to coastal areas during warmer months for date season work and pearl fishing.

subTribes.name: Bani Hadiyah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal section of the larger Shihuh confederation
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Pearling and farming - seasonal work in pearling industry and agriculture in mountain regions
subTribes.description: The Bani Hadiyah is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, originating in Ras Al Khaimah and also in areas of Northern Oman. They are one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, along with the Bani Shatair. The tribe is divided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. Traditionally, they practiced seasonal migration, working in Ras Al Khaimah during the pearling season and farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter.

subTribes.name: Bani Shatair
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Pearling and farming - worked in Ras Al Khaimah and Rams during pearling season, farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter
subTribes.description: Bani Shatair is a tribal name originating in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE and areas of Northern Oman. They are one of two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, traditionally engaged in seasonal migration between coastal pearling work and mountain farming. The village of Sha'am was originally settled entirely by the Bani Shatair.

relations.context: Frequent neighbors sharing cultural similarities and traditions, with common intermarriage especially with Al Haramsha of Bani Idaid Shihuh
Allied with Shihuh against Sharjah during independence struggles
Closely allied tribe that settled the wadis and farms to the south of Wadi Bih while Shihuh inhabited mountains to the north
Frequently in conflict with the Sharqiyin of the east coast of the UAE, but would settle differences to make common cause against Sharjah
Frequently in conflict with the Al Qasimi of both Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah
During pearling season, both Bedouin Tunaij and Shihuh would work as seasonal labor on the coast

history: The Shihuh is an Arab tribe inhabiting the northern Hajar Mountain range, specifically the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula) in the UAE and Oman. The tribe's Arab lineage traces to the wave of immigration from Yemen in the second century CE, specifically groups of Malik bin Fahm immigrants who moved north and established the first Arabian settlements in Oman. This migration led to conflict with the Persians who claimed Omani territory, culminating in the great Salut Battle where the Persians were defeated, paving the way for Oman's Arabization. The Shihuh are divided into two main sections: Bani Hadiyah (found mainly on the western coast including Khasab) and Bani Shatair (centered around Kumzar). The Bani Hadiyah splits into several subsections: the Bani Muhammad; Bani Ali; Bani Ham Mazyud and Khanazirah. The Bani Shatair splits into the Khanabil; Kumazarah; Mahabib and Maqadilah. At the turn of the 20th century, they numbered approximately 21,500 people. The tribe incorporated Iranian elements, particularly the Kumazarah subsection who spoke Kumzari, a Southwestern Iranian language. Historically difficult to govern due to their mountainous terrain, the Shihuh maintained fierce independence and were frequently in conflict with the Al Qasimi rulers of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, generally preferring Muscat's suzerainty. They played pivotal roles in regional politics, including the 1921-1922 battle for Rams and conflicts in Dibba and Kalba. The tribe practiced seasonal migration between mountain agriculture in winter and coastal work during date and pearl seasons. In 1970-71, following the British-backed Omani coup, British and Trucial Oman Scout forces invaded Musandam, ending local tribal rule and establishing permanent Omani military control.

name: Shuraifat
legitimacyNotes: Claims descent from Sharif lineage, traditional leadership in tribal confederation structures
description: The Shuraifat are a tribal group historically present in the Arabian Peninsula with documented alliances to the Al Bu Falah confederation. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are known for their pastoral lifestyle. The tribe has maintained its identity through centuries of regional political changes and continues to exist as an active tribal entity in the modern Gulf context.

relations.context: Allied during seasonal migrations and nomadic movements

history:
The Shuraifat are a tribal group within the Al-Muntafiq confederation, specifically part of the Al Ajwad division. The Al-Muntafiq was a large Arab tribal confederation predominantly settled in southern Iraq's provinces and northern Kuwait, with most tribes tracing their genealogy to the Banu 'Uqayl of the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation of Najd. The confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and held control over the region of Basrah under Ottoman suzerainty. In 1521, they successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif (eastern Saudi Arabia) on the Ottomans' behalf, before being expelled by Banu Khalid.

During the Ottoman era, from the late eighteenth century onwards, most Al-Muntafiq tribes, including the Shuraifat, converted from Sunni to Shia Islam and transitioned from nomadic to sedentary life, taking up agriculture in southern and western Iraq. The Shuraifat participated in the seasonal nomadic migrations with the Albu Salih clans during the early-to-mid 20th century. These migrations involved moving south into the northeastern parts of Arabia during autumn, traveling through the Wadi Batin valley into Kuwait and reaching the Arabian Gulf coast for trade with local villages and towns, before returning north in spring to their tribal territories for summer.

By the 1940s-1950s, a majority of the Shuraifat nomads had left the nomadic lifestyle and settled in countries, cities, and towns that were either part of, or close to the indigenous lands of the Muntafiq confederation. The confederation as a whole is not homogeneous in terms of sect/religion, with centuries of intermarriage and intermingling creating a mix of Sunni and Shia tribes. Many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belong to the Muntafiq tribal confederation, including members of the Shuraifat tribe.

The city of Nasiriya in southern Iraq was named after one of the Al-Muntafiq sheikhs, and the surrounding province was known as 'Al-Muntafiq Province' until 1976. The Al-Muntafiq confederation was divided into three major divisions: Bani Saeed, Bani Malik, and Ajwad, with the Shuraifat being listed among the Al Ajwad division tribes alongside Al Usoom, Al Shuwailat, Al Humaid, Al Rufai, Al Bdour, Al Juwarin, and Al Ghezzi.

name: Shwaihiyin
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal structure and lineage recognition
description: The Shwaihiyin are a smaller tribal group found in the Arabian Peninsula. Limited historical documentation exists regarding their specific origins and historical role in Gulf society. They maintain traditional tribal structures and are recognized within the broader Arabian tribal system. Their current status and activities require further research for comprehensive documentation.
history: The Shwaihiyin are a subsection of the larger Bani Kaab tribe, documented as one of the Arab tribes with presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. According to early 20th century records, the Shwaihiyin were one of the two remaining nomadic sections of the Bani Kaab, alongside the Drisah, while other tribal sections including the Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Yidwah and Zahairat had settled by the turn of the 20th century. The tribe's traditional territories were associated with areas around the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions east of Buraimi, including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta, and Wadi Qor. The Shwaihiyin became prominently involved in Gulf politics during the mid-19th century when a blood feud erupted between them and the Huwalah tribe in Sharjah in 1855. At this time, they were described as recent immigrants to Sharjah, numbering approximately 500 fighting men. Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi relocated the Shwaihiyin from Sharjah to Hamriyah to resolve the conflict, but this move triggered the first rebellion by Hamriyah against Al Qasimi rule. The resulting siege of Hamriyah in May 1855 saw fierce fighting, with the Shwaihiyin and local defenders holding out against a combined force from Sharjah and Ajman. British mediation eventually resolved the conflict, resulting in the Shwaihiyin being removed from Hamriyah. Like other sections of the Bani Kaab, the Shwaihiyin were involved in the complex tribal politics of southeastern Arabia. As part of the broader Bani Kaab confederation, they participated in the shifting alliances that characterized the region, including the 1844 alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan against Wahhabi forces, and later alignment with Saudi Arabia in the mid-20th century. During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab, including the Shwaihiyin, supported the Saudi incursion and were involved in fighting against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies.

name: Sinjarah
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Shammar confederation with traditional pastoral and territorial rights
ancestorStory: Descended from Sinjar, a prominent figure within the Shammar tribal lineage. The name is associated with the historic Sinjar region and their ancestral connections to Shammar.
description: The Sinjarah are a tribal branch of the larger Shammar confederation, one of the most prominent tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula. They traditionally practiced nomadic pastoralism across the northern Arabian Peninsula and have historical presence in areas that extend into modern Gulf states. As part of the Shammar, they share the confederation's Adnani lineage and have maintained their tribal identity while adapting to modern state structures. They remain active within the broader Shammar tribal framework in Saudi Arabia and neighboring regions.

relations.context: Both are branches of the greater Shammar confederation
Fellow Shammar tribal branches with common ancestry

history:
The Sinjarah are one of the three main branches of the Shammar tribal confederation, alongside Abdah and Al-Aslam, all descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabia in 115 CE under the leadership of Usma bin Luai. The Tayy invaded and conquered the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim, establishing themselves in what became known as Jabal Shammar by the 14th century. The Shammar confederation is of Qahtanite lineage, tracing their descent through the Tayy, contradicting earlier claims of Adnani origin. The Sinjarah branch includes clans such as Al Thabit, Al Ghafilah, Al Zameel (sometimes referred to as Al Souid or Al Faddaghah), and Al Zomail.

According to 1932 population estimates, the Sinjarah comprised approximately 25% of the Badu Shammar population, which totaled 150,000-200,000 people, making them a significant minority within the confederation after Abdah (37%) and Al-Aslam (30%). Like other Shammar branches, the Sinjarah were primarily nomadic camel-herders and horse-breeders in northern Najd, though some became city-dwellers in Ha'il or agriculturists in surrounding oases. These divisions were based on profession and personal interest rather than family or blood-line stratifications within the tribe.

The Sinjarah participated in the broader Shammar confederation's complex political history throughout the 18th and 19th centuries. They were involved in conflicts with the Saudi states, including the battles against the first Saudi state from 1779 onwards when Jabal Shammar fell under Saudi domination. The Sinjarah supported the Al Rashid emirate of Jabal Shammar (1836-1921), with their tribesmen providing military support alongside other Shammar branches for the Al Rashid's campaigns that eventually captured Riyadh in 1895 and expelled the Saudi leaders to Kuwait.

Following the Saudi conquest of Ha'il in 1921 and the defeat of the Al Rashid emirate, the Sinjarah, like other Shammar branches, were incorporated into modern Saudi Arabia. Many Shammar fled to Syria and Iraq following Al Rashid's defeat, while others eventually pledged allegiance to Ibn Saud in Riyadh. Despite modernization and urbanization in the 20th century, the Sinjarah maintain their tribal identity within the larger Shammar framework and continue to participate in cultural festivals and traditional activities that preserve their ancient nomadic heritage.

folkLegends.title: Migration of Tayy under Usma bin Luai
folkLegends.story: Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE. These mountains were renamed to Jabal Tayy (Tayy's Mountain), and then again in the 14th century, after the tribe changed their name, to Jabal Shammar.

folkLegends.title: First Chiefs of Shammar
folkLegends.story: Oral tradition mentions that the first chiefs of the Shammar tribe, Arar and Omair, were of the 'Abda family of Dhaigham, who ruled Shammar from Jabal Shammar.

nameEtymology: In the Namara inscription dating from 328 CE, the name 'Shammar' is believed to refer to a city in Yemen, though it may refer to the city where the Himyarite King Shammar Yahri'sh lived, or one named after him. The area of the two mountains subsequently came to be known as 'Jabal Shammar' (Shammar's Mountain) from the 14th century, the first time that the Shammar as a tribe were noted in literature.

name: Southern Bani Qitab
legitimacyNotes: Southern branch of Bani Qitab with traditional territorial claims in southern regions
ancestorStory: Southern branch of the Bani Qitab lineage that settled in the southern territories of the Arabian Peninsula. They maintained distinct identity while sharing common ancestry with northern Bani Qitab branches.
description: The Southern Bani Qitab represent a tribal branch of the larger Bani Qitab confederation that historically occupied southern territories of the Arabian Peninsula. As a Qahtani lineage, they were part of the ancient South Arabian tribal system that predated Islam. Their traditional economy was based on nomadic pastoralism, moving with their herds across the southern desert regions. The tribe's historical significance lies in their role as part of the broader Bani Qitab tribal network, though their specific influence in GCC politics appears to have diminished over time.

relations.context: Part of broader Qahtani tribal confederation

history: The Southern Bani Qitab represent a branch of the larger Bani Qitab tribal confederation that became geographically and politically distinct from their northern kinsmen in the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe's historical significance emerges clearly from late 19th and early 20th century tribal politics, where they played important roles in regional conflicts and alliances. The Bani Qitab as a whole were a significant Bedouin confederation that maintained both northern and southern branches, with the southern section eventually settling in territories that became incorporated into Oman's administrative structure. During the late 19th century, the broader Bani Qitab confederation was actively involved in the complex web of tribal alliances and conflicts that characterized the region. In 1875, they were attacked by Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi at Dhank, when he sent a mounted force of 200 Manasir and Bani Hajir Bedouin against them. This attack was part of Zayed's broader campaign to increase Abu Dhabi's influence in the Buraimi area, and it led to the Bani Qitab applying to Dubai for help, resulting in a standoff between the opposing forces. The incident demonstrates the strategic importance of the Bani Qitab in the regional balance of power and their ability to secure external support when threatened. The tribal confederation maintained significant military capability and political influence well into the 20th century. During the early 1900s, conflicts arose between the Bani Qitab and the Na'im tribe, which required intervention from the Trucial Sheikhs. In September 1905, the first meeting between the five Trucial Sheikhs took place in Dubai specifically to settle the dispute between these two tribal groups. The Bani Qitab had sought support from Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla, the young ruler of Umm Al Quwain, who was eager to expand his influence among the interior tribes. A second meeting in April 1906 at Khawaneej resulted in a treaty that allocated tribal responsibilities, with Rashid bin Ahmad taking responsibility for the Bani Qitab, Ghafalah, and Bani Ka'ab tribes. The Southern Bani Qitab's eventual integration into Oman's administrative framework marked their formal separation from the broader tribal network that continued to operate across the Trucial States, distinguishing their historical trajectory from their northern relatives who maintained greater political autonomy in the emerging UAE.

name: Subay'
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tracing to the ancient and powerful Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in medieval times
ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period.
description: Subaie' is an Arabian tribe living in the center of southern Najd. The tribe comprises both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections, with the sedentary members having resided in central Arabia for centuries. Families belonging to Subay' can be found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. They were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and have served as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

history: Subay' (Arabic: سبيع) is an Arabian tribe of North Arabian (Adnanite) stock tracing its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who dominated central Arabia in the medieval period. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe comprised both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections. The original grazing lands of the family's bedouins were in the region of Ranyah and Kurmah, on the border between Nejd and 'Asir, before they migrated to roam the areas of central Nejd around Riyadh alongside the closely related Suhool tribe. Some sections moved further north and established the town of Rumah. The sedentary members have resided in central Arabia for centuries, representing a possible legacy of Banu 'Amir's medieval heyday, with families belonging to Subay' found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. Historically, Al-Subaies were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and continued this martial tradition, serving as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. In the Al-Qassim Province's tribal conflicts from the 1600s to 1907, Unaizah was ruled by the Al Sulaim dynasty from the Subay tribe, demonstrating their political influence in the region. Today, the tribe is mostly found in Saudi Arabia, Qatar, United Arab Emirates, and Kuwait, maintaining both their bedouin heritage and urban presence across the Arabian Peninsula.

name: Al Sudairi
legitimacyNotes: Noble lineage within Najdi tribal structure, political prominence through intermarriage with Al Saud
ancestorStory: The Al Sudairi trace their lineage to Sudairi, an ancestor from the Najd region. They became particularly prominent through strategic marriages and alliances with the Al Saud family.
description: Al Sudairi is a prominent Najdi clan historically based in central Saudi Arabia. They are most famous for their strategic intermarriages with the Al Saud ruling family, particularly through Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi, who became the mother of several Saudi kings including Fahd, Sultan, and Salman. The clan has maintained significant political influence in modern Saudi Arabia through these royal connections. They remain an active and influential family within the Kingdom's political and social structure.

relations.context: Multiple strategic marriages including Hassa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi to King Abdulaziz

history:
The Al Sudairi are a prominent Najdi clan that forms part of the noble Dawasir tribe, historically settled in Al Ghat, an oasis town located in central Arabia approximately 250 kilometers northwest of Riyadh. The family's documented genealogy traces through Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Turki bin Suleiman Al Sudairi, known as Ahmed Al Kabeer (The Great), who served as an administrator under Faisal bin Turki, ruler of the Second Saudi State. Ahmed Al Kabeer was assigned to different regions including Al Ahsa (where Sara bint Ahmed was born), Al Ghat, and Buraimi before his death in 1860. His brother Abdullah bin Muhammad also served as governor of Al Ahsa under Faisal bin Turki.

The Al Sudairi's political prominence emerged through strategic matrimonial alliances with the Al Saud family, beginning with Sara bint Ahmed Al Sudairi (c.1860-1910), daughter of Ahmed Al Kabeer. Sara married Abdul Rahman bin Faisal and became the mother of King Abdulaziz, the founder of Saudi Arabia. This established a crucial connection that would define the clan's future influence in the Kingdom. Sara's siblings also held important positions - her brother Muhammad became governor of Al Ahsa, while Turki served as governor of Saudi Oman.

The family's power reached its zenith through Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi (1900-1969), great-granddaughter of Ahmed Al Kabeer, who became one of King Abdulaziz's most important wives. Born in Al Ghat, she was the daughter of Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi, a powerful tribal chief and early supporter of King Abdulaziz who served as governor of Washm, Sudair, Qassim, and Aflaj provinces. Hussa's marriage to King Abdulaziz produced twelve children, including seven sons known as the 'Sudairi Seven' - among them future kings Fahd and Salman, as well as Sultan and Nayef who both served as crown princes. Her influence extended beyond motherhood as she actively participated in political decisions and organized daily dinner gatherings to maintain unity among her sons.

The Al Sudairi family's integration into the royal structure was further strengthened through multiple marriages across generations. Hussa's sisters Muhdi and Sultana married into the Al Saud family - Muhdi married Prince Nasser and became mother to Turki bin Nasser, while Sultana became King Faisal's first wife and mother to Abdullah Al Faisal. Hussa's brothers were appointed as governors across various Saudi provinces: Turki bin Ahmed governed Asir Province, Abdulaziz bin Ahmed governed Quraiyat al Milh and Wadi Sirhan, Khalid bin Ahmed governed Tabuk and later Najran provinces and served as minister of agriculture, Muhammad bin Ahmed governed Northern Province, Abdul Rahman bin Ahmad governed Jauf, and Musa'id bin Ahmed governed Jizan Province. Today, through King Salman's reign and the position of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, the Al Sudairi continue to wield significant political influence in Saudi Arabia.

They gained prominence through involvement in the pearl trade and later transitioned into government service and business. The family has maintained significant influence in UAE politics and commerce, with members serving in various governmental and diplomatic roles. They remain an active and influential family in contemporary UAE society.

history:
The Sudan (Arabic: السودان), singular Al Suwaidi (Arabic: السويدي), is an Arab tribe established across the United Arab Emirates, Qatar and other Gulf states, traditionally serving as close associates of the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. By the turn of the 20th century, the settled Sudan numbered approximately 5,000 people distributed across the coastal areas of the Trucial States, with 405 houses in Abu Dhabi and Batin, 250 houses in Dubai, 300 in Sharjah, and 12 in Ajman. The tribe also maintained significant island settlements, with 20 houses on Abu Musa and 40 families on Sirri Island. The Sudan subsisted primarily on fishing and pearling and notably did not own date gardens in the inland oases, distinguishing them from many other tribal groups.

The tribe played crucial roles in Gulf political affairs throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries. In 1824, a dissident faction of Sudan in Sharjah built a tower in Deira with encouragement from Tahnun bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan, leading to conflict when Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi expelled them. This dispute caused both Abu Dhabi and Sharjah to fail sending boats to the pearling in 1825, creating grave economic shock. However, in 1829, approximately 400 Sudan permanently settled Deira after leaving Sharjah in 1826 following disputes with Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi. The tribe's political importance was cemented through marriage alliances, as Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan's mother was a Suwaidi, and he married the daughter of Sudan Sheikh Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi.

In the 1890s, the Sudan attempted territorial expansion when they conceived with Zayed the idea of establishing a fort at Al Zorah in Ajman. In 1897, a section of the Sudan under Sultan bin Nasser requested permission to settle Al Zorah with Zayed's support, which was initially granted by the British Resident. However, the Ruler of Ajman built a defensive fort at one of the waterways, and Sheikh Saqr bin Khalid Al Qasimi appealed to prevent this non-Al Qasimi stronghold. British intervention ultimately blocked the scheme, with Major Percy Cox's visit confirming the decision to abandon the settlement project.

The Sudan also established presence in Qatar, where members from Abu Dhabi settled Bida'a (modern Doha) on Qatar's eastern coast in 1766. However, their Qatar presence faced challenges when British forces suspected the tribe of harboring pirates. In 1801, British representative David Seton attempted to bombard Bida'a but was prevented by shallow waters. More seriously, in February 1841, a British squadron arrived to punish headman Salimayn bin Nasir al-Suwaidi for allegedly harboring the pirate Jasim bin Jabir (Raqraqi), resulting in fines and the destruction of the pirate's ship. The Sudan's control of Bida'a ended in May 1843 when Isa bin Tarif of the Al Bin Ali tribe forced the near-bankrupt Sudan to leave for Lingah. In the modern era, the Al Suwaidi family transitioned into government service and business, with members serving in various governmental and diplomatic roles while maintaining significant influence in contemporary UAE politics and commerce.

description: The text provided is about Sudan as a modern nation-state in Northeast Africa, not about a tribe called Sudan. Sudan is a country with a population of 51.8 million people as of 2025, occupying 1,886,068 square kilometres. The name Sudan derives from the Arabic bilād as-sūdān (بلاد السودان), meaning 'The Land of the Blacks', referring to the dark skin of the indigenous people. The text covers the country's history from prehistoric times through various kingdoms and colonial periods to modern independence.

history: The Sudan tribe (Arabic: السودان, singular Al Suwaidi) is an Arab tribe primarily settled in the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and other Gulf states, traditionally serving as close associates of the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. At the turn of the 20th century, the tribe numbered approximately 5,000 people settled across the coastal areas of the Trucial States, with 405 houses in Abu Dhabi and Batin, 250 houses in Dubai, 300 in Sharjah, and 12 in Ajman. They also established settlements on Persian Gulf islands, including 20 houses on Abu Musa and 40 families on Sirri Island. The Sudan primarily subsisted on fishing and pearling and notably did not own date gardens in the inland oases, distinguishing them from other tribal groups. Members of the Sudan from Abu Dhabi settled Bida'a (modern-day Doha) on Qatar's eastern coast in 1766, establishing one of the earliest Arab settlements in the area. The tribe played significant roles in regional politics, particularly through their alliance with the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi. Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan's mother was a Suwaidi, and he married the daughter of Sultan bin Nasir Al Suwaidi, the Sheikh of the Sudan. In 1897, a section of the Sudan under Sultan bin Nasser requested permission to settle Al Zorah in Ajman with Zayed's support. The tribe faced various conflicts and displacements throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries, including being suspected of piracy by British authorities in 1801 and 1841. In Qatar, British forces bombarded their settlement at Bida'a in 1841, leading to fines and eventual displacement when Isa bin Tarif of the Al Bin Ali tribe forced the near-bankrupt Sudan to leave Bida'a and settle in Lingah in 1843.

They appear to maintain traditional tribal structures and are known to have participated in the complex network of tribal alliances that characterize Arabian Peninsula social organization. Their current status and specific territorial base within the GCC region requires further documentation.

relations.context: Closely related family that roamed central Nejd together with Subay'

history:
The Suhool (Arabic: السهول, singular Sahli) are an Arab tribe originating from the Najd region of central Saudi Arabia, descended from the ancient Banu 'Amir confederation, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. They are considered cousins of the Subay' tribe, sharing common Amirid lineage through this ancient tribal confederation that dominated central Arabia for centuries after the rise of Islam. The Suhool maintained the ancestral territories of their Banu 'Amir forebears, controlling lands that included Al-Ard, Al-Arid, Al-Uwairid, and Rwaygheb in the Riyadh region, with their presence extending beyond Najd to Kuwait, Iraq, Qatar, and Bahrain.

Historically, the Suhool exercised significant territorial control in central Arabia. Arab geographers and tribal historians documented that the Suhool once penetrated deep into Najd, settling in Sawad Bahla (known as 'the width') and controlling the country of Ardh Shamam. However, they later withdrew from these extensive territories, retaining only Al-Rawdah, known as Rawdah Al-Ard, as noted by Sheikh Muhammad bin Abdullah bin Blaihed and Sheikh Saad bin Junaidel.

The tribe is organized into ten major clans, each with numerous sub-branches: Al-Brazat (including Al Rashid), Al Muhaimid (Al-Maratin, Al-Sha'f), Dhahran (Al-Suwaidan, Al-Hadha, Al-Zayed, Al-Munikher, Al-Fatamin, Al-Abboud), Al-Zaqa'in (Al-Ruwaidan, Al-Thunayan, Al-Khudair, Al-Shalhoub, Al-Damkh, Al-Hamadin, Al-Saif, Al-Tarjam, Al-Qanaat), the Sa'ub family (Al Mudheesh, Al Ali, Al Mufreh, Al Muqass), Al Obaid (Al Qatian, Al Jarboa), Al-Qabbaneh (Al-Shakhatla, Al-Muharka, Al-Drivers, Al-Ghaliz, Al-Hawazma, Al-Jalal, Al-Jariba, Al-Zaid, Al-Anaqid, Al-Theeb), Al-Mahaniyah (Al-Fadl, Al-Faleh, Al-Hamoud, Al-Flayh, Al-Arefaj), the Juror clan (Al Hoymel, Al Razaan, Al Ateeq, Abu Jada), and Al Manjal (Al Mufarrej, Al Alyan, Al Al-Walah).

In 1873, the Suhool demonstrated their continued political significance by participating alongside the Ajman, Mutair, Subay', and Dawasir tribes in supporting Imam Saud bin Faisal Al Saud during the Second Battle of Talal against Muslit bin Rubayan. Despite fielding 3,000 fighters against Muslit's 800 men, this campaign ended in defeat for the Imam's forces, marking a decisive moment in the political history of central Arabia and illustrating the complex tribal alliances that characterized 19th-century Arabian politics.

history: Al-Suleimaniyah is mentioned in historical records as one of the Riverain Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) of Northern Sudan, distinct from their Arabian Peninsula namesake. According to Wikipedia sources on the Manasir people, al-Suleimaniyah constituted one of the settled riverine clans inhabiting the Fourth Cataract region of the Nile in Dar al-Manasir, alongside other clans such as al-Wahabab, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, and al-Hamsab. The Manasir trace their origins to multiple possible ancestors including Mansur bin Qahtan of the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), or alternatively to descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad. Critical research suggests the Manasir community represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities. The riverine Manasir, including al-Suleimaniyah, pursued small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils near the Nile and were renowned for date cultivation and mud house construction. In Ottoman Iraq records, Suleimaniyah appears as an administrative center where military regiments were stationed, with İsmail Pasha serving as kaymakam under the Sixth Army command headquartered in Baghdad during the 1850s-1860s consolidation period.

Seasonal migrations extending into Mesopotamian borderlands for grazing
