name: Taghlib
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal legitimacy through Adnanite lineage and historical dominance in northern Arabia
ancestorStory: Descended from Taghlib ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit, who was known for his nobility and strength. The tribe's name means 'to overcome' or 'to prevail', reflecting their martial reputation.
description: Taghlib was one of the most powerful and prestigious Arab tribes of pre-Islamic Arabia, primarily based in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia. They were known for their fierce independence, martial prowess, and their famous war with their cousins Banu Bakr known as the War of Basus. The tribe played a significant role in pre-Islamic poetry and warfare, with many members remaining Christian after the rise of Islam. While historically important to Arabian tribal heritage, they had limited direct presence in the modern Gulf states, with their influence primarily in Iraq and northern Arabian regions.

relations.context: Descended from Adnanite lineage through Wa'il ibn Qasit

history:
The Banu Taghlib were a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribe descended from Taghlib ibn Wa'il ibn Qasit, tracing their lineage through the Rabi'a confederation to the Adnanites. Originally inhabiting Najd, the tribe belonged to the larger Rabi'a branch alongside the tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Anizah. Their full genealogy follows: Taghlib ibn Wā'il ibn Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afṣā ibn Duʿmī ibn Jadīla ibn Asad ibn Rabīʿa ibn Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. The tribe was organized around the al-Araqim, descendants of six sons of Bakr ibn Hubayb ibn Amr ibn Ghanm, with the two largest divisions being Jusham and Malik, collectively called al-Rawkān ('the two horns'). The Hamdanid dynasty would later trace its descent to the Malik division via their ancestor Adi ibn Usama ibn Malik.

In the late 5th century, the Taghlibi chieftain Kulaib ibn Rabiah was murdered by his brother-in-law Jassas ibn Murra al-Shaybani of the Banu Bakr, precipitating the famous Basus War between the two cousin tribes. After their decisive defeat at the battle of Yawm al-Tahaluq, the bulk of Taghlib fled Najd for the Lower Euphrates region, later migrating further north to Upper Mesopotamia (Jazira) after their chieftain Amr ibn Kulthum assassinated the Lakhmid king Amr ibn al-Hind in 568 CE. The region of Diyar Bakr in southern Turkey takes its name from their presence alongside their cousins Banu Bakr.

The Taghlib embraced Miaphysite Christianity and remained largely Christian long after the advent of Islam. During the early Muslim conquests, sections initially fought against Muslim armies but eventually secured special treatment, negotiating to pay zakat rather than jizya while retaining their Christian faith. Under the Umayyads, they allied with the dynasty and achieved a decisive victory over rebellious Qaysi tribes at Yawm al-Hashshak. Their champion during this period was the court poet al-Akhtal, who engaged in 'verbal warfare' with the Qaysi poet Jarir.

During Abbasid rule, significant numbers converted to Islam in the 9th century, particularly through the persuasion of Malik ibn Tawk, the Taghlibi governor who founded the fortress town of al-Rahba. The tribe's political pinnacle came with the Hamdanid dynasty (880s-1002 CE), descendants of the Adi ibn Usama line, who established autonomous emirates in Mosul (under Nasir al-Dawla from 935) and Aleppo (under Sayf al-Dawla from 945). However, the Hamdanids relied on non-Arab ghilman and bureaucrats rather than their Taghlibi tribesmen for governance. After the early Hamdanid period, the Taghlib disappeared from historical records as a distinct tribal entity, having 'disappeared into obscurity' like many Arab tribes of the region.

folkLegends.title: The Araqim Snake Eyes
folkLegends.story: The al-Araqim division of Taghlib got their name because the descendants of six sons of Bakr ibn Hubayb ibn Amr ibn Ghanm all had eyes that resembled those of arāqim (speckled snakes)

folkLegends.title: Descendants in Anizah
folkLegends.story: According to the medieval geographer Yaqut al-Hamawi, the modern descendants of Taghlib belong to the Anizah tribe, specifically to the Banu Bishr branch, residing in the Najd region

nameEtymology: The tribe's name means 'to overcome' or 'to prevail', reflecting their martial reputation, though the research also notes their progenitor was called Taghlib ibn Wa'il, also known as Dithar ibn Wa'il

name: Tamīm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Adnani lineage with documented pre-Islamic origins and extensive genealogical records
ancestorStory: Tamīm ibn Murr was a prominent tribal ancestor from the Adnani branch of Arabs. The tribe traces its lineage through Mudar to Adnan, establishing them as northern Arabs with ancient Arabian Peninsula roots.
description: Banū Tamīm is one of the largest and most prominent Adnani tribes of ancient Arabia, historically based in Najd and eastern Arabian Peninsula. They were renowned for their poetry, tribal pride, and military prowess during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. The tribe played significant roles in early Islamic conquests and produced notable poets like al-Farazdaq. Today, Tamīm descendants are found throughout the Gulf states, particularly in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Qatar, and UAE, where they maintain their tribal identity and social prominence.

relations.context: Historical alliances in Najd region
Both tribes migrated from Najd to Gulf coast

history: The Banū Tamīm (Arabic: بَنُو تَمِيم) are one of the largest and most prominent Adnanite Arab tribes, tracing their lineage through Tamīm ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'add ibn Adnan to the biblical Ishmael. The tribe originated in Najd and the Hejaz regions of the Arabian Peninsula, with the word Tamim meaning 'strong and solid' in Arabic. During the pre-Islamic period, they occupied numerous wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century. The Banu Tamim are traditionally divided into four main branches: Amr, Banu Hanzala, Banu Sa'd, and Al-Ribbab (Al-Rabbab). The tribe came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. A hadith records Muhammad's praise: 'They will be the sternest of my Ummah against the Dajjal.' After the Islamic conquests, they spread extensively across the caliphates, establishing significant power in various regions. They founded the Aghlabid dynasty which ruled Ifriqiya (Tunisia, Algeria, and Libya) from 800-909 CE. The Al Thani ruling family of Qatar, from the Banu Tamim, moved from the Najdi town of Ushaiger to the Gebrin oasis in southern Najd before settling in Qatar around the 1720s, first in Sikak, then Zubarah, and finally Doha in the 19th century under Mohammed bin Thani. The tribe produced notable poets like al-Farazdaq and Jarir, military commanders like al-Qa'qa'a ibn Amr during the Rashidun conquests, and religious scholars including Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founder of the Nejdi Salafi movement. Today, Tamim descendants are found throughout Saudi Arabia, Qatar, UAE, Kuwait, Iraq, Oman, Jordan, Lebanon, and across North Africa including Algeria, Morocco, Tunisia, and Libya, as well as in Iran's Khuzestan province.

folkLegends.title: Meeting with Jesus
folkLegends.story: According to the Zaydi Shi'a scholar Al-Mansur Abdullah, Tamim ibn Murr met with Jesus and became an adherent to Christianity. Other narrations say that Tamim was one of the Disciples of Jesus.

folkLegends.title: Descent from Abraham
folkLegends.story: In the genealogical tradition of the tribe, there is a direct line drawn from Abraham through Ishmael, Adnan, Ma'add, Nizar, Mudar, Ilyas, Amr (Tabikhah), 'Ud, Murr, to Tamim.

nameEtymology: The word Tamim in Arabic means 'strong and solid.' It can also mean those who strive for perfection. The name is derived from the Arabic root meaning firmness and strength.

name: Tanukh
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal confederation with historical leadership over multiple tribes in Mesopotamia and Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from Tanukh ibn Malik ibn Fahm from the Azd tribal group. The confederation was formed when various tribes gathered and confederated under the Tanukh name in ancient times.
description: Tanukh was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that controlled large territories between Mesopotamia and the Arabian Peninsula from the 3rd-6th centuries CE. They established kingdoms in Al-Hirah and other areas, serving as foederati to both Byzantine and Sassanid empires. The confederation included various Qahtani tribes and played a crucial role in early Arab political organization. While historically significant, the confederation dissolved after the Islamic conquests, with constituent tribes dispersing throughout the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Some Al Qasimi genealogies trace partial descent through ancient Tanukh lines

history:
The Tanukh was a major Arab tribal confederation whose documented history spans from the 2nd century CE to the 17th century, with origins tracing to eastern Arabia (Bahrayn). The confederation was formed in the 2nd century CE as confirmed by Ptolemy's Geography (c. 150 CE), representing an alliance of various Arab tribes, primarily from Qahtani origins, including groups that had migrated from southern Arabia. Under King Jadhima al-Abrash in the 3rd century, whose existence is attested by Greek-Nabatean inscriptions found at Umm al-Jimal, the Tanukh migrated to the middle Euphrates valley in central Iraq and established themselves as a significant political force, founding kingdoms in Al-Hirah and serving as clients to both Byzantine and Sassanid empires.

By the 4th century, segments of Tanukh had moved to Byzantine Syria, becoming the first Arab foederati (tribal confederates) of the empire, maintaining Orthodox Christian faith and serving as frontier guards. Their premier position was lost after a rebellion in the 380s CE during Emperor Theodosius I's reign, suppressed by magister militum Richomer. During the Islamic conquests of the 630s, the Tanukh fought alongside Byzantine forces at battles including Dumat al-Jandal (634) and Homs (637), before submitting to Muslim commander Abu Ubayda ibn al-Jarrah at their settlements near Qinnasrin and Aleppo in 638.

Under Umayyad rule (661-750), the majority remained Christian and became integrated into the Quda'a confederation, fighting for Caliph Marwan I at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684. According to Werner Caskel, it was after this battle that Tanukh was enlisted into the Quda'a confederation as the Kalb sought to buttress their alliance against the Qays tribes. Their fortunes declined under the Abbasid Caliphate when Caliph al-Mahdi forcibly converted them to Islam in 780, executing their Christian chief Layth ibn al-Mahatta when he refused conversion.

Following attacks during the Fourth Muslim Civil War (811-837), Tanukhid settlements shifted from Aleppo to Ma'arrat al-Nu'man and the coastal mountains, giving the region the medieval name 'Jabal Tanukh.' The final chapter occurred in Mount Lebanon's Gharb district, where the Buhturid family of Tanukh became leading Druze converts in the 11th century, establishing a hereditary emirate that lasted through Crusader, Ayyubid, and Mamluk periods until their elimination by rival Druze families in the 1630s. Notable Tanukhids include the renowned poet-philosopher al-Ma'arri (973-1057), a member of the Banu Sulayman branch of Tanukh from Ma'arrat al-Nu'man.

folkLegends.title: Jadhima al-Abrash Folk Hero Legend
folkLegends.story: Jadhima plays an epic role as the folk hero-king of the Tanukh in traditional narrative, with numerous traditions around him and his companions becoming the subject of poetry and proverbial wisdom. Episodes include his boon companions, the marriage of his sister Riqash to Adi, and his marriage to and death by al-Zabba (Zenobia). Some fragments of poems are attributed to him, and he is listed among the pre-Islamic poets by later anthologists.

name: Tasm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient pre-Islamic Arabian tribe with legendary status
ancestorStory: According to Arab genealogical tradition, Tasm descended from Tasm ibn Laud ibn Sam ibn Nuh. They were among the ancient Arab tribes that inhabited the Arabian Peninsula in pre-Islamic times.
description: Tasm was an ancient pre-Islamic Arabian tribe mentioned in classical Arab genealogical literature and historical accounts. They are traditionally placed among the early Qahtani tribes that inhabited various parts of the Arabian Peninsula before the rise of Islam. According to historical sources, they were eventually absorbed or disappeared through tribal conflicts and migrations that characterized pre-Islamic Arabia. Their historical significance lies primarily in Arab tribal genealogical traditions rather than direct influence on modern Gulf states.

relations.context: Both traced to Qahtani genealogical tradition

history:
Tasm was an ancient pre-Islamic Arabian tribe that dominated the region of Al-Yamama in the Arabian Peninsula alongside the Jadis tribe from approximately 110 BCE until their complete extinction in the 6th century CE. According to Arab genealogical tradition, Tasm descended from Tasm ibn Laud ibn Sam ibn Nuh, placing them among the earliest Qahtani tribes and classifying them as al-Arab al-Ba'ida (extinct Arabian tribes). Both Tasm and Jadis were contemporary to other ancient Arabian tribes including the Amalek, 'Ad, and Thamud, and established a significant civilization in Al-Yamama, constructing numerous palaces and fortresses.

By the 6th century CE, Tasm had gained dominance over their tribal neighbors, the Jadis, with both tribes ruled by a king named Imliq. This king implemented oppressive policies including droit du seigneur over Jadis virgins and control over trade routes, which created severe tensions between the two allied tribes. The oppression eventually led to a rebellion in which Jadis warriors assassinated King Imliq during a performance by revealing hidden swords from sand, marking the beginning of the end for both tribal entities.

Following the assassination of their king, both tribes faced retaliation from the Himyarite armies. According to Tabari and Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi, the Jadis tribe failed to prepare for the incoming Himyarite forces despite warnings from a woman of their tribe, resulting in their complete massacre with only children taken as slaves. Tasm also became extinct around the same period in the 6th century CE, though the exact circumstances of their disappearance are unclear in historical sources.

After their extinction, the palaces and fortresses that Tasm and Jadis had constructed in Al-Yamama were taken over by the Banu Hanifa tribe. The legacy of Tasm and Jadis became part of the broader narrative of the al-Arab al-Ba'ida, representing the ancient pre-Islamic Arab peoples who disappeared before the rise of Islam through warfare, natural disasters, and political upheavals that characterized early Arabian Peninsula history. Their historical significance lies primarily in Arab tribal genealogical traditions and as examples of the tribal conflicts that shaped pre-Islamic Arabia.

folkLegends.title: The Assassination of King Imliq
folkLegends.story: King Imliq ruled both Tasm and Jadis tribes but implemented oppressive policies including droit du seigneur over Jadis virgins. This led to a rebellion in which Jadis warriors killed Imliq during a performance by revealing hidden swords they had concealed in sand.

name: Taulantii
history:
The Taulantii were an ancient Illyrian people who inhabited the southeastern Adriatic coast of what is now Albania, representing one of the oldest and most significant tribal groups in classical antiquity. Their name, meaning 'swallow-men' (connected to Albanian 'dallëndyshe'), reflects totemic practices common among Illyrian peoples. First recorded by Hecataeus of Miletus in the 6th century BC, the Taulantii dominated the coastal plains between the rivers Drin and Vjosa, with their territory centered around present-day Tirana and extending between the Mat and Shkumbin valleys. Their strategic location made them crucial players in Mediterranean politics, particularly around the Greek colony of Epidamnos-Dyrrhachion (modern Durrës). The tribe included several sub-groups, notably the Chelidonioi (another name for the Taulantii themselves, translated from Illyrian by Greek colonists) and the Abroi, a northern subgroup known for their technique of preparing mead from honey.

The Taulantii reached their political and military apex under King Glaukias (ruled c. 335-295 BC), who defied Alexander the Great during the siege of Pelium in 335 BC and maintained independence when other Illyrian tribes fell under Macedonian control. Glaukias offered asylum to the infant Pyrrhus of Epirus after his father Aeacides was expelled, raising him for ten years as an adoptive son alongside his wife Beroea, who was a member of the Aeacid dynasty. In 306 BC, Glaukias established Pyrrhus on the Epirote throne with Illyrian military support, demonstrating the tribe's significant political influence beyond their borders. This relationship strengthened through marriage alliances, as Pyrrhus later married the Illyrian princess Bircenna, daughter of Bardylis II.

After Glaukias, the Taulantian territory was ruled by successive kings who continued to exercise authority over the crucial port city of Dyrrhachion. Monunius (from about 280 BC) and his successor Mytilos minted silver and bronze coins respectively in Dyrrhachion, bearing their names and the city's symbol, indicating continued control over this vital commercial center. The tribe played a significant role in the civil war at Epidamnos in 435 BC, when exiled oligarchs allied with the Taulantii to besiege the city, ultimately contributing to the broader conflict that became a pretext for the Peloponnesian War as recorded by Thucydides.

The Taulantii's power gradually declined following Roman expansion into the region. After the Roman victory in the Third Illyrian War in 167 BC, they were among the tribes rewarded with freedom by the Roman Senate for abandoning King Gentius before his defeat. However, their independence was finally ended during Octavian's Illyrian campaign of 35-33 BC, when they were defeated alongside other Illyrian peoples including the Oxyaei, Pertheenatae, Bathiatae, and others. This marked the conclusion of nearly six centuries of Taulantian prominence in Balkan politics and their integration into the Roman provincial system.

Welcomed Corinthian and Corcyrean colonists back to Epidamnos after defeating Liburni, establishing mixed Greek-Illyrian population

folkLegends.title: Sons of Illyrius
folkLegends.story: According to a mythological tradition reported by Appian (2nd century AD), the Taulantii were among the South-Illyrian tribes that took their names from the first generation of descendants of Illyrius, the eponymous ancestor of all Illyrian peoples. Taulas, one of the six sons of Illyrius, was considered the eponymous ancestor of the Taulantii.

nameEtymology: The term 'Taulantii' is connected with the Albanian word 'dallëndyshe', meaning 'swallow'. The ethnonym Chelidonioi, also reported by Hecataeus as neighboring the Taulantii, is the Greek translation of Taulantii, as 'khelīdṓn' means 'swallow' in Ancient Greek. The name suggests the practice of animal totemism, which was widespread among Illyrian peoples.

name: Taym Allah
legitimacyNotes: Tribal legitimacy based on Quraishi lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
ancestorStory: Descended from Taym Allah ibn Tha'laba of the Quraysh tribe. The tribe traces its lineage to the noble Quraishi heritage of pre-Islamic Arabia.
description: Taym Allah is a historical Arabian tribe with Quraishi origins, tracing their lineage to Taym Allah ibn Tha'laba. They were part of the broader Quraysh tribal confederation in pre-Islamic Arabia and had connections to the Hijaz region. The tribe played a minor role in early Islamic history but gradually dispersed and merged with other tribal groups. Their descendants are believed to have settled in various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, though the tribe no longer exists as a distinct political entity in the modern Gulf states.
history: The Taym Allah tribe represents a complex case of multiple distinct Arabian tribal groups sharing similar names but different origins and histories. The research reveals at least two separate tribes: Banu Taym of the Quraysh in Mecca, and Taym Allah of the Banu Bakr confederation in eastern Arabia. The Quraishi Banu Taym descended from Taym ibn Murrah ibn Ka'b ibn Lu'ay ibn Ghalib ibn Fihr, residing near the Kaaba in Mecca as part of Quraysh al-Bitah. This clan produced prominent Islamic figures including Caliph Abu Bakr, Talha ibn Ubaydallah, and Aisha bint Abi Bakr. The eastern Arabian Taym Allah, originally called Taym Allat, descended from Tha'laba ibn Ukaba ibn Sa'b ibn Ali ibn Bakr ibn Wa'il, belonging to the Rabi'a tribal confederation. They embraced Monophysite Christianity before Islam and formed part of the Lahazim alliance with other Bakrite tribes. During the pre-Islamic period, they fought alongside their allies against the Tamim tribe and participated in the famous Battle of Dhi Qar against the Lakhmids in 611 CE. Initially opposing Muslim expansion during the Ridda wars and Iraqi conquests, they later converted to Islam and produced notable military commanders in the eastern provinces, particularly in Khurasan and Sijistan. Members like Aws ibn Tha'laba defended Herat against Zubayrid forces, while others served in Umayyad campaigns in Transoxiana. A small section eventually settled in Wadi al-Taym in Lebanon, where the heterodox Druze faith took root in the 11th century.

migrationPath.description: Taym Allah tribesmen migrated to eastern provinces following Islamic conquests
Small section of tribe settled in Lebanese valley, later becoming center of Druze faith

timelineEvents.description: Taym Allah fought alongside Bakrite tribesmen against Lakhmid Arab vassals of Sasanian Empire

timelineEvents.title: Ridda Wars Participation
timelineEvents.description: Taym Allah fought against Muslim expansion under Abjar ibn Bujayr during rebellions in eastern Arabia

name: Thamalah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal confederation with customary leadership through recognized sheikhs
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Thamalah ibn Auf, who was known for his leadership and tribal unity. The tribe traces its lineage through the ancient Arab genealogical traditions.
description: Thamalah is a Qahtani tribal confederation historically based in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Asir region. They have been known for their pastoral traditions and participation in regional trade networks. The tribe has maintained traditional alliances with neighboring groups like Ghamid and has played a role in the tribal politics of the region. Today they remain active as part of the broader tribal structure in Saudi Arabia.

relations.context: Closest tribes in lineage, part of Azd Shanu'ah

history: Thamalah is a Qahtani tribal confederation that traces its lineage through the Azd Shanu'ah, sharing close genealogical ties with the Ghamid, Zahran, and Banu Lahb tribes. According to the research, Thamalah is among the tribes descended from Malik ibn Nasr ibn Azd ibn Al-Ghawth ibn Nabt ibn Malik ibn Zayd ibn Kahlan ibn Saba' ibn Yashjub ibn Ya'rub ibn Qahtan. The Azd Shanu'ah tribes, including Thamalah, were historically located in the southwestern Arabian Peninsula, particularly in what is now the Al-Bahah and Asir regions of Saudi Arabia. The research indicates that Thamalah maintained close relationships with neighboring Azd tribes like Ghamid, who played significant roles in the military campaigns of the Sharifs of Mecca and in resistance against Ottoman forces throughout the 17th-19th centuries. As part of the broader Azd confederation, Thamalah would have been involved in the complex tribal politics of the Hejaz region, though specific details about their individual military or political activities are not provided in the available sources. The tribe's connection to the Azd Shanu'ah lineage places them among the ancient Arabian tribes that maintained their territorial presence in southwestern Arabia from pre-Islamic times through the modern Saudi state.

name: Thyni
description: Thyni appears to reference an ancient Thracian tribe from the Bithynia region of modern-day Turkey, not a recognized Arabian Peninsula or Gulf tribal entity. This name does not correspond to any documented tribal formation in GCC genealogical records or Arabian tribal histories. If this refers to a specific Arabian tribal group, additional contextual information would be needed for accurate identification.
history: The Thyni were an ancient Thracian tribe that inhabited southeastern Thrace in the Balkans, closely related to the Bithynians. According to historical sources, the Thyni and Bithyni migrated together from Thrace to Asia Minor during the early Iron Age, following the Bronze Age collapse. Xenophon praised the Thyni as formidable warriors, noting that 'Teres, with a large army, was said to have had his baggage train taken from him by the natives, who are called Thyni and are supposed to be the most dangerous of all the tribes, especially at night fighting.' The Thyni included clubs among their weapons and often served as mercenaries in organized armies. When they settled in Asia Minor alongside the Bithynians, they expelled or subdued the Mysians, Caucones and other minor tribes. The region they settled became known as Thynia, named after their prominence there, while the adjacent area became Bithynia after their Bithynian allies. Both tribes were later incorporated into the Lydian monarchy under King Croesus around 550 BCE, and subsequently became part of the Persian satrapy of Phrygia when Lydia fell to Cyrus the Great in 546 BCE. This represents an ancient Thracian tribal entity with no connection to Arabian Peninsula genealogy or Gulf tribal structures.

migrationPath.description: Migration from Thracian homeland to Asia Minor alongside Bithynians during early Iron Age

timelineEvents.title: Thraco-Phrygian Migration

name: Tunaij
description: The Tunaij is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates that primarily settled in the inland oasis town of Dhaid in Sharjah, the Ras Al Khaimah town of Rams, and small numbers at Hamriyah. At the turn of the 19th century, they numbered around 4,000 people in the Northern Emirates, with 1,500 being Bedouin. They were influential in tribal politics due to their ability to raise a force of 500 fighting men. The Bedouin Tunaij used Dhaid as their center with a fortified tower protecting their settlement. The Tunaij of Rams were primarily involved in pearl fishing, and during pearling season both Bedouin Tunaij and Shihuh would work as seasonal laborers on the coast.

relations.context: Traditionally associated together with the settlement of Rams, north of Ras Al Khaimah
The Tunaij have been linked to the Bani Qitab
Both tribes maintained similar arrangements at Dhaid with fortified settlements
During pearling season, both Bedouin Tunaij and Shihuh would work as seasonal labor on the coast

history:
The Tunaij (Arabic: الطنيج, singular Al Tunaiji الطنيجي) is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates that has been linked to the Bani Qitab. By the turn of the 19th century, approximately 4,000 Tunaij inhabited the Northern Emirates, with 1,500 being Bedouin, making them influential in tribal politics due to their ability to raise a force of 500 fighting men. The tribe primarily settled in three locations: the inland oasis town of Dhaid in Sharjah, where the Bedouin Tunaij established their center with around 70 houses protected by a fortified tower; the coastal town of Rams in Ras Al Khaimah, where all 400 houses were settled by the Tunaij by the early 19th century; and small numbers at Hamriyah.

The Tunaij of Rams were primarily involved in pearl fishing, operating a fleet of 3 pearl boats and 10 fishing boats. During the pearling season, both Bedouin Tunaij from the interior and members of the mountain Shihuh tribe would come to the coast to work as seasonal laborers on Tunaij pearling expeditions. The tribe maintained dual economic strategies - the Bedouin section based at Dhaid focused on pastoralism and date cultivation, while the coastal Tunaij at Rams specialized in maritime activities.

The Tunaij became significantly involved in the 1819 British punitive expedition against the Qawasim. When British forces landed at Rams on December 18, 1819, and fought their way inland through date plantations to Dhayah Fort, many Tunaij herders and farmers from the date groves fled to the hilltop fortification for protection. The Tunaij formed the majority of the 400 people who held out in Dhayah Fort for three days under heavy bombardment from British mortars and cannons, enduring conditions without proper sanitation, water, or effective cover from the sun.

After the British brought up two 24-pound cannons from HMS Liverpool and subjected the fort to sustained bombardment that breached its walls, the defenders finally surrendered at 10:30 AM on December 22, 1819. Of the 400 people who surrendered, only 177 were identified as men of fighting age, indicating that the majority were civilian refugees including women, children, and elderly. This resistance was part of the broader conflict that led to the General Maritime Treaty of 1820, with Hassan bin Ali, identified as the 'Sheikh of Zyah' (actually Sheikh of Rams and Dhayah), becoming a co-signatory of the treaty that established the foundations of what would become the United Arab Emirates.

timelineEvents.title: British Landing at Rams
timelineEvents.description: British expeditionary force landed at Rams on December 18, 1819, as part of punitive expedition against the Qawasim

timelineEvents.title: Siege of Dhayah Fort
timelineEvents.description: Tunaij herders and farmers fled to Dhayah Fort where 400 people, mostly Tunaij tribal members, held out for three days under heavy British bombardment before surrendering on December 22

timelineEvents.title: General Maritime Treaty
timelineEvents.description: Hassan bin Ali, Sheikh of Rams and Dhayah, signed the General Maritime Treaty of 1820 following the Tunaij resistance at Dhayah Fort

name: 'Ujman
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on lineage and historical presence in eastern Arabia
ancestorStory: Descended from Ajm ibn Mansur ibn Yam, tracing their lineage to the Qahtanite tribes. The tribe formed as a confederation of related clans in the Najd region.
description: The 'Ujman are a major Bedouin tribal confederation historically based in eastern Saudi Arabia, particularly in the al-Ahsa region. They were one of the most powerful nomadic tribes in the Arabian Peninsula during the 19th and early 20th centuries, known for their fierce independence and frequent conflicts with the Ottoman authorities and later the Saudi state. The tribe played a significant role in the early consolidation of Saudi Arabia under Ibn Saud, initially as allies but later in rebellion during the Ikhwan movement. Today, members of the 'Ujman are settled throughout eastern Saudi Arabia and maintain their tribal identity while being integrated into modern Saudi society.

relations.context: Competition over grazing rights and territorial disputes in eastern Arabia
Initially allied with Saudi expansion but later rebelled during Ikhwan period
Both tribes claim descent from Qahtanite origins in Yemen

history: The 'Ujman (Al-Ajman or al-'Ijman) are a major Qahtanite Arab tribal confederation descended from Banu Yam, which belongs to the larger Banu Hamdan confederation that traces its lineage to the ancient Kahlan group. The tribe originated from southwestern Arabia where their parent tribe Banu Yam was historically based in the region extending from northern Yemen to Najran. The 'Ujman branched off from Banu Yam and migrated eastward to settle primarily in the al-Ahsa region of eastern Saudi Arabia, where they established themselves as one of the most powerful nomadic Bedouin confederations. Their dialect, shared with their cousin tribe Al-Murrah, belongs to the southern Najdi Arabic group and retains distinctive south Arabian features like the '-ish' feminine suffix, reflecting their Yam heritage. The 'Ujman became major players in eastern Arabian politics during the 18th-20th centuries under prominent leaders like Sheikh Rakan bin Hithlain, a noted poet who became the maternal grandfather of Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. In 1861, the 'Ujman were defeated by Faisal bin Turki of the Second Saudi State after challenging his rule in the 1850s, though Faisal later married into the tribe to cement relations. Initially, the 'Ujman opposed Ibn Saud, supporting the rival Saud Al Kabir branch of the Al Saud family. However, around 1900, a section of the tribe led by Dhaydan bin Hithlain joined the Ikhwan movement, providing crucial military support to Ibn Saud's campaigns for territorial consolidation. This alliance eventually soured when the 'Ujman, alongside the Utaybah and Mutayr tribes, rebelled against Ibn Saud's authority, culminating in their decisive defeat at the Battle of Sabilla in 1929, which effectively ended the Ikhwan rebellion. Following this defeat, nearly all 'Ujman members abandoned their traditional nomadic lifestyle and settled throughout the Persian Gulf states, particularly in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, where their main tribal territory is known as Wadi Al-Ajman, spanning from Al Nairyah governorate to the town of Judah. The tribe has maintained its prominence in modern Saudi Arabia, with members like Seeta bint Fahd Al Damir of the 'Ujman becoming the fourth wife of King Khalid bin Abdulaziz and mother to seven of his children.

migrationPath.description: Migration from ancestral Banu Yam territories in southwestern Arabia to eastern Saudi Arabia

nameEtymology: The name Al-Ajman or al-'Ijman (Arabic: العُجمان, singular Ajmi Arabic: العجمي) is derived from the root word meaning 'foreigner' or relating to non-Arab peoples, though the specific reason for this designation is not explained in the available sources.

name: Urayna
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal lineage and historical presence in Arabian Peninsula
description: Urayna is a historical Arabian tribe mentioned in early Islamic sources and classical Arab genealogical texts. The tribe was traditionally associated with the central Arabian Peninsula region. Like many smaller Arabian tribes, their historical presence is documented primarily through genealogical records and early Islamic historical accounts. Their current status and activities in the modern GCC states are not well documented in contemporary sources.
history:
The Urayna was a historical Arabian tribe mentioned in early Islamic sources and classical Arab genealogical texts, traditionally associated with the central Arabian Peninsula region. According to traditional genealogical records, the Urayna was one of the clans within the broader Bajila tribal confederation, alongside other clans including Ahmas, Qasr, and Zayd ibn al-Ghawth. The Bajila confederation, which included Urayna as a subdivision, was identified in Arab genealogical tradition as either Qahtanite (southern Arabian) or Adnanite (northern Arabian) in origin, though the exact lineage remains uncertain among traditional genealogists.

The tribe gained particular historical significance during the early Islamic period through a controversial incident that profoundly influenced Islamic jurisprudence. According to early Islamic sources, members of the Urayna tribe feigned conversion to Islam with the intent to steal Muslims' possessions and killed a young shepherd who had been sent to teach them about the faith. This incident led to the revelation of Quranic verse 5:33, known as the Hirabah verse, which specified punishments for those who 'wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive to spread disorder in the land.' The original meanings of the triliteral root ḥrb, from which hirabah derives, include both despoiling someone's wealth or property and fighting or committing sinful acts.

During the early years of Islam, the Urayna clan became involved in the internecine fighting that led to the splintering and dispersal of the broader Bajila tribal confederation. Due to blood feuds with neighboring tribes and conflicts between various Bajila clans, including Urayna, the confederation became fragmented and many of its constituent clans became attached to larger tribal groups. Some Bajila clans joined the Banu 'Amir between Ta'if and Jabal Tuwayq, while others affiliated with the Jadila of Qays who inhabited the area south of Ta'if.

A notable historical connection to the Urayna occurred during the Second Muslim Civil War period (685-687 CE), when the Persian convert Abu Amra Kaysan became a mawla (client) of the Urayna clan within the Bajila tribe. Kaysan served as a prominent leader of the mawali faction in al-Mukhtar al-Thaqafi's pro-Alid movement, commanding al-Mukhtar's personal guard and overseeing punishments of Arab nobles in Kufa who had participated in events surrounding the killing of al-Husayn in 680 CE. The extinct Shia sect known as the Kaysanites is most likely named after this Abu Amra Kaysan, who had become affiliated with the Urayna clan. Like many smaller Arabian tribes of the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods, Urayna's historical presence is primarily documented through genealogical records and early Islamic historical accounts, with their current status in modern times being poorly documented.

timelineEvents.title: Feigned Conversion Incident
timelineEvents.description: Members of Urayna tribe feigned conversion to Islam, stole Muslim possessions, and killed a shepherd, leading to the revelation of Quran 5:33

timelineEvents.title: Tribal Splintering
timelineEvents.description: Urayna clan involved in internecine fighting within Bajila confederation leading to tribal dispersal

timelineEvents.title: Kaysan Affiliation
timelineEvents.description: Persian convert Abu Amra Kaysan became mawla of Urayna clan during Second Muslim Civil War

name: Uriad
legitimacyNotes: Kurdish tribal groups that migrated to Arabian Peninsula, maintaining distinct identity
description: Kurdish tribal groups or individuals who migrated to the Arabian Peninsula at various historical periods. Some Kurdish families settled in different parts of the Gulf region and were incorporated into local tribal structures. Their presence in the Arabian Peninsula represents broader patterns of migration and settlement, though specific details about the Uriad Kurds are not well-documented in standard Arabian tribal genealogies.
history: The Uriad Kurds represent part of the broader Kurdish diaspora in the Arabian Peninsula, originating from the Iranian Kurdish populations described in historical sources. Kurdish tribes have been documented since the 9th century, when Ibn Khurdubah reported their use of the term 'Zūma' to designate tribes. The research shows that Kurdish tribes, including those in Iran, have complex internal structures divided into sub-tribes, clans, lineages and households, using terms like 'Ahiret, Tira, Hoz, il, Khel, Tayfa or Taifa, Zuma and Rama'. Iranian Kurds, numbering 9-10 million people, are primarily bilingual in Kurdish and Persian and inhabit western Iran's Kurdistan, Kermanshah, West Azerbaijan, Ilam, and Lorestan provinces. The Fars Province research specifically mentions Kurdish tribes including Uriad, Zangana, Chegini, Kordshuli and Kuruni as residing in Fars Province alongside the indigenous Persian population. This indicates that the Uriad Kurds were among the Kurdish groups who migrated from their traditional Iranian Kurdistan homeland to settle in Fars Province in southern Iran, and subsequently some may have continued migration patterns that brought Kurdish families to various parts of the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, where they were incorporated into local tribal structures as part of broader historical migration patterns.

migrationPath.description: Migration from traditional Kurdish homeland in western Iran to Fars Province in southern Iran

timelineEvents.title: Early Kurdish Tribal Documentation
timelineEvents.description: Ibn Khurdubah reports Kurdish tribes using the term Zūma to designate tribal units

timelineEvents.title: Kurdish Tribal Locations Recorded
timelineEvents.description: Ya'qubi records Kurdish tribes in Kermanshah, Saymara, Hulwan and Spahan regions

name: Utaybah
legitimacyNotes: One of the largest and most influential tribal confederations in central Arabia, with traditional authority over vast territories in Najd and Hijaz
ancestorStory: Descended from the Hawazin tribal group through Utaybah ibn Hawazin. The tribe traces its lineage to the ancient Hawazin confederation that was prominent during the time of Prophet Muhammad.
description: Utaybah is one of the largest and most powerful tribal confederations in Saudi Arabia, traditionally based in the Najd region with territories extending into the Hijaz. They played a significant role in the unification of Saudi Arabia under Ibn Saud, initially opposing then later supporting the Saudi state. The tribe is divided into two main branches: Barqa and Ruq, and remains influential in modern Saudi society with members holding important positions in government and business. They maintain strong tribal identity and traditional structures while adapting to contemporary Saudi Arabian society.

relations.context: A rivalry developed between Mutayr and Utaybah, who inhabited roughly the same areas as Mutayr in central Arabia after both tribes migrated from Hejaz.
Allied with Ajman in Ikhwan rebellion against Ibn Saud
Initially opposed then became allies in Saudi unification
Both major nomadic tribes with overlapping traditional territories

history:
Utaybah is one of the largest Arabian tribal confederations, descended from the ancient Hawazin tribal group through Utaybah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin. According to the 8th-century genealogist Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's manuscript verified in 1988, after most Hawazin clans dispersed to Iraq, Syria, Egypt, and Persia, those who remained in Najd and Hejaz formed the largest Hawazin alliance under the name Utaybah. Their lineage traces through Qays ʿAylān to Mudar, establishing them as Adnanite Arabs descended from Ishmael. The tribe is primarily found in Saudi Arabia with distribution spanning throughout the kingdom, especially in Najd and Hejaz, as well as Kuwait, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates.

During the 18th century, Utaybah superseded the Mutayr and Qahtan tribes to become the dominant force in central Najd. In the 19th century, they were deeply involved in complex struggles between the Sharifs of Mecca, Ibn Rashid, and the Al Saud. In 1872, Utaybah chief Muslit bin Rubayan attacked western settlements of Riyadh, defeating and critically wounding Saud bin Faisal. The tribe cooperated with both Al Saud and the Sharifs of Mecca depending on circumstances, sometimes providing refuge to the Sharifs in times of adversity.

During the early 20th century Ikhwan movement, Utaybah leaders Sultan ibn Bjad and Eqab bin Mohaya played crucial roles in Ibn Saud's conquest of Al-Hasa, Ha'il, and the Hejaz regions. However, the tribe split during the 1928-1929 Ikhwan rebellion, with the Barga branch under Sultan ibn Bjad rebelling against Ibn Saud while the Roug branch under Omar bin Rubayan remained loyal. The rebellion was crushed at the Battle of Sabilla on March 29, 1929, where Utaybah rebels fought alongside Mutayr and Ajman against Ibn Saud's forces. Post-rebellion, the tribe was divided between the victorious Roug and defeated Barga branches.

In modern Saudi Arabia, Utaybah remains influential with members in government and business. However, they gained notoriety when Juhayman al-Otaybi led the 1979 seizure of the Great Mosque of Mecca on November 20, 1979. The rebels, mostly Saudi Utaybahs along with some Egyptians, Pakistanis and American converts, held the mosque until December 4, resulting in many deaths among civilian hostages, Saudi security personnel, and most rebels including Muhammad al-Qahtani. Juhayman and 67 surviving rebels were captured and publicly beheaded. The tribe is divided into three major branches: Barga, Rog (including Al-Hafah), and Banu Saad, with each branch further divided into clans and families.

migrationPath.description: Early settlement expansion from ancestral Hawazin territories in central Najd eastward
Territorial expansion westward toward Hijaz region, establishing control over trade routes
Consolidation of tribal territories across central Arabian Peninsula

folkLegends.title: The Banner Origin
folkLegends.story: According to Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's manuscript, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard - one of the banners that belong to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from a man, Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin, and the clans of Hawazin all united under one of his descendants in early times during the first centuries.
folkLegends.source: 8th-century manuscript by genealogist Hisham ibn al-Kalbi

nameEtymology: According to Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century manuscript, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the Hawazin tribe, named after Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin

name: Utub
legitimacyNotes: maritime trading confederation with established merchant families ruling Gulf states
ancestorStory: Named after their ancestor Utbah, the confederation emerged from Najdi origins and migrated to the Gulf coast in the 17th-18th centuries seeking maritime trade opportunities.
description: The Utub are a major tribal confederation originally from Najd who migrated to the Gulf coast and became dominant maritime traders and pearl merchants. They established themselves in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Qatar, with branches including Al Sabah (ruling family of Kuwait), Al Khalifa (ruling family of Bahrain), and Al Jalahma. The confederation played a crucial role in Gulf commerce and politics, controlling key trading ports and pearl beds. Today, Utub descendants continue to hold significant political and economic influence across the Gulf states.

relations.context: Described as being the second largest and most powerful tribe after the Utub in Bahrain
Al Khalifa are a major branch of the Utub confederation
Al Sabah are a branch of the Utub confederation
Al Jalahma are part of the Utub tribal confederation

history:
The Utub (العتوب) are a tribal confederation that emerged from Najdi origins, with most clans including Al-Sabah and Al-Khalifa tracing their lineage to the Anizah tribe, while some branches like Al Bin Ali descend from Banu Sulaym. Both lineages trace back to Adnani roots through the patriarch Adnan, who is considered a descendant of Ishmael son of Abraham. During a prolonged drought in the 17th century, the Utub clans migrated from their homeland in the Najd region, particularly from the area of Al-Aflaj known as Al-Haddar, around 1674-1676. They traveled through Al Aflaj, Qatif, and Al Ahsa before reaching Freiha in northwestern Qatar where they established a fortified coastal hub with access to maritime trade routes and pearling waters.

After successive military conflicts with the dominant Al Musallam tribe under nominal Ottoman support, the Utub departed Qatar for Iraq near Basra in 1716. They were expelled from Umm Qasr by the Ottomans due to their raiding activities on caravans and ships in Shatt al-Arab. Following their expulsion, they migrated to Kuwait around 1715 and established a government under the Al-Sabah family with permission from Bani Khaled rulers. Around the 1760s, the Al Jalahma and Al Khalifa clans migrated to Zubarah in Qatar, leaving Al Sabah as sole proprietors of Kuwait. The Al Bin Ali had settled at Zubarah in 1732, founding and ruling the town and making it one of the most important pearl trading centers in the Persian Gulf.

The Utub achieved their greatest military success in 1783 when they launched an expedition from Zubarah and conquered Bahrain under the leadership of Ahmed bin Muhammad Al Khalifa and Salama bin Saif Al Bin Ali. About 2,000 Persian troops had attacked Zubarah on 17 May 1783, but after suffering defeat, they retreated. An Utub naval fleet from Kuwait arrived in Bahrain the same day and set Manama ablaze, ultimately overrunning the Persian garrisons. The Al Khalifa established ruling authority in Bahrain that continues today. The confederation also expanded their influence to East Africa, with the Al Bin Ali clan participating in the conquest of Mombasa in 1837 alongside the Sultan of Muscat and Oman.

Throughout their history, the Utub developed into skilled maritime traders, pearl merchants, and naval powers. They served various regional powers, assisting the Imam of Oman Sultan bin Saif II in invading Bahrain in 1717 to expel the Safavids, and even participated in the Safavid request to invade Oman in 1697. The confederation established themselves across multiple Gulf states: Al Sabah became the ruling family of Kuwait, Al Khalifa the ruling family of Bahrain, Al Bin Ali maintained significant presence in Qatar and parts of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, while Al Jalahma remained influential merchants and traders without establishing their own political entity.

migrationPath.description: Migration due to prolonged drought, traveling through Al Aflaj, Qatif, and Al Ahsa
Departure after conflicts with Al Musallam tribe
Expelled by Ottomans due to raiding activities, established government under Al-Sabah

folkLegends.title: Origin of the Name Utub
folkLegends.story: Some sources suggest the name derives from the verb 'ataba' meaning to migrate or move, applied because of their frequent migrations and movements from place to place. However, the name was already in use before their northern migration, as evidenced by Yusuf al-Bahrani referring to them as 'Utub' in 1700 when describing an attack on Bahrain that occurred before their move north.
folkLegends.source: Arabic historical sources and Yusuf al-Bahrani's Luluat al-Bahrain

folkLegends.title: Ancestral Origin from Utbah
folkLegends.story: The confederation is said to be named after their ancestor Utbah, with some sources tracing this to Utbah ibn Rabah ibn Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah, while others trace Al Bin Ali specifically to Utbah ibn Khuffaf ibn Imru al-Qais ibn Bahthah ibn Sulaym.
folkLegends.source: Genealogical traditions recorded in Arabic sources

nameEtymology: The name 'Utub' (العتوب) has disputed origins. Some sources claim it derives from the ancestor Utbah, while others suggest it comes from the verb 'ataba' (عتب) meaning to migrate or move, due to their frequent relocations. However, evidence shows the name was used before their major northern migration, suggesting the migration theory may be incorrect.

name: al-Wahabab
legitimacyNotes: Religious and scholarly authority through association with Wahhabi movement
ancestorStory: Named after or associated with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the 18th century Islamic reformer who founded the Wahhabi movement. The family became prominent religious scholars and allies of the Al Saud.
description: Al-Wahabab are a scholarly family associated with the Wahhabi religious movement founded by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in 18th century Najd. They formed a crucial alliance with the Al Saud family, providing religious legitimacy to Saudi political authority. The family produced generations of prominent Islamic scholars and judges who helped establish and maintain the religious foundation of the Saudi state. They remain influential in Saudi Arabia's religious establishment today.

relations.context: Historic politico-religious alliance between Al Wahhab scholars and Al Saud rulers

history:
Based on available research, al-Wahabab (الوهاب) are documented as one of the riverine Manasir clans (Manasir al-Nil) along the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in Northern Sudan. The Sudanese Manasir, including the al-Wahabab clan, constitute a Sunni Arab riverine tribe of Northern Sudan who inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in an area they call Dar al-Manasir. Similar to their neighboring tribes, the upstream Rubatab and downstream Shaiqiyah, the Manasir are described as an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language.

The Manasir community, including al-Wahabab, is understood by critical research as having formed through voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in adjoining areas. Earlier travelers witnessed Beja and Bisharin influences from the Red Sea Hills among the Manasir, and strong similarities of their burial customs with that of the Nubians can still be observed.

The al-Wahabab clan is classified among the Manasir al-Nil (riverine Manasir) rather than the Manasir al-Badiyah (Bedouin Manasir). The riverine Manasir pursue small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile, with dates being their most important cash crop sold on the national market. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses and historically floated wood from the region of Atbarah.

The relationship between this Sudanese al-Wahabab clan and any Arabian Peninsula family remains unclear from available sources. The existing data's association with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and the Saudi religious establishment is not supported by the research provided, which focuses exclusively on the Sudanese Manasir context.

migrationPath.description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab fled al-Uyaynah after opposition to his teachings and sought protection in Diriyah
Family members exiled to Egypt and Istanbul after Ottoman-Egyptian destruction of first Saudi state
Return to Najd during establishment of second Saudi state under Turki ibn Abdullah

timelineEvents.title: Population Census
timelineEvents.description: Federal Department of Statistics of Sudan recorded 30,000 de facto Manasir population including al-Wahabab clan members

folkLegends.title: Mansur bin Qahtan Origin
folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.title: Kawahla Descent
folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.title: Shaiqi al-Hankab Migration
folkLegends.story: Other historians insist that they are descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights. According to this opinion Mansur is a direct descendant of King Sabir.

folkLegends.title: Abbasid Lineage
folkLegends.story: According to current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor Mansur belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad.

name: Wuld Ali
legitimacyNotes: Lineage authority through descent from Ali ancestor
ancestorStory: Named after their progenitor Ali, though specific genealogical details vary by regional branch. The name suggests descent from a notable figure named Ali within their broader tribal confederation.
description: Wuld Ali is a tribal branch found across various parts of the Arabian Peninsula, with the name indicating descent from an ancestor named Ali. The tribe has historically been involved in pastoral nomadism and has maintained traditional Bedouin customs. Different branches of Wuld Ali may be found in various GCC countries, though their specific historical prominence and current status varies by region. They represent one of the many smaller tribal units that form part of the complex tribal structure of the Arabian Peninsula.
history: The Wuld Ali are documented as a major branch of the Anizah tribal confederation, one of the most prominent Arabian tribes tracing their lineage to the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The Wuld Ali first entered Syria around the mid-18th century, establishing themselves in the Hawran region during the same period that their fellow Anizah branch, the Hasana, dominated the plains around Hama and Homs. Their presence in the Levant was part of the broader Anizah expansion northward from their strongholds in northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula. During the Ottoman period, the Wuld Ali operated under the traditional khuwwa ('brotherhood') system, collecting tribute from settled populations in exchange for protection from other tribal raids. In the early 19th century, they faced significant rivalry from the Ruwallah tribe, another Anizah branch that followed them into the Hawran and competed for regional influence. The Wuld Ali also engaged in conflicts with the Bani Sakher during their northward migrations in the late 16th century, suffering defeats that established long-standing enmities between the tribes. Their leadership structure included the Al-Aida family as sheikhs of the tribe, and the Al-Taiyar family among their notable sheikhs. In Jordan's history, they were eventually pushed northward out of southern territories by the resurgent Bani Sakher under the Ahl Al-Shamal confederacy in the early 19th century, with the Wuld Ali Anazah being forced into the Al Mafraq area. The tribe maintained their traditional Bedouin customs and pastoral lifestyle throughout these territorial changes, adapting to the new political boundaries established in the 20th century while retaining their Anizah tribal identity.

migrationPath.description: Northward expansion from northwestern Arabian oasis strongholds into Syrian territories as part of broader Anizah tribal movement
Forced northward migration due to pressure from resurgent Bani Sakher under the Ahl Al-Shamal confederacy

name: Yaarubah
legitimacyNotes: Elected Imams ruling Oman based on Islamic principles and tribal consensus
ancestorName: Yarub bin Sultan bin Saif bin Malik
ancestorStory: Yarub bin Sultan was elected as Imam of Oman in 1624, founding the Ya'aruba dynasty that expelled Portuguese forces and unified Oman. The dynasty emerged from the Azd tribal confederation of interior Oman.
description: The Ya'aruba were a ruling dynasty that governed Oman from 1624 to 1741, originating from the Azd tribal confederation. Under their rule, Oman became a major maritime power, controlling trade routes in the Indian Ocean and establishing colonies along the East African coast including Zanzibar. The dynasty successfully expelled Portuguese colonial forces and unified the Omani interior under the Imamate system. Their rule ended with internal succession disputes in the early 18th century, leading to the rise of the Al Bu Said dynasty.

relations.context: Al Bu Said replaced Ya'aruba rule in Oman after civil war

history:
The Yaarubah (Ya'aruba) trace their genealogical origins to ancient Qahtani lineage through Yarub bin Qahtan, positioning them among the earliest Arab settlers in Oman. According to historical sources, during the 8th century BCE, Yaarub, the descendant of Qahtan, is believed to have ruled the entire region of Yemen, including Oman. The Yaarubah are considered to have been the first settlers in Oman from Yemen, arriving even before the major Azd tribal migration that occurred around 120-200 CE following the collapse of the Marib Dam. This early settlement established their presence in the interior regions of Oman, where they became part of the broader Hinawi tribal confederation aligned with the Azd.

The tribe's most significant historical period began in the 17th century when they established the Yaruba dynasty that would rule Oman from 1624 to 1744. The dynasty emerged during a period of Portuguese colonial occupation, with Nasir bin Murshid becoming the first Yaarubah Imam in 1624 when he was elected in Rustaq, marking the beginning of Omani reunification under their leadership. The Yaarubah Imams, operating under the Ibadhi Imamate system, successfully expelled Portuguese colonial forces from Oman in the 1650s and established a powerful maritime empire that extended across the Indian Ocean to East Africa.

Under the Yaruba dynasty, Oman became a major regional power controlling important trade routes in the Indian Ocean. Imam Saif bin Sultan captured Fort Jesus at Mombasa in 1698 after a two-year siege and occupied Bahrain in 1700, demonstrating the extent of Yaarubah maritime influence. The dynasty established colonies along the East African coast including Zanzibar, making Oman a significant competitor to Portuguese and later British imperial interests in the Indian Ocean. Their rule represented the golden age of Omani naval power and territorial expansion.

However, internal rivalries within the house of Yaruba after the death of Imam Sultan in 1718 severely weakened the dynasty. The situation deteriorated when Imam Saif bin Sultan II sought help from Nader Shah of Persia against his rivals in 1737. Persian forces initially aided the Yaruba but eventually rebelled against them in 1743, leading to Persian control of the Omani coast until 1747. After the Omanis expelled the Persians, Ahmed bin Sa'id Albusaidi became the elected Imam in 1744, ending Yaarubah rule and establishing the Al Bu Said dynasty that continues to govern Oman today. The fall of the Yaruba dynasty marked the end of over a century of Yaarubah political dominance in Oman and the broader Gulf region.

migrationPath.description: Ancient Yaarubah migration from Yemen as first Arab settlers in Oman, predating the major Azd tribal migration

name: Yammahi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through hereditary sheikhs
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to an ancestor named Yamaha, though the specific genealogical details vary in different tribal accounts. They are considered part of the broader tribal confederation system of the UAE.
description: The Yammahi are a tribal branch primarily based in the UAE, particularly in the northern emirates. Historically, they were involved in oasis agriculture and date cultivation, settling in various towns and villages. The tribe maintained traditional leadership structures and participated in the broader tribal politics of the region. Today, members of the Yammahi tribe continue to be active in UAE society while maintaining their tribal identity.

relations.context: Historical cooperation in northern UAE regions
Traditional relationships in Ras Al Khaimah region

history: The Yammahi are one of the three notable sections of the Sharqiyin tribe, alongside the Hafaitat and Hamudiyin. The Sharqiyin were traditionally a Ghafiri-aligned tribe and the dominant force along the east coast of the Trucial States, known as Shamailiyah, being the second most numerous tribe in the area by the early 19th century. The Yammahi section, along with other Sharqiyin groups, settled extensively along the east coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain. By the turn of the 20th century, the entire Sharqiyin confederation was approximately 7,000 strong. The Yammahi historically populated the mountainous areas around Jabal Yibir (Jabal Al-Mebrah) and the Wadi Awsaq region in what is now Fujairah and Ras Al Khaimah. They were part of the broader Sharqiyin struggle for independence from Sharjah rule, with multiple rebellions occurring in 1866, 1876, 1879, and 1884, ultimately achieving practical independence in 1901 under the leadership of the Hafaitat section's Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Sharqi. Today, the Al Yammahi continue to be one of the prominent tribal populations in Dibba Al-Fujairah and maintain their presence in the mountainous regions of Fujairah emirate.

migrationPath.description: Migration northward as part of Qahtani tribal movements during early Islamic period

name: Yidwah
description: Yidwah is a tribal name that appears in Arabian genealogical records, though detailed historical documentation about this particular tribal group is limited in available sources. Without sufficient verified historical data, the specific origins, territorial base, and role in Gulf regional politics cannot be definitively established. Further research in local historical archives and oral traditions would be needed to provide comprehensive information about this tribe's significance in GCC history.
history:
The Yidwah are a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe, an Arab tribal confederation prominent in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah, are associated with territories around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah, and areas east of Buraimi including Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. Among the various Bani Kaab subsections - which include the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat - the Yidwah were among the settled sections by the turn of the 20th century, distinguishing them from the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections.

In 1844, the Yidwah, as part of the Bani Kaab confederation, allied with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nayhan of the Bani Yas in a tribal coalition that successfully drove Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. This alliance was part of a broader confederation of interior southeastern Arabian tribes united against Wahhabi expansion. The Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah, were rivals to the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes, having pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain.

Tribal alliances in southeastern Arabia proved fluid over time. By the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had shifted to supporting Saudi interests, with their sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province Governor that 'Our territories are yours.' During the Buraimi Dispute of the 1950s, the Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah subsection, supported the Saudi incursion under Shaikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and fought against British forces and the Trucial Oman Levies. By the turn of the 20th century, the settled sections of Bani Kaab, including the Yidwah, comprised part of a population of some 7,250, of whom 1,150 remained Bedouin.

name: Zaab
traditionalEconomy: pearling, date cultivation, fishing, and livestock (sheep and cattle)
description: The Za'ab is an Arab tribe of the Arabian Peninsula, principally in the United Arab Emirates. Originally settled the coastal village of Jazirat Al Hamra with around 500 houses at the turn of the 20th century, and also established themselves in Kalba with 150 families. The tribe was approximately 3,300 strong and maintained date palms in the inland village of Khatt. They operated a fleet of 25 pearling boats which provided their principal income until the pearl market crash in the late 1920s. Following disputes with Ras Al Khaimah rulers in 1968, the majority accepted Sheikh Zayed's offer to relocate to Abu Dhabi, leaving behind an almost completely abandoned village of 2,500 people.

relations.context: The Zaab became dependents of the Al Qasimi rulers of Ras Al Khaimah by 1853, but this relationship deteriorated leading to the 1968 migration
Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan offered the Zaab tribe refuge in Abu Dhabi during their 1968 dispute with Ras Al Khaimah

history:
The Za'ab (Arabic: زعاب, singular Za'abi الزعابي) is an Arab tribe of the Arabian Peninsula, principally in the United Arab Emirates. According to Arabic genealogical sources, the tribe's lineage is disputed, tracing either to Za'b ibn Malik ibn Khifaf ibn Imru al-Qays ibn Buhtha ibn Sulaym ibn Mansur of the Mudar branch (Adnanite), or to Za'ab ibn Murra of the Abd al-Qays lineage of Rabi'a. By the early 19th century, the Za'ab had established themselves prominently in the coastal village of Jazirat Al Hamra (The Red Island), where they built approximately 500 houses and developed it into a significant pearling center. The tribe also maintained 150 families in Kalba and extensive date palm groves at Khatt village in the Jiri plain, where they would relocate during summer harvest seasons to escape coastal humidity. At the time, the tribe numbered approximately 3,300 people and operated a fleet of 25 pearling boats, which served as their primary economic foundation until the pearl market collapse in the late 1920s.

Sheikh Rajib bin Ahmed Al Zaabi served as the independent ruler of Jazirat Al Hamra in 1820 and was one of four signatories to the original 1820 treaty with the British following their punitive expedition against Ras Al Khaimah in 1819. The settlement was initially recorded in the treaty as 'Jourat Al Kamra.' Following the Perpetual Maritime Truce of 1853, Jazirat Al Hamra became incorporated into Ras Al Khaimah under Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi, and the Za'ab became dependents of the Qasimi rulers. The island settlement was divided into two sections: the small northern quarter of Umm Awaimir and the southern Manakh. Although the Za'ab maintained some 500 sheep and 150 cattle, there were no palm groves on the island itself, though they tended approximately 20,000 date palm trees around Khatt village according to J.G. Lorimer's records.

Persistent disputes with Ras Al Khaimah authorities culminated in 1968 when disagreements with Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi led the majority of the tribe to accept Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan's offer to relocate to Abu Dhabi. This migration resulted in the abandonment of their ancestral village of 2,500 inhabitants, leaving Jazirat Al Hamra almost completely deserted. The relocation established the Al Zaab district in Abu Dhabi, where the tribe continues to reside today. The abandoned settlement had also been called Jazirah Al Zaab after the tribe, reflecting their historical dominance of the area.

migrationPath.description: Mass migration of Za'ab tribe from Jazirat Al Hamra to Abu Dhabi following disputes with Ras Al Khaimah rulers, accepting Sheikh Zayed's relocation offer

name: Zahairat
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership through established sheikhly families
ancestorStory: Descended from Zuhair, a prominent ancestor within the broader tribal confederation. The tribe traces its lineage through established Arabian genealogical traditions.
description: The Zahairat are a tribal branch found primarily in the northern Arabian Peninsula and parts of the Gulf region. They have historically been involved in nomadic pastoralism and have maintained traditional tribal structures. The tribe has adapted to modern Gulf state development while preserving their cultural identity. They maintain presence in several GCC countries with established family networks.

relations.context: Historical movement patterns in northern Arabia
Pastoral cooperation in Arabian Peninsula

history:
The Zahairat are documented as a subsection of the Bani Kaab tribe (Arabic: بني كعب), which has historical presence in Oman and the United Arab Emirates. The Bani Kaab are associated with areas around and north of the Omani Wilayat of Mahdah and regions east of Buraimi in the UAE, including the Wadi Kadra, Wadi Hatta and Wadi Qor. The tribe consists of multiple subsections including the Drisah, Makatim, Misaid, Miyadilah, Miyalisah, Mizahamiyin, Nawaljiyin, Salalat, Sawalim, Shwaihiyin, Yidwah and Zahairat. By the turn of the 20th century, the Zahairat were among the settled sections of the Bani Kaab, as opposed to the nomadic Drisah and Shwaihiyin subsections, with a total tribal population of approximately 7,250 of whom 1,150 were Bedouin.

In 1844, the Bani Kaab, including the Zahairat, formed an alliance with Sheikh Khalifa bin Shakhbut Al Nahyan of the Bani Yas in a tribal confederation united to drive Wahhabi forces from Buraimi. This alliance reflected the broader pattern of tribal coalitions in southeastern Arabia during the mid-19th century. Despite their alliance under the Bani Yas, the Bani Kaab maintained rivalries with the Na'im and Bani Qitab tribes, and had pushed sections of the Na'im north as far as the Jiri plain.

The tribe's political alignments shifted significantly over the following century. By the 1940s, the Bani Kaab had allied with the Saudis, with their Sheikh Obaid bin Jumah declaring to the Governor of Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, 'Our territories are yours.' Interestingly, although the Na'im and Bani Kaab were frequently opposed to each other, they came together in the 1940s when oil concessions loomed, opposing the oil companies, the imposition of the rule of the Trucial Sheikhs, the Sultan of Muscat and the British alike.

During the 1950s Buraimi Dispute, the Zahairat's parent tribe supported the Saudi incursion under Sheikh Rashid bin Hamad of the Al Bu Shamis and were involved in fighting against the British force, the Trucial Oman Levies. This represented the culmination of their alliance with Saudi Arabia and their opposition to British influence in the region. The Zahairat, as part of the settled sections of the Bani Kaab, have maintained their presence in the UAE and Oman while adapting to the modern development of the Gulf states.

migrationPath.description: Migration of Zahairat segments during early Islamic expansion eastward to Omani coastal regions
Movement inland to establish settlements in Omani wadis and oasis towns

name: Zahran
legitimacyNotes: This appears to be a genuine blood lineage tracing back to the ancient Azd confederation, with well-documented genealogical records and historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula.
ancestorName: Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd
ancestorStory: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'. The full lineage traces back to Al-Azd through Zahran ibn Ka'ab.
description: Zahran (Arabic: زهران), also known as Banū ʿZahrān ibn Kaʿab, is one of the oldest Arabian tribes in the Arabian Peninsula and is regarded as one of the largest tribes in Al Bahah Province. Al Baha is the homeland of Zahran and Ghamid. The tribe is well-known from pre-Islamic times and many joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The name Zahran means 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic.

relations.context: Zahran is an offshoot of the Azd tribe confederation
Al Baha is the shared homeland of both Zahran and Ghamid tribes
Alliance formed through Al-Jadara family connections and intermarriage
Al-Jadara formed direct alliance with Banu Al Du'al in Mecca

history:
The Zahran tribe traces its lineage to Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd, making it an Azdite offshoot of Qahtanite origin. The name Zahran, meaning 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic, refers to their shared common ancestor. The tribe's homeland is centered in Al-Bahah Province, particularly the Sarawat Mountains region, which they share with the neighboring Ghamid tribe. The Zahran are part of the larger Azd Shanu'ah tribal confederation, characterized by their light, muscular build and eloquent poetry traditions.

In the 3rd century CE, many Zahranid branches migrated under the leadership of Malik ibn Fehm, who became one of the first Arabians to settle in Oman. This migration led to conflict with Persian forces in the great Salut Battle, contributing to Oman's Arabization. Malik ibn Fehm later established the Tanukh tribal confederation centered in Al-Hira, with his son Jadhima 'the Leper' becoming king of Tanukh in the second half of the 3rd century CE. The Al-Jadara family, descended from Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama, settled in Mecca and became known as 'the Wall-Masons' for reconstructing the Kaaba's walls after Abraham and Ishmael. They formed crucial alliances with Banu Al Du'al and Quraysh, with Fatimah bint Sa'd Al-Jadari marrying Kilab ibn Murrah of Quraysh, bearing Qusay ibn Kilab, an ancestor of Prophet Muhammad.

The tribe was well-known before and after Islam, with many members joining Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The tribe is notable for producing Abu Hurairah, one of Islam's most prolific hadith narrators from the Banu Daws clan of Zahran. Abu Hurairah converted to Islam through Tufayl ibn 'Amr in 629 CE and became one of the inhabitants of the Suffah, participating in various military expeditions and later serving as governor of Bahrain under Caliph Umar.

The Zahran tribe comprises three main divisions: Banu Daws (including Banu Manhib, Banu Fahm and Banu Ali), Banu 'Amr (including Banu Bashir, Banu Harir, Banu Jundob, and Banu 'Adwan), and Banu Aws (including Banu Hasan, Bal-Khirmar, Banu Kinanah, Banu 'Amir and Ahl Baydan). Modern demographic changes occurred in the 1960s and 1970s when many Zahranis migrated to major Saudi cities like Riyadh, Jeddah, Mecca, and Dammam seeking better opportunities. Oman's current ruling family, Al Said, claims descent from Zahran through Malik ibn Fehm, while the tribe maintains its traditional homeland in Al-Bahah while having established significant presences across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.

Establishment of Tanukh tribal confederation under Malik ibn Fehm
