folkLegends.title: The Wall-Masons of Mecca
folkLegends.story: Al-Jadara were a Zahranid family that settled in Mecca and were called 'Al-Jadra' (the Wall-Masons) because their progenitor, Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama, is said to be the first to reconstruct the walls of the Kaaba after Abraham and Ishmael, earning him the title 'Amer the Builder'.

folkLegends.title: The Destruction of Dhu l-Khalasah
folkLegends.story: Dhu l-Khalasah was an idol worshiped by the Daws clan in a valley in Zahran's homeland. In the early 20th century, local clans returned to honoring this idol during times of security crisis and poverty. King Abdulaziz (Ibn Saud) ordered its destruction between 1921-1925 CE, carried out by Abdulaziz Al Ibrahim who led a campaign that demolished the structure and threw its ruins into a nearby valley.

nameEtymology: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'.

name: Zangana
description: The Zangana are a Kurdish tribe historically based in the mountainous regions of Kurdistan, primarily in what is now Iraqi Kurdistan and parts of Iran. They are not traditionally associated with the Arabian Peninsula or Gulf states, making their relevance to GCC tribal genealogy minimal. Any presence in the Gulf region would likely be modern migration rather than historical tribal settlement.
history: The Zangana are a Kurdish tribe historically based in Kermanshah province of Iran and parts of Iraqi Kurdistan, with no connection to Arabian Peninsula tribal genealogy. They are mentioned in Kurdish tribal records as one of three regiments in the army of the Median ruler Keyqubad around 854 BCE, descending from their forefather Zengan and forming a branch of the Bûdî tribe. According to the 1659 historical work Kurdgalnamek by Axwend Mihemed Salih Zengene Beluç, the Zangana were among Kurdish tribal regiments that migrated eastward from the Zagros Mountains to consolidate Median rule over regions including Turan, Kerman, Makran, and Zabulistan - notably toward Central Asia and Balochistan, not the Arabian Peninsula. The tribe speaks a distinct Kurdish dialect, though in recent decades they have increasingly assimilated linguistically into Sorani Kurdish practices in their areas of residence. The Zangana suffered significant displacement during Saddam Hussein's Al-Anfal Campaign of 1988, which specifically targeted Kurdish populations, and again during the 1991 refugee crisis following the failed Kurdish uprising against the Iraqi government. Their settlement patterns shifted dramatically since the late 1980s due to these forced relocations by the Iraqi regime. Any presence of Zangana individuals in Gulf Cooperation Council states would represent modern economic migration rather than historical tribal settlement patterns, as their traditional territories and migration routes were consistently within the Kurdish regions of Iran, Iraq, and historically toward Central Asian territories.

migrationPath.description: Kurdish tribal regiments including Zangana migrated eastward from Zagros Mountains to consolidate Median rule over Central Asian and Balochistan regions under Keyqubad

folkLegends.title: Median Origins Under Keyqubad
folkLegends.story: According to the Kurdgalnameh, the Zangana were one of three regiments in the army of Median ruler Keyqubad, who declared war on Afrasiyab around 854 BCE. The Kurdish tribal regiments loyal to the Median state left the Zagros Mountains eastwards to Turan, Kerman, Makran, and Zabulistan, consolidating Median rule over these regions. The Zangana, along with the Birakhoyi, were both branches of the Bûdî tribe named after forefather Bûd, with the Zangana specifically descended from their forefather Zengan.
folkLegends.source: Kurdgalnamek (1659) by Axwend Mihemed Salih Zengene Beluç

nameEtymology: The Zangana tribal name derives from their forefather Zengan, according to the Kurdgalnamek historical work

name: Zoubi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal leadership structure with recognized sheikhs
ancestorStory: The tribe traces its lineage to Za'ab, an ancestor from the northern Arabian Peninsula. They migrated to various parts of the Gulf region over several centuries.
description: The Zoubi (Al Zaabi) is a prominent Arabian tribe with branches across the Gulf region, particularly in the UAE and northern Saudi Arabia. They are known for their traditional pastoral lifestyle and have maintained significant influence in tribal politics. The tribe has historically been involved in regional alliances and trade networks. Today, members of the tribe continue to play important roles in Gulf society while maintaining their cultural heritage.

relations.context: The Mahameed alliance with Zoubi
Historical cooperation in northern Arabian Peninsula

history:
The research reveals a fundamental discrepancy regarding the Zoubi tribal identity. The existing data describes the Zoubi as a Gulf Arabian tribe tracing lineage to Za'ab from northern Arabia, but the documented research shows the Zoubi (al-Zu'bi) as a definitively Levantine Arab clan centered in southern Syria, northern Jordan, and northern Israel. The documented Zoubi are an agricultural clan of approximately 160,000 members who claim descent from the family of Prophet Muhammad through their connection to Abdul Qadir Gilani (1077-1166), founder of the Qadiriyya Sufi order. Their ancestor Ahmad Ali Al Gilani was later titled Al-Zoubi, establishing the genealogical link between the Levantine Zoubi and the broader Gilani Sayyid lineage.

The Zoubi traditionally provided the leaders of the Qadiriyya Sufi order in the Hauran region of southern Syria, from which they derived their political prestige even after Sufism's decline in the early 20th century. They are the largest of the extended agricultural clans of the Hauran plains and inhabit sixteen villages in Syria's Daraa Governorate, including the capital Daraa and villages like Khirbet Ghazaleh, Tafas, al-Musayfirah, and Muzayrib. In Jordan, they have extensive presence in Irbid Governorate, particularly in al-Ramtha and Irbid. In Israel, the clan is predominant in villages east of Afula in the Jezreel and Beisan valleys, and forms one of the principal Muslim families in Nazareth.

During French Mandatory Syria (1920-1946), the Zoubi were rivals of the Hariri clan and aligned themselves with French authorities while the Hariri supported Syrian nationalism. This political alignment was partly due to the Sufi inclination toward governing authorities. Under Ba'ath Party rule post-1963, lower-ranking Zoubi members gained prominence, most notably Mahmoud Al-Zoubi who served as Syria's Prime Minister from 1987-2000. The clan's political influence subsequently declined under President Bashar al-Assad, who showed more favor to their traditional rivals, the Hariri.

In Palestine/Israel during the 1930s, the head of the Zoubi family was Muhammad Said, based in the village of Nein. During the 1936-1939 Palestinian revolt, the Zoubi largely opposed Amin al-Husayni. Muhammad Said and his relatives facilitated land sales to the Jewish National Fund, leading to assassination attempts against family members in 1947. During the 1948 Palestine war, the Zoubi forged peace agreements with neighboring Jewish settlements, with Seif el-Din el-Zoubi serving as an intermediary between Israeli authorities and Arab inhabitants of the Nazareth area.

name: Zughba
legitimacyNotes: Branch of Banu Hilal confederation with historical migration and settlement rights
ancestorStory: Descended from Hilal ibn Amir, the Zughba were one of the major branches of the Banu Hilal confederation. They participated in the great Hilalian migrations westward in the 11th century.
description: The Zughba were a major branch of the Banu Hilal tribal confederation, descended from Adnani lineage. They were among the prominent Arab tribes that participated in the great Hilalian migrations of the 11th century, moving westward across North Africa. Historically based in the Arabian Peninsula before their migration, they were known for their nomadic pastoralism and warrior traditions. While their main historical impact was in North Africa, some branches may have maintained presence in Arabian territories, though their primary legacy lies outside the immediate GCC region.

relations.context: Both trace descent through Adnani lineage and share historical connections through Hilalian tribal networks

history: The Zughba were a major sub-tribe of the Banu Hilal confederation, descended from the Adnani lineage through Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a. Originally based in Najd in the central Arabian Peninsula, they were among the six main Hilali sub-tribes alongside Athbaj, Riyah, Jusham, Adi, and Qurra. In 1037-1038, a faction of Zughba settled in Tripoli alongside Banu Qurra, establishing an early presence in North Africa. The great migration came in 1050-1051 when the Fatimid Caliphate dispatched the Banu Hilal westward to punish the rebellious Zirids of Ifriqiya. The Fatimids provided each tribesman with a camel and money to cross the Nile, with severe drought in Egypt also motivating the migration. By the 12th century, Zughba had become the westernmost Hilali tribe, controlling territory from Tlemcen in the west to Algiers in the east, living near the Athbaj tribe to their south and east. Over time, they were settled in Morocco along with the Khult and Riyah tribes, playing a crucial role in the rural Arabization of the western Maghreb. The tribe included notable sub-branches such as the Beni Amer, who occupied the Oran region and were described by Leo Africanus as having 6,000 well-equipped cavalry. Like other Hilali tribes, they adopted Maliki Sunni Islam after their conquest of the predominantly Sunni Maghreb, despite initially being Isma'ili Shia. Their migration was part of a massive Arab movement estimated at 500,000 to 1 million people, fundamentally transforming North African culture and spreading Bedouin Arabic throughout the region.

name: Banu Hilal
legitimacyNotes: Claimed Adnanite lineage and historical role in major Arabian migrations
ancestorStory: Descended from Hilal ibn Amir of the Hawazin tribal confederation. Known for their legendary migration and conquests across North Africa in the 11th century.
description: Banu Hilal was a major Adnanite tribal confederation originally based in the Arabian Peninsula, particularly Najd and the eastern regions. They are most famous for their epic 11th-century migration westward across North Africa, known as the Hilalian invasion, which significantly impacted the demographic and cultural landscape of the Maghreb. While their main historical significance lies in North Africa, branches of the tribe remained in Arabia and some elements can be traced in the genealogies of various Gulf tribes. Today they exist primarily as a historical entity, though their legacy continues through various tribal branches and their famous oral epic, the Sirat Bani Hilal.

relations.context: Some Al Muntafiq branches claim Hilali origins
Both tribes involved in major Arabian migrations

history:
The Banu Hilal (بنو هلال) was a major Arab tribal confederation of the Hawazin group, descending from Hilal ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'a ibn Mu'awiya ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin ibn Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khaṣafa ibn Qays ibn Aylan ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them part of the Adnanite lineage. Originally based in Najd in central Arabia, particularly around the areas between Ta'if and Mecca, and between Medina and Najd, they comprised six main sub-tribes: Athbadj, Riyah, Jusham, Adi, Zughba, and Rabi'a. The tribe did not accept Islam until after Muhammad's victory at the Battle of Hunayn in 630 CE, but notably did not participate in the Ridda Wars after Muhammad's death in 632.

In the early 8th century, some Banu Hilal migrated to Egypt, and by the 9th century, along with Banu Sulaym, they moved from Najd to Iraq and later to the Levant. In the 970s, they joined the Qarmatian sect in attacks against the Fatimid Caliphate, resulting in their forced relocation to Upper Egypt by Fatimid caliph al-Aziz in 978, where they were prohibited from crossing the Nile or leaving Upper Egypt due to their continued conflicts. The most significant chapter in their history began in the 11th century when the Zirid dynasty of Ifriqiya proclaimed independence from the Fatimid Caliphate.

In retaliation, the Fatimids in 1050-1051 dispatched the Banu Hilal and Banu Sulaym westward to defeat the Zirids, providing each tribesman with a camel and money to cross the Nile. An estimated 200,000 to 1,000,000 Arab nomads participated in this migration. Upon arriving in Cyrenaica, they found the region largely empty except for a few Zenata Berbers. The Banu Sulaym settled in Cyrenaica while the Hilalians continued westward, eventually reaching as far as Morocco's Atlantic coast by the 12th century. This migration, known as the Hilalian invasion, fundamentally transformed North Africa through Arabization of the Berber population, spread of Bedouin Arabic dialects, and the shift from agricultural to nomadic lifestyles.

The Banu Hilal later came under various dynasties including the Almohads, who defeated them at the Battle of Setif and forced many to Morocco. Their epic journey became immortalized in the Arabic oral tradition as 'Sirat Bani Hilal' or Taghribat Banu Hilal, one of the most celebrated folk epics in Arab literature. Today, they exist primarily as a historical entity, though their legacy continues through various tribal branches across North Africa and their descendants can be traced in numerous Maghrebi Arab tribes.

folkLegends.title: Taghribat Banu Hilal
folkLegends.story: The accounts and records that the folk poet Abdul Rahman al-Abnudi gathered from the bards of Upper Egypt culminated in the Taghribat Bani Hilal, an Arab epic describing the journey of the tribe from Arabia to the Maghreb. The tale is divided into three main cycles. The first two bring together unfolding events in Arabia and other countries of the east, while the third, called Taghriba (march west), recounts the migration of the Banu Hilal to North Africa.

nameEtymology: Named after their progenitor Hilal ibn Amir, as recorded in traditional Arab genealogical sources

name: Banu Kinanah
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Adnani lineage tracing to Kinanah ibn Khuzaymah, legitimacy through direct descent from Adnan
ancestorStory: Kinanah was the son of Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah, from whom descended the noble lineages of Quraysh and other prominent tribes. His descendants settled primarily in the Hejaz region and became influential in pre-Islamic Arabian society.
description: Banu Kinanah was one of the major Adnani tribes of pre-Islamic Arabia, primarily based in the Hejaz region around Mecca. They are most famous for being the parent tribe of Quraysh, the tribe of Prophet Muhammad, making them one of the most prestigious lineages in Arab genealogy. The tribe played a significant role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and trade, with various branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula. While the tribal structure has largely dissolved into modern state systems, their genealogical significance remains important in GCC societies where Adnani descent is traced through Kinanah.

relations.context: Both trace Adnani descent through ancient Arabian lineages

history:
Banu Kinanah was one of the major Adnani tribes of pre-Islamic Arabia, tracing their lineage to Kinanah ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah. According to traditional Arab genealogy, they descended from Ishmael through Adnan, making them part of the northern Arabian tribal confederation. The tribe was divided into six principal branches: Nadr, Malik, Milkan, Amir, Amr, and Abd Manat. Most significantly, the Nadr branch became the parent tribe of Quraysh, from whom the Prophet Muhammad descended, elevating Kinanah's genealogical importance in Islamic history. Their traditional territory extended from the Tihama coastline near Mecca northeastward to the borders of their tribal relatives, the Banu Asad.

The Abd Manat branch included strong subgroups like Bakr ibn Abd Manat (with Mudlij, Du'il, Layth, and Damra divisions) and formed part of the Ahabish confederation. As polytheists, they worshipped the goddess al-Uzza before Islam. Fihr ibn Malik ibn Nadr emerged as a Kinani leader after their victory against Himyarite forces, and his descendant Qusayy ibn Kilab captured Mecca with Kinani backing, particularly from Ya'mar ibn Amr of the Layth group, though the Bakr opposed Qusayy. The Fijar War erupted when the Kinani exile al-Barrad ibn Qays killed a Banu Kilab chief, leading to conflict between Kilab-Hawazin forces and the Quraysh-Kinanah alliance.

During the early Islamic period, various Kinani groups played different roles in Muhammad's conquests. The Mudlij promised not to attack Quraysh at Badr, while the Ghifar, Layth, and Damra supported Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. The Bakr's attack on Muhammad's allies, the Khuza'a, actually precipitated this conquest. Following the Islamic conquests, Kinanah became a major component of the Arab garrison in Jund Filastin (Palestine), alongside Judham, Lakhm, Khuza'a, Khath'am, and Azd tribes. Caliph Umar appointed two Kinani governors - Alqama ibn Mujazziz in Jerusalem and Alqama ibn Hakim in Lydda around 638-639 CE.

When Crusaders captured Ascalon in 1153, Kinani emirs and fief-holders migrated to Fatimid Egypt, where they were resettled in Damietta by vizier Ṭalāʾīʿ ibn Ruzzīk and became known as the Kinaniyya. Under the Ayyubids, they served as second-tier troops but fled during Louis IX's landing at Damietta in 1249, leading to executions of their commanders by Sultan as-Salih Ayyub. A weakened Kinani presence was still recorded around Mecca in 844/845 CE. Modern descendants of Banu Kinanah are found across Arabia, Iraq, Egypt, Sudan, Palestine, Tunisia, Morocco, Syria, and Yemen, maintaining their status as one of the 'Great Skulls of Arabia' - the historically most powerful pre-Islamic tribes.

migrationPath.description: Migration as part of Arab tribal garrison in Jund Filastin following Muslim conquest

folkLegends.title: Origin from Ishmael
folkLegends.story: According to Islamic tradition, the Kinana and other descendants of Ishmael gradually dispersed throughout northern Arabia after Ishmael married a woman of the Yemenite Jurhum tribe and settled in the vicinity of Mecca, but they lost their original faith and fell into idolatry over time.

name: Banu Abs
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arabian tribe with well-documented pre-Islamic genealogy and warrior traditions
ancestorStory: Abs ibn Baghid was the eponymous ancestor of the tribe, descended from Ghatafan. The tribe became famous for their role in the pre-Islamic War of Dahis and al-Ghabra against their rival Banu Dhubyan.
description: Banu Abs was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the Ghatafan confederation, traditionally based in the Najd region of central Arabia. They are most famous for their involvement in the legendary War of Dahis and al-Ghabra, a prolonged conflict with Banu Dhubyan that became emblematic of pre-Islamic tribal warfare. The tribe produced the famous warrior-poet Antarah ibn Shaddad, one of the authors of the Mu'allaqat. While historically significant in Arabian tribal heritage, they have limited direct relevance to modern GCC tribal structures.

relations.context: Both claim Adnani descent through various branches

history: The Banu Abs are an ancient Arabian tribe belonging to the Northern Adnanite Arabs, with their genealogical line traced as: Abs ibn Baghid ibn Rayth ibn Ghatafan ibn Sa'd ibn Qays ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. Originally inhabiting the northern Hejaz region around what is today called Hurrat Bani Rasheed near Medina, they formed a branch of the powerful Ghatafan confederation and were recognized as one of the Jamarat of the Arabs - the most powerful tribes that took no allegiance to anyone but themselves. The tribe became legendary for their role in the famous 40-year War of Dahis and al-Ghabra against their brother tribe Banu Dhubyan in the 6th century. This conflict, named after the racing horses Dahis (belonging to Qays ibn Zuhayr of Banu Abs) and al-Ghabra (belonging to Hudhayfah ibn Badr of Banu Fazara), began over a disputed horse race and escalated when Qays's brother Malik was killed by Ibn Badr. The tribe produced the renowned warrior-poet Antarah ibn Shaddad (c. 525-615), author of one of the Mu'allaqat and protagonist of the epic romance 'Antar and Abla'. The Banu Abs gained significant political influence during the Umayyad period through the marriage of Caliph Abd al-Malik (r. 685-705) to the Absi noblewoman Wallada, whose sons al-Walid I and Sulayman became caliphs. Her relatives, particularly al-Qa'qa ibn Khulayd, established the town of Hiyar Bani Qa'qa in northwestern Syria and held important governorships. By the Fatimid period, parts of the tribe were expelled to Upper Egypt around 975-996 CE for supporting the Qarmatians. Modern descendants are widely dispersed, with the Bani Rasheed (tracing to 7th-century warrior Rasheed Al-Zaul Al-Absi) found across Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, UAE, Sudan, and Eritrea, while the Al Hubus (Habsi) tribe dominates eastern Oman and parts of UAE, maintaining their reputation for courage and tribal solidarity.

folkLegends.title: The Prophet Khalid ibn Sinan
folkLegends.story: Absi traditions tell of their prophet Khalid ibn Sinan who taught them Biblical monotheism, and the worship of God as 'al-ahad al-samad', prior to Muhammad. It was told that Khalid saved his tribe (accounts differ on how) and that most men of the Abs rejected this prophet at the time. Some have claimed that Khalid prophesied the 'last' prophet after him, who in Islam is Muhammad.

folkLegends.title: Sasanian Battle Tradition
folkLegends.story: Their traditions recall 3,000 of them repelling a Sasanian attack of 20,000.

folkLegends.title: King Zuhayr's Prophecy
folkLegends.story: Another tradition has it that King Zuhayr had predicted Muhammad, but Zuhayr is not called prophet for that.

nameEtymology: Named after their eponymous ancestor Abs ibn Baghid

name: Banu Asad
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribe with documented pre-Islamic lineage and historical significance
ancestorStory: Asad ibn Khuzaymah was a prominent figure among the northern Arab tribes. The tribe traces its lineage through him to the ancient Arab genealogical traditions.
description: Banu Asad was a major ancient Arab tribe of northern Arabia, descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah of the Adnanite lineage. They were prominent in pre-Islamic Arabia and played significant roles in early Islamic conquests and tribal politics. The tribe was known for their warrior culture and nomadic lifestyle across the Arabian Peninsula. While historically important, their direct influence in modern GCC states is limited as many branches merged with other tribal confederations over the centuries.

relations.context: Some branches of Al Muntafiq claim descent from Banu Asad

history:
Banu Asad is an ancient and influential Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzaymah of the Adnanite lineage. Their full genealogy traces through Asad ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan, making them cousins of Prophet Muhammad through their shared ancestor Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah. The tribe gained prominence in the 6th century when they successfully revolted against the Kingdom of Kindah, killing King Hujr (father of the famous poet Imru' al-Qais) and contributing to the kingdom's ultimate collapse after a prolonged war.

In the 7th century, Banu Asad migrated to Iraq and settled primarily in Kufa near the banks of the Euphrates River, with additional settlements in Basra and Ahvaz. During the early Islamic period, the tribe aligned with Ali ibn Abi Talib in the Battle of the Camel and played a crucial role in the Battle of Karbala (680 CE). Many tribal members died alongside Husayn ibn Ali, including prominent figures like Habib ibn Muzahir (commander of the left flank), Muslim ibn Awsaja al-Asadi, and others. The tribe gained lasting honor among Shia Muslims by burying the bodies of Husayn and his companions three days after the massacre on the 13th of Muharram, with the help of Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin.

In 998 CE, Ali ben Mazyad established the virtually independent Mazyadid emirate in the Kufa area, backed by a powerful tribal army. The Mazyadids enjoyed great influence for over a century, acquiring titles and subsidies from the Buyids in return for military services. Their most lasting achievement was founding Hillah in 1101, which became their capital and remains one of Iraq's main cities. The dynasty was known for their generosity to poets and scholars, with historical sources praising their role as 'refuge of those who were in need' and 'shelter for the expectants.'

Today, Banu Asad members are primarily found in Iraqi cities including Baghdad, Basra, Najaf, Kufa, Karbala, Nasiriyah, Amarah, Kut, Hillah, and Diyala. The tribe has several branches outside Iraq: Banu Kahil in Northern Sudan (migrated from Hijaz), communities in Iran's Khuzestan province (Ahvaz) alongside Banu Tamim and other Arab tribes, families in Kuwait (including the Al-Mazeedi who played important roles in Kuwait's constitutional development), branches in Yemen's Hadhramaut region, Oman, and even India's Karnataka state where Persian-speaking members arrived during Tipu Sultan's rule and established the Thokur Jamia Masjid in Mangalore. Modern family names include Assadi, Al-Assadi, and Al-Mazeedi, with both Sunni and Shia branches represented across different regions.

migrationPath.description: Major migration to Iraq in 7th century, settling near Euphrates River banks around Kufa and Karbala

name: Quda'a
legitimacyNotes: Major ancient Arabian tribal confederation with established genealogical legitimacy
ancestorStory: Quda'a was a son of Ma'add, establishing one of the major tribal confederations of ancient Arabia. The confederation encompassed numerous tribes across the Arabian Peninsula.
description: Quda'a was one of the major tribal confederations of pre-Islamic Arabia, comprising numerous tribes across the Arabian Peninsula. They played significant roles in early Islamic conquests and migrations. Many of their constituent tribes later settled in various parts of the Gulf region and Levant. The confederation's influence declined over time as constituent tribes developed independent identities and territories.

relations.context: Some Al Qawasim branches trace lineage to Quda'a confederation
Al Kathiri claim descent from Quda'a tribal confederation

history: The Quda'a (قضاعة) was one of the major tribal confederations of pre-Islamic and early Islamic Arabia, comprising numerous constituent tribes including the prominent Banu Kalb, Tanukh, Juhayna, Bali, and Udhra. The confederation's genealogical origins were complex and politically motivated - initially they claimed descent from the northern Arabian ancestor Ma'add (making them Adnanite), but during the Second Muslim Civil War (683-692 CE), they made a pivotal genealogical realignment around 685-690 CE, abandoning their claimed Adnanite lineage in favor of southern Arabian Himyarite descent to forge the Yaman faction alliance against their Qaysi rivals. This controversial genealogical shift was politically motivated to strengthen their position but sparked centuries of scholarly debate among Arab genealogists. The confederation's territories spanned northwestern Arabia and Syria, with constituent tribes like the Banu Kalb having been present in Syria for centuries before Islam's advent in the 630s, and tribes like the Udhra dwelling in the Wadi al-Qura region in the northern Hejaz near the southern approaches of Syria. During the early Islamic conquests, the Quda'a played crucial roles in the conquest of Egypt (639-641) under Amr ibn al-As, with many tribes relocating there and later migrating southward into Sudan during the Fatimid and Mamluk periods. The confederation reached its zenith under the Umayyad Caliphate (661-750), particularly during Mu'awiya's rule, who married Maysun bint Bahdal of the Kalb tribe and granted the Quda'a privileged positions, yearly stipends, and consultation rights. The confederation's influence gradually declined after the Umayyad period as constituent tribes developed independent identities, though some remained prominent into the 11th century around Damascus. The Quda'a were involved in various conflicts with other tribal confederations, including raids by constituent tribes like the Banu Sulaym under al-Abbas ibn Mirdas against 'the tribes of Zubayd and Quda'a' in Yemen, indicating both internal complexity and external conflicts within the broader tribal landscape of pre-Islamic Arabia.

migrationPath.description: Bali and other Quda'a tribes transferred to Egypt during Muslim conquest under Amr ibn al-As

folkLegends.title: Genealogical Realignment Legend
folkLegends.story: During the Second Muslim Civil War, the Quda'a abandoned their claimed descent from the northern Ma'add ancestor in favor of southern Arabian Himyarite lineage to forge the Yaman faction alliance against their Qaysi rivals. This genealogical shift was consolidated around 685-690 CE.

name: Banu Kalb
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribe with established lineage tracing to Qudaa
ancestorStory: Kalb ibn Wabrah was the progenitor of the tribe, part of the Qudaa tribal confederation. The tribe became prominent in Syria and the northern Arabian Peninsula during pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods.
description: Banu Kalb was a major Arab tribe of Qahtani origin that dominated parts of Syria and northern Arabia during the pre-Islamic and early Islamic periods. They played a significant role in early Islamic conquests and politics, notably through their alliance with the Umayyad dynasty. The tribe was particularly influential during the reign of Caliph Marwan ibn al-Hakam, whose wife was from Banu Kalb. While historically centered in the Levant, branches of the tribe spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and some segments are found in modern Gulf states.

relations.context: Both trace descent to Qahtani lineage through Qudaa confederation

history:
The Banu Kalb (Arabic: بنو كلب, 'Sons of Kalb') was a major Arab tribe of Qahtani origin that emerged as a dominant force in northwestern Arabia and central Syria from the 4th century CE onwards. According to Arab genealogical tradition, the tribe descended from Kalb ibn Wabara, whose name means 'dog' in Arabic. The tribe was part of the larger Quda'a tribal confederation, with all branches descending from Rufayda ibn Thawr ibn Kalb, except for three small clans. The most prominent branch was the Banu Abdallah ibn Kinana, particularly the Banu Janab subbranch, which provided the tribe's chiefs and included the aristocratic Banu Haritha ibn Janab family.

In pre-Islamic times, the Kalb dominated the al-Jawf depression, including Dumat al-Jandal oasis, and controlled the vast Samawa desert between Syria and Mesopotamia. Under the legendary chieftain Zuhayr ibn Janab in the early 6th century, the tribe wielded significant influence among northern Arabian Bedouins, destroying the Ghatafan's rival sanctuary to the Ka'aba and conducting military campaigns for the Aksumite ruler Abraha against the Taghlib and Bakr tribes. As Byzantine foederati, the Kalb embraced Monophysite Christianity and were charged with guarding the empire's eastern frontier against Sassanian Persia and the Lakhmids.

The tribe's political prominence peaked during the early Islamic period through strategic alliances with the Umayyad dynasty. Key figures like al-Asbagh ibn Amr of Dumat al-Jandal established early pacts with Prophet Muhammad around 627-628, though most of the tribe remained Christian at Muhammad's death in 632. The Kalb's neutrality during the Muslim conquest of Syria (633-638) and subsequent conversion positioned them as crucial allies for the emerging Islamic state. Mu'awiya ibn Abi Sufyan, the future Umayyad caliph, forged marital ties with the tribe, notably marrying Maysun, daughter of the preeminent chieftain Bahdal ibn Unayf.

These marriages became the foundation of Umayyad power, with the Kalb receiving significant privileges including consultation rights in major decisions and annual stipends for 2,000 nobles. The tribe's military support proved decisive at the Battle of Siffin (657) and later at the crucial Battle of Marj Rahit (684), where they defeated their main rivals, the Qays, establishing the long-running Yaman-Qays factional conflict. Under the Marwanid Umayyads, the Kalb remained influential until the pro-Qaysite Marwan II (744-750) marginalized them, leading to their gradual political decline under the Abbasid Caliphate.

After the mid-11th century, the Kalb's influence waned as they transitioned from nomadism to sedentarism, particularly in the Hauran region. With the exception of occasional mentions as allies of Turkish atabegs or caravan raiders, the Bedouin tribes largely disappeared from the political map by the end of the 11th century. The Kalb continued its shift to sedentarism into the 12th century, especially in the Hauran, and after this point, the tribe no longer appears consistently in the historical record as a major political force.

folkLegends.title: Zuhayr ibn Janab's Extraordinary Longevity
folkLegends.story: According to Arab legends, Zuhayr ibn Janab lived to 450 years of age, and his grandfather Hubal lived to 650. Zuhayr was recorded as one of the muʿammarūn (centenarians) and was noted in Arab fables for his extremely long life.

folkLegends.title: The Destruction of Ghatafan's Sacred Sanctuary
folkLegends.story: When the Ghatafan tribe constructed a haram (religious sanctuary) for their idols at a place called 'Buss' to rival the Ka'aba in Mecca, Zuhayr ibn Janab resolved to destroy it. He exclaimed 'By God, that will never happen while I am alive! I will never leave Ghatafan alone while they take a haram', then gathered his Kalbi tribesmen, defeated the Ghatafan and destroyed their sanctuary.
folkLegends.source: Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani and traditional Arab sources

folkLegends.title: Zuhayr's Death by Suicide
folkLegends.story: Zuhayr ibn Janab is said to have killed himself by drinking unmixed wine after being disobeyed by his followers.

nameEtymology: Kalb means 'dog' in Arabic. According to genealogical tradition, Kalb's mother Asma bint Duraym was known as Umm al-Asbu (lit. 'mother of wild animals') because all of her children were named after wild animals.

name: Banu Kinda
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal lineage, held kingship over various Arab tribes in pre-Islamic era
ancestorStory: Descended from Kinda ibn Afsa ibn Adi ibn Haritha ibn Amr ibn Amir, establishing one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederations. The tribe produced the famous Kindite kings who ruled over much of central and eastern Arabia.
description: Banu Kinda was one of the most powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederations, originating from Yemen and later establishing kingdoms across central and eastern Arabia. They were known for their royal lineage and produced several kings who ruled over other Arab tribes in Najd and the eastern Arabian Peninsula during the 5th and 6th centuries CE. The confederation played a crucial role in pre-Islamic Arabian politics and warfare, with branches settling throughout the Arabian Peninsula. While the original confederation dissolved after the rise of Islam, various Kindite clans integrated into other tribal structures across the Gulf region.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtani lineage
Historical presence in eastern Arabian Peninsula

history:
The Banu Kinda, also known as simply Kinda or Kindah (Arabic: كِنْدَة), was one of the most prominent pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederations, originating from South Arabia in the region west of Hadramawt as early as the 3rd century CE. The tribe's genealogy traced back through Kinda ibn Afsa (nicknamed Thawr ibn Ufayr) to the semi-legendary Kahlan, making them ethnically distinct from non-Arab South Arabians like the Himyar. The Kinda consisted of three principal branches: the Banu Mu'awiya (the leading branch), the Sakun, and the Sakasik, with the latter two often grouped together as the Ashras. From the Banu Mu'awiya descended the royal households, including the Banu Akil al-Murar who would rule in central Arabia, and the Banu Wali'a in Hadramawt.

Initially serving as Bedouin auxiliaries to the Sabaean Kingdom, the Kinda later allied with the Himyarite Kingdom under the Jewish king Dhu Nuwas in the early 6th century. In the mid-5th century, part of the tribe migrated northward with Himyarite support and established the Kingdom of Kinda over the Ma'add tribal confederation in central and northern Arabia. This kingdom was founded by Hujr, who established the Banu Akil al-Murar royal household, and reached its zenith under his grandson al-Harith ibn Amr. Al-Harith captured the Lakhmid capital of al-Hira and became a Byzantine federate, but the kingdom began to fragment when he divided rule among his four sons - Hujr, Ma'dikarib, Shurahbil, and Salama.

Internal strife among al-Harith's sons led to the kingdom's collapse by the late 6th century. The brothers became absorbed in their constituents' blood feuds, with several Kindite kings being killed in internecine fighting. The decisive blow came with defeats at battles including Shi'b Jabala (circa 550-580 CE), which forced approximately 30,000 Kindites to abandon their settlements in Najd and Yamama and return to Hadramawt. The Kindite migration included departures from their major settlements of Ghamr Dhi Kinda in Najd and Hajar and al-Mushaqqar in the Yamama.

The Kinda accepted Islam during Muhammad's lifetime, with leaders like the Banu Wali'a and al-Ash'ath ibn Qays sending deputations to the Prophet. Muhammad granted the Wali'a a designated portion of tax revenue from Hadramawt. However, the tribe rebelled during the Ridda Wars (632-633 CE) when the Muslim governor Ziyad ibn Labid al-Ansari withheld their designated tax revenues. Al-Ash'ath emerged as the most powerful Kindite chief during this period, leading resistance from the fortress of Nujayr before ultimately being defeated and pardoned. After the Ridda Wars, surviving Kindite leaders like al-Ash'ath played important roles in the early Muslim conquests and held prominent positions throughout the Caliphate, serving as governors and military commanders in Iraq, Syria, Armenia, and Azerbaijan.

migrationPath.description: Kinda migration from South Arabia to establish kingdom over Ma'add confederation with Himyarite support
Return migration of approximately 30,000 Kindites to ancestral homeland after kingdom's collapse

folkLegends.title: The Founding of Kindite Rule over Ma'add
folkLegends.story: According to medieval Arabic literary sources, the subordination of the nomadic tribes of Ma'add to Kindite rule was initiated by the Ma'add themselves, especially the Bakr ibn Wa'il division, to bring order to constantly feuding constituent tribes. The Bakr sent envoys to the king of Himyar, inviting him to be their king. Instead, the king delegated the role to Hujr for unclear reasons, leading to the establishment of Kindite monarchy over central Arabia.
folkLegends.source: medieval Arabic literary works of al-Isfahani and Ibn Habib

nameEtymology: The name 'Kinda' was a nickname for the tribe's progenitor, Thawr ibn Ufayr, according to genealogical traditions

name: Banu Lakhm
legitimacyNotes: Ancient Arab tribal confederation with royal dynasty ruling the Lakhmid Kingdom
ancestorStory: Descended from Lakhm ibn Adi ibn Haritha ibn Amr ibn Amir, tracing back to the Azd. They established the powerful Lakhmid Kingdom in southern Iraq as vassals of the Sassanian Empire.
description: Banu Lakhm was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that established the Lakhmid Kingdom in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. They served as vassals to the Sassanian Empire and controlled important trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. The Lakhmids were known for their capital at Hira and played a crucial role in Arab-Persian relations before Islam. The confederation dissolved after the Islamic conquests, with descendants scattered across various regions including parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

relations.context: Both trace descent from Qahtanite origins in southern Iraq region

history: The Banu Lakhm was a major pre-Islamic Arab tribal confederation that established the powerful Lakhmid Kingdom in southern Iraq from the 3rd to 7th centuries CE. Founded by Amr ibn Adi ibn Nasr around 293-302 CE, they ruled from their capital at al-Hira as vassals of the Sassanian Empire, controlling crucial trade routes between Arabia and Mesopotamia. The Lakhmids played a pivotal role in Arab-Persian relations, with notable rulers including al-Mundhir III (r. 503-554) who extended Sassanian influence in southern and western Arabia, and engaged in wars with the Byzantine-allied Ghassanids of Syria. The kingdom ended with the assassination of the last king, al-Nu'man III (r. 580-602), by Sassanian emperor Khosrow II. A separate branch of the Lakhm had settled in Syria by the 4th century, initially as Byzantine allies alongside the Judham and Amila tribes, dwelling in the southern Levant from Tabuk through the desert regions east of the Arabah Valley and Dead Sea. During the Islamic conquest of Syria, Lakhm tribesmen fought on both Byzantine and Muslim sides at the Battle of Yarmouk in 636. Following the conquest, the Lakhm became closely associated with the Judham and formed a key component of the Arab tribal soldiery in Jund Filastin (military district of Palestine) under the Umayyad Caliphate. By the 9th-10th centuries, notable figures still claimed Lakhmid descent, including scholar Sulayman ibn Ahmad al-Tabarani of Tiberias, maintaining the prestige of their connection to the former kings of al-Hira.

name: Banu Taghlib
legitimacyNotes: Ancient tribal lineage tracing to Adnanite origins, historically powerful in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia
ancestorStory: Taghlib ibn Wa'il was the eponymous ancestor of the tribe, part of the larger Bakr wa Taghlib confederation. The tribe became known for their fierce independence and rejection of tribute payments.
description: Banu Taghlib was a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribe of Adnanite origin based in northern Arabia and Mesopotamia. They were known for their fierce independence, military prowess, and their famous refusal to pay tribute, preferring to pay double the jizya rather than zakat to maintain their autonomy. The tribe had a long-standing rivalry with their cousins Banu Bakr, leading to the famous Basus War that lasted for decades. While historically significant in Arabian tribal politics, they had limited direct influence in the modern Gulf states region.

relations.context: Descended from Adnanite lineage through Rabi'a ibn Nizar

history:
The Banu Taghlib were a powerful pre-Islamic Arab tribe of Adnanite origin, descending from Taghlib ibn Wa'il through the genealogical line: Taghlib ibn Wāʾil ibn Qasit ibn Hinb ibn Afṣā ibn Duʿmī ibn Jadīla ibn Asad ibn Rabīʿa ibn Nizār ibn Maʿadd ibn Adnān. Originally Bedouin inhabitants of the Najd, they belonged to the Rabi'a confederation and were among the most powerful and cohesive nomadic tribes of pre-Islamic Arabia. The tribe was organized around the al-Araqim, descendants of six sons of Bakr ibn Hubayb ibn Amr ibn Ghanm, with the largest divisions being Jusham and Malik, collectively known as al-Rawkān ('the two horns'). In the late 5th century, the assassination of their chieftain Kulaib ibn Rabiah by Jassas ibn Murra al-Shaybani of Banu Bakr precipitated the famous Basus War, a forty-year conflict that ended with the Taghlib's decisive defeat at Yawm al-Tahaluq, forcing the bulk of the tribe to flee Najd for the Lower Euphrates region. The tribe migrated further north to Upper Mesopotamia (Jazira) after their chieftain Amr ibn Kulthum assassinated the Lakhmid king Amr ibn al-Hind in 568 CE.

Unlike most Arab tribes, the Banu Taghlib embraced Miaphysite Christianity and remained largely Christian long after the advent of Islam. During the early Muslim period, they initially opposed the Muslims during the Ridda Wars (632-633) but later switched allegiance while retaining their Christian faith. Notably, they refused to pay jizya like other Christian subjects and successfully negotiated with Caliph Umar to pay zakat or sadaqah instead, arguing they were Arabs and should be treated like Muslims. During the Umayyad period (661-750), they allied with the Umayyads against the Qaysi tribes, with their poet al-Akhtal serving as the official court poet. Mass conversion to Islam occurred in the second half of the 9th century during Abbasid rule, partly through the persuasion of Malik ibn Tawk. The tribe reached its political zenith through the Hamdanid dynasty, descendants of the Adi ibn Usama line, who established autonomous emirates in Mosul (930s) and Aleppo (945) until their demise in 1002. After the early Hamdanid period, the Taghlib disappeared from historical records as a distinct tribal entity, with modern sources suggesting their descendants assimilated into the Anizah tribe, specifically the Banu Bishr branch in the Najd region.

folkLegends.title: The Basus War Origin
folkLegends.story: The forty-year Basus War began when Kulaib ibn Rabiah of Taghlib killed a camel belonging to a woman named Basus, who was related to Jassas ibn Murrah of Banu Bakr. The lament of Basus, presented poetically, provoked Jassas to kill Kulaib in revenge, setting off retaliatory violence between the two tribes that lasted for decades.

nameEtymology: Named after the progenitor Taghlib ibn Wa'il, also known as Dithar ibn Wa'il

name: Banu Uqayl
legitimacyNotes: Descended from Uqayl ibn Ka'b ibn Rabi'ah, established authority through military prowess and territorial control
ancestorStory: Uqayl was a prominent leader of the Rabi'ah tribal group in pre-Islamic Arabia. His descendants became known for their warrior traditions and established themselves across the Arabian Peninsula and Iraq.
description: Banu Uqayl was a major Bedouin tribe of Adnani lineage that dominated large parts of central and eastern Arabia, as well as Iraq, during the medieval period. They established the Uqaylid dynasty which ruled parts of Iraq and Syria from the 10th to 12th centuries. The tribe was known for its military capabilities and played significant roles in early Islamic conquests. Today, their descendants are scattered across the Arabian Peninsula, with some branches integrated into modern Gulf tribal structures.

relations.context: Both tribes had presence in Iraq and eastern Arabia
Traditional competition for grazing lands and territorial control

history:
Banu Uqayl was a major Arab tribe belonging to the Banu Ka'b branch of the large Banu Amir confederation, which had its original homeland in the western Arabian Peninsula on the border between the Hejaz and the Najd. The tribe traced its Adnani lineage through the genealogy: Uqayl ibn Ka'b ibn Rabi'ah ibn Amir ibn Sa'sa'ah ibn Mu'awiyah ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin. The Uqayl branch moved southwards from their original territory and settled in the large valley known as al-Aqiq (modern-day Wadi al-Dawasir), which they later claimed was granted to them by the Prophet Muhammad. During the Abbasid Caliphate, most of the Banu Amir moved from the Najd into Iraq and Syria, with the Uqaylids being among the last to leave.

Settling on the banks of the Euphrates, a section of Banu Uqayl founded the Uqaylid dynasty in the 10th century, which controlled Mosul and other regions of Upper Mesopotamia while remaining largely nomadic. When the Uqaylid dynasty fell, three large Uqaylid tribes - the Khafaja, the Ubadah, and al-Muntafiq - settled in Lower Mesopotamia and remain there to the present day. Another section of the Uqayl, possibly coming from Iraq according to Ibn Khaldun, took over the deserts of eastern Arabia around al-Ahsa Oasis, where they allied with the Qarmatians like many other Banu Amir groups.

After the Qarmatians fell to the Uyunid dynasty in 1076, Usfur ibn Rashid deposed the Uyunids in the mid-13th century and founded the Usfurid dynasty, which lasted until 1330. The most powerful Uqaylid dynasty were the Jabrids, who rose in the early 15th century when Ajwad ibn Zamil's elder brother deposed the last Jarwanid ruler in Qatif. At their height, the Jabrids controlled the entire Arabian coast on the Persian Gulf, including the islands of Bahrain, and regularly led expeditions into central Arabia. One contemporary scholar described Ajwad ibn Zamil as 'the king of al-Ahsa and Qatif and the leader of the people of Najd.' The Jabrids lost Bahrain to the Portuguese in 1521 and their kingdom collapsed soon afterwards, though one branch remained active in Oman for nearly three centuries.

The fate of the non-Omani Jabrids remains uncertain. Some historians believe they are identical with the 'Jubur' section of the Bani Khalid, who eventually took control of the region after the Jabrids. The Bani Khalid tribe itself is believed to be partly of Uqaylid origin. Notable members of Banu Uqayl throughout history included Ibrahim ibn Asim al-'Uqayli (Umayyad commander and governor of Sistan), Ishaq ibn Muslim al-Uqayli (Umayyad commander and governor of Arminiya), Layla al-Akhyaliyya (Umayyad classical poet), Muhammad ibn al-Musayyab (chieftain and founder of Uqaylid Emirate), and Ibn Mulhim (Fatimid commander and governor of Aleppo).

timelineEvents.title: Rise of Jabrids
timelineEvents.description: Ajwad ibn Zamil's elder brother deposed last Jarwanid ruler and founded Jabrid dynasty

timelineEvents.title: Death of Ajwad ibn Zamil
timelineEvents.description: Death of most prominent Jabrid ruler who controlled entire Arabian Gulf coast

timelineEvents.title: Portuguese Conquest of Bahrain
timelineEvents.description: Jabrids lost Bahrain to Portuguese, kingdom collapsed soon afterwards

folkLegends.title: Grant of al-Aqiq Valley
folkLegends.story: The Uqayl branch claimed that the large valley known as al-Aqiq (modern day Wadi al-Dawasir) was granted to them by the Prophet Muhammad when they settled there after moving southwards from their original homeland.

name: Banu Murrah
legitimacyNotes: Traditional Bedouin tribal authority based on genealogical descent and customary leadership
ancestorStory: Descended from Murrah, a tribal progenitor traced through various Arabian genealogical lines. The tribe claims ancient Arabian heritage with roots in the central Arabian Peninsula.
description: Banu Murrah is a Bedouin tribe historically based in the central and eastern Arabian Peninsula, with presence extending into modern-day Saudi Arabia and the UAE. They were traditionally nomadic pastoralists who migrated seasonally across tribal territories. The tribe maintained traditional Bedouin social structures and played a role in regional tribal politics during the pre-modern period. Today, members of the tribe are settled citizens integrated into modern Gulf state societies while maintaining tribal identity.

relations.context: Both tribes share the Murrah ancestral connection and similar naming conventions

history: The Al Murrah (Arabic: آل مرة) are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe, which belongs to the Qahtanite branch of Arabian tribes, specifically to Banu Hamdan from southwestern Arabia. According to the research, Banu Yam was the progenitor tribe of both Al Murrah and the Ujman tribes of eastern Saudi Arabia and the Arabian Gulf coast. The tribe's genealogical lineage traces through the Qahtanite rather than Adnanite branch, distinguishing them from many other prominent Arabian tribes. Historically, the Al Murrah were camel-herding nomads who controlled and travelled through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula, covering as much as 3,000 kilometers annually in their seasonal migrations. Seven clans make up the Saudi branch of the Al Murrah, comprising approximately 15,000 individuals. The tribe developed a distinctive Arabic dialect that is part of the southern Najdi group, sharing linguistic characteristics with their parent tribe Banu Yam, including some south Arabian features like the '-ish' feminine suffix that differs from neighboring east Arabian bedouin dialects. The Al Murrah maintained extensive territorial control across southeastern Saudi Arabia, the Jafurah desert south of Al-Ahsa, parts of the Empty Quarter (Rub' al Khali), and extending into Qatar and the UAE. Their traditional territory was described as being as vast as France, encompassing areas from the Dahna sands in the west to the borders with Oman in the east, and from the southern borders of Al-Ahsa province down into the central Empty Quarter. In Qatar, the Al Murrah historically made up a large proportion of the ethnic population but experienced significant political upheaval in recent decades. In 1885, some Al Murrah members renounced allegiance to Qatar and departed, later facing military confrontation with loyalist forces. A majority of the tribe supported Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani, the former Emir deposed in 1995, leading to political tensions with the new government. Eight months after the 1995 coup, 119 Al Murrah members attempted to overthrow the new Emir Sheikh Hamad Al Thani but failed, resulting in death sentences for 19 and life imprisonment for 33 in February 2000, though none were executed. In 2017, Qatar revoked citizenship of a sheikh and 54 tribespeople, and they were barred from the 2021 Qatari general election, leading to protests. The tribe maintains friendly relations with UAE and Saudi Arabia while opposing the current Iranian regime.

migrationPath.description: Post-Islamic expansion eastward following trade routes and seeking new pastures
Migration into Rub al-Khali seeking refuge from Saudi expansion and accessing date palm oases
Settlement along Gulf coast engaging in pearling industry and coastal trade

name: Banu Zayd
legitimacyNotes: Lineage-based tribal authority through descent from Zayd
ancestorStory: Descended from Zayd, with multiple branches claiming this lineage across different regions. The name represents one of the most common tribal appellations in Arabian genealogy.
description: Banu Zayd represents one of several tribal groups claiming descent from an ancestor named Zayd, a common name in Arabian tribal genealogy. Various branches of this lineage have been documented across the Arabian Peninsula, with some settling in areas that later became part of modern GCC states. The tribe primarily engaged in traditional Bedouin pastoralism and followed seasonal migration patterns. Most Banu Zayd branches have been absorbed into larger tribal confederations or have become dormant as distinct political entities.
history:
Banu Zayd represents a Najdi tribal lineage that traces its roots to Zayd ibn Ali, who settled in the town of Shaqra in the Najd region of central Arabia. This represents one specific branch among several tribal groups across the Arabian Peninsula that claim descent from ancestors named Zayd, which was a common name in Arabian genealogy. The research does not provide details about the broader genealogical classification (Adnani vs Qahtani) or sub-tribal divisions of this particular Banu Zayd branch.

Unlike many tribal appellations that have become dormant as distinct political entities, this Najdi Banu Zayd branch has maintained significant prominence in modern Saudi Arabia through its members' contributions to religious scholarship, banking, and Islamic institutions. The tribe has produced several notable figures including Muhammad bin Abdul Karim Al-Issa, who serves as Secretary General of the Muslim World League, and members of the Al-Rajhi banking family such as Saleh Abdul Aziz Al Rajhi, co-founder of Al-Rajhi Bank.

The tribe has also contributed significantly to Islamic scholarship and religious leadership. Prominent religious figures from Banu Zayd include Bakr Abu Zayd (1944-2008), a leading Salafi scholar and member of both the Saudi Council of Senior Scholars and the Permanent Committee for Islamic Research and Issuing Fatwas, who served as President of the International Islamic Fiqh Academy from 1985 to 2008. Other notable religious scholars include Abdullah ibn Jibreen, a Saudi Islamic jurist, and Saud Al-Shuraim, who serves as one of the imams and preachers of al-Masjid al-Haram in Mecca.

The research indicates that this particular Banu Zayd branch has successfully adapted to the modern Saudi state structure while maintaining its tribal identity, with members achieving prominence in both religious institutions and the business sector, particularly in Islamic banking through the Al-Rajhi family's establishment of one of the world's largest Islamic banks.

migrationPath.description: Early Islamic expansion from western Arabia to central Najd regions
Eastward migration during Umayyad period seeking new grazing lands and trade opportunities
Settlement in Omani interior regions during Abbasid period fragmentation

name: Al Sulaimi
legitimacyNotes: Traditional tribal authority based on genealogical descent and consensus
ancestorStory: Al Sulaimi trace their lineage to Sulaim ibn Mansour from the Qays Aylan branch. They are part of the broader Bani Sulaim confederation that spread across Arabia.
description: Al Sulaimi are a tribal branch descended from the larger Bani Sulaim confederation, traditionally based in western Arabia with some branches migrating to the Gulf region. They maintained nomadic pastoral traditions while some sections settled in oasis communities. The tribe has branches present in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states, maintaining their tribal identity and customs. They remain active in contemporary Gulf society while preserving their genealogical connections to the broader Sulaimi lineage.

relations.context: Both tribes involved in nomadic movements across Arabian Peninsula
Historical alliances among Adnani tribes in central Arabia

history: Al Sulaimi are a tribal branch descended from the larger Bani Sulaim confederation, tracing their lineage to Sulaim ibn Mansour from the Qays Aylan branch within the Adnani genealogical tree. According to Islamic tradition, this places them among the Adnanite Arabs who trace their descent through Adnan to Ishmael, son of Abraham, distinguishing them from the Qahtanite Arabs of southern Arabia. The Qays Aylan were a significant tribal confederation within the Mudar branch of Adnanites, historically inhabiting northern, western, and central Arabia. Research reveals a particularly significant connection between Al Sulaimi and the Al Bin Ali tribe, who used the distinctive Al Sulaimi Flag until the 1960s across Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait. This flag, consisting of four red and three white stripes with seven red triangles facing west, became a symbol of considerable political importance throughout the Gulf region and notably influenced the current flags of Bahrain and Qatar. The Al Bin Ali, who were associated with the Utub confederation, were politically important and moved between Qatar and Bahrain, becoming the original dominant group in the Zubarah area. They were renowned for their wealth, courage, and practical independence in Bahrain, where they maintained self-governance and were uniquely exempt from paying taxes to the ruling Al Khalifa. The Al Sulaimi flag held deep cultural significance and was prominently displayed on ships during wartime and pearl diving seasons, as well as during special occasions including weddings, Eid celebrations, and war dances (Ardha). The flag's political importance extended beyond the Al Bin Ali, as it was also used by the Al-Khalifa and Al-Sabah ruling families when they were in Kuwait during the mid-eighteenth century, demonstrating the widespread recognition and respect for Al Sulaimi identity across the Gulf region.

migrationPath.description: Early Abbasid period migration of Sulaimi clans eastward following trade routes and seeking new grazing lands
Al Sulaimi families moved to coastal areas of eastern Arabia for pearling and maritime trade opportunities

timelineEvents.title: Al Sulaimi Flag Usage by Ruling Families
timelineEvents.description: Al-Khalifa and Al-Sabah used the Al Sulaimi Flag when they were in Kuwait during the mid-eighteenth century

timelineEvents.title: End of Al Sulaimi Flag Usage
timelineEvents.description: The Al Bin Ali tribe discontinued use of the Al Sulaimi Flag across Bahrain, Qatar, and Kuwait
